   • or the full log book (V5C) if it’s already in your name  
3. Enter the 12-digit reference number and follow the prompts to set up a Direct Debit or make a card payment.

If you don’t pay on time  
• DVLA will send an automatic reminder letter.  
• An £80 penalty is issued if the tax is late (reduced to £40 if you pay within 28 days).  
• The car can be clamped or removed, and you may be taken to court, where the fine can rise to £1,000 plus back-dated tax.

Keep the Direct Debit in place or make sure you renew promptly each year to avoid penalties.

More details: https://www.gov.uk/vehicle-tax

prompt: What should I do if I need to apply for an Accessibility Certificate for my bus to carry disabled passengers?
generated_response:
To run a bus or coach in the UK that carries fare-paying disabled passengers you must hold a Public Service Vehicle (PSV) Accessibility Certificate (sometimes called a PSVAR certificate) for each vehicle.  
Follow these steps:

1. Check the vehicle is in scope  
   • Your bus or coach must have more than 22 passenger seats and be used on a public service or scheduled school/college run.  
   • It must meet the technical requirements in the Public Service Vehicle Accessibility Regulations 2000 (PSVAR) – for example wheelchair-accessible entrance, priority seating, visual and audible next-stop information (where required).  

2. Gather the evidence  
   • Manufacturer’s documentation that the vehicle, or any equipment you have fitted (ramps, lifts, destination displays, etc.), complies with PSVAR.  
   • Vehicle details: registration number, VIN (chassis number), seating capacity and date of first use.

3. Book an accessibility inspection  
   • Contact the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) to arrange an inspection at an Authorised Testing Facility (ATF) – you can combine this with the vehicle’s annual PSV roadworthiness test if convenient.  
   • Tell the ATF you also need a PSVAR inspection so they can allocate the extra time slot.  

4. Apply and pay the fee  
   • Complete DVSA form PSVA1 (or the latest version specified by DVSA).  
   • Pay the current fee (about £80) – the ATF normally collects this on DVSA’s behalf.  

5. Pass the inspection  
   • A DVSA vehicle examiner will check doors, ramps/lifts, wheelchair spaces, handrails, seat heights, destination equipment and on-board information systems.  
   • If everything is compliant, the examiner issues a Pass statement.

6. Receive and keep the certificate  
   • DVSA will post you the PSVAR Accessibility Certificate (blue A4 sheet) within a few days.  
   • Keep the original on the vehicle or in the operator’s licence records – you must produce it on request during roadside or depot checks.  

7. If the vehicle is modified later  
   • Any change that affects accessibility (e.g. seating layout, new lift) means you must arrange a re-inspection and obtain an updated certificate.

Useful links  
• Overview of bus and coach compliance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving  
• DVSA enquiries (PSV accessibility): enquiries@dvsa.gov.uk or 0300 123 9000

prompt: I need to find a driving test centre near me in Scotland. How do I use my postcode to locate the nearest one and what information should I look for?
generated_response:
To see which practical driving test centres are closest to you:

1. Go to the GOV.UK service and search for “driving test centres”, or use this page directly: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving  
2. Select “Book your driving test”.  
3. On the booking page choose “Find a driving test centre”.  
4. Type your home postcode (for example “EH1 2AA”) and press ‘Search’.  
5. You’ll get a list of the nearest centres. Pick one to see:  
   • full address and map link  
   • the next available test dates and times  
   • phone number and email (if you need to rearrange)  
   • facilities (parking, toilets, wheelchair access)  
   • any special instructions, such as where to wait or meet the examiner.  

Make a note of:
• the exact address and postcode – you’ll need this for the day of your test  
• arrival instructions (most centres ask you to arrive no more than 10 minutes early)  
• what facilities are or are not available (for example, no toilets at some rural sites)  
• the reference number you’ll get when you book.  

If you can’t find a suitable slot, check nearby postcodes or look again later – new appointments are added regularly.

prompt: How can I find out if my local council offers any subsidised transport options for students aged 16 to 19?
generated_response:
To find out whether your council offers subsidised transport for 16- to 19-year-old students, follow these steps.

1. Check the national scheme first  
   • If you live in Scotland and are under 22, you can already travel free on most local and long-distance buses with a Young Scot National Entitlement Card.  
   • Apply (or update an existing card) on your council’s website or at a participating Post Office.

2. Look on your council’s website  
   • Go to your council’s homepage and search for “school or college transport”, “post-16 travel”, or “Learner Travel”.  
   • Download any guidance note or policy.  Many councils list:  
     – Free or discounted bus passes for full-time students  
     – Mileage payments or taxi arrangements for rural areas  
     – How household income or distance from college affects eligibility.

3. Contact the council directly  
   • Phone or email the Education or Transport department.  
   • Have the course details, your age and home-to-college distance ready—they will ask.

4. Ask your college or sixth-form support team  
   • Colleges often administer bursaries or travel vouchers funded by the council or the Scottish Funding Council.  
   • They can confirm current local deals and help with any forms.

5. If you need further help  
   • Visit transport.gov.scot, search “Young Persons’ Free Bus Travel”.  
   • If you still can’t find an answer, phone the council’s general enquiries line and ask to speak to “school transport (post-16)”.

prompt: I'm 17 and want to ride a motorcycle. Do I need to take CBT first, and if so, what are the rules around riding after completing it?
generated_response:
Yes. At 17 you must complete Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) before you ride on public roads.

What CBT lets you do
1. Ride a motorcycle or scooter up to 125 cc (maximum power 11 kW) for 2 years.
2. You must display L-plates (D-plates in Wales), cannot carry passengers and cannot ride on motorways.
3. The CBT certificate (DL196) expires after 2 years. To keep riding you must either:
   • repeat CBT, or  
   • pass the theory test and practical tests for an A1, A2 or A licence.

Next steps
1. Book a CBT course with an approved training body (ATB).  
2. Take your UK provisional licence (or full car licence) to the course.  
3. On passing, keep the DL196 with you when riding.  
4. Decide before the 2-year expiry whether to take full motorcycle tests or redo CBT.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/motorcycle-cbt

prompt: I am planning to ride my motorcycle with a visor. Are there specific regulations about what type of visor or goggles I can use?
generated_response:
When you ride in the UK you can use either a visor fitted to the helmet or separate goggles, but they must meet the legal safety standard and be suitable for the conditions.  

1. Safety standard  
   • Look for one of these marks on the visor / goggles:  
     – A British Standard “BSI Kitemark” (usually BS 4110 or BS EN 1938).  
     – An “E” mark in a circle followed by a number (ECE Regulation 22).  
   Any of these shows the eye-protection has passed the required impact- and abrasion-tests.

2. Condition  
   • The surface must be clear, not badly scratched or crazed.  
   • Replace it if vision is noticeably reduced.

3. Tinted or dark visors  
   • They are legal in daylight, but the tint must still let through at least 50 % of visible light if you ride at night or in poor visibility.  
   • Very dark “race” visors (less than 50 % light transmission) must not be used on the public road after dark or in fog, heavy rain, etc.

4. Fit and use  
   • Make sure the visor/goggles seal properly with your helmet to stop wind and debris.  
   • Carry a clear spare if you expect to ride after dusk.

Further guidance: GOV.UK – Motorcycle helmets: the law  
https://www.gov.uk/motorcycle-helmet-law

prompt: I want to ride an e-scooter in the UK. Do I need a special licence and what are the requirements?
generated_response:
You do not need a “special” licence for an e-scooter, but you must hold at least a Category Q entitlement on your driving licence (this is included automatically in a provisional or full UK car/motorcycle licence).

Key points to know

1. Where you can ride  
   • Public roads, cycle lanes and tracks – only on approved rental e-scooters in the official trial areas.  
   • Pavements – not allowed.  
   • Private e-scooters – you can only use them on private land with the landowner’s permission. Using a privately-owned e-scooter on public roads or spaces is illegal.

2. Licence and age  
   • Minimum age: 16.  
   • Licence: a provisional or full UK driving licence (categories AM, A or B) automatically includes Category Q, so that is enough. You do not need to take an extra test.

3. Insurance and tax  
   • Rental e-scooters are covered by the operator’s insurance.  
   • You do not need to arrange vehicle tax or MOT.

4. Safety rules  
   • Recommended: wear a cycle helmet and hi-vis clothing.  
   • You must follow the Highway Code, obey traffic signs and signals, and ride sober.  
   • Maximum speed is capped at 15.5 mph (25 km/h) – the rental operator sets this.

5. Before you ride  
   1. Check the GOV.UK map or your local council site to see if your area has an official e-scooter trial.  
   2. Download the approved operator’s app, register and scan your driving licence.  
   3. Read the in-app safety briefing.  
   4. Unlock the scooter and do a quick brake and light check.  
   5. Ride only in the permitted zones and park in the marked bays.

If you only own a private e-scooter and want to ride on public roads, you’ll need to wait until the law changes. Until then, restrict it to private land.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/electric-scooter-trials-guidance-for-users

prompt: I am currently a teacher in Wales and want to start teaching people to drive motorcycles. What qualifications and steps do I need to take?
generated_response:
To teach learner motorcyclists you must be certified by the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA).  
Below is the usual route for someone in Wales.

1. Check you meet the basic requirements  
   • Hold a full UK category A (unrestricted) motorcycle licence for at least 3 years.  
   • Be at least 21 years old.  
   • Have no more than 5 penalty points on your driving licence in the last 4 years.  
   • Be able to read a number-plate at 20 metres (with glasses or contacts if needed).  

2. Decide how you want to teach  
   • Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) instructor – teaches the one-day course every learner must take.  
   • Direct Access Scheme (DAS) instructor – teaches learners on bikes over 125 cc.  
     (You must qualify as a CBT instructor first, then take a separate DAS assessment.)

3. Join (or set up) an Approved Training Body (ATB)  
   • You can only deliver training through a DVSA-approved ATB.  
   • Most new instructors join an existing local ATB; later you can apply to run your own school if you wish.

4. Book and pass the DVSA instructor assessment  
   • Your ATB submits form CT65 on your behalf and pays the assessment fee (about £210).  
   • Assessment lasts 5 days at a DVSA centre and covers:  
     – Riding ability and road safety knowledge.  
     – Teaching a simulated CBT session.  
   • Pass and you receive a certificate valid for 4 years.  

5. Start instructing CBT courses  
   • Teach only under the ATB’s name and follow DVSA rules on ratios, lesson content and record keeping.  

6. (Optional) Qualify as a DAS instructor  
   • After 6 months as a CBT instructor you can take a 2-day DAS assessment.  
   • On passing you may train learners on machines over 125 cc and lead test-standard on-road rides.

7. Keep your registration valid  
   • Renew every 4 years (shorter if you get 6 or more penalty points).  
   • DVSA may carry out unannounced quality checks.  
   • Tell DVSA if you change ATB, address or have any court convictions.

Where to find the official guidance and forms  
GOV.UK – ‘Register as a motorcycle trainer (CBT and DAS)’  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving  

If anything is unclear, contact DVSA Motorcycle Instructor Services on 0300 200 1122.

prompt: I want to register as a Pass Plus approved driving instructor, but I'm not sure where to find my user ID from the DVSA. How do I locate it and what do I need to complete the registration?
generated_response:
To register as a Pass Plus approved driving instructor you need two things:  
• your DVSA “user ID” (used for all ADI online services)  
• the details DVSA ask for on the Pass Plus application.

Follow these steps.

1. Find your DVSA user ID  
   a. Look at your green ADI registration certificate (badge) – the 6-digit “user ID” is printed on the front, usually near your photo.  
   b. If you cannot see it, check any recent letter or email from DVSA about your ADI registration or Standards Check – the number is shown at the top under “Your user ID”.  
   c. Still can’t find it? Phone DVSA ADI enquiries on 0300 200 1122 (Monday-Friday, 8 am-4 pm). Ask them to confirm or resend your user ID.

2. Gather the other details you will need  
   • your 8-digit ADI personal reference number (PRN) – also on your badge and letters  
   • your full name, home address, phone number and email address  
   • the county or area in which you will offer Pass Plus training

3. Apply to join the Pass Plus instructor register  
   Email DVSA at passplus@dvsa.gov.uk with all the items in Step 2 and your user ID.  
   DVSA will check your ADI status and email you back to confirm your Pass Plus registration (or let you know if they need anything else).  

4. Keep the confirmation email safe – it is your proof that you can offer Pass Plus and order the official training paperwork when you need it.

Useful link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I manage a transport business in Wales and need to apply for a Certificate of Initial Fitness for a new bus. What steps should I follow to ensure the application is processed correctly?
generated_response:
Below is the usual way to obtain a Certificate of Initial Fitness (COIF) for a new bus you intend to operate in Wales.  
If the vehicle already has an EC Whole Vehicle Type Approval (or a National Small-Series approval) and the maker has given you a Certificate of Conformity, you do not need a COIF – you can skip straight to adding the vehicle to your operator’s licence.  

1. Check the vehicle’s paperwork  
   • Ask the manufacturer or dealer whether the bus is covered by EU/UK type approval and comes with a Certificate of Conformity.  
   • If it is not, continue with a COIF application.  

2. Download and complete form PSV 425 ‘Application for a Certificate of Initial Fitness’ (search “PSV 425 GOV.UK” or go via Vehicle approval > Buses and coaches).  

3. Gather the supporting documents  
   • A dimensioned seating and standing plan (show wheelchair spaces if fitted).  
   • Details of emergency exits, lighting, gangways and handrails.  
   • Evidence the vehicle meets the Public Service Vehicle Accessibility Regulations (PSVAR) if it will be used on scheduled services.  
   • Proof of any seat-belt or tachograph installations, if fitted.  

4. Pay the application fee  
   • The current fee is shown on the GOV.UK page and on form PSV 425.  
   • Pay by card over the phone or enclose a cheque made payable to ‘DVSA’.  

5. Send the form, fee and documents to:  
   DVSA – PSV Approvals  
   The Ellipse, Padley Road  
   Swansea SA1 8AN  

6. Await DVSA acknowledgment  
   • DVSA will check the paperwork and contact you if anything is missing.  

7. Book the physical inspection  
   • Once the paperwork is cleared, DVSA will invite you to book an appointment at an Authorised Testing Facility (ATF) or DVSA site near you.  
   • Take the bus, the acknowledgement letter and any original documents to the inspection.  

8. Vehicle inspection  
   • The examiner checks construction, safety and accessibility items.  
   • If the bus passes, you receive a stamped COIF (PSV 336 (Rev)) and a vehicle ‘fitness’ plate.  
   • If it fails, rectify the faults and book a retest (a reduced fee applies if within 14 days).  

9. Add the vehicle to your operator’s licence  
   • Update your vehicle list on VOL (the online operator-licensing service) or via paper form, quoting the COIF number.  
   • Keep the certificate with the vehicle’s maintenance file – Traffic Commissioners may ask to see it.  

10. Keep records up to date  
    • Retain copies of the application, inspection report and COIF for at least 15 months.  
    • Display the fitness plate in the saloon as required.  

Relevant GOV.UK links  
Vehicle approvals (buses and coaches): https://www.gov.uk/vehicle-approval  
Search for form PSV 425 within the above section if you need a fresh copy.

prompt: How do I apply to tax my HGV if I live in Scotland?
generated_response:
To tax (register Vehicle Excise Duty for) your heavy goods vehicle (HGV) in Scotland you follow the same DVLA process used across the UK.

1. Check your paperwork is up to date  
   • V5C registration certificate (log book) in your name – or the V11 ‘Tax it now’ reminder.  
   • Valid HGV annual test (MOT) certificate.  
   • A valid goods vehicle plating certificate (if the vehicle needs one).  
   • Current insurance that covers the vehicle.

2. Decide how you want to pay  
   • Online is quickest: go to ‘Tax your vehicle’ on GOV.UK.  
   • In person: take the documents to a Post Office branch that deals with vehicle tax for goods vehicles.  
   Note: Direct Debit is not available for vehicles in the HGV (goods) tax class, so you pay the full 12-month amount in one go.

3. Tax the vehicle online  
   • Go to https://www.gov.uk/vehicle-tax  
   • Enter the 11-digit reference from your V5C (or 16-digit reference from the V11).  
   • Confirm the details, choose the 12-month option, and pay with a debit or credit card.  
   • You will get an email receipt straight away; DVLA’s records update instantly.

4. Tax the vehicle at a Post Office (if you prefer)  
   • Take the V5C or V11, annual test certificate and plating certificate, plus proof of insurance.  
   • Pay by card, cash or cheque.  
   • The clerk will hand back your documents and give you a receipt. DVLA’s records update within 5 days.

5. Keep proof of taxation in the cab until DVLA confirms it is taxed. You do not need to display a paper tax disc.

If you have problems (for example, the vehicle details are wrong or you have lost your V5C), contact DVLA on 0300 790 6802 before you drive the vehicle.

Most HGVs over 12 tonnes also need the separate HGV Levy when driving in Great Britain, but this is currently suspended. Check GOV.UK for updates before you travel.

prompt: I want to start learning to drive. What are the steps I need to follow?
generated_response:
Here are the usual steps for getting your full car driving licence in Scotland:

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must be at least 17 and be able to read a number plate from 20 metres.  
   • Make sure you have a valid UK passport or other accepted ID and a National Insurance number.

2. Apply for a provisional driving licence  
   • Apply online (takes about 5 minutes) or by post using form D1.  
   • Cost: £34 online or £43 by post.  
   • You should receive the licence within a week or so.  
   GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

3. Study for the theory test  
   • Use the Highway Code, DVSA’s Theory Test Kit app, and free mock tests online.  
   • Book the test once you feel confident (currently £23).  
   • You’ll need your provisional licence number to book.

4. Pass the theory test  
   • You must pass the multiple-choice and hazard-perception parts on the same day.  
   • Keep your pass certificate number safe—you’ll need it to book the practical test.

5. Take driving lessons  
   • Most learners use an Approved Driving Instructor (ADI). Check they’re DVSA-registered.  
   • If practising in your own or someone else’s car, the supervising driver must be over 21 and have held a full licence for 3 years; display L-plates and arrange learner insurance.  
   • Aim for at least 40–50 hours of professional tuition plus private practice if possible.

6. Book and take the practical driving test  
   • Wait times vary—book early using your theory pass certificate number (currently £62 on weekdays).  
   • Bring your provisional licence and theory pass certificate on the day.  
   • The test covers eyesight, ‘show me, tell me’ vehicle-safety questions, 40-minute drive, one reversing manoeuvre, and 20 minutes of independent driving using sat-nav or signs.

7. After you pass  
   • The examiner will send off your pass to DVLA; your full licence should arrive within 3 weeks.  
   • Sort suitable insurance before driving alone.  
   • Consider Pass Plus or other post-test training for extra confidence and possible insurance discounts.

8. If you don’t pass first time  
   • You can rebook the practical test after 10 working days. Keep practising on any areas flagged by the examiner.

prompt: I have a driving licence with code 44(12). What does this mean and how does it affect my ability to drive a motorcycle?
generated_response:
Code 44 on a UK driving licence means you may only ride a motorcycle that has a specific adaptation to suit a medical or physical need.  
The number in brackets identifies the exact adaptation – in your case ‘(12)’. For example, it could be an adapted brake/gear control or another change that lets you ride safely.

How this affects you  
1. You can only ride a bike in the category shown on your licence (A, A2, A1 or AM) if that bike has the 44(12) adaptation fitted.  
2. Riding a standard, un-adapted motorcycle would break the conditions of your licence and could lead to penalty points, a fine or your insurance being invalid.  
3. If you think you no longer need the adaptation, you must:  
   a. Contact DVLA’s Drivers Medical Enquiries (0300 790 6806).  
   b. Send any medical evidence they ask for.  
   c. Be prepared to take another practical test on a normal motorcycle if DVLA require it.  
4. Keep proof that the motorcycle you ride has the required adaptation (for example, fitting invoice or manufacturer’s confirmation).

Where to check the exact meaning of 44(12) and what to do next  
• Driving licence codes and what they mean – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/driving-licence-codes  
• Contact DVLA about a medical condition: https://www.gov.uk/contact-the-dvla/y/contact-the-drivers-medical-enquiries-team  

If you’re unsure about the adaptation or how to prove it is fitted, phone DVLA or a qualified motorcycle instructor before riding.

prompt: How much does the instructional ability test for driving instructors cost?
generated_response: The instructional ability test for trainee driving instructors (ADI Part 3) costs £111. You pay the fee online when you book the test on GOV.UK.

prompt: As a teacher, I need to know the difference between the drivers' hours rules for goods vehicles and buses or coaches.
generated_response:
Below is a side-by-side summary of the main differences between the drivers’ hours rules for goods-vehicle drivers and for bus or coach drivers.  
Where “EU rules” are mentioned, this also covers the UK’s retained EU law.

1. Which rules normally apply  
   • Goods vehicles (over 3.5 tonnes): EU rules if the journey is commercial and goes outside the UK or the vehicle is over 3.5 t; otherwise GB domestic rules may apply.  
   • Buses/coaches (passenger-carrying vehicles with 9+ seats): EU rules if on regular or occasional services beyond 50 km; otherwise GB domestic or separate domestic PSV rules.

2. Daily driving limits  
   • Goods: 9 hours (can be increased to 10 hours twice a week).  
   • Buses/coaches: 9 hours (can be increased to 10 hours twice a week).  
   (Same under EU rules; see below for rest-break differences.)

3. Daily rest  
   • Goods: Minimum 11 hours (can be reduced to 9 hours up to three times between weekly rests).  
   • Buses/coaches: Same 11-hour rule, but coach drivers on international occasional services may split rest into 3 hours + 9 hours.

4. Breaks during the day  
   • Goods: At least 45 minutes after 4½ hours’ driving (can be split 15 min + 30 min).  
   • Buses/coaches: At least 45 minutes after 4½ hours’ driving OR for domestic regular services within 50 km, 30 minutes after 5½ hours (may be taken as 15 min + 15 min).

5. Weekly driving limits  
   • Goods: 56 hours in any one week and 90 hours in any two consecutive weeks.  
   • Buses/coaches: Same under EU rules; domestic services within 50 km usually capped at 5 hours continuous driving before 30-min break and 10 hours total in a day.

6. Weekly rest  
   • Goods: 45 hours (can be reduced to 24 hours every other week, if made up later).  
   • Buses/coaches: 45 hours; reductions allowed in same way, but international occasional services may compensate differently when a second driver is carried.

7. Tachographs  
   • Goods: Tachograph required for vehicles in scope of EU rules; digital tachograph cards issued to goods-vehicle drivers.  
   • Buses/coaches: Tachograph also required for in-scope PSVs; the card type is the same but the operator may need a ‘company card’ for each licence. Domestic 50 km services often exempt.

8. Record-keeping under GB domestic rules  
   • Goods: Must keep a written log-book when driving more than 4 hours in a day.  
   • Buses/coaches: Must keep a domestic PSV record sheet when driving more than 5 hours in a day (unless tachograph fitted).

9. Penalties and enforcement  
   • The DVSA enforces both sets of rules. Fixed penalties, prohibition notices and court action apply to both, but passenger services can also face Traffic Commissioner action on the PSV operator’s licence.

Practical steps if you’re teaching this topic  
1. Identify whether the vehicle and journey fall under EU or GB domestic rules.  
2. Use the correct limits and rest requirements shown above.  
3. Explain the purpose and use of tachographs and log-books.  
4. Highlight exceptions (e.g. school minibuses under 3.5 t, 50 km urban bus routes).  
5. Direct learners to the official guidance on GOV.UK for up-to-date details.

Most relevant GOV.UK link  
Drivers’ hours, tachographs and record keeping guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How do I know if my electric bike needs vehicle approval?
generated_response:
Most electric bikes (e-bikes) do not need any kind of vehicle approval if they meet the rules for an EAPC (Electrically Assisted Pedal Cycle).  

Follow these checks:  

1. Look at the motor label or handbook.  
   • The motor must be no more than 250 watts.  

2. Ride the bike (or look at the display).  
   • The motor must stop giving power once you reach 15.5 mph (25 km/h).  

3. Check the controls.  
   • You must pedal for the motor to work.  
   • Any throttle that can move the bike without pedalling must cut out above 3.7 mph (6 km/h).  

4. Count the wheels.  
   • It must have 2 or 3 wheels.  

If your bike passes all four checks, it is an EAPC. You do not need vehicle approval, tax, insurance or a driving licence—just keep to normal cycling rules.  

If it fails any check (for example, a 500 watt motor or a throttle that works up to full speed), it is treated as a moped or motorbike. You would need:  
• type approval,  
• registration and number plates,  
• vehicle tax and insurance,  
• the correct driving licence, and  
• to wear an approved motorbike helmet.  

Still unsure? Take the bike and its paperwork to a reputable e-bike shop or contact the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) before you ride it on the road.

Most relevant GOV.UK page: https://www.gov.uk/electric-bike-rules

prompt: What should I do if I don't have the V5C log book when buying a vehicle?
generated_response:
It’s usually safest not to buy a vehicle if the seller cannot give you the V5C log book (the registration certificate).  
If you still want to go ahead, follow these steps:

1. Ask the seller whether they have the green ‘new keeper’ slip (V5C/2).  
   • If they do, make sure the details match the vehicle and the seller’s ID.  
   • If they don’t, treat the sale with extreme caution – it could be stolen, cloned or on finance.

2. Check the vehicle’s background before paying anything.  
   • Use the free GOV.UK tools to confirm the MOT history and tax status.  
   • Consider a paid HPI or similar check to see if it’s stolen, written-off or has outstanding finance.

3. If you decide to buy:  
   a) Get a dated, signed receipt showing the vehicle details, price and both parties’ names and addresses.  
   b) Apply for a new V5C as soon as you own the vehicle:  
      • Fill in form V62 ‘Application for a vehicle registration certificate’ (available online or at Post Offices).  
      • Send it to DVLA with a £25 cheque or postal order.  
      • DVLA will post the replacement V5C to you (allow up to 6 weeks).

4. Tax and insure the vehicle.  
   • If you have the V5C/2 slip you can tax online or at a Post Office straight away.  
   • If you do not have the slip, take form V62, a valid MOT certificate and your insurance to a Post Office that deals with vehicle tax – they can tax it while your V5C is being processed.

5. Do not drive the vehicle on public roads until it is taxed, insured and (if over 3 years old) has a valid MOT.

Useful links  
• Vehicle log book (V5C) – lost, stolen or damaged: https://www.gov.uk/vehicle-log-book  
• Check the vehicle’s MOT history: https://www.gov.uk/check-mot-history  
• Check vehicle tax: https://www.gov.uk/check-vehicle-tax  
• General driving and vehicle advice: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: Can I pay a DVSA roadside fine by post and what details are required?
generated_response:
Yes. If you do not want to pay online or by phone, you can post a cheque or postal order to the DVSA Fixed Penalty Office.  

1. Make the cheque or postal order payable to ‘DVSA’. Do not send cash.  
2. Write on the back:  
   • your fixed-penalty (notice) reference number  
   • your full name and address  
   • your driving-licence number (or your date of birth if you do not have a UK licence)  
   • the vehicle registration number (if it appears on the notice)  
3. Put the payment and the payment slip that came with the notice in an envelope.  
4. Post it to the address shown on your notice (normally: DVSA Fixed Penalty Office, PO Box 582, Darlington DL1 9FS).  
5. Keep your proof of posting. The payment must reach DVSA within 28 days of the notice date, otherwise the fine can increase and enforcement action may start.  

If anything on your notice is different (for example a different address or a shorter deadline) follow the details printed on it. If you are unsure, call DVSA on 0300 123 9000 before you post your payment.  

Most people now pay online using the GOV.UK service (link below) as it is instant and you receive confirmation straight away.  

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/pay-dvsa-roadside-fine

prompt: What are the consequences of having incorrect number plates on my vehicle, and how can I avoid them?
generated_response:
Using number plates that are wrong in any way – the characters, spacing, font, colour or material – is treated as having “incorrect number plates”. The consequences can be:

1. Fixed-penalty notice: the police can issue an on-the-spot fine of up to £100.  
2. Court prosecution: the fine can rise to £1,000 if the case goes to court.  
3. MOT failure: the vehicle will automatically fail its MOT if the plates are incorrect, obscured, damaged or illegible.  
4. Vehicle stop or seizure: police ANPR cameras may not recognise the plate; this can lead to the vehicle being stopped or, in serious cases (for example cloned or false plates), seized.  
5. Possible insurance problems: driving with illegal or false plates could invalidate your cover.

How to avoid problems

1. Buy plates only from a Registered Number Plate Supplier (RNPS).  
   • Take proof of identity and proof you’re entitled to the registration (V5C log book, V750, V778, etc.).  
2. Follow the legal display rules:  
   • Use the standard “Charles Wright” font.  
   • Keep exactly the required spacing and character size.  
   • Front plate: white background; rear plate: yellow background; both must be reflective with no background pattern.  
   • No screws or fixings obscuring characters.  
   • Plate must show the supplier’s name and postcode plus the BS AU 145e mark.  
3. Keep plates clean and undamaged; replace faded, cracked or delaminating plates promptly.  
4. If a plate is lost or stolen, report it to the police and get replacements straight away.  
5. Never alter or mis-space characters to spell words or hide the age identifier.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance:  
Displaying number plates – https://www.gov.uk/displaying-number-plates

prompt: What should I do if I need to find a theory test centre in Northern Ireland instead of Scotland?
generated_response:
You book and sit your theory test in Northern Ireland through the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA), not the DVSA service used in Scotland.  
1. Go to the nidirect website (type “nidirect theory test” into any search engine).  
2. Select “Book, change or cancel your theory test”.  
3. Scroll to “Find your nearest test centre” and enter your town or postcode – the page lists all DVA theory-test centres in Northern Ireland with maps and opening times.  
4. Choose the centre that suits you and follow the on-screen steps to book online, or phone the DVA booking line on 0345 600 6700 (Monday-Friday).  
5. Make sure you have your UK driving-licence number, an email address or mobile number, and a debit/credit card ready when you book.  

You do not need to cancel anything already arranged in Scotland unless you have an existing booking there; if you do, cancel it through the DVSA service first to avoid losing your fee.

Most people find it helpful to print or save the confirmation email/text once the Northern Ireland booking is complete.

Relevant GOV.UK starting page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving (then choose “Driving tests and learning to drive” → “Book your driving theory test” and follow the Northern Ireland link shown on that page).

prompt: I need to change my address on my driving licence and also my name. How do I do this and what forms do I need to fill out?
generated_response:
You can update both the address and the name on a Great-Britain photocard driving licence by applying to DVLA through the post. It’s free (unless you want to change the photograph as well).  

1. Get form D1 ‘Application for a driving licence’.  
   • Pick it up from most large Post Office branches, or order it online from DVLA.  

2. Fill in the form.  
   • Tick the boxes for both ‘change of address’ and ‘change of name’.  
   • Use BLOCK CAPITALS and black ink.

3. Gather the supporting documents.  
   • Your current photocard licence (both the photocard and paper counterpart if you still have it).  
   • Original proof of your new name – for example: deed poll, statutory declaration, marriage or civil-partnership certificate (photocopies are not accepted).  
     – If the document is not in English you must include an official translation.  
   • No proof of address is needed; you just write the new address on the form.

4. Check the photo.  
   • If your photocard photo is still valid you don’t need a new one.  
   • If the photo is over 10 years old, or you want to update it, include a recent passport-style colour photo and the fee shown on the D1 form (currently £14 by cheque or postal order).

5. Post everything to:  
   DVLA, Swansea, SA99 1BN.  
   (The exact address is printed on the form.)  

6. Wait for your new licence.  
   • You should get it within about 3 weeks if DVLA doesn’t need more information.  
   • Keep the tracking receipt if you use recorded delivery.

Driving while you wait:  
You can carry on driving as normal until the new licence arrives, as long as the details on your existing licence are otherwise valid.

Find out more or order the D1 form online:  
https://www.gov.uk/change-address-driving-licence  
https://www.gov.uk/change-name-driving-licence

prompt: How do I report an illegal driving instructor?
generated_response:
If you think someone is giving paid driving lessons without being an Approved Driving Instructor (ADI), you can report them to the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA).

1. Gather the facts  
   • The instructor’s name (and the name of their driving school, if any).  
   • Car make, model, colour and registration.  
   • Where and when you saw them giving lessons.  
   • Anything that makes you think they’re not qualified (for example, no green or pink ADI badge displayed on the windscreen).

2. Contact the DVSA Fraud and Integrity Team  
   • Email: fraudandintegrity@dvsa.gov.uk  
   • Phone (free): 0800 030 4103 (Monday to Friday, 8 am – noon).  

   Say you want to report a suspected illegal driving instructor and give the details you collected. You can ask to stay anonymous if you wish.

3. DVSA action  
   The DVSA will check whether the person is on the ADI register. If they’re not, DVSA enforcement officers can investigate and prosecute.

Keep any evidence you have (screenshots of adverts, texts, receipts) in case DVSA contacts you for more information.

Further information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: What information can I get from checking a vehicle's MOT history?
generated_response:
Checking a vehicle’s MOT history on GOV.UK will show you:  
1. Whether the vehicle passed or failed each MOT test since digital records began (2005).  
2. The date of every test and when the current MOT expires.  
3. Odometer readings recorded at each test (useful for spotting mileage discrepancies).  
4. All recorded reasons for failure (“major” and “dangerous” defects).  
5. Advisory and minor defects noted by the tester – things that may need attention soon.  
6. The test location (testing station number and postcode).  
7. The make, model, colour, fuel type and date first used, as held on the MOT database.  
8. If a test certificate was withdrawn or a test was voided.

How to check:  
1. Go to the GOV.UK “Check MOT history” service: https://www.gov.uk/check-mot-history  
2. Enter the vehicle’s registration number.  
3. Confirm the vehicle details shown and, if asked, pick the correct colour.  
4. View the full history; you can expand each test result for defect details.

You do not need to register or pay—​the service is free.

prompt: How much does it cost to get a criminal record check for becoming a driving instructor, and where can I get it done?
generated_response:
To register as a driving instructor you must get a Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) criminal record check that is specially set up for driving instructors.  

Cost  
• The check costs £6 (there is no extra fee for people living in Scotland).  

Where and how to apply  
1. Go to GOV.UK and search for ‘Criminal record check to become a driving instructor’ – this takes you to the online DBS application form.  
2. Create or sign in with a Government Gateway account.  
3. Fill in the online form (you’ll need your National Insurance number, addresses for the last 5 years and driving licence details).  
4. Prove your identity. The online service will tell you which identity documents are accepted and whether you can do this online or need to take the papers to a participating Post Office branch.  
5. Pay the £6 fee by debit or credit card and submit the form.  

What happens next  
• You’ll get a DBS reference number by email (and a paper certificate by post).  
• Keep the reference number – you must enter it when you later apply to the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) to become a qualified instructor.  

If you need help  
• Phone DBS on 0300 020 0190 (Monday to Friday).  
• A friend, family member or local library can help you get online if needed.  

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: After buying a used car, what are the steps I need to follow to register it, tax it, and insure it?
generated_response:
Here is what you need to do as soon as you buy a used car in Scotland (the rules are UK-wide).  

1. Get the log book (V5C) in your name  
   1.1 If you bought the car from a dealer, they normally tell DVLA online and give you the green ‘new keeper’ slip.  
   1.2 If you bought privately, fill in section 6 of the V5C with your details, make sure the seller signs section 8, tear off the green slip for yourself, and post the rest to DVLA, Swansea, SA99 1BA.  
   1.3 Your new V5C should arrive within about 4 weeks.  

2. Arrange insurance before you drive the car  
   • Shop around, choose a policy that starts on the day you take ownership.  
   • You must have at least third-party cover; keep proof (paper or digital certificate).  

3. Check MOT status  
   • If the car is over three years old it needs a valid MOT certificate.  
   • Use the DVLA checker online; if it has less than a month left, book an MOT as soon as possible.  

4. Tax the vehicle (Vehicle Excise Duty)  
   4.1 You cannot transfer tax from the previous keeper.  
   4.2 Use the 12-digit reference on your green slip, or the new V5C once it arrives:  
        • online at GOV.UK,  
        • by phone (0300 123 4321), or  
        • at a Post Office that issues vehicle tax.  
   4.3 Pay for 6 or 12 months by card, Direct Debit or cash (at Post Office).  
   4.4 You’ll get an email or receipt; no paper disc is needed on the windscreen.  

5. Update any personal details if needed (e.g. driving licence address).  

6. Keep documents safe in case you need to show them to the police or insurer.  

You can drive the car on public roads only when you have:  
• insurance in place, and  
• either current tax or a pre-booked MOT appointment (if untaxed and without MOT).  

For full guidance and online services: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: What are the legal requirements for having vehicle insurance in the UK?
generated_response:
In the UK you must have at least third-party motor insurance for any vehicle that is used or kept on a public road. The main legal points are:

1. What must be covered  
   • The policy must cover damage or injury you cause to other people, their passengers or property (third-party cover).  
   • Comprehensive and third-party-fire-and-theft policies also meet the law because they include third-party cover.

2. When insurance is required  
   • The law works on “continuous insurance enforcement”. The vehicle must be insured at all times unless it has a valid Statutory Off Road Notification (SORN) and is kept off public roads.  
   • You still need insurance when the vehicle is parked on the street or being towed.  
   • If you borrow or test-drive a vehicle, you (or the vehicle) still need third-party cover.

3. Penalties for not having insurance  
   • Fixed-penalty: £300 fine and 6 penalty points.  
   • Court can impose an unlimited fine, disqualify you from driving and order the vehicle to be seized and destroyed.  
   • The Motor Insurance Database is used to check every vehicle automatically.

4. Proof of insurance  
   • You must be able to show a valid certificate of motor insurance if the police ask.  
   • Insurers also upload details to the Motor Insurance Database, which police and DVLA check electronically.

5. Exceptions  
   • Vehicles with a SORN kept entirely off public roads.  
   • Vehicles owned by certain visiting forces or used in authorised off-road motorsport events.  
   • Any other exemption is very limited—assume you need insurance unless you have written confirmation otherwise.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/vehicle-insurance

prompt: I'm a retired woman in Wales and I want to become a motorcycle trainer. How much does it cost to take the DVSA special test and what happens if I don't pass it?
generated_response:
The DVSA ‘special test’ you must pass to become a Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) motorcycle instructor costs £90 each time you take it.

If you do not pass:  
1. The examiner will give you a test report showing the faults that led to the fail.  
2. You must book and pay for another test – the same £90 fee applies each time.  
3. You can usually book again straight away, but the next available date is likely to be at least a few days later.  
4. There is no limit on the number of times you can re-take, but most people arrange some extra practice with an instructor before trying again.

Full details and how to book are on GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/become-motorcycle-instructor

prompt: I'm modifying a goods vehicle and need to increase an axle weight. What steps do I need to take?
generated_response:
To legally increase the plated axle weight on a goods vehicle you must have the change approved and recorded by the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) and, once accepted, by DVLA.  
Follow these steps:

1. Check the technical feasibility  
   • Speak to the vehicle or axle manufacturer (or a qualified automotive engineer) to confirm the axle, suspension, tyres and brakes can safely take the new weight.  
   • Obtain written confirmation and any supporting calculations or drawings – DVSA will ask for this.

2. Fit any required parts  
   • Install uprated components (axle, springs, airbags, tyres, etc.) if the manufacturer or engineer says they are needed.  
   • Keep invoices and fitter’s statements as evidence.

3. Apply to DVSA for a “notifiable alteration”  
   • Complete form VTG 11 (Notifiable alteration – goods vehicle).  
   • Tick the section for “increase in plated axle weight” and attach the technical evidence.  
   • Email or post the form to the DVSA Notifiable Alterations team and pay the current fee.

4. Present the vehicle for inspection (if DVSA ask)  
   • DVSA will either accept the paperwork or ask you to book an inspection at an Authorised Testing Facility (ATF).  
   • Take the vehicle, evidence and its existing plating certificate (VTG 6/7).

5. Receive the new plating certificate  
   • If DVSA are satisfied, they will issue an updated plating certificate showing the higher axle (and gross) weight.  
   • Keep the old and new plates together until the new one is fixed to the vehicle.

6. Update the vehicle log book (V5C)  
   • Fill in section 4 of the V5C with the new revenue weight and send it to DVLA.  
   • DVLA will return an amended V5C.

7. Tell anyone else who needs to know  
   • Inform your insurer and, if you run under an operator’s licence, update the licence vehicle list and ensure your planning for axle and gross weights reflects the new limits.  

Relevant GOV.UK guidance and forms:  
• Notifiable alterations for HGVs – https://www.gov.uk/guidance/notifiable-alterations-for-heavy-goods-vehicles-hgvpsv  
• Get or replace a plating certificate (VTG 6) – https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/goods-vehicle-plate-vtg6  
• Vehicle testing and ATF locations – https://www.gov.uk/topic/driving-transport/vehicle-testing

prompt: What should I do if I need to inform DVLA that I've sold the vehicle of someone who has passed away?
generated_response:
If you are the executor or next of kin and you have sold the vehicle that belonged to someone who has died, you must let DVLA know in writing.

1. Find the vehicle’s log book (V5C).
   • Fill in Section 2 (“Selling or transferring my vehicle to a new keeper”) with the buyer’s details and the date of sale.  
   • Tear off the green slip (V5C/2) and give it to the buyer – they will need it to tax the vehicle.

2. Write a short covering letter that includes:  
   • the deceased person’s full name, address and date of death  
   • the vehicle’s registration number, make and model  
   • the date you sold it and the buyer’s name and address  
   • your own name, address and your relationship to the person who has died (for example, executor or next of kin)

3. Post the covering letter and the rest of the V5C (not the green slip) to:  
   DVLA – Sensitive Casework Team  
   Swansea  
   SA99 1ZZ  

4. Cancel the vehicle’s insurance and apply for any road-tax refund that may be due (DVLA will do the refund automatically once they receive the V5C).

5. Keep a copy of the letter and proof of postage for your records. DVLA will update their records; you do not need to do anything else.

Relevant GOV.UK page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How does the body process alcohol, and why is it different for everyone?
generated_response:
When you drink alcohol, your body treats it like a toxin and tries to break it down as quickly as possible.

1. Absorption  
   • Alcohol reaches your stomach and small intestine.  
   • About 20 % is absorbed straight from the stomach into your blood; the rest is absorbed in the small intestine.  

2. Distribution  
   • Your blood carries the alcohol to the brain, liver and the rest of your body.  
   • This is why you start to feel the effects within minutes.

3. Breakdown in the liver  
   • The liver does most of the work.  
   • Enzymes (mainly one called ADH) turn alcohol into other chemicals, which are then changed into water and carbon dioxide.  
   • The liver can only process a small, fixed amount (roughly one standard drink per hour). Anything extra stays in your blood until the liver catches up.

4. Removal  
   • The harmless end-products leave in your breath, urine and sweat.

Why the speed is different for everyone

• Body size and weight – a smaller or lighter person has less blood and tissue to “dilute” the alcohol.  
• Sex – on average, females have less water in their bodies and different hormone levels, so the same drink can raise their blood-alcohol level higher.  
• Genetics – we each make slightly different versions of the liver enzymes. Some break alcohol down fast; others work more slowly.  
• Food in the stomach – eating before or while drinking slows absorption.  
• Health and tiredness – if your liver is under strain, or you are ill, it will clear alcohol more slowly.  
• Medication and other drugs – some medicines block or speed up the liver enzymes.  
• Drinking habit – regular heavy drinkers may process alcohol a bit faster at first, but this does not stop long-term damage.

Practical tips

1. Eat before you drink and sip water between alcoholic drinks.  
2. Know your own limits; do not try to match friends drink for drink.  
3. Plan travel so you never drive or ride with someone who has been drinking.  
4. Allow plenty of time: only time, not coffee or a cold shower, will lower your blood-alcohol level.  
5. If you feel unwell or are worried about someone who has drunk too much, call 999 (in an emergency) or NHS 111 for advice.

prompt: I'm a teacher and I need to explain the seat belt law to my students. What are the key points I should cover?
generated_response:
Below are the main points you can share with your class about the UK seat-belt law (the rules are the same in Wales, England and Scotland).

1. Who must wear a seat belt  
   • All drivers and front-seat passengers must wear a seat belt if one is fitted.  
   • Rear-seat passengers aged 14 or over must also wear a seat belt.  
   • Children under 14 must use the correct child car seat or booster until they reach 135 cm tall or their 12th birthday (whichever comes first); after that they must wear a seat belt.

2. Driver’s legal responsibility  
   • The driver is legally responsible for:  
     – themselves; and  
     – anyone under 14 in the vehicle.  
   • Failing to ensure a child is correctly restrained can lead to a fixed-penalty fine of £100 (up to £500 if it goes to court).

3. Exemptions  
   • Seat belts need not be worn if:  
     – you are reversing, or supervising a learner who is reversing;  
     – you have a medical exemption certificate;  
     – you are making deliveries in a goods vehicle and travel less than 50 metres between stops.  
   • Taxi and private-hire drivers are exempt while carrying passengers, but passengers themselves must buckle up.

4. Vehicles without seat belts  
   • If a classic car was first used before 1965 and has no seat belts, children under 3 cannot be carried, and children 3–11 may only sit in the back.

5. Penalties for adults (14+)  
   • Not wearing a seat belt can result in a £100 fixed penalty notice or a fine of up to £500 if prosecuted.

6. Safety message  
   • Wearing a seat belt reduces the risk of death or serious injury by about 50 %. It is the simplest and most effective way to stay safe in a vehicle.

For full details see “Seat belts: the law” on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How do I register a new vehicle after buying it?
generated_response:
Registering a vehicle simply means telling DVLA that you are now the “keeper” of the car or motorbike. What you do depends on where you bought it.

1. If you bought from a motor dealer  
   a. The dealer usually registers the vehicle online for you.  
   b. They will hand you the green ‘new keeper’ slip (V5C/2). Keep this as proof you are the keeper until your log book (V5C) arrives.  
   c. Your new log book should arrive in about 4–6 weeks.

2. If you bought privately (from the previous keeper)  
   a. You and the seller fill in Section 2 of the log book (V5C) together.  
   b. The seller must post the rest of the V5C to DVLA.  
   c. You keep the green ‘new keeper’ slip.  
   d. OR you can register online yourself: go to GOV-UK → Driving and transport → Vehicle log book → ‘Tell DVLA you’ve bought a vehicle’. You will need:  
      • The 12-digit reference number printed on the green slip.  
      • The vehicle’s registration number.  
      The online service is free and open 7 am–7 pm daily. You will get an email confirmation straight away.

3. If the vehicle is brand-new and has never had a V5C  
   The dealer normally handles registration. If not, you must complete form V55/4 (available from DVLA), pay the £55 first-registration fee and send it with proof of ID and insurance to DVLA, Swansea, SA99 1BE.

4. While you wait for the log book  
   • Make sure the vehicle is insured.  
   • Tax it (you can do this online with the 12-digit number from the green slip).  
   • Make sure it has a valid MOT if it is over 3 years old.  
   You can legally drive as soon as these three things are in place.

Most people receive the new V5C within a month. If it has not arrived after 6 weeks, contact DVLA.

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving (choose ‘Vehicle log book (V5C)’).

prompt: How much does the ADI part 2 test cost?
generated_response:
The Approved Driving Instructor (ADI) part 2 test costs £111.  
You pay this fee when you book the test with the Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA). For how to book and pay, visit GOV.UK or phone the DVSA booking line.

prompt: I want to transport goods from the UK to another country. What are the first steps I need to take?
generated_response:
Here are the usual first things to sort out when you plan to move goods from the UK to another country.  
1. Work out exactly what you’re sending  
   • List each item and find its commodity (HS) code using the Trade Tariff tool.  
   • Check whether the goods are restricted (for example, food, chemicals, military--use items) – some need an export licence.  

2. Decide who is exporting and get an EORI number (if you don’t already have one)  
   • Businesses need a UK Economic Operator Registration and Identification (EORI) number that starts with GB to make customs declarations.  
   • If you’re sending goods as a private individual, you do not normally need an EORI, but the courier or freight agent will still need the commodity codes.  

3. Check the rules, duties and taxes in the destination country  
   • Look up any import bans, licences, or tariffs the destination authority applies to your goods.  
   • Factor in VAT or other sales taxes that may be charged when the goods arrive.  

4. Choose how you will move the goods and who will handle customs  
   • Options include a courier, postal service, freight forwarder or your own vehicle.  
   • If you or an employee will drive a lorry or van, make sure the driver holds the correct licence and Driver CPC, and that the vehicle has the right permits and insurance (see Driving, lorries and buses on GOV.UK).  

5. Make the export customs declaration  
   • Most exporters use a freight forwarder or customs agent who submits the declaration on their behalf through the Customs Declaration Service.  
   • Keep copies of the export declaration and any licences for at least 4 years.  

6. Keep records and track the shipment  
   • Retain invoices, transport documents and proof of export.  
   • Confirm the goods have cleared customs in the destination country and any duties have been paid.  

Useful GOV.UK starting point:  
Driving, lorries and buses – includes licence and vehicle requirements if you will drive the goods yourself  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: Can I request information about a vehicle's registered keeper from DVLA and what is the process for doing so?
generated_response:
You can ask DVLA for the name and address of a vehicle’s registered keeper, but only if you have what the law calls a “reasonable cause” (for example, a road-traffic collision, parking dispute, or tracing the owner of an abandoned vehicle). You cannot request the details just out of curiosity.

Follow these steps:

1. Check you have a reasonable cause and evidence to support it (photos, witness statements, invoices, police reference number, etc.).  
2. Download form V888 “Request by an individual for information about a vehicle” from GOV.UK, or phone DVLA 0300 790 6802 to ask for a paper copy.  
3. Complete the form, explaining clearly why you need the keeper’s details and listing the evidence you’re enclosing.  
4. Enclose the £5 fee (cheque or postal order made out to ‘DVLA’).  
5. Post the form, fee and copies of your evidence to:  
   Vehicle Record Enquiries  
   DVLA  
   Swansea  
   SA99 1AJ  
6. DVLA will review your application. If they accept your reason, they will post the keeper’s name and address to you. If not, they will tell you why your request was refused and return your payment.

If you need the information urgently for legal proceedings, consider asking a solicitor or the police to request it on your behalf.

More information and the V888 form: https://www.gov.uk/request-information-from-dvla

prompt: Can I book a theory test without providing my pupil's details?
generated_response:
No. The Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) will not accept a theory-test booking unless you give the key details of the person who is going to sit the test.

What you must have  
• The pupil’s full name (exactly as it appears on their provisional licence)  
• Their 16-digit driving-licence number  
• A contact email (yours or theirs) for the confirmation  
• A debit or credit card to pay the £23 fee (car theory test)

How to book  
1. Ask your pupil for their provisional licence so you can copy the name and licence number.  
2. Go to the official service: https://www.gov.uk/book-theory-test  
3. Pick the vehicle category (car, motorcycle, etc.), test centre, date and time.  
4. Enter the pupil’s details when the form asks for them.  
5. Pay the fee and keep the confirmation email—its reference number lets you change or cancel later.

Approved Driving Instructors (ADI)  
If you use the DVSA Business Service to book on behalf of pupils, the system still requires each pupil’s licence number and other personal details before it will lock in a slot. Trainer-booker credits let you pay in advance, but you must add the pupil’s details before the test or the booking will be cancelled.

prompt: Can I ride my quad bike on the road without an MOT certificate?
generated_response:
You can only ride a quad bike on public roads without an MOT in very limited circumstances.  
1. Light agricultural use – If all the following apply, an MOT is not required:  
   • The quad is registered and taxed in the ‘light agricultural vehicle’ class.  
   • It is used solely for agriculture, horticulture or forestry.  
   • Any trips on the road are directly related to that work (for example, between fields that are less than 1.5 km apart).  
   In every other case the quad must have a valid MOT once it is 3 years old (or from its date of first registration if it was previously abroad).  
2. All other quads – For leisure, commuting, business deliveries, sport, etc. – the bike is treated like a motorcycle:  
   • It must be type-approved, registered, display number plates, be insured and, if over 3 years old, have a current MOT certificate.  
   • You also need the correct category B (car) or B1 licence, a crash helmet that meets British standards, and vehicle tax.  
3. If your quad is under 3 years old, you can ride it on the road without an MOT provided it meets all other legal requirements (registration, insurance, tax, lights, indicators, etc.).  
4. Penalties – Riding on the road when an MOT is required but missing can lead to a £1,000 fine, your insurance becoming invalid, and penalty points.  
5. Next steps if you need an MOT:  
   1) Find an MOT station that tests class 1 or 2 motorcycles and confirm they can test quad bikes.  
   2) Book the test – you are legally allowed to ride directly to and from a booked MOT without a valid certificate.  
   3) Fix any defects and keep the MOT up to date every 12 months.

Most people using a quad for day-to-day road travel will need an MOT once the machine is 3 years old.

prompt: What are the steps to become a driving instructor?
generated_response:
To work legally as a paid car-driving instructor in Scotland you must qualify and register as an Approved Driving Instructor (ADI).  

1. Check you meet the basic rules  
   • Be at least 21 and have held a full UK or EU car driving licence for at least 3 years.  
   • Have no more than 5 penalty points and be able to pass a “fit and proper” person check (criminal record).  

2. Apply to start the ADI process  
   • Create a DVSA online account and apply to join the ADI register (there is a fee).  
   • The Driver and Vehicle Standards Agency (DVSA) will carry out an enhanced criminal-record check through Disclosure and Barring Service / Disclosure Scotland.  

3. Pass the three ADI qualifying tests (you must take them in order)  
   Part 1 – Theory and hazard perception (computer-based).  
   Part 2 – Advanced driving ability test (1-hour driving test).  
   Part 3 – Instructional ability test (you teach a real learner while the examiner watches).  
   • You have up to 3 attempts at each part and must complete all 3 within 2 years of passing Part 1.  

4. Optional: Get a trainee (PDI) licence  
   • After passing Parts 1 and 2, and completing at least 40 hours instructor training, you can apply for a pink trainee licence (valid 6 months).  
   • This lets you charge for lessons while gaining experience before taking Part 3.  

5. Register as an Approved Driving Instructor  
   • When you pass Part 3, pay the ADI registration fee.  
   • You’ll receive a green ADI badge to display in the car. Registration lasts 4 years.  

6. Keep your registration up to date  
   • Every 4 years renew your registration and every 2 to 4 years pass a Standards Check to show you still meet the required teaching level.  
   • Tell DVSA if you change address or get any motoring convictions.  

Useful link  
Become a driving instructor (ADI): https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I'm considering taking the CBT instructor assessment in Wales. Is there any cost involved?
generated_response:
There’s no fee to take the DVSA Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) instructor assessment itself – the test is free.  

However, you may still have some costs:  
1. Your local Approved Training Body (ATB) might charge you for any practice sessions, use of a motorbike or the training area before the assessment.  
2. If you pass, the ATB has to register you with DVSA for a CBT1 instructor certificate. DVSA’s fee for the certificate is £67 and it lasts 4 years.  
3. You’ll need to renew the certificate every 4 years (the renewal fee is the same).  

Check with the ATB that’s sponsoring you for their training charges and any other local costs.  

More details: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How do I set up my own motorcycle training business?
generated_response:
Setting up a motorcycle training business involves two parts: (a) becoming an approved instructor and (b) setting up and running the business.

1. Check you meet the basic instructor rules  
   • You must be at least 21, have held a full UK category A motorcycle licence for 3 years or more, and have no more than 5 penalty points in the last 4 years.  

2. Qualify as an instructor  
   a. Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) instructor  
      • Book and pass a 2-day DVSA instructor assessment.  
   b. Direct Access Scheme (DAS) instructor (to teach larger bikes)  
      • After passing CBT, complete a further 2-day DAS assessment.  
   Apply and book through DVSA: https://www.gov.uk/become-motorcycle-instructor  

3. Register an Approved Training Body (ATB)  
   • You need an off-road training site that meets DVSA size and safety rules, at least 2 suitable motorcycles, safety equipment and log-books.  
   • Fill in form ATB1 and send it to DVSA with site diagrams and insurance evidence.  
   Guidance and forms: https://www.gov.uk/become-motorcycle-instructor/approved-training-body  

4. Set up the business side  
   1. Choose a legal structure (sole trader, partnership or limited company).  
   2. Register with HMRC (and Companies House if limited).  
   3. Open a business bank account.  
   4. Arrange insurance:  
      • Public liability and professional indemnity.  
      • Employer’s liability (if you hire staff).  
      • Motorcycle fleet insurance (covers bikes used by trainees).  

5. Sort premises, equipment and safety  
   • Secure storage for bikes, a classroom or office, helmets, jackets, gloves and radios.  
   • Write risk assessments and a health-and-safety policy; keep maintenance records for bikes and PPE.  

6. Put booking and finance systems in place  
   • Simple online booking tool or phone/email diary.  
   • Keep income / expense records; consider accounting software or an accountant.  

7. Market your courses  
   • List on the DVSA’s “find a motorcycle trainer” service automatically once your ATB is approved.  
   • Create a basic website, social-media pages and posters in local cafés, colleges and bike shops.  

8. Stay compliant  
   • Renew instructor registration every 4 years.  
   • Keep insurance, site approval and equipment certificates up to date.  
   • Notify DVSA of any changes to instructors, sites or vehicles.  

Local help  
Business Wales (03000 6 03000) offers free start-up advice, workshops and some small grants or loans.

prompt: I need to apply for my first GB driver digital tachograph card. What are the steps I should follow and what documents do I need to prepare?
generated_response:
Below is the standard process for getting your first Great Britain (GB) driver digital tachograph card.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must hold a valid GB photocard driving licence for the vehicle categories you’ll be driving.  
   • Your driving-licence photo must be in date (taken within the last 10 years). If it isn’t, renew the licence first.

2. Decide how to apply  
   a) Online – quickest (usually 5–10 working days)  
      • Go to “Apply for a digital tachograph driver card” on GOV.UK.  
      • Have ready:  
        – Driving-licence number and National Insurance number (if you know it).  
        – A UK-issued debit or credit card to pay the £32 fee.  
      • The service re-uses the photo and signature already held for your photocard licence, so you don’t usually need to upload anything.  

   b) By post – if you can’t apply online (up to 15 working days)  
      • Pick up form D777B/DL (Application for a digital tachograph driver card) from most Post Offices or order it from DVLA.  
      • Fill in sections 1–10.  
      • Enclose:  
        – Your GB photocard driving licence (photocopies are not accepted).  
        – One passport-style colour photo (only if you still have the older paper licence).  
        – A cheque or postal order for £32 made payable to ‘DVLA’ (write your full name on the back).  
      • Send the form and documents to:  
        DVLA, Swansea, SA99 1ST.

3. Wait for your card  
   • Cards are normally issued within 10 working days (online) or 15 working days (post).  
   • It will arrive by secure mail; someone may need to sign for it.

4. When it arrives  
   • Insert the card into the vehicle unit for at least 1 minute before driving.  
   • Keep the card with you whenever you’re working/driving a tachograph-equipped vehicle.  

5. Keep it up to date  
   • The card is valid for 5 years. Apply for a renewal no later than 15 working days before it runs out.  
   • Tell DVLA within 7 days if it’s lost, stolen or damaged.

Key documents/information you should gather now  
• Your valid GB photocard driving licence.  
• Debit/credit card, cheque or postal order for the £32 fee.  
• One passport photo (only if you hold the old-style paper licence).  
• National Insurance number (helpful but not essential).

Relevant link  
Apply online: https://www.gov.uk/apply-for-a-digital-tachograph-driver-card

prompt: I am an agricultural worker in Wales and I want to know if I can take time off for personal reasons. What are the rules regarding statutory leave and time off?
generated_response:
As an agricultural worker in Wales you have the same core statutory rights to leave as other employees, plus extra holiday that comes from the Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order. The main types of leave you can use for personal reasons are:

1. Paid annual leave  
   • You are entitled to at least 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year (this can include bank holidays).  
   • The Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order currently gives most full-time farm workers a total of 6 weeks’ paid holiday. Your contract may give more.  
   • To book annual leave you must give notice that is at least twice the length of the leave you want to take, plus 1 day (e.g. 3 days’ notice for 1 day off) unless your contract says otherwise.

2. Time off for dependants (unpaid)  
   • You can take a “reasonable” amount of unpaid time off to deal with an emergency involving a spouse, partner, child, parent or someone who depends on you for care.  
   • There is no set limit, but it is normally 1–2 days per incident and you should tell your employer as soon as possible.

3. Parental leave (unpaid, planned)  
   • If you have worked for your employer for at least 1 year you can take up to 18 weeks’ unpaid leave for each child under 18.  
   • You can take up to 4 weeks per child per year and must give at least 21 days’ notice.  

4. Parental bereavement leave  
   • If a child dies or is stillborn after 24 weeks’ pregnancy, you can take 2 weeks’ leave.  
   • Statutory Parental Bereavement Pay may be available if you meet the earnings test.

5. Sick leave  
   • You can take time off when ill and, if you earn at least £123 a week (2023–24 rate), receive Statutory Sick Pay for up to 28 weeks.

6. Compassionate or other special leave  
   • There is no automatic statutory right, but many farm employers offer paid or unpaid compassionate leave. Check your contract or staff handbook.

Practical steps to take:

1. Check your contract or staff handbook to see the exact holiday allowance and any workplace rules.  
2. Decide which type of leave suits your situation (annual leave if you need planned paid time off; time off for dependants if it is an emergency, etc.).  
3. Give your employer the required notice in writing (email is fine) stating the dates and type of leave.  
4. Keep a copy of all correspondence.  
5. If you think you are being refused a right you are entitled to, raise it informally first, then follow your employer’s grievance procedure or get advice from Acas (0300 123 1100).

Useful GOV.UK pages  
• Holiday entitlement: https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights  
• Time off for dependants: https://www.gov.uk/time-off-for-dependants  
• Parental leave: https://www.gov.uk/parental-leave  
• Parental bereavement leave and pay: https://www.gov.uk/parental-bereavement-pay-leave  
For extra Welsh agricultural holiday rights, search “Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order” on the Welsh Government site or ask your union or the Agricultural Wages Helpline (0300 062 1068).

prompt: I'm starting a small retail business and need to hire someone. What steps should I follow to ensure I'm following the correct procedures for employing someone and setting up their contract?
generated_response:
Below is a simple checklist you can follow when taking on your first employee in England.

1. Confirm the person can work in the UK  
   • Check and copy original right-to-work documents before they start.

2. Decide the pay and hours  
   • Pay at least the National Minimum Wage/National Living Wage.  
   • Keep to the Working Time Regulations on hours, breaks and annual leave.

3. Take out Employers’ Liability Insurance  
   • You must have cover of at least £5 million in place from the first day the employee works.

4. Register as an employer with HMRC  
   • Do this online no later than 2 weeks before the first payday so you can run PAYE.

5. Set up payroll  
   • Choose payroll software or an external provider.  
   • Use PAYE to deduct Income Tax and National Insurance, give payslips and report to HMRC.

6. Prepare and issue the written terms (‘statement of employment particulars’)  
   • Most key terms (pay, hours, place of work, holiday, etc.) must be given on or before the first working day.  
   • Include any wider contract terms (probation period, notice, confidentiality, etc.).  
   • Provide statutory policies such as disciplinary and grievance procedures.

7. Draft workplace policies relevant to retail  
   • Health & safety (including manual handling and slips/trips).  
   • Data protection, equal opportunities, sickness and absence.  
   • Age-restricted sales procedures (e.g. alcohol, knives) if applicable.

8. Advertise the job and recruit fairly  
   • Write an accurate job description.  
   • Use fair selection methods and avoid discrimination under the Equality Act 2010.  
   • Keep interview notes for at least 6 months.

9. Once you choose someone  
   • Make a written offer, agree a start date and keep copies.  
   • Collect their P45 or have them complete HMRC’s starter checklist.

10. Enrol the employee into a workplace pension (auto-enrolment)  
    • If they are aged 22–State Pension age and earn £10,000+ a year, you must enrol and contribute.

11. Keep records  
    • Right-to-work checks, payroll, contracts, hours worked, holidays, sickness and any accidents.  
    • Most employment records must be kept for at least 3 years; some (e.g. PAYE) for 6 years.

12. Provide an induction and training  
    • Show them emergency procedures, health & safety, and any point-of-sale systems.  
    • Give regular supervision and keep training records.

GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: If I was dismissed without notice and my employer went insolvent, what steps should I take to claim the money I'm owed for my notice period?
generated_response:
If your employer has gone insolvent and you were dismissed without any notice, you can claim Statutory Notice Pay from the government’s Redundancy Payments Service (RPS).  

1. Get the case reference  
   • The insolvency practitioner (sometimes called the administrator or liquidator) dealing with your employer will write to you.  
   • Their letter/email will give you a case reference number and tell you the online service to use. You need this reference before you can claim. If you have not had it yet, contact the practitioner’s office and ask for it.  

2. Gather your details  
   • National Insurance number.  
   • Bank account details (for payment).  
   • The date you were dismissed and the date your employer became insolvent (this will be in the practitioner’s letter).  
   • Recent payslips or your contract showing your normal weekly pay.  

3. Apply online to the Redundancy Payments Service  
   • Go to: https://www.gov.uk/claim-redundancy  
   • Choose “Start now” and enter the case reference when asked.  
   • Complete the “Notice Pay” section of the form (you can also claim any unpaid wages or holiday pay at the same time).  
   • Submit the form – there is no fee.  

4. What happens next  
   • The RPS will check the details with the insolvency practitioner.  
   • They pay Statutory Notice Pay direct to your bank (up to 8 weeks’ pay, capped at the statutory weekly limit). Tax and National Insurance are taken off.  
   • If you claim benefits during what would have been your notice period, the equivalent amount is deducted from the payment.  

5. Need help?  
   • RPS helpline: 0330 331 0020 (Mon–Fri, office hours).  
   • If you aren’t sure how to fill in the form, the insolvency practitioner or Acas (0300 123 1100) can explain.  

Keep copies of all forms and letters. There is a 6-month time limit from the date your employment ended, so apply as soon as you have the reference.

prompt: What are the different ways I can ensure my employees have a say in how the business is run, and how does this affect their ownership stake?
generated_response:
There are two broad ways to give staff a voice:

A. Voice without changing ownership  
1. Regular staff-management meetings or “employee forums”.  
2. Recognising a trade union or setting up a works council.  
3. Using the Information and Consultation of Employees (ICE) Regulations – if 2 % of employees ask, you must set up a formal consultation arrangement.  
4. Appointing an employee-elected director to the board (not a legal requirement, but permitted under UK company law).  

These options let employees influence decisions, but they do not give them a legal share of the business. Ownership remains with the existing shareholders.

B. Voice that also gives staff an ownership stake  
1. Share incentive plans (SIPs) or Save As You Earn (SAYE) schemes – employees buy or are awarded shares while you keep overall control.  
2. Enterprise Management Incentives (EMIs) – tax-favoured share options for key staff.  
3. Employee shareholder status (rarely used now) – staff receive shares in return for giving up some employment rights.  
4. Employee Ownership Trust (EOT) – you sell 51 % or more of the company to a trust that holds the shares for all employees. Staff elect trustees and usually have a representative on the board.  
   • Day-to-day control is usually shared through an employee council.  
   • Tax reliefs apply to the selling owner and to bonuses paid to employees.  

How ownership is affected  
• Options in Group A: no change to shareholding.  
• Options 1-3 in Group B: employees gain individual shareholdings (from a few per cent to whatever you grant). You keep any shares you do not issue.
