• Option 4 (EOT): the trust, on behalf of all employees, becomes the majority owner. You, as the former owner, can keep up to 49 % or exit completely.

Practical next steps  
1. Decide whether you only need a consultation mechanism (Group A) or want employees to own shares (Group B).  
2. If consultation only:  
   a. Talk to staff about the format they prefer (forum, council, union).  
   b. Draft written terms of reference and schedule regular meetings.  
   c. Register an ICE agreement if 2 % or more staff request it.  
3. If offering shares:  
   a. Speak to an accountant or solicitor about which share scheme fits your goals.  
   b. Check eligibility and register the scheme with HMRC.  
   c. Communicate clearly to employees how voting rights and dividends will work.  
4. If considering an EOT:  
   a. Get an independent business valuation.  
   b. Arrange finance for the trust to buy your shares (often vendor-financed).  
   c. Create a trust deed, appoint trustees, and inform HMRC to claim the tax reliefs.  
5. Keep written records of any new structures and update your company’s Articles of Association if share rights change.  

Further guidance  
GOV.UK – Employing people hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people  
(search within for “employee ownership” and “share schemes”)

prompt: What are the key steps to prepare my business to employ staff?
generated_response:
To get your business ready to take on its first employee, follow these steps in order:

1. Check you can afford it  
   • Draw up a simple forecast of wages, National Insurance (NI), pension contributions, holiday pay, sick pay and any equipment or training costs.

2. Register as an employer with HMRC  
   • Do this before the first payday (it can take up to 5 working days).  
   • You’ll receive your PAYE reference numbers.

3. Take out Employers’ Liability insurance  
   • Minimum £5 million cover is a legal requirement for most businesses once you employ staff.  
   • Have the certificate displayed (or accessible online) on the first day the employee starts.

4. Decide how you will run payroll  
   • Choose payroll software or use an accountant/bookkeeper.  
   • Set up to calculate Income Tax, NI, statutory payments and submit Real Time Information (RTI) to HMRC each payday.

5. Confirm the right to employ  
   • Check each candidate’s right to work in the UK and keep copies of the evidence.  
   • If the role needs a DBS check (e.g. working with children), apply in good time.

6. Prepare the employment contract and policies  
   • Issue a written statement of employment particulars on or before day 1.  
   • Include pay, hours, holiday, notice periods and key policies (disciplinary, grievance, health & safety, data protection).  
   • Decide on the pay rate and ensure it meets or exceeds the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage.

7. Set up workplace pension auto-enrolment  
   • Choose a pension scheme and assess the worker.  
   • Enrol eligible staff and make employer contributions by the legal deadline.  
   • Complete your declaration of compliance to The Pensions Regulator.

8. Register for and meet Health & Safety duties  
   • Conduct a risk assessment and record significant findings.  
   • Provide any necessary training, protective equipment or first-aid arrangements.  
   • Display the health and safety poster or give the leaflet to staff.

9. Prepare for statutory record-keeping and reporting  
   • Keep payroll records, hours worked, holiday, sickness, maternity/paternity and right-to-work evidence for at least 3 years (some records longer).  
   • Put GDPR-compliant data protection measures in place.

10. Plan induction and ongoing management  
    • Give an induction covering the job, policies, fire safety and safeguarding (if relevant).  
    • Set up a simple system for performance reviews and day-to-day support.

Useful GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: Can I get legal advice if I'm facing an employment tribunal claim?
generated_response:
Yes. Whether you are the person making the claim or the one responding, you can get legal advice and representation before and during an Employment Tribunal case.  

1. Free initial help  
   1.1 Call Acas (0300 123 1100) for confidential guidance and to start the mandatory Early Conciliation process.  
   1.2 Contact Citizens Advice Scotland for free, independent advice on your rights and options.  
   1.3 Check if a local Law Centre, university legal clinic or advocacy charity can take on employment cases.  

2. Professional legal representation  
   2.1 Search the Law Society of Scotland’s “find a solicitor” service for a solicitor who handles Employment Tribunal work.  
   2.2 Ask about fixed-fee consultations or “no-win-no-fee” arrangements (still rare in employment cases but available in some firms).  

3. Help that might cover the cost  
   3.1 Trade union membership – many unions provide free legal support for workplace disputes.  
   3.2 Legal-expenses insurance – look at your home, car or credit-card policies; some include cover for employment claims.  
   3.3 Legal aid – employment matters are only funded in limited circumstances, but you can check eligibility with the Scottish Legal Aid Board.  
   3.4 For low-income parties, the Employment Tribunals (Scotland) often allow lay representatives (friends, family, advisers) if you cannot afford a lawyer.  

4. Preparing for the Tribunal  
   4.1 Gather contracts, pay slips, emails and notes of conversations.  
   4.2 Keep a timeline of events and any attempts to resolve the dispute.  
   4.3 Meet all tribunal deadlines (response forms, evidence bundles, hearing dates). Your adviser can help with this.  

Relevant GOV.UK link:  
Employing people (includes links to Employment Tribunal guidance and Acas services) – https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I need to stop employing people in my business. What steps do I need to take to inform HMRC and close my PAYE scheme?
generated_response:
If you no longer have anyone on your payroll and want to close your PAYE scheme, do the following:

1. Run your last payroll  
   a. Process leavers for every employee and give them their P45s.  
   b. Send your Final Full Payment Submission (FPS) to HMRC – tick/flag the box that says ‘This is the final submission for this PAYE scheme’.  
   c. Enter the ‘date scheme ceased’ (the day after the last payday).  

2. Send an Employer Payment Summary (EPS) if needed  
   • Only do this if you still have statutory payments to reclaim or need to report no-payments for the final tax month.  
   • Tick/flag ‘final submission’ on the EPS as well.

3. Pay any outstanding PAYE, National Insurance and student loan deductions by the usual deadline (22nd of the next tax month if paying electronically).

4. Keep your payroll records for at least 3 full tax years after the end of the tax year in which the scheme closed.

5. Check your HMRC online account  
   • Within a few days it should show the scheme as ‘ceased’.  
   • If it doesn’t, phone the Employer Helpline (0300 200 3200) and ask them to close the scheme, giving the cessation date you put on the FPS.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm a reservist in Scotland and my employer has asked about my training schedule. What should I tell them about the time off for training and how does it affect my job?
generated_response:
Your employer is entitled to know what time you will be away, but the law gives you clear protection.  

1. What training you must do  
   • Most reservists do about 27 days a year – usually one evening a week, a number of weekend exercises and a 2-week annual camp.  
   • Your unit will give you a written “Training Notice” that lists the exact dates each year.  
   • Give a copy of that notice to your head-teacher or HR as soon as you receive it (the MOD recommends at least 6 weeks’ notice for the 2-week camp).  

2. Time off and pay  
   • Employers do not have to give paid leave for reserve training.  
   • Many teachers use ordinary annual leave or unpaid special leave; some local authorities have a policy granting additional paid or unpaid “forces leave”. Check your school’s leave policy or local-authority HR guide.  
   • Agree in writing which days will be taken as annual leave, unpaid leave or special leave so your pay and pension deductions are handled correctly.  

3. Protection of your job  
   • Under the Reserve Forces (Safeguard of Employment) Act 1985 and the Employment Rights Act 1996, it is unlawful to treat you unfavourably, refuse promotion or dismiss you because you are a reservist.  
   • If you are later mobilised for active service, you have the right to return to the same job (or a suitable alternative) on comparable terms. Your continuous employment and pension reckonable service are preserved.  

4. Good practice when speaking to your employer  
   1. Arrange a short meeting with your head-teacher/HR.  
   2. Take the Training Notice and a summary of the statutory protections (your unit or SaBRE can provide one).  
   3. Propose how classes will be covered while you are away.  
   4. Record the agreed dates and type of leave in an email or memo.  

5. Where to get help  
   • SaBRE (Supporting Britain’s Reservists and Employers) helpline: 0800 389 5459 – for you or your employer.  
   • ACAS helpline: 0300 123 1100 – if you think you’re being treated unfairly.  

Key GOV.UK guidance:  
Employing people: Reservists, the armed forces and mobilisation – gov.uk/employee-reservist

prompt: I need to check a job applicant's right to work in the UK. Do I need to use their share code, or can I check their original documents instead?
generated_response:
You can do either a manual check of original documents or an online check using a share code – it depends on the immigration status of the applicant.  

1. Ask the applicant which type of evidence they have.  
   • If they have an online immigration status (for example a visa in the EU Settlement Scheme, a Biometric Residence Permit/Card issued after 2015, a Frontier Worker Permit or an eVisa) you must use the Home Office online service.  
   • If they are a British or Irish citizen, or hold another document in List A or List B of acceptable documents, you can carry out a manual check instead.  

2. Online check (when required or chosen)  
   a. Ask the applicant for their 9-character share code and date of birth.  
   b. Go to “View a job applicant’s right to work details” on GOV.UK and enter the details.  
   c. Confirm that the photo matches the person you intend to employ and download/save the Home Office response for your records.  

3. Manual check of original documents (where allowed)  
   a. See Lists A and B on GOV.UK for the documents you can accept (e.g. a British passport, an Irish passport or passport card, or a current visa sticker in a passport issued before 6 April 2022).  
   b. Check the original document in the applicant’s presence (in person or over an adjusted check if the current temporary COVID concession still applies).  
   c. Make a clear copy showing all pages, note the date you made the check, and keep the copy securely for at least two years after employment ends.  

4. Do not accept an expired Biometric Residence Permit/Card, a digital status print-out or a photocopy as evidence – you will not have a statutory excuse if you do.  

5. If you are unsure at any stage, use the Employer Enquiry Service or seek professional advice before the person starts work.  

Key link: https://www.gov.uk/check-job-applicant-right-to-work

prompt: I'm a student in Scotland and I want to understand my contract and working hours. How do I find information about my rights and responsibilities regarding my working hours and contract terms?
generated_response:
You have the same basic employment rights as any other worker in the UK.  
To check your working hours, breaks and contract terms:

1. Read your contract or written statement  
   • You must receive this no later than your first day.  
   • It should set out your hours, pay, holiday entitlement, notice period and job title.

2. Check the Working Time Regulations  
   • You cannot be made to work more than an average 48 hours a week unless you sign an “opt-out”.  
   • You are entitled to 11 hours’ rest between shifts and a 20-minute break if you work more than six hours a day.  
   • You must have at least one full day off every week (or two days every fortnight).  

3. Make sure you are being paid at least the National Minimum Wage for your age.  

4. Keep your own record of hours worked, breaks taken and any changes agreed with your employer.  

5. If something is missing or seems wrong  
   • Raise it informally with your manager first.  
   • If that does not fix it, use your employer’s formal grievance procedure (ask HR or look in your handbook).  
   • For free, confidential help contact Acas on 0300 123 1100 or Citizens Advice Scotland on 0800 028 1456.  

Useful GOV.UK starting point  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people – follow links to “Employment contracts” and “Working hours”.

prompt: What are the conditions for applying for an injunction to stop industrial action that affects my business?
generated_response:
To stop planned or ongoing industrial action you must ask a civil court for an injunction (called an “interdict” in Scotland).  
The court will only grant it if you can show that the action is likely to be unlawful.  The most common grounds are that the trade union has not followed the legal rules set out in the Trade Union and Labour Relations (Consolidation) Act 1992.

1. Check whether the action is legally protected  
   • Was there a properly run secret postal ballot?  
   • Did a majority vote “yes”?  
   • Has the union given you at least 14 days’ written notice of the dates and type of action (or 7 days if you agreed)?  
   • Is the action starting within 4 weeks of the ballot result (or 12 weeks if you agreed)?  
   • Is the action limited to the staff and workplace described in the ballot paper and the notice?  
   • Is it primary action (not secondary action in support of another employer’s dispute)?  
   • Is any picketing peaceful and at or near the employees’ own workplace?

   If the answer to any of these is “no”, the action may lose its legal protection and you can apply for an injunction.

2. Act quickly  
   • Apply to the Court of Session (or a local sheriff court) before the action starts if possible.  
   • The application should set out which legal requirements have been breached and how your business will suffer if the action goes ahead.  
   • You will usually need to give the union at least 24 hours’ notice of the hearing and serve them with your evidence.  

3. Prepare the evidence  
   • Copies of the strike notices, ballot papers (if you have them), correspondence, and any witness statements.  
   • An estimate of the financial loss or other harm the strike will cause.  

4. Provide an undertaking in damages  
   • The court will normally require you to promise to pay the union’s losses if it later turns out the injunction should not have been granted.  

5. Court decision  
   • If the court is satisfied the action is probably unlawful and the balance of convenience favours you, it will grant the injunction/interdict.  
   • Once served, the union must call off the industrial action immediately or it will be in contempt of court.  

6. Get professional help  
   Employment injunctions are technical and time-critical.  Speak to a solicitor experienced in employment or commercial litigation before you apply.

Further guidance:  
Industrial action and disputes – employers (GOV.UK)  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I work in a retail store and am an agency worker. Can I take time off for training?
generated_response:
Under current UK employment law, the statutory right to request “time to train” only applies to employees. Agency workers are classed as “workers”, so you don’t automatically have a legal right to take time off for training that isn’t required for your job.

What you can do:  
1. Check your agency contract. See if it already allows paid or unpaid study leave.  
2. Talk to your agency consultant. Explain the training you want and ask whether they can arrange paid release or schedule your shifts around it.  
3. Speak to the store’s manager. Hirers sometimes agree to flexible shifts so agency staff can attend courses, especially if the skills will help the business.  
4. If the training is legally required (for example, health and safety), the agency or hirer must organise it and pay you for the time.  
5. Look at free or subsidised courses you can do outside working hours—start with the National Careers Service or local colleges.  
6. If you reach a long-term agreement to stay with the retailer, you could ask to be taken on as an employee; at that point the statutory “time to train” rules would apply.

Useful link  
Agency workers’ rights and training (GOV.UK): https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: Can I employ a 12-year-old for a summer job in my shop, such as helping with customer service or stocking shelves?
I recently left my job and am trying to find a new one. Can I ask my previous employer for a reference, and what should I do if they refuse?
generated_response:
Yes. You can always ask a past employer for a reference, but in most jobs they are not legally required to give one. Here is what to do.

1. Make the request  
   • Write or email the manager or HR team who holds your personnel file.  
   • Ask for a “basic factual reference” (dates of employment, job title) or a fuller reference covering your duties and performance – whichever you prefer.  
   • Give a clear deadline and the name/email address of the person who will receive it.

2. If they agree  
   • Check the reference is accurate.  
   • If you believe it is misleading or factually incorrect, you can:  
     – Ask the employer to amend it.  
     – Raise a formal grievance if they refuse.  
     – Consider legal advice if the reference is negligent and costs you a job.

3. If they refuse or do not reply  
   • Ask if they will at least confirm your employment dates and job title – many employers are happy to do this.  
   • Offer alternative referees to prospective employers, such as:  
     – Another manager or supervisor from the same workplace.  
     – A colleague, client or supplier who can comment on your work.  
     – A professional, community or voluntary contact who knows you well.  
   • Explain the situation to your new employer; most accept other references once they know your former employer’s policy.  
   • Keep evidence that you made a reasonable attempt to obtain a reference (emails, letters) in case your next employer asks.

4. Regulated roles (for example in financial services, healthcare or working with children/vulnerable adults)  
   • More detailed references may be mandatory. If you are moving into one of these roles, tell the new employer straight away and ask them to request the reference directly—they often have set procedures.

5. If you need further help  
   • Check Acas for free impartial advice (0300 123 1100).  
   • Citizens Advice can offer guidance if you think a refusal is discriminatory or a reference is unfair.  
   • For general employment guidance visit GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I heard that a union can be derecognised if the number of workers in the bargaining unit drops below 50%. How do I know if this applies to my workplace and what should I do next?
generated_response:
To work out whether your union could be derecognised because fewer than half the workers in the bargaining unit are union members, follow these steps.

1. Confirm how the union is recognised  
   • Ask your union rep or HR whether recognition was gained through a voluntary agreement with the employer or through the statutory Central Arbitration Committee (CAC) procedure.  
   • The 50 % rule applies only to unions that have statutory recognition (or a voluntary agreement that mirrors the statutory rules).

2. Identify the bargaining unit  
   • The bargaining unit is the group of employees covered by the recognition agreement (for example, all shop-floor staff in one store).  
   • Check the written recognition agreement or ask HR/your rep for the exact description.

3. Check current numbers  
   • Total workers: ask HR for the current headcount in the bargaining unit.  
   • Union membership: ask your union branch or rep for the latest verified membership figure for that same group.  
   • Work out the percentage: (union members ÷ total workers) × 100.

4. Understand the legal trigger  
   • If union membership falls below 50 % and at least 3 years have passed since recognition, the employer can apply to the CAC to end recognition.  
   • Alternatively, 10 % of workers in the bargaining unit can apply for a derecognition ballot.  
   • Recognition continues unless the CAC formally approves derecognition or a ballot votes against the union.

5. Decide what to do next  
   • If membership is close to or below 50 %, speak to your union about a recruitment drive to increase membership.  
   • Keep clear records of membership growth so you can demonstrate majority support if challenged.  
   • Encourage non-members to join by explaining the benefits (collective bargaining over pay, hours and holidays).  
   • If you’re unsure of the figures or process, contact Acas for free, confidential advice on 0300 123 1100.

6. Stay informed  
   • Ask for copies of any correspondence between the employer and the union about recognition status.  
   • Check GOV.UK guidance on employing people and trade-union recognition for full legal details: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm looking after my child and want to take some time off work to focus on their needs. How do I apply for unpaid parental leave and what rights do I have while I'm away?
generated_response:
Unpaid parental leave lets you take up to 18 weeks off for each child (before their 18th birthday) without losing your job.  
Here’s how it works and what to do.

1. Check you qualify  
   • You must be an employee (not a worker or self-employed).  
   • You must have worked for your employer for 12 months or more.  
   • You must have parental responsibility (your name on the birth or adoption certificate or a formal agreement).

2. Work out how much time you can take  
   • Total allowance: 18 weeks per child.  
   • You can normally take up to 4 weeks of that allowance in any leave year for each child.  
   • Leave is taken in whole weeks (e.g. 1 week, 2 weeks) unless your employer agrees otherwise or your child is disabled.

3. Give notice to your employer  
   • Write or email at least 21 days before the first day you want off.  
   • State: that you are requesting statutory unpaid parental leave, your child’s name/date of birth, the start date and length of leave.  
   • Your employer may ask for evidence of your parental responsibility. A copy of the birth certificate is usually enough.

4. Understand possible postponement  
   • An employer can postpone the leave for “business reasons” (for example, if several staff are off at the same time) but must:  
     – discuss this with you, and  
     – give you a new start date within 6 months of your original request.  
   • They cannot postpone if the leave is to start immediately after your child is born or adopted.

5. Know your rights while on leave  
   • Your job, terms and conditions, and continuous employment are protected.  
   • You still build up (accrue) holiday entitlement and pension contributions.  
   • After up to 4 weeks’ leave you have the right to return to exactly the same job; if you take more than 4 weeks, you must be offered your old job or, if that is not reasonably practicable, a suitable alternative with the same terms.  
   • You cannot be treated unfavourably or dismissed for taking or asking for parental leave.

6. Keep records  
   • Keep a copy of your request and any reply.  
   • Track how many weeks of the 18-week allowance you have used.

If you need more flexibility you can also ask for flexible working or consider other family-related leave. Full details are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/parental-leave

prompt: I was dismissed from my job without proper notice. How can I find out if I am owed any compensation or if I can challenge the decision?
generated_response:
If you think you were dismissed without the right notice, you may have a claim for either:

• wrongful dismissal (breach of contract – for example, no or too little notice), and/or  
• unfair dismissal (the reason or process for dismissal was not fair).

Follow these steps:

1. Check your employment details  
   a. Look at your contract, staff handbook and any recent letters or emails from your employer.  
   b. Note your start and end dates, contractual notice period and any disciplinary procedures stated.  

2. Work out your basic rights  
   • Wrongful dismissal: Every employee is entitled to at least one week’s notice after one month’s service, rising to a maximum of 12 weeks after 12 years. If you were not paid for the full notice period, you can claim the pay you should have received.  
   • Unfair dismissal: You normally need a minimum of 2 years’ continuous service, unless the reason is automatically unfair (for example, pregnancy, whistleblowing or asserting statutory rights).

3. Gather evidence  
   • Payslips, the dismissal letter (or emails), your contract, and any notes of meetings.  
   • A timeline of events, including dates of conversations and copies of messages.

4. Try to resolve it informally first  
   Contact your former employer (for example in writing) asking for written reasons for your dismissal and any unpaid notice pay. This sometimes leads to a settlement.

5. Start ACAS Early Conciliation (mandatory)  
   If informal steps fail, contact ACAS within 3 months minus 1 day of the date your employment ended. Early Conciliation is free and is a legal requirement before you can make a tribunal claim.  
   Tel: 0300 123 1100 or search “ACAS Early Conciliation”.

6. Consider an Employment Tribunal claim  
   • Wrongful dismissal and/or unfair dismissal claims must normally be lodged within the tribunal no later than a month after your Early Conciliation certificate is issued.  
   • You can claim compensation for lost earnings, notice pay, holiday pay, and in unfair dismissal cases, a basic and compensatory award.  

7. Get advice  
   • Speak to an employment solicitor, your trade union (if any), Citizens Advice or ACAS.  
   • Legal Aid is rare for employment cases, but many solicitors offer a free initial chat or a no-win-no-fee arrangement.

8. Keep to time limits  
   Employment tribunal deadlines are strict. Submit the ACAS Early Conciliation request within 3 months minus 1 day of your dismissal date, even if you are still negotiating with your employer.

Useful GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I need to check an employee's immigration status because they don't have their documents. What steps should I follow to use the Employer Checking Service and avoid fines?
generated_response:
To avoid a civil penalty you must get and keep proof that the person has the right to work before they start (or within 60 days for existing staff whose permission has expired).  
Use the Employer Checking Service (ECS) only when the worker cannot show acceptable documents or give you a share code because, for example, they:  
• have an outstanding application or appeal with the Home Office  
• arrived in the UK before 1989 and have no papers  
• are a Ukrainian guest with an entry stamp but no Biometric Residence Permit yet  

Follow these steps:

1. Confirm ECS is needed  
   • Ask the worker if they can give you a digital share code or any original documents on the right-to-work list.  
   • If not, explain you must contact the Home Office.

2. Collect the information you’ll need  
   • Worker’s full name, date of birth, nationality and Home Office reference number (if they have one).  
   • Your business name, address and contact details.  
   • Job title, hours and proposed start date.

3. Make the check  
   • Go to GOV.UK > Employing people > “Employer Checking Service”.  
   • Complete the online form (or phone if you cannot get online). Submit the details.

4. Keep the response  
   • You’ll usually get a ‘Positive Verification Notice’ (PVN) within 5 working days. Print or save it.  
   • A PVN gives you a statutory excuse against a fine for 6 months from the date on the notice.

5. Follow up when required  
   • Diarise a reminder to repeat the check before the PVN expires.  
   • If the worker still cannot show their own evidence, submit another ECS request.  
   • If the Home Office later sends a ‘Negative Verification Notice’, you must normally end employment.

6. Keep records safely  
   • Retain the PVN and any later notices for at least 2 years after the person leaves your employment.  
   • Make sure only authorised staff can see the records and do not discriminate by only checking certain groups.

Doing each step and keeping the PVN on file protects you from civil penalties of up to £20,000 per illegal worker.

Most relevant GOV.UK link  
Employing people – all right-to-work guidance and the Employer Checking Service form: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I need to know what steps I should take if my partner's baby is in neonatal care and I want to take leave and get pay. How do I apply for this and what do I need to do to ensure I get the right amount of leave and pay?
generated_response:
You have a few different rights you can use straight away while your baby is in neonatal care.  The choice depends on how much leave you have already taken and how long you need to be off work. 

Step-by-step

1. Check whether you still have Statutory Paternity Leave available  
   • Up to 2 weeks’ leave (paid at £172.48 a week or 90% of your average weekly earnings, whichever is lower).  
   • You can now split it into 2 separate 1-week blocks and take it at any point in the first 12 months after the birth if the baby is in hospital.  
   • Tell your employer in writing at least 28 days before the start of each week you want to take.  
   GOV.UK: www.gov.uk/paternity-pay-leave

2. Consider Shared Parental Leave (SPL) if you need more time and your partner is willing to curtail part of her maternity leave/pay  
   • Up to 50 weeks’ leave and 37 weeks’ pay can be shared.  
   • You can take SPL in blocks (paid or unpaid) and return in between.  
   • You must give your employer 8 weeks’ notice and provide form SC6 plus your partner’s maternity leave details.  
   GOV.UK: www.gov.uk/shared-parental-leave-and-pay

3. Use Parental Leave for additional unpaid time (if your child will spend or has spent at least 1 week in hospital)  
   • Up to 18 weeks’ unpaid leave for each child up to their 18th birthday.  
   • Limited to 4 weeks per year for each child unless your employer agrees more.  
   • Give 21 days’ notice in writing.  
   GOV.UK: www.gov.uk/parental-leave

4. Ask about discretionary neonatal/compassionate leave in your school or local-authority policy  
   • Many education employers offer extra paid or unpaid days where a newborn is in neonatal intensive care.  
   • Provide a hospital letter or certificate confirming the baby is an inpatient.

5. If you suddenly need to deal with an emergency (for example, a change in the baby’s condition) you can take unpaid ‘time off for dependants’ without notice  
   • Usually 1–2 days at a time to arrange care or be at the hospital.  
   GOV.UK: www.gov.uk/time-off-for-dependants

6. Keep evidence and stay in touch  
   • Keep copies of hospital admission letters and any forms you give your employer.  
   • Agree in writing the dates you will be off and the type of leave for each period so your pay is processed correctly.

Looking ahead  
A new law – the Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Act 2023 – will introduce up to 12 weeks of extra paid leave for parents of babies who spend 7 days or more in neonatal care. It is not expected to start before April 2025, so the steps above are the current options.

If anything is unclear, speak to your school’s HR/payroll team or check Acas (0300 123 1100) for free advice.

prompt: What should I do if I receive a claim against me and conciliation hasn't worked?
generated_response:
If Early Conciliation with Acas has finished and you have now been sent an Employment Tribunal claim (form ET1), you must respond quickly.

1. Read the claim form carefully  
   • Note the case number, deadlines and what the claimant says happened.

2. Decide if you will accept all, part or none of the claim.

3. Complete form ET3 (the “response”)  
   • Explain which parts you agree or disagree with and why.  
   • Attach any key documents that support your version of events.

4. Send the ET3 to the tribunal office within 28 days of the date it was sent to you  
   • Miss the deadline and the tribunal can decide the case without hearing your side.  
   • Keep proof of posting or upload confirmation.

5. Gather evidence  
   • Witness statements, policies, emails, records of meetings, pay slips, etc.  
   • Keep everything organised – you may later be asked for a “bundle”.

6. Consider settlement at any stage  
   • You can still negotiate directly or through Acas after proceedings have started.  
   • A legally-binding settlement can be reached using an Acas COT3 agreement or a settlement agreement signed by an independent adviser.

7. Prepare for hearings  
   • The tribunal may hold a preliminary hearing by phone or video to set the timetable.  
   • Follow any case management orders (for example, deadlines for exchanging documents or witness statements).

8. Attend the final hearing (if the case is not settled)  
   • Bring copies of your documents and witness statements.  
   • After hearing both sides the tribunal will issue its decision.

9. If you lose, comply with any award or order promptly.  
   • You can ask for written reasons or, in limited circumstances, appeal to the Employment Appeal Tribunal.

Help and guidance  
• Acas helpline: 0300 123 1100 (free, impartial advice)  
• Respond to an employment tribunal claim – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/respond-employment-tribunal-claim

prompt: I've tried talking to my manager about a problem at work, but nothing has been resolved. How do I formally raise a grievance and what happens next?
generated_response:
If an informal chat with your manager has not fixed the issue, you can make a formal workplace grievance.  

1. Check your employer’s grievance policy  
   • Ask HR or look in your staff handbook or intranet.   
   • The policy should set out timescales and who to send the grievance to.  

2. Put your grievance in writing  
   • Write a brief letter or email headed ‘Formal Grievance’.  
   • Explain what has happened, when, who was involved and how it has affected you.  
   • State what outcome you want (for example, an apology, training, change of rota).  
   • Keep a copy.  

3. Your employer arranges a grievance meeting  
   • You must be given reasonable notice.  
   • You have the legal right to take a colleague or trade-union representative with you.  
   • At the meeting you can explain your concerns, show any evidence and say how you’d like it resolved.  

4. Employer investigates and gives a written decision  
   • They should look into the facts and speak to any witnesses.  
   • You should receive the decision in writing, with reasons and any action they will take.  

5. If you disagree, you can appeal  
   • Write an appeal letter within the deadline set out in the decision (usually 5–10 working days).  
   • The appeal should be heard by someone not previously involved, where possible.  

6. Still unresolved?  
   • Contact Acas for free early conciliation before taking any legal action (0300 123 1100).  
   • You normally have 3 months less 1 day from the problem (or final decision) to start an employment tribunal claim.  

Useful link  
Acas guidance (via GOV.UK): https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm a retired person in Wales and I want to know how to report my employer for not paying me the correct National Minimum Wage. What steps should I take?
generated_response:
You do not have to tackle this on your own. Follow these steps to report an employer who is not paying the National Minimum Wage (NMW):

1. Check your pay records  
   • Make sure you have copies of payslips, contracts, timesheets or any diary notes that show the hours you worked and what you were paid.  
   • Keep these safe; you may need to share them later.

2. Consider raising it with your (former) employer first  
   • It can be quicker to resolve if you write or speak to your manager or payroll department and explain the underpayment.  
   • If you feel uncomfortable or have already tried, move to step 3.

3. Call the Acas helpline for free, confidential advice  
   • Telephone 0300 123 1100 (Monday–Friday, 8am–6pm).  
   • Tell them you believe you have been paid below the NMW and would like to report it.  
   • They can talk you through your options and start a formal complaint to HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) on your behalf if you wish.  
   • You can remain anonymous—your employer will not be told who made the report.

4. Report directly to HMRC (optional)  
   • Complete the online form “Report an employer to HMRC if they’re not paying the minimum wage” on GOV.UK.  
   • Or phone the HMRC NMW enquiry line: 0800 917 2368 (Monday–Friday, 8am–6pm).  
   • Again, you can choose to stay anonymous.

5. Keep track of the case  
   • HMRC will investigate and can order your employer to repay the wage owed, plus a penalty.  
   • You do not have to pay anything, and the process will not affect your State Pension or benefits.

6. Seek further help if needed  
   • If you are a member of a trade union, let them know—they can support you.  
   • Citizens Advice Cymru can also help: 0800 702 2020.

Relevant GOV.UK pages:  
National Minimum Wage: enforcement and complaints – https://www.gov.uk/national-minimum-wage-compliance  
Acas helpline – https://www.acas.org.uk/contact

prompt: I'm managing an employee in Scotland who has taken multiple periods of sickness. How do I know if these periods are considered linked and if I need to calculate SSP manually?
generated_response:
Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) rules say that separate sickness absences can be treated as one continuous period – called “linked periods of sickness” – if BOTH of these tests are met:  
1. Each absence lasts 4 or more calendar days in a row (these are called Periods of Incapacity for Work – PIWs).  
2. The gap between the end of one PIW and the start of the next is 8 weeks (56 days) or less.

If an absence fails either test (e.g. it was only 1-3 days long, or the gap was more than 8 weeks), it is not linked and a new PIW starts.

What this means for you:  

1. List every spell of sickness. Write down the first and last day off sick for each spell.  
2. Check the length of each spell. Only keep those lasting 4 calendar days or more.  
3. Starting with the oldest spell, compare the gap to the next spell:  
   • If the gap is 56 days or less, the spells link. Treat them as one continuous period when counting SSP.  
   • If the gap is more than 56 days, the link is broken; start a new SSP period.  
4. Keep linking forward until a gap of more than 56 days is reached.  
5. Once you have the final linked period, work out SSP:  
   • You pay SSP only for qualifying days after the first 3 “waiting days” of the FIRST PIW in the linked chain.  
   • The maximum you pay is 28 weeks’ SSP across the whole linked period.  
6. If the employee has already had 28 weeks’ SSP in that linked period, no further SSP is due until a break of more than 8 weeks occurs.  
7. Record-keeping: keep sickness dates, SSP paid, and how you linked periods for at least 3 years after the end of the tax year.

If your payroll software cannot do this automatically, you can check the GOV.UK SSP manual calculator spreadsheet or do the steps above by hand.

Full guidance: https://www.gov.uk/statutory-sick-pay

prompt: I'm a teacher and I want to work part-time. How do I legally request a change to my working hours and what should I expect from my employer?
generated_response:
You have a legal right to ask for flexible working, which includes moving from full-time to part-time.

1. Check any local policy  
   • Most schools and academies have their own flexible-working policy.  
   • Read it first so you know any deadlines or forms they use.

2. Make a written request  
   • Head it “Statutory request for flexible working”.  
   • State the change you want (for example, reduce to 0.6 FTE, working Monday-Wednesday).  
   • Say when you would like the change to start.  
   • Explain how the work could be covered and how it might benefit the school (this is optional but helps).  
   • Date and sign it, and keep a copy.

3. Your employer’s duties  
   • They must deal with the request “reasonably” and give you a decision within 2 months, unless you both agree to more time.  
   • They should invite you to a meeting to discuss it and allow you to be accompanied by a colleague or union rep if you wish.  
   • They can only refuse for one of eight business reasons set out in law (for example, significant cost or impact on pupil learning).  
   • If they agree, they should issue a written variation to your contract. Your pay, pension contributions and holiday will be pro-rata to your new hours.

4. If the request is refused  
   • You should receive a written explanation.  
   • You can appeal internally.  
   • If the process was not handled fairly you may take the matter to Acas early conciliation and, if necessary, to an employment tribunal.

5. Practical tips  
   • Submit the request at least a term in advance so timetabling can be planned.  
   • Talk informally with your headteacher first; collaborative solutions often go through more smoothly.  
   • You can make up to two statutory requests in any 12-month period.

Key resource: GOV.UK Flexible Working – how to make a request and what employers must do  
https://www.gov.uk/flexible-working

prompt: How do I calculate my holiday entitlement if I work part-time and irregular hours?
generated_response:
You will always get at least 5.6 weeks of paid holiday a year, even if the hours you work change from week to week.  
Here is a simple way to work it out:

1. Gather your payslips for the last 52 weeks in which you were paid.  
   • Skip any weeks where you earned nothing (for example, weeks you did not work).  
2. Add up all the hours you were paid for in those 52 weeks.  
3. Divide that total by 52.  
   • This gives your average paid hours per week.  
4. Multiply the average weekly hours by 5.6.  
   • The answer is the total number of paid holiday hours you can take in the year.  
5. When you book time off, subtract the hours you would have worked on those days from your total. Keep a running balance so you know how much is left.  
6. If you leave the job part-way through the year, your unused holiday should be paid to you. Your employer must use the same method to work out what you are owed.  

Example  
• Total hours paid over 52 weeks: 1,040 hours  
• Average per week: 1,040 ÷ 52 = 20 hours  
• Annual holiday: 20 × 5.6 = 112 hours (about 14 working days if you usually do an 8-hour shift).  

Tip: Some employers let you build up holiday as you go. A quick rough guide is 12.07 % of the hours you work (about 7 minutes for every hour worked).  

If your employer uses a different method, it must still give you at least the result you would get with the steps above. If you think it is wrong, ask them to show their calculation or contact Acas for free advice (0300 123 1100).  

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm thinking about buying a small business, but I'm worried about the employees. How can I know if their jobs will stay the same if the business changes hands?
generated_response:
When you buy a business, most or all of the employees’ rights usually transfer to you under the TUPE regulations (Transfer of Undertakings – Protection of Employment). In practice this means their jobs, pay, holiday entitlement and length of service stay the same on the day you take over.  
Here is what to do before and after the sale:

1. Check if TUPE applies  
   • It normally applies when a business (or part of it) moves to a new owner and keeps the same type of work and customers.  
   • If you are buying only assets (for example equipment) and not taking over the ongoing work, TUPE may not apply.

2. Ask the seller for “employee liability information”  
   • By law the current owner must give you written details of every employee who will transfer (names, ages, employment contracts, any pending grievances, etc.).  
   • They have to send this at least 28 days before completion. Review it carefully so you know each person’s terms and any costs such as holiday owed or pension contributions.

3. Plan how you will inform and consult staff  
   • The current owner must consult affected employees (normally through reps or a union) but in practice you will often meet them too.  
   • Explain who you are, when the transfer happens, and confirm that their existing terms and service dates will continue.

4. Respect existing contracts after the hand-over  
   • You cannot change pay, hours or other key terms just because of the transfer.  
   • Changes are only allowed if you have a genuine economic, technical or organisational reason (for example a necessary re-structure) and the employee agrees.

5. Recognise accrued rights and length of service  
   • Continuous service carries over, so redundancy entitlement or unfair-dismissal protection dates stay the same.  
   • Staff keep any outstanding holiday and any collective agreements in force on the transfer date.

6. Factor in inherited liabilities  
   • You take on responsibility for any existing disciplinary cases, tribunal claims or unpaid wages once the transfer is complete.  
   • Make sure your purchase price and contracts reflect those liabilities.

If you are unsure whether TUPE will apply or how to handle the consultation, take legal advice before signing the purchase agreement.

More detail: https://www.gov.uk/transfers-takeovers

prompt: I'm a nurse looking to hire a part-time caregiver for elderly patients. How do I ensure that the caregiver is being paid correctly according to the National Minimum Wage for different types of work?
generated_response:
To pay a part-time caregiver the correct National Minimum Wage (NMW) you need to work out their “average hourly rate” for each pay period and check it against the legal rate for their age. Follow these steps.

1. Find the current NMW rate that applies  
   • 21 and over: National Living Wage (NLW)  
   • 18–20, 16–17, apprentice: lower NMW bands  
   Current figures are on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/national-minimum-wage-rates  

2. Identify the type of work  
   a. Time work – paid by the hour (most care shifts).  
   b. Salaried hours – same basic hours every year with equal monthly pay.  
   c. Output work – paid per task or visit.  
   d. Unmeasured work – hours vary (e.g. “as required” standby).  
   You must check the average hourly rate differently for each type.

3. Work out the pay reference period  
   This is usually the period you pay them (weekly or monthly) and can’t exceed 31 days.

4. Record the hours that count  
   • For time work: all hours they are required to be working, including travel between clients.  
   • For unmeasured work: agree a written “realistic average” of hours up-front.  
   • Sleep-in shifts: only the time the worker is awake for duties counts toward NMW unless a different agreement is in place.

5. Calculate gross pay for the period  
   Include basic pay, piece rates, and commission.  
   Exclude: tips that customers pay directly, most allowances, and expenses reimbursed.  
   If you provide accommodation, you can only offset up to the accommodation offset rate—anything above that reduces their effective pay.

6. Check the average hourly rate  
   Average hourly rate = total NMW-eligible pay ÷ total NMW-eligible hours in the pay period.  
   Compare this with the correct NMW/NLW rate. If it falls short, top up their pay.

7. Keep records for 6 years  
   HMRC can ask for evidence; accurate timesheets and pay slips protect both you and the caregiver.

8. Use the government calculator  
   GOV.UK provides an online “National Minimum Wage calculator” that will walk you through the sums for each work type.

9. If in doubt, get advice  
   Contact Acas on 0300 123 1100 or visit https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people for detailed guidance.

prompt: I'm a teacher in Scotland and I need to know what steps I should take to prepare for Making Tax Digital for Income Tax as a sole trader.
generated_response:
Making Tax Digital (MTD) for Income Tax Self-Assessment (ITSA) will change how sole traders keep records and send information to HMRC.  

Below are the practical steps you can take now:

1. Check if and when you’ll need to join  
   • From April 2026 you must follow MTD if your total business or property income is more than £50,000 a year.  
   • From April 2027 the threshold falls to £30,000.  
   Add up all your self-employment and rental income (before expenses) to see which date applies to you.

2. Review how you keep records  
   • MTD requires digital record-keeping (for example, dates, amounts, and category of each income or expense).  
   • Spreadsheets are allowed but must link to “bridging” software that can send data to HMRC.

3. Choose compatible software early  
   • Look at MTD-compatible packages that suit small service businesses (many offer education discounts).  
   • Check that it can handle Scottish income tax rates, produce quarterly updates, and create an End-of-Period Statement (EOPS).  
   • If you prefer spreadsheets, identify bridging software that links your sheet to HMRC.

4. Sign up (optional) for the MTD pilot  
   • You can volunteer before it becomes mandatory.  
   • This lets you test your software and get used to sending quarterly updates.

5. Set up your digital processes  
   • Enter income and expense details as you go (for example after issuing tuition invoices or buying classroom materials).  
   • Keep digital copies of receipts (photo or scan) in case HMRC asks for evidence.

6. Plan for quarterly updates  
   • From your start date you’ll send four summaries a year plus:  
     – an End-of-Period Statement to finalise each business or property income source  
     – a Final Declaration (replacing the current Self Assessment return).  

7. Check your HMRC credentials  
   • Make sure you have a Government Gateway user ID with access to your Self-Assessment account.  
   • When you sign up to MTD, link the software to this account.

8. Allow time to learn the software  
   • Book short training sessions or use online tutorials.  
   • Run a “dummy” quarter so you feel confident before you go live.

9. Update your budgeting and diary  
   • Quarterly updates don’t change your payment deadlines (31 January and 31 July), but they may highlight tax due sooner.  
   • Set reminders for the new reporting dates.

Most people find that starting six to twelve months ahead gives enough time to test software and iron out issues.

Relevant GOV.UK link:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm a nurse in Wales and I'm considering selling shares in a company that I've owned for several years. What are the specific requirements I need to meet to qualify for Business Asset Disposal Relief, especially regarding the company's activities and my shareholding?
generated_response:
To qualify for Business Asset Disposal Relief (BADR) when you sell your shares, HMRC checks four main conditions.  You must meet all of them for at least the two years (24 months) up to the date the sale completes.

1. The company’s activities  
   • It must be a trading company or the holding company of a trading group.  
   • “Trading” means it carries on genuine trading activities and any non-trading activities (for example, letting surplus property or holding excess cash) are not “substantial”. HMRC treats non-trading activities as substantial if they make up roughly 20 % or more of the company’s turnover, asset base, expenses or management time.

2. Your role in the company  
   • You must have been an employee or office-holder (e.g. director or company secretary) of the company, or of another company in the same trading group, for the full 24-month qualifying period.

3. Your shareholding  
   For the same 24-month period you must have held:  
   • at least 5 % of the company’s ordinary share capital; and  
   • at least 5 % of the voting rights; and  
   • EITHER  
     – at least 5 % entitlement to profits available for distribution and to assets on a winding-up, OR  
     – at least 5 % entitlement to the proceeds if the whole company were sold.  

4. Ownership period  
   • You must have owned the shares yourself for at least 24 months up to the date of sale (husband-and-wife or civil-partner transfers count as your own period of ownership).

Additional points  
• Gains that qualify are taxed at 10 % instead of the standard Capital Gains Tax rate, up to your lifetime limit of £1 million of gains.  
• Claim the relief on your Self Assessment tax return (or make a separate claim) by 31 January following the end of the tax year after the sale.  
• Keep records that show how each of the four tests above are met—company accounts, minutes appointing you as an officer, share certificates, etc.

If you are not currently an employee/office-holder or your shareholding is under 5 %, consider whether you can change this before the two-year clock starts.  If the company has substantial investment activities, the directors may need to reduce these before the sale.

For full guidance and claiming instructions see:  
https://www.gov.uk/business-asset-disposal-relief

prompt: I'm running a small business and want to claim tax relief on some equipment I bought. How do I calculate the writing down allowances for these items?
generated_response:
Writing-down allowances (WDAs) let you deduct a percentage of the remaining cost of plant and machinery each year after you’ve claimed any Annual Investment Allowance (AIA).

1. Check if you can use the Annual Investment Allowance first  
   • Most business equipment qualifies for 100% AIA, up to a total spend of £1 million in your accounting year.  
   • If you claim AIA on the full cost of an item, there is no WDA for that item.

2. Put anything you can’t (or choose not to) cover with AIA into the right “pool”  
   • Main pool – most plant and machinery: 18% WDA.  
   • Special rate pool – integral features (e.g. heating systems), long-life assets, certain cars, etc.: 6% WDA.  
   • Single-asset pool – items with any non-business use, or that you elect to keep separate.

3. Work out the tax written-down value (TWDV) at the start of the period  
   Opening TWDV (what was left unclaimed last year)  
   + Cost of new assets added to the pool this year  
   – Sale or disposal proceeds of any assets in the pool this year  
   = Balance to carry forward.

4. Apply the WDA rate to that balance  
   • Main pool: Balance × 18%  
   • Special rate pool: Balance × 6%  
   • Single-asset pool: Balance × 18% (or 6% if it would normally be special-rate).

5. Enter the WDA figure on your tax return  
   • For a sole trader or partnership: Self Assessment SA100/SA103.  
   • For a company: CT600 (‘capital allowances’ section).  
   Deduct the allowance from your trading profits to reduce the taxable profit.

6. Carry forward the remaining balance  
   Balance – WDA = new TWDV for next year’s calculation.

Extra points to watch  
• Cars: rate depends on CO₂ emissions (electric or ≤50 g/km 100% FYA, ≤130 g/km 18%, above 130 g/km 6%).  
• Small Pools Allowance: if the main pool’s balance after additions and disposals is £1,000 or less, you can claim the whole amount instead of 18%.  
• Keep invoices, dates of purchase, how you worked out any business/private split, and your pool calculations for at least 6 years.

For the detailed rules and current rates see:  
https://www.gov.uk/capital-allowances  
https://www.gov.uk/capital-allowances/annual-investment-allowance

prompt: What are the consequences if I fail to register with CIS before taking on a subcontractor?
generated_response:
If you act as a contractor under the Construction Industry Scheme (CIS) but start paying a subcontractor before you have registered with HMRC, the following can happen:  

1. You break the CIS rules – HMRC can open a compliance check to recover any tax that should have been deducted and charge interest on the late amount.  
2. Monthly CIS return penalties – each return that is late because you were not registered is treated as late:  
   • £100 as soon as it is one day late,  
   • £200 after 2 months,  
   • £300 (or 5 % of the deductions, whichever is higher) after 6 months,  
   • another £300 (or 5 %) after 12 months.  
   (Maximum £3,000 per return if it remains outstanding.)  
3. Penalties for incorrect returns – up to £3,000 for each return that omits or gives wrong information about a subcontractor.  
4. Late payment penalties – if the deductions you should have made are paid late, HMRC can add 1 % to 5 % of the outstanding amount, plus daily interest.  
5. You may be held liable for the full amount of CIS deductions that should have been taken from your subcontractor, even if you did not actually deduct them.  
6. Reputational risk – HMRC can withdraw your own gross-payment status (if you ever apply for it) and may refuse future authorisations.  

What to do now  
1. Register as a CIS contractor online or by calling the HMRC CIS helpline as soon as possible.  
2. Work out the payments already made to subcontractors and the tax that should have been deducted.  
3. File the outstanding monthly CIS returns and pay any deductions (plus interest) straight away.  
4. Keep records of all payments and deductions going forward and submit returns by the 19th of every month.  

Further guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm a nurse and I've been asked to help manage a trust for a patient. What steps should I take to ensure I understand the tax implications and responsibilities of being a trustee?
generated_response:
Below is a simple checklist to help you get up to speed with the tax duties that come with being a trustee.

1. Read the trust deed  
   • Ask the solicitor or existing trustee for the latest deed and any “letter of wishes”.  
   • Check the type of trust (for example bare, interest-in-possession, discretionary). Tax rules differ by type.

2. Decide if the trust must be registered  
   • Most UK trusts now have to be entered on HMRC’s Trust Registration Service (TRS), even if they do not yet owe tax.  
   • You will need the trust’s details, the settlor’s and beneficiaries’ information and proof of identity.  
   • Guidance: gov.uk/guidance/register-a-trust-as-a-trustee

3. Get a Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) if tax is due  
   • HMRC will issue a UTR once the trust is registered or when you tell them the trust needs to file a return.

4. Open a separate trust bank account  
   • Keep the trust’s money completely separate from your own and the patient’s personal accounts.  
   • This makes record-keeping and tax reporting much easier.

5. Keep full records during the year  
   • Income received (interest, dividends, rent).  
   • Capital gains or losses on any assets sold.  
   • Any payments to or for beneficiaries.  
   • Expenses paid from trust funds.

6. File the annual Trust and Estate Tax Return (form SA900) if HMRC asks for one  
   • Return due by 31 October (paper) or 31 January (online) after the end of the tax year.  
   • Give each beneficiary a form R185 showing any income passed to them and the tax already paid.

7. Understand the main taxes that could apply  
   • Income Tax – rates depend on the trust type.  
   • Capital Gains Tax – normally 20 % (28 % on residential property) after an annual allowance that is lower than for individuals.  
   • Inheritance Tax – discretionary and some other trusts face 10-year (periodic) and exit charges.  
   • Stamp Duty Land Tax or LBTT (in Scotland) on property the trust buys.

8. Pay any tax by the deadlines  
   • Income Tax and CGT: 31 January after the tax year.  
   • Inheritance Tax: within 6 months of the event (for example, the 10-year anniversary).

9. Report major changes promptly  
   • New or lost trustees or beneficiaries.  
   • Changes to the trust’s address or residence status.  
   • Winding up the trust or selling major assets.

10. Seek professional help when needed  
   • A solicitor or specialist accountant can explain complex points and submit the tax return for you.  
   • Their fees can usually be paid from the trust fund.

Key HMRC resources  
• Trusts and taxes: gov.uk/trusts-taxes  
• Register a trust: gov.uk/guidance/register-a-trust-as-a-trustee  
• Self-Assessment Trust and Estate Tax Return (SA900): gov.uk/government/collections/self-assessment-forms-for-trustees  

If anything is unclear, contact HMRC’s trusts helpline on 0300 123 1072 or speak to a qualified adviser before signing any documents.

prompt: I need to pay Machine Games Duty, but I'm not sure how to calculate the amount I owe. What steps should I take to ensure I file the correct return and make the payment on time?
generated_response:
Below is the quickest way to work out your Machine Games Duty (MGD), file the return and pay on time.

1. Check you’re registered  
   • You must be registered for MGD before you can file a return. If you have not already done so, register online through Government Gateway (it takes about 15 minutes and you must register at least 14 days before your machines are made available for play).  

2. Gather the figures for the accounting period  
   • An MGD accounting period is normally three months (ending 31 March, 30 June, 30 September and 31 December).  
   • For each machine, note:  
     – Total stakes/coins inserted  
     – Total cash prizes paid out  
     – Refunds, cancelled plays and VAT (these should be excluded).  
   • Net takings = stakes received minus cash prizes paid out.

3. Identify the duty rate for each machine  
   • Lower rate (5%) – maximum stake 10p, maximum cash prize £8.  
   • Standard rate (20%) – chargeable proportion up to and including 20%.  
   • Higher rate (25%) – chargeable proportion over 20%.  
   (Most fruit-machines and quiz machines fall into the standard rate; higher-jackpot machines usually fall into the higher rate.)

4. Calculate the duty  
   • For each machine: Net takings × applicable rate.  
   • Add the duty from all machines to get the amount due for the period.

5. Complete the MGD return  
   • Log in to your MGD account on GOV.UK.  
   • Enter total net takings and duty due (the online form does the final calculation check for you).  
   • Submit the return no later than 30 days after the end of the period (for example, period ending 30 June – return due 30 July).

6. Pay the duty by the same deadline  
   • Faster Payments, CHAPS or debit card – usually same-day/next working day.  
   • Bacs – allow 3 working days.  
   • Direct Debit – set up at least 10 working days before the first collection; HMRC collects on the due date.  
   • Quote your 15-digit MGD reference with every payment.

7. Keep records  
   • Keep all MGD calculations, machine takings and payout records for at least 4 years in case HMRC asks to see them.

If anything is unclear, or your machines don’t fit the usual stake/prize limits, phone the HMRC MGD helpline on 0300 200 3700 before you submit.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance:  
Machine Games Duty – rates, registration, returns and payment: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/machine-games-duty

prompt: I heard about the 'Breathing Space' scheme. Can I still proceed with a bankruptcy petition if the debtor is using this scheme?
generated_response:
No. Once you receive notification that a debtor has entered a Breathing Space (Debt Respite) moratorium you must not present a new bankruptcy petition against them.  

For the whole of the moratorium:  
1. Pause any enforcement action, including starting bankruptcy proceedings.  
2. Stop adding interest, fees or charges to the qualifying debt.  
3. If you had already presented a petition before the Breathing Space started, the court will adjourn the hearing until the moratorium ends. You must not take any further steps unless the court gives specific permission.  
4. Keep an eye on the end-date in the Insolvency Service’s Breathing Space register or the notification you were sent.  
   • Standard Breathing Space lasts 60 days.  
   • A Mental Health Crisis Breathing Space lasts for the period of the crisis treatment plus 30 days, so it can be much longer.  
5. As soon as the moratorium ends you are free to reassess the situation and, if you still wish, file a bankruptcy petition in the usual way.

Trying to continue or start bankruptcy during Breathing Space could lead the court to dismiss your petition and you may be ordered to pay costs, so it is safest to wait.

prompt: I'm a teacher in Scotland and I need to apply for a National Insurance number. What do I need to do and how long will it take?
generated_response:
To get a National Insurance (NI) number you need to:  

1. Check you don’t already have one  
   • Look at any old payslip, P45, P60 or a personal tax account.  
   • If you have an NI number, you don’t need to re-apply.  

2. Phone the National Insurance number application line  
   • Telephone: 0800 141 2075 (Textphone: 0800 141 2438).  
   • Lines are open Monday–Friday, 8 am–6 pm.  
   • Say you need to apply for an NI number and that you live and work in Scotland.  

3. Complete the application pack  
   • The Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) will post you a form (and sometimes offer an online form instead).  
   • Fill it in and send the requested identity documents (passport, biometric residence permit, etc.).  

4. Attend an Evidence of Identity interview – only if DWP ask you to  
   • Most people now send documents by post, but you may be asked to go to a local Jobcentre Plus.  
   • Take the original ID documents you listed on the form.  

5. Get your NI number confirmation letter  
   • After your documents are accepted, your NI number is created and a confirmation letter is posted to you.  
   • You can give the number to your employer and keep the letter for your records.  

How long it takes  
• From the day you first phone, allow about 4–6 weeks to receive your NI number.  
• You can start your teaching job while you wait – give your employer your temporary details and tell them you’ve applied for an NI number.  

Relevant GOV.UK page  
Apply for a National Insurance number: https://www.gov.uk/apply-national-insurance-number

prompt: I work as a nurse and need to understand if I have to register with HMRC for anti-money laundering regulations. What are the requirements and steps to register?
generated_response:
You do not need to register with HMRC for anti-money-laundering supervision if you are working solely as a nurse (whether employed by the NHS, an agency, or self-employed). HMRC’s money-laundering regulations apply to businesses that carry on certain high-risk financial or professional activities – for example:  

1. Accountancy service providers  
2. Tax advisers  
3. Estate and letting agents  
4. High-value dealers, art market participants, casinos, money-service businesses, trust or company service providers, etc.  

Healthcare professionals, including nurses, are not on that list. Unless you also run one of the activities above as a separate business, you have no obligation to register, pay the annual supervision fee, or carry out the related customer-due-diligence checks.

If you ever expand into a line of work that does fall under the regulations (for example, setting up a bookkeeping side business), you would then need to:

1. Check whether a professional body already supervises you – if not, HMRC will be the default supervisor.  
2. Register online through your Government Gateway account before you start trading in that activity.  
3. Pay the registration and annual renewal fees.  
4. Put the required anti-money-laundering policies and staff training in place.  

For full details see “Anti-money-laundering supervision: register or renew” on GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/guidance/anti-money-laundering-supervision-register-or-renew

prompt: I'm the executor of my uncle's will and need to submit Inheritance Tax. What form do I use to report the value of his property and land?
generated_response:
To report the value of your uncle’s house, land or any other interests in land you normally use schedule IHT405 (Houses, land, buildings and interests in land).  

1. Check first whether the estate is an “excepted estate” in Scotland.  
   • If it is excepted, you do not send form IHT400 or IHT405 to HMRC – instead you complete form C1 (Confirmation) and form C5(SE) for the Sheriff Court.  
   • If the estate is not excepted, you must send HMRC the main Inheritance Tax account (form IHT400) together with schedule IHT405 for the property and land details.  

2. Download the forms and notes:  
   • Form IHT400 and all schedules, including IHT405: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/inheritance-tax-forms  
   • Form C1 and C5(SE) (for excepted estates in Scotland): https://www.gov.uk/apply-confirmation-scotland  

3. Fill in schedule IHT405 with:  
   • The full address of each property or piece of land.  
   • Your best estimate of the open-market value at the date of death (get an independent valuation if possible).  
   • Details of any joint ownership, mortgages or tenancies.  

4. Send the completed IHT400 with schedule IHT405 (and any other relevant schedules) to HMRC, pay any tax due, then submit form C1 to the Sheriff Court to obtain Confirmation (Scottish probate).  

If you are unsure whether the estate is excepted, use HMRC’s guidance on “excepted estates” or call the Inheritance Tax and probate helpline (0300 123 1072) before sending the forms.

prompt: I'm a nurse and I've received a Simple Assessment letter. How do I pay the tax I owe and what happens if I don't pay on time?
generated_response:
Answer:
You pay a Simple Assessment bill directly to HMRC – you do not file a Self Assessment tax return.

1. Find the details on your Simple Assessment letter  
   • Amount due (shown as “Tax you must pay”).  
   • Your payment reference (usually 14 or 15 characters, for example ‘SA123456789A’).  
   • The payment deadline – often 31 January but follow the exact date on the letter.

2. Choose a payment method  
   a) Online or telephone banking (Faster Payments, CHAPS or Bacs)  
      • Use HMRC’s bank account:  
        Account name: HMRC Cumbernauld  
        Sort code: 08-32-10  
        Account number: 12001039  
      • Quote your payment reference.  
      • Same-day with Faster Payments or CHAPS; three working days with Bacs.  

   b) Debit or corporate credit card online  
      • Go to GOV.UK → ‘Pay your Self Assessment tax bill’.  
      • Enter the amount and your payment reference.  
      • Personal credit cards are not accepted.  

   c) Cheque by post  
      • Make it payable to ‘HM Revenue and Customs only’ followed by your payment reference.  
      • Include the payslip from the letter.  
      • Allow at least 3 working days before the deadline.

3. Check HMRC has received the payment  
   • Your online or telephone banking receipt is proof.  
   • HMRC will credit the payment to your tax record using the reference you gave.

If you miss the deadline  
• Interest: charged daily from the day after the due date.  
• Late-payment penalties:  
  – 5 % of the unpaid tax after 30 days.  
  – Another 5 % after 6 months.  
  – Another 5 % after 12 months.  
• HMRC can take further action (debt collection agency, direct recovery from wages or bank, or court proceedings).

If you cannot pay in full by the deadline, contact HMRC as soon as possible (0300 200 3822) to discuss a Time to Pay arrangement. This can stop further penalties if agreed before the due date.

Most relevant link  
GOV.UK – Tax: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  

Justification:
• Assumes the Simple Assessment letter contains payment reference, amount and due date.  
• Gives step-by-step instructions suited to low digital literacy.  
• Covers all common payment options and the consequences of late payment per HMRC rules.  
• Advises contacting HMRC early if the user can’t pay, the safest next step.  
• Provides the overarching GOV.UK tax link for further detail.

prompt: I recently moved house and need to update my address with HMRC. How do I do this, and what do I need to prepare?
generated_response:
You can update your address with HMRC online in a few minutes.  
1. Sign in to your Personal Tax Account at www.gov.uk/personal-tax-account with your Government Gateway user ID and password. If you do not yet have an account, you can create one first (you will need your National Insurance number and a UK passport or recent payslip/P60 to prove your identity).  
2. From the home screen choose “Tell us about a change” ➔ “Change of address”.  
3. Enter:  
   • your National Insurance number (will pre-fill if you are signed in)  
   • the date you moved  
   • your new full address and postcode  
4. Check the summary and submit. HMRC will automatically update:  
   • Income Tax records (including any Self Assessment account)  
   • National Insurance, State Pension and pension credit records  
   • Child Benefit and Tax Credits (if you receive them)  
5. You will get on-screen confirmation. Keep a note or print the receipt for your records.

If you cannot use the online service, phone HMRC’s general enquiries line on 0300 200 3300 (open Mon–Fri) with the same details ready, or write to the address on your most recent HMRC letter.

Prepare before you start  
• Your Government Gateway login (or the information needed to create one)  
• National Insurance number  
• Old and new addresses and the exact date you moved  
• Your Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) if you complete Self Assessment (helps but is not essential)

Other bodies to tell  
Updating your Personal Tax Account does not change your address for:  
• Your employer or pension provider (tell them separately so PAYE records match)  
• DVLA, DWP or your local council (council tax, electoral roll) – each organisation has its own process.

Most people find the online method is processed within 24 hours, but allow up to 3 weeks for all linked systems to show the new address.

Relevant GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/tell-hmrc-change-of-details

prompt: I'm running a limited company in Wales and need to file my accounts and tax return. What steps should I follow to ensure everything is done correctly and on time?
generated_response:
Below is the standard timetable and checklist for a small private limited company based in Wales.  
(HMRC and Companies House rules are UK-wide, so the Welsh location makes no difference to the process.)

1. Confirm your accounting period  
   • Your financial (accounting) year usually ends on the last day of the month you incorporated.  
   • Check the “next accounts made up to” date on the Companies House register.

2. Keep your bookkeeping up to date  
   • Keep invoices, receipts, bank statements and payroll records for at least 6 years.  
   • Make sure they reconcile to your business bank account.

3. Prepare the annual accounts  
   • Profit-and-loss statement, balance sheet, notes and (if not a micro-entity) a directors’ report.  
   • Most small Welsh companies can file “micro-entity” or “abridged” accounts to reduce detail.  
   • Double-check that the balance sheet is signed by a director.

4. File the accounts with Companies House  
   • File online via WebFiling or accounting software using your company authentication code.  
   • Deadline: 9 months after the accounting year-end (21 months after incorporation for your first set).  
   • Late filing penalties start at £150 and rise quickly, so allow a safety margin.

5. Work out the Corporation Tax bill  
   • Adjust your accounting profit for tax rules (capital allowances, disallowed expenses, etc.).  
   • Small companies usually pay 19% or 25% depending on profits; check the current rate bands.

6. Pay Corporation Tax  
   • Payment is due 9 months and 1 day after the accounting period ends.  
   • Pay electronically using the 17-character Corporation Tax reference in your HMRC online account.

7. Submit the Company Tax Return (CT600) to HMRC  
   • Includes the CT600 form, full iXBRL-tagged accounts and detailed tax computations.  
   • Must be filed online through HMRC software or commercial software.  
   • Deadline: 12 months after the accounting period ends (penalties from £100 upwards if late).

8. File the annual Confirmation Statement (CS01)  
   • Confirms shareholdings, officers and PSCs.  
   • Due every 12 months; most companies submit this at the same time each year.  
   • Fee: £13 online.

9. Optional but sensible  
   • Appoint a qualified accountant if you are unsure about tax adjustments or iXBRL tagging.  
   • Set calendar reminders a month before each deadline.  
   • Use Companies House and HMRC email alerts for extra prompts.  
   • If you prefer, you can complete filings through the medium of Welsh by contacting Companies House and HMRC.

10. If something may be late  
   • Contact Companies House or HMRC before the deadline and explain; extensions are rare but possible in exceptional circumstances.  
   • Otherwise, file as soon as possible to limit penalties and interest.

Key GOV.UK pages  
• File annual accounts: https://www.gov.uk/file-annual-accounts  
• Company Tax Returns: https://www.gov.uk/company-tax-returns  
• Corporation Tax: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax/corporation-tax

prompt: I'm a nurse and I want to know how to estimate my Income Tax for the current year. I earn a salary and have a pension contribution. How do I use the service to calculate my tax and National Insurance payments?
generated_response:
Here’s the simplest way to work out your Income Tax and National Insurance (NI) for this tax year:

1. Collect the figures you’ll need  
   • Your gross pay (the amount before any deductions).  
   • How often you’re paid (weekly, monthly, 4-weekly).  
   • The percentage or £ amount you pay into your NHS pension or any other workplace pension.  
   • Your current tax code (it’s on your payslip).  

2. Use HMRC’s online PAYE tax calculator  
   a. Go to GOV.UK and search for “Check how much Income Tax you’ll pay” or start from the Tax section: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  
   b. Choose the ‘Estimate your Income Tax for the current year’ service.  
   c. Follow the on-screen questions:  
      • Enter your pay frequency and the amount of each payment.  
      • When asked about deductions, tick that you pay into a pension and type in the percentage or amount.
