      • Confirm your tax code if the service shows one, or overwrite it if your payslip shows a different code.  

3. Review your results  
   • The service shows your estimated Income Tax and NI for each pay period and for the whole tax year.  
   • It also shows your take-home pay after these deductions and your pension contribution.  

4. If anything looks wrong  
   • Check your figures, especially the pension percentage and pay frequency.  
   • If your tax code is wrong, contact HMRC on 0300 200 3300 or update it through your Personal Tax Account.  

Tip: You can save or print the final page so you can compare it with future payslips.

prompt: I inherited a pension pot from my late partner and want to know if I need to pay tax on the money I receive. What steps should I take to understand my tax obligations and how to report them to HMRC?
generated_response:
What you pay depends on three things:
1. The type of payment you are getting.  
   • Regular survivor’s pension from a defined-benefit scheme – the income is treated like wages and is taxed under PAYE.  
   • Lump-sum death benefit or inherited drawdown/annuity from a defined-contribution pot – the tax position depends on your late partner’s age when they died and when the provider pays you.

2. The age of your partner when they died.  
   • Died before age 75 and the pension is paid out within 2 years – normally tax-free.  
   • Died at 75 or older, or the provider pays out after 2 years – the money is taxable at your normal Income Tax rate.

3. How the provider pays you.  
   • Lump sums are often paid with emergency tax deducted.  
   • Regular payments are put on PAYE, with tax taken off each month.

Steps to work out and report any tax:

1. Contact the pension provider  
   • Ask whether the payment is a lump sum, survivor’s pension or flexi-access drawdown.  
   • Ask for written confirmation of:  
     – your late partner’s age at death,  
     – the date the provider first knew of the death,  
     – the gross amount being paid and any tax already deducted,  
     – the P45 or payslip for the payment, if available.

2. Check whether tax should have been taken  
   • Use the information above with the rules shown in points 1-3 to see if the payment ought to be tax-free or taxable.  
   • If it should be tax-free but tax was deducted, you can reclaim it (see step 5).

3. Tell HMRC if you already fill in a Self Assessment tax return  
   • Enter the gross amount of any taxable payments in the ‘UK pension income’ box.  
   • Include any tax already deducted so HMRC can work out any balance due or refund.

4. If you do not normally complete Self Assessment  
   • HMRC will usually adjust your tax code once they receive the P45 details from the pension payer.  
   • If you think the code is wrong, phone HMRC on 0300 200 3300 (have your National Insurance number and the provider’s letter to hand).

5. Reclaim overpaid tax on a lump-sum death benefit (if tax was taken but the payment should be tax-free)  
   • Download form R40 and send it to HMRC; or  
   • Claim online through your Personal Tax Account.

6. Keep all paperwork for at least 22 months after the end of the tax year, in case HMRC asks for evidence.

Useful GOV.UK guidance  
• Paying tax on your private pension: https://www.gov.uk/tax-on-pension  
• Self Assessment: https://www.gov.uk/self-assessment-tax-returns  
• Claim a tax refund form R40: https://www.gov.uk/claim-tax-refund

If at any point you are unsure, call HMRC or consider speaking to a regulated financial adviser.

prompt: What should I do if I'm facing a court claim for debts and I'm not sure whether to go bankrupt or seek other options?
generated_response:
If you receive a claim form from the county court (often called a ‘CCJ claim’) you need to act quickly – even if you think bankruptcy might be the end result.  

1. Check the claim form date. You normally have 14 days from the date it was served to send back the Acknowledgement of Service or a full reply (the N9A/N9B forms).  
2. Gather the facts:  
   • the exact amount owed,  
   • whether you agree you owe it, and  
   • any evidence (statements, correspondence) that supports your position.  
3. Get free, independent debt advice before you decide on bankruptcy or another solution. Contact one of these services immediately:  
   • National Debtline – 0808 808 4000 (England).  
   • StepChange Debt Charity – 0800 138 1111 or stepchange.org.  
   • Citizens Advice – citizensadvice.org.uk or your local office.  
4. While you wait for advice, complete and return the court forms so you don’t get a default judgment. You can:  
   • admit the debt and offer affordable instalments,  
   • dispute the debt (give reasons and any evidence), or  
   • ask for 28 days extra by sending an Acknowledgement of Service.  
5. Discuss all debt-relief options with the adviser:  
   • Debt Management Plan – flexible, informal, keeps assets.  
   • Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) – formal agreement, lasts 5-6 years, avoids bankruptcy.  
   • Debt Relief Order (DRO) – for debts under £30,000, low assets/income.  
   • Bankruptcy – writes off most debts after 12 months but you may lose assets and it affects credit for 6 years.  
6. If bankruptcy is the best route, your adviser will help you apply online (£680 fee). If another solution suits, they’ll help you set that up and inform the court/creditor.  
7. Keep copies of everything you send and make sure the court deadlines are met. Missing a deadline can result in the court entering judgment against you, adding costs and enforcement action.

Key GOV.UK pages:  
• Bankruptcy – how to apply, what happens: https://www.gov.uk/bankruptcy  
• Individual Voluntary Arrangements: https://www.gov.uk/individual-voluntary-arrangements  
• Replying to a county court claim: https://www.gov.uk/respond-to-court-claim-for-money

prompt: I'm a retired person who has been paying the reduced rate of National Insurance. How do I stop paying the reduced rate and start paying the standard rate?
generated_response:
Answer:
Only a few very specific groups can pay a “reduced rate” of National Insurance.  
The two most common are:  
• the Married Woman’s or Widow’s reduced-rate election (only ever available to women who opted in before 11 May 1977), and  
• self-employed people who claimed the old Small Earnings Exception (now replaced by the Small Profits Threshold).

If you are a man aged 65 + it is unlikely you are legally paying a reduced rate at all, because:

1. Once you reach State Pension age you normally stop paying National Insurance on any earnings.  
2. The Married Woman’s/Widow’s election has never applied to men.

What to do next

1. Check your most recent payslip or the letter from HMRC that shows the type of National Insurance you are paying (Class 1 letter, Class 2, Class 3, etc.).  
2. If you think the wrong rate is being taken, phone HMRC’s National Insurance helpline on 0300 200 3500 (textphone 0300 200 3519). Have your National Insurance number to hand.  
3. Explain that you believe you are paying a reduced rate and want to confirm whether you should be paying National Insurance at all, and—if so—at the standard rate.  
4. HMRC will:  
   a. confirm whether you are within a group entitled to reduced-rate NI,  
   b. tell you if any form (for example CF9) is needed to cancel the reduced-rate election, or  
   c. correct your record and instruct your employer’s payroll (or tell you how to pay the correct voluntary Class 3 rate) if an error has been made.  
5. Keep any written confirmation from HMRC. If you are still working, give a copy to your employer’s payroll so they deduct the correct rate in future.

Relevant GOV.UK link  
National Insurance: general guidance – https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

Justification:
• A male pensioner is normally exempt from NI, so paying any rate—reduced or standard—may be an error.  
• Only HMRC can confirm the contributor’s category and, if necessary, issue or cancel a reduced-rate election.  
• The steps focus on checking records first, then contacting HMRC, which is the safest way to correct NI status.  
• GOV.UK helpline and forms are the official route; linking to the general NI page keeps advice up to date.

Missing info: Are you still receiving employment or self-employment earnings, or are you making voluntary contributions only?

prompt: I recently moved house and need to update my address with HMRC. How do I do this and what do I need to prepare beforehand?
generated_response:
You can tell HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) about your new address online in a few minutes.

1. Gather what you’ll need  
   • Your Government Gateway user ID and password (or set one up during the process).  
   • Your National Insurance number.  
   • Your new full address and the date you moved.  
   • If you complete Self Assessment, have your Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) to hand.

2. Sign in and update your address  
   1. Go to GOV.UK and search “tell HMRC change of address”, or start from: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  
   2. Choose the “Tell HMRC about a change of address” service.  
   3. Sign in with your Government Gateway details.  
   4. Follow the on-screen prompts to enter the new address and the date you moved.  
   5. Submit the change and keep the confirmation screen or email for your records.

3. What happens next  
   • HMRC will update your Income Tax, National Insurance and (if you file) Self Assessment records automatically.  
   • If you receive tax credits or Child Benefit you may be asked to confirm the new address separately within the same service.  
   • Allow a few days for the change to show on your personal tax account.

4. Let others know  
   • If you are employed, HMRC will inform your employer’s payroll system once the update is processed, but it’s still wise to tell your employer directly.  
   • Update your address with any workplace pension provider and other government bodies (e.g. DVLA, DWP) as needed.

prompt: I'm running a limited company and received a notice to deliver a Company Tax Return. What should I include in my tax return and when do I need to submit it?
generated_response:
What to include in your Company Tax Return  
1. Form CT600 – this is the main return and must be completed in full.  
2. Statutory (annual) company accounts for the same accounting period, tagged in iXBRL format.  
3. A detailed Corporation Tax computation showing how you moved from your accounting profit to the taxable profit.  
4. Any supplementary pages that apply to your company (for example, loans to participators, R&D relief, capital allowances).  
5. Supporting schedules, calculations or elections you want HMRC to consider (also in iXBRL or PDF if HMRC agrees).  

When and how to submit  
1. Check the accounting period shown on HMRC’s “notice to deliver”.  
2. File the return online using:  
   • HMRC’s free service (if your accounts are straightforward), or  
   • Commercial software (needed by most companies).  
3. Deadline: 12 months after the end of the accounting period.  
4. Pay any Corporation Tax due 9 months + 1 day after the period ends (you pay before you file).  
5. Keep records for at least 6 years in case HMRC asks to see them.  

Penalties if you miss the filing deadline  
• £100 immediate penalty (or £500 if late 3 years in a row).  
• Further penalties after 3, 6 and 12 months, plus interest on late tax.  

Useful GOV.UK starting point  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  

If you are unsure about tagging your accounts or choosing software, consider speaking to a qualified accountant or checking the detailed guidance on GOV.UK.

prompt: My relative has been placed in care and needs help with their tax credits. What steps should I take to manage their claim effectively?
generated_response:
If your relative is no longer able to manage their own affairs, you can look after their tax-credit claim by following these steps:

1. Get legal authority to act for them  
   • If they already made a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA) for property and financial affairs, register it with HMRC.  
   • If there is no LPA and they cannot now make one, apply to become their “appointee” for tax credits through the Tax Credit Office. Phone 0345 300 3900 and ask for form TC689 (“Authority to act”). HMRC will arrange a short interview before confirming the appointeeship.

2. Collect the details you will need  
   • Their National Insurance number, tax-credit award notice, recent payslips or benefit letters, details of any childcare payments, and the date they moved into care.

3. Tell HMRC about every relevant change within 1 month  
   • Date they entered the care home or hospital.  
   • Any change to their income, working hours, childcare costs or address.  
   • If they will be in permanent residential care, ask whether the claim should stop and whether they need to move to Universal Credit instead.

4. Decide where future payments should go  
   • You can ask HMRC to pay future tax credits into your relative’s existing account or a new account you manage for their benefit.  
   • Keep a clear record of every payment and how the money is spent in case HMRC asks for evidence.

5. Review the annual renewal pack  
   • Each April HMRC sends a renewal pack. Complete it on their behalf by the deadline (usually 31 July) or the payments will stop.

6. Keep copies and stay in touch  
   • Keep copies of forms, letters and any evidence you send.  
   • If anything else changes, phone the Tax Credit Helpline (0345 300 3900) straight away.

Most people now have to claim Universal Credit instead of new tax-credit claims, but existing tax-credit awards continue until HMRC ends them or there is a major change. Always check before cancelling a claim.

Relevant GOV.UK pages  
• Tax credits: report changes https://www.gov.uk/child-tax-credit-change-of-circumstances  
• Tax credits helpline and forms https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax/tax-credits

prompt: I'm a student in Wales and I want to know what information I need to provide to use the Self Assessment tax calculator for estimating my tax bill. What details should I enter?
generated_response:
To get a realistic figure from HMRC’s Self Assessment tax calculator you should have the following information for the tax year you’re checking (6 April to 5 April):

1. Personal details  
   • National Insurance number  
   • Date of birth and postcode (to apply the right personal allowance and Welsh rates of Income Tax)  
   • Whether you were resident in the UK for the whole tax year  

2. Income you received in the tax year  
   a. Employment  
      – Total pay and tax already deducted (P60 or last P45)  
   b. Self-employment or casual work  
      – Turnover and allowable expenses to give your profit or loss  
   c. Savings and investments  
      – Interest from bank or building-society accounts (gross and tax deducted)  
      – Dividends from UK or overseas shares  
   d. Property (if any)  
      – Rental income and related expenses  
   e. Other taxable income  
      – Scholarships or bursaries that are taxable  
      – Taxable state benefits (e.g. Jobseeker’s Allowance)  
      – Capital gains if you sold shares or other assets  

   Note: Maintenance loans and most student grants are not taxable, so you do not enter them.

3. Tax already paid or deducted at source  
   • PAYE tax from P60/P45  
   • Tax deducted by banks on interest (if any)  
   • Any payments on account you have already made for Self Assessment  

4. Deductions, reliefs and allowances you want to claim  
   • Gift Aid donations  
   • Personal pension contributions you paid directly to a provider  
   • Student loan repayments already taken through PAYE (Plan 1, Plan 2 or Post-graduate loan)  
   • Professional subscriptions or course-related expenses you have paid yourself  
   • Marriage Allowance transfer or Blind Person’s Allowance if these apply  

5. Other information  
   • Class 2 National Insurance (if you were self-employed)  
   • Any losses you want to carry forward or sideways  
   • Whether you want to claim Child Benefit High-Income Charge or repay coronavirus support grants  

Enter each figure in the calculator exactly as it appears on your paperwork; the tool will do the rest and show how much tax you still owe or are due back.

Most students only need their P60 (or payslips), any bank-interest statement and details of pension or Gift Aid payments, but it is worth checking every heading above to be sure nothing is missed.

You can start from GOV.​UK > Money and tax > Self Assessment:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I need to submit my Self Assessment tax return, but I'm not sure if I should do it online or print it out. What's the best way to go about it?
generated_response:
For most people the quickest, easiest and safest way to file a Self Assessment return is online through GOV.​UK.

Advantages of filing online  
• You get an extra three months to file – deadline is 31 January (paper returns must reach HMRC by 31 October).  
• HMRC work out your tax as you go, so you can see what you owe before you submit.  
• Any refund is processed faster.  
• You receive immediate confirmation that HMRC has your return.  
• You can amend the return online up to 12 months after the 31 January deadline.

How to file online  
1. Check you have a Government Gateway user ID and password.  
   • If you’ve never filed online, select ‘Create sign in details’ and follow the prompts.  
   • HMRC will post you an activation code – allow up to 10 days (21 days if abroad).  
2. Sign in at gov.​uk and add the Self Assessment service to your account.  
3. Gather the information you’ll need (for example P60s, P45s, invoices, bank interest, pension contributions, Gift Aid donations).  
4. Complete each section of the online return. Use the ‘Save and continue later’ button if you need breaks.  
5. Check the calculation HMRC shows, correct any errors, then select ‘Submit’.  
6. Make any payment due by 31 January (and 31 July if you have a second payment on account).  
7. Download or print the receipt page for your records.

If printing/paper might suit you  
Use form SA100 and any relevant supplementary pages. They must reach HMRC by 31 October. Allow time for postage and keep proof of posting. Note that paper returns are processed manually, so refunds and any HMRC queries can take longer.

Help and guidance  
GOV.​UK Self Assessment: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax  
HMRC Self Assessment helpline: 0300 200 3310 (Mon–Fri)

prompt: I heard there are council garages available, but I'm not sure how to apply. Can you explain the process?
generated_response:
Council garages are managed locally, so the exact rules vary by area, but the process is broadly the same everywhere in Wales and the rest of the UK.

1. Find your local council  
   • Go to GOV .UK and enter your postcode at “Find your local council”.  
   • This takes you to your council’s website or its contact details.

2. Check garage availability  
   • Search the council site for “garages” or “garage waiting list”, or phone the housing/estates team.  
   • Councils usually keep a waiting list and publish the weekly rent.

3. Apply to join the waiting list  
   • Complete the online form or request a paper form.  
   • You’ll normally need:  
     – Your name, address and contact details  
     – Proof of ID and address  
     – Vehicle details (if you have one)  
     – Any disability-related parking needs

4. Wait for an offer  
   • The council will write or email when a garage becomes free.  
   • You may be offered more than one location—respond by the stated deadline or you’ll stay on the list.

5. Sign the licence and pay the rent  
   • Read the licence agreement (different from a tenancy).  
   • Pay the first month’s rent and any deposit or key fee.  
   • Keep up the weekly or monthly payments—non-payment can lead to the licence being ended quickly.

6. Collect the keys and move in  
   • Arrange a time with the council to collect keys and check the condition of the garage.  
   • You’re usually responsible for minor upkeep; report any serious faults straight away.

Useful link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services (select “Garages and parking spaces” then follow the link to “Rent a garage or parking space from the council” and enter your postcode).

If you need more detail, contact your council’s housing or estates team—phone numbers are on the council website.

prompt: If I am a landlord with multiple flats rented out on assured shorthold tenancies, how many votes do I get in an RTM company and what happens if the RTM process doesn't result in the building being managed by them?
generated_response:
In a Right to Manage (RTM) company you get one vote for each flat you own on a long lease.  
• At a show-of-hands vote you still only raise one hand, but if any member asks for a poll (a count based on flats) you are credited with one vote per qualifying flat – so three flats means three votes.  

If the RTM claim is not completed (for example the company withdraws, misses a deadline or the tribunal refuses the claim):  
1. Management stays exactly where it is now – with the freeholder or the managing agent they appoint.  
2. The leaseholders who signed up to the RTM still have to pay the landlord’s ‘reasonable costs’ incurred in dealing with the claim.  
3. The same group can start a fresh RTM application, but they must wait 12 months and serve a new claim notice.  
4. If leaseholders still want change sooner, they can instead:  
   a) apply to the First-tier Tribunal for the appointment of an independent manager, or  
   b) explore collective enfranchisement (buying the freehold).  

More detail is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/right-to-manage-a-landlord

prompt: My leaseholder association is not recognized by the landlord. How can I resolve this issue through the tribunal?
generated_response:
If your landlord refuses to recognise your leaseholders’ association, you can ask the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) to issue a “certificate of recognition”. Once you have the certificate, the landlord must treat your group as a Recognised Tenants’ Association (RTA).

Follow these steps.

1. Check you meet the basic requirements  
   • At least 50% of the qualifying leaseholders in the block must be members (the tribunal may accept slightly less in small blocks).  
   • The association needs simple written rules (a constitution) that cover: name and address, aim of representing members on service-charge and management issues, how people join, voting rights, and how officers are elected.  
   • The association must be independent of the landlord and managing agent.

2. Gather evidence  
   • A membership list with flat numbers and signatures or written confirmation from each member.  
   • A copy of your constitution.  
   • Minutes of recent meetings.  
   • Copies of any letters/emails where you have already asked the landlord to recognise the association and their reply (or lack of reply).

3. Complete the tribunal application  
   • Use the form for “Leasehold – Other” applications (ask the tribunal office if you are unsure which to use).  
   • Quote section 29 of the Landlord and Tenant Act 1985 and state you are applying for a certificate of recognition.  
   • Attach the evidence listed above.  
   • Pay the £100 application fee (a further £200 hearing fee is payable only if a hearing is needed).  
   • Send the form and documents to the regional First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber – Residential Property) that covers your area, and send a copy to the landlord.

4. After you apply  
   • The tribunal may ask for extra information or arrange an informal hearing where both sides can speak.  
   • If satisfied, the tribunal issues the certificate (usually valid for 4–5 years).  
   • Give a copy to the landlord and keep it safe—renew it before it expires.

5. If the landlord still ignores the association  
   • Show them the certificate—by law they must now consult the RTA on major works and give financial summaries.  
   • If they fail to do so, you can take further action through the tribunal or county court.

Useful link  
Find the application form, fees and your local tribunal office under “Leasehold and shared ownership problems” on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: Is there any support available for people who have missed mortgage payments?
generated_response:
Help is available. Act quickly—support options close off if you fall further behind.

1. Speak to your lender  
   • Tell them you’re struggling. By UK rules they must consider options such as a temporary payment plan, switching to interest-only or extending the term.  
   • If your lender has signed the “Mortgage Charter”, you can normally ask for:  
     – Up to 6 months on interest-only, or  
     – A term extension you can reverse within 6 months, and  
     – No repossession for 12 months after your first missed payment.

2. Get free, independent debt advice  
   • Contact Citizens Advice Scotland (0800 028 1456), StepChange (0800 138 1111) or MoneyHelper (0800 138 7777).  
   • An adviser can speak to the lender for you, check benefits and outline Scottish schemes.

3. Check benefit support and government loans  
   • If you receive Universal Credit, Pension Credit or certain legacy benefits, you may qualify for a Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) loan to cover interest on up to £200,000 of your mortgage.  
   • You can ask Jobcentre Plus or the Universal Credit helpline to add SMI—there is no asset test.

4. Consider “Breathing Space”  
   • A registered debt adviser can apply for Breathing Space. For 60 days, most interest, fees and court action are frozen while you work on a plan.

5. Scottish Home Owners’ Support Fund  
   • If you are at risk of repossession, you may apply for:  
     – Mortgage to Rent: a social landlord buys your home and rents it back to you.  
     – Mortgage to Shared Equity: the Scottish Government buys a share, reducing what you owe.  
   • Apply through a debt adviser or download forms from mygov.scot.

6. Check for insurance or workplace help  
   • Mortgage payment protection insurance, critical-illness cover or a teacher union hardship fund may pay your arrears.

7. Keep records and get everything in writing  
   • Note dates, who you spoke to and what was agreed.  
   • Send follow-up emails or letters confirming any repayment plan.

Useful links  
Housing and local services (GOV.UK): https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  
MoneyHelper mortgage arrears guide (search “MoneyHelper missed mortgage payments”).  
Scottish Home Owners’ Support Fund (search “mygov.scot home owners support fund”).

prompt: Are there any other schemes similar to Rent to Buy in the UK besides England?
generated_response:
Rent to Buy is only run by Homes England, but the other UK nations have their own “rent now, buy later” offers.

1. Wales – Rent to Own – Wales  
   • You rent a newly built home from a participating housing association for 5 years.  
   • A share of the rent (and any increase in the home’s value) is set aside as a deposit.  
   • You then have the option to buy the home, using that deposit plus a normal mortgage.  
   Apply through a housing association listed on gov.wales or ask your local council’s housing team.

2. Northern Ireland – Rent to Own (Co-Ownership)  
   • You rent a new-build property from Co-Ownership for 3 years.  
   • Part of each monthly payment is saved toward a deposit.  
   • After 3 years you must buy the home outright, usually with a mortgage.  
   Start by registering with Co-Ownership (co-ownership.org).

3. Scotland – Mid-Market Rent-to-Buy pilots  
   • Some councils and housing associations offer “Rent to Buy” or “Rent Save Buy” on a pilot basis (for example, in the Highlands or by Link Housing).  
   • You rent for 5 years; part of the rent is put aside as a deposit; you then buy the property.  
   • Availability is limited and varies by area.  
   Check your local council’s housing pages or search “Rent to Buy Scotland” plus your council name.

Other affordable-ownership routes (all UK nations)  
• Shared Ownership / Co-Ownership  
• First Homes (England only)  
• Help to Buy (Scotland, Wales)  
See GOV.UK Affordable home ownership schemes: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services/owning-your-home

Steps to take next  
1. Decide where in the UK you want to live.  
2. Visit the relevant national scheme website or speak to the local council’s housing advice team.  
3. Check eligibility (age, income, first-time buyer status).  
4. Register your interest and gather documents (ID, proof of income).  
5. View available properties and follow the application process set by the housing association or scheme operator.

prompt: I submitted a listed building consent application, but the decision came late. What should I do now?
generated_response:
Because the authority took longer than the statutory 8 weeks to issue the listed building consent, you now have two main options.

1. Check the decision notice
   a. Read the exact wording, any conditions and the date it was issued.  
   b. Note the “date of decision” – your appeal deadline runs from that date.

2. If you are content with the permission  
   • Simply keep the notice in a safe place and comply with any conditions when you start work.

3. If you disagree with the decision (refusal or conditions you feel are unreasonable)  
   a. You can appeal to the Scottish Ministers through the Directorate for Planning and Environmental Appeals (DPEA).  
   b. The appeal must be lodged within 3 months of the decision date.  
   c. Follow the online appeal form and submit the plans, decision notice and any supporting statements.

4. If the delay itself caused you loss or you wish to complain  
   a. First write to the planning authority’s planning service manager, setting out the timeline and issues.  
   b. If you are not satisfied with their response, use the council’s formal complaints process.  
   c. After that, you can escalate to the Scottish Public Services Ombudsman (SPSO).

5. Keep records  
   • Save copies of all correspondence, the application, the late decision notice and any evidence of costs caused by the delay.

Further information:  
Planning permission and building regulations – GOV.UK overview: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I'm trying to understand if I can get help with my mortgage interest payments. What are the conditions for receiving Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI)?
generated_response:
Support for Mortgage Interest (SMI) is a government-backed loan that can help pay the interest on your mortgage or certain home-improvement loans. To qualify, ALL the following conditions have to be met.

1. You or your partner must own the home (including shared-ownership and some houseboats or park homes).  
2. You must be receiving at least one of these income-related benefits:  
   • Universal Credit (UC) – you must have had no earned income for the last assessment period and have been on UC for 3 months in a row.  
   • Income Support – after receiving it for 39 weeks in a row.  
   • Income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance – after receiving it for 39 weeks in a row.  
   • Income-related Employment and Support Allowance – after receiving it for 39 weeks in a row.  
   • Pension Credit – no waiting period.  
   (You cannot get SMI if you are on contribution-based JSA/ESA only, Working Tax Credit, or most other benefits.)  

3. The loan you need help with must be:  
   • A mortgage or other qualifying loan taken out to buy your home, or  
   • A loan taken out for certain repairs or improvements (for example, fixing structural defects).  

4. The amount SMI can cover is limited to interest on up to £200,000 of outstanding mortgage (or up to £100,000 if you’re getting Pension Credit). It does not pay:  
   • The mortgage capital,  
   • Buildings insurance, service charges, or mortgage arrears.  

5. You must agree to take SMI as a LOAN:  
   • DWP pays the interest direct to your lender.  
   • A second-charge is placed on your property.  
   • You repay the SMI loan (with interest) when you sell or transfer ownership, or earlier if you choose.  

How to claim  
1. Contact the office that pays your qualifying benefit and say you want to claim Support for Mortgage Interest.  
2. They’ll send you an SMI loan offer letter and agreement form.  
3. Return the signed agreement within the deadline.  
4. DWP will arrange payments direct to your lender once the waiting period has passed.  

If your earnings start again while on Universal Credit, your SMI stops and the loan balance remains repayable later.  

More information and the current standard interest rate are on GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/support-for-mortgage-interest

prompt: I'm a landlord in England and my tenant has an excluded tenancy. What do I need to do if they refuse to leave after giving them reasonable notice?
generated_response:
If your tenant is an “excluded occupier” (for example a lodger who shares the kitchen or bathroom with you in your own home) you do not need a court order to evict them.  

1. Check they really are an excluded occupier  
   • They must live in your home and share living space or facilities with you.  
   • If they have self-contained accommodation (their own flat within the house) they are not excluded and you would need a court order.  

2. Give them written notice  
   • State the date the notice is served and the date it ends.  
   • The notice period must be “reasonable” – usually at least the length of their rent period (for example 1 week’s notice if they pay weekly, 1 month if they pay monthly).  
   • Keep a copy and proof of delivery.  

3. After the notice ends and they still refuse to leave  
   • You may change the locks when they are out or ask them to leave and remove their belongings.  
   • Do not use or threaten violence – that is illegal.  
   • If you are worried about confrontation, arrange for an independent witness or ask the police to attend.  

4. Look after their possessions  
   • If they are not there when you change the locks, store their belongings safely and give them a reasonable chance to collect them.  

5. Return any deposit promptly, minus any agreed deductions for damage or unpaid rent.  

If at any point you are unsure whether the tenancy is excluded, get legal advice or apply to the county court for a possession order instead.  

More information: https://www.gov.uk/evicting-tenants/excluded-occupiers

prompt: I'm selling a home that I rent out to tenants. What do I need to do about the tenants?
generated_response:
Answer:
Below is a simple overview of what you usually need to do in Scotland when you plan to sell a rented-out home.

1. Decide whether the tenants will stay or leave  
   a. Sell with tenants (“sitting tenants”) – the tenancy continues and the buyer becomes the new landlord.  
   b. End the tenancy before completion – you must follow the legal notice process.

2. If you sell with the tenants in place  
   1. Tell the tenants in writing that you are marketing the property and keep them updated.  
   2. Give at least 24 hours’ written notice before any viewing and get their agreement to enter.  
   3. On completion:  
      • Transfer the tenancy deposit to the buyer or the approved deposit scheme account named by the buyer.  
      • Hand over all current tenancy paperwork (lease, inventory, safety certificates).  
      • Give the tenants the new landlord’s contact details within 28 days.  
      • Update your landlord registration online to show that you are no longer the owner-landlord.

3. If you want the tenants to leave before you sell  
   1. Check the tenancy type (most private lets that started on or after 1 Dec 2017 are Private Residential Tenancies).  
   2. Serve the correct “Notice to Leave” (Ground 1 – you intend to sell). Use the Scottish Government template.  
      • If the tenant has lived there 6 months or more, give at least 84 days’ notice.  
      • If they have lived there less than 6 months, give at least 28 days’ notice.  
   3. Keep proof that the notice was received (recorded delivery or signed hand-delivery).  
   4. If the tenant does not leave after the notice period, apply to the First-tier Tribunal (Housing & Property Chamber) for an eviction order.  
   5. Once the tenancy ends: read the meters, inspect the property, agree any deductions, and return the deposit through the approved scheme within the required timescale.

4. Throughout the process  
   • Continue to meet all repair, safety and deposit-scheme duties until the sale completes or the tenancy legally ends.  
   • Do not attempt to “self-evict” or change locks—this is illegal.  
   • Keep written records of every step; they will be needed by your solicitor or, if necessary, the tribunal.

5. Get professional help  
   Selling a tenanted property can be complex. Speak to:  
   • A solicitor or letting agent experienced in Scottish lettings law.  
   • Your mortgage lender (if you have one) to confirm any conditions on selling a buy-to-let property.

Relevant GOV.UK link  
Housing and local services (general guidance): https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

Justification:
• Assumes the property is in Scotland, so Scottish tenancy law applies (Notice to Leave, First-tier Tribunal).  
• Gives two common options—sell with tenants or end the tenancy—because the user did not say which they want.  
• Lists statutory notice periods and documents for Private Residential Tenancies (the most common current tenancy).  
• Highlights ongoing landlord duties and the illegality of self-eviction to keep the user compliant and tenants protected.  
• Advises professional help and lender check because legal and financial implications vary.

Missing info: None

prompt: I'm considering renting a property in England, but I heard about a scheme called Rent to Buy. Is this scheme still available, and how does it work?
generated_response:
Rent to Buy is still running in England, but it is only available on a limited number of new-build homes provided by housing associations.  

How it works  
1. You rent a newly built home at 20% below the normal market rent for up to 5 years.  
2. The lower rent is meant to help you save for a deposit.  
3. After (or during) the 5-year period you can:  
   • buy the home outright, or  
   • buy a share through Shared Ownership, or  
   • move on if you decide not to buy.  

Who can apply  
• You must be a first-time buyer or returning to the market after a relationship breakdown.  
• Your household income must be £80,000 a year or less (£90,000 in London).  
• You must intend to buy the property when you can.  

How to find a Rent to Buy home  
1. Go to the GOV.UK “Own Your Home” service and choose “Rent to Buy” under affordable home ownership schemes (link below).  
2. Follow the link to your local Help to Buy agent (sometimes called an affordable home-ownership agent) and register with them.  
3. Check local housing association websites and property portals for homes advertised as “Rent to Buy”, “Rent to Save” or “Intermediate Rent”.  
4. When a property is released, apply directly to the housing association; they will check your income and circumstances.  

Other things to note  
• Availability is patchy; most homes are in the North-West, Midlands and some parts of the South-West.  
• You will sign a tenancy agreement; normal tenant obligations apply.  
• If you decide not to buy after 5 years, the landlord can let the home to another eligible household.  

Relevant GOV.UK link  
Affordable home ownership schemes, including Rent to Buy: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I live in a council home in Wales and I'm considering buying it. Can I apply for the Right to Buy scheme with my spouse, who also lives with me but isn't part of the same tenancy?
generated_response:
Unfortunately, the Right to Buy and Right to Acquire schemes were abolished in Wales on 26 January 2019.  
This means:

1. No new Right to Buy applications can be made for council or housing-association homes in Wales.  
2. Because the scheme has ended, it is not possible to apply whether you do so on your own or jointly with your spouse.  

If you are keen to move into home ownership:

• Ask your landlord (the council or housing association) about any local low-cost home-ownership schemes they take part in.  
• Look at Welsh Government schemes such as “Help to Buy – Wales” or “Rent to Own – Wales”. Your local authority’s housing or home-ownership team can explain eligibility and how to apply.  
• Consider independent financial advice to see what other mortgage or shared-equity options might suit your circumstances.

For general information on housing options you can start at GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: How do I find out if my local council provides pest control services in Wales?
generated_response:
To check whether your local council in Wales offers pest-control services, follow these steps:  

1. Find your council’s website  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search “find your local council”, or visit the Housing and local services section: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  
   • Enter your postcode and note the name of your county or city council.  

2. Look for pest-control information  
   • On the council website, type “pest control” into the site’s search box or look under headings such as “Environment”, “Housing” or “Public protection”.  
   • The page will usually list the types of pests they treat, any charges, and how to book a visit.  

3. If you can’t find the information online  
   • Phone the council’s main customer service number (shown at the top or bottom of their website).  
   • Tell them your postcode and ask whether they provide pest-control services or if they can recommend an approved contractor.  

4. Keep a note  
   • Write down any reference numbers, fees or appointment times you’re given.  

If you can’t reach anyone or the council doesn’t offer the service, ask for contact details of local pest-control firms that meet the council’s standards.

prompt: I'm a landlord in Wales and I received a civil penalty notice for not checking my tenant's right to rent. How can I challenge this decision?
generated_response:
You can ask the Home Office to cancel (or reduce) the civil penalty if you believe it was issued in error.  
Because the ‘right to rent’ scheme currently applies only in England, landlords of properties in Wales should not receive these penalties.  

Follow these steps:

1. Check the notice  
   • Make sure it’s addressed to you and relates to the specific property.  
   • Confirm the tenancy is entirely in Wales and has no English address.  

2. Gather evidence  
   • Tenancy agreement showing the property’s Welsh address.  
   • Council tax or utility bills confirming the location.  
   • Land Registry title or mortgage statement if you have it.  

3. Submit an objection to the Home Office (informal review)  
   • You must do this within 28 days of the notice date.  
   • Write to the address or email given on the notice.  
   • State that the property is in Wales and therefore outside the scope of the Immigration Act 2014 ‘right to rent’ scheme.  
   • Attach copies of your evidence.  
   • Ask for the penalty to be cancelled (or, if relevant, reduced).  

4. Wait for the Home Office decision  
   • They should write back confirming whether they have cancelled, reduced or upheld the penalty.  

5. If the penalty is upheld, appeal to the County Court  
   • You must file the appeal within 28 days of the Home Office decision letter.  
   • Use Form N161 (County Court appeal notice).  
   • Set out the same argument and include your evidence.  
   • There is a court fee; you can apply for help with fees if needed.  

6. If you need help  
   • Contact a housing solicitor or advice agency (e.g. Shelter Cymru).  
   • If you are unsure about time limits, phone the number on the penalty notice straight away.  

Relevant GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I want to know how I can get a more precise record of my property boundary in Wales. What options are available to me if I need to resolve a dispute with my neighbor?
generated_response:
If you need a clearer record of your boundary – and a way to sort out a disagreement – you can go further than the usual Land Registry title plan.

1. Check what you already have  
   • Get a copy of the title register and title plan for your property and your neighbour’s (£3 each online). They show the “general boundaries”.  
   • Sometimes that is enough to confirm where the line is.

2. Gather better evidence  
   • Look out old deeds, conveyancing plans, fence receipts, photographs or statements from previous owners – anything that could show where the boundary has historically been.  
   • If the land is unmapped (rare), consider having a chartered land surveyor draw a scaled plan.

3. Apply for a “determined boundary”  
   • This is the only way HM Land Registry will show an exact legal boundary in England & Wales.  
   • Ask a suitably qualified surveyor to draw a detailed plan that meets Land Registry rules.  
   • Complete form DB and send it with the plan, evidence and the £90 fee.  
   • Land Registry will notify your neighbour; if they object, the case can be referred to the Land Registration division of the First-tier Tribunal.

4. Make a boundary agreement instead (quicker and cheaper)  
   • If you and your neighbour can agree the line, record it in writing, sign and date it and send a copy to HM Land Registry with form ID1/ID2 for each owner.  
   • Land Registry will file the agreement with both titles (fee £45) – it does not alter the register but can be relied on if the issue resurfaces.

5. Resolve the dispute  
   • Talk first – many disputes end once both sides have the same information.  
   • If that fails, consider mediation. A mediator approved by the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS) typically costs far less than court.  
   • As a last resort you or your neighbour can apply to the First-tier Tribunal (Property Chamber) or the County Court for a decision. Legal advice is recommended before starting formal action.

Key links  
• Your property boundaries – disputes, determined boundaries, agreements: https://www.gov.uk/your-property-boundaries  
• HM Land Registry: get information about property and land: https://www.gov.uk/search-property-information-land-registry  
• RICS neighbour dispute mediation: https://www.rics.org/uk/regulation/dispute-resolution-service/neighbour-disputes

prompt: Can I use the Rent to Buy scheme in England if I live in London?
generated_response:
You normally can’t use the national Rent to Buy scheme if you live in Greater London. Homes England only funds Rent to Buy properties outside London.  

Instead, Londoners have a similar option called London Living Rent. London Living Rent lets you rent a new-build home at a discounted rate (around two-thirds of local market rent) for 3–10 years and then gives you the chance to buy it, usually through Shared Ownership.  

What to do next  
1. Check if the home you want is in a London borough. If it is, look for “London Living Rent” rather than “Rent to Buy”.  
2. Use the Mayor of London’s Homes for Londoners search tool or ask local housing associations about London Living Rent properties.  
3. Register your details with the landlord of any London Living Rent property you’re interested in. They will check you:  
   • have a household income under £60,000 (£90,000 for larger homes)  
   • do not already own a home  
   • can show the rent and future purchase would be affordable for you.  
4. If you’re considering areas outside Greater London, you can look for Rent to Buy homes on the Homes England property search or by speaking to housing associations in those areas.  

Find housing schemes: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I'm renting a mobile home on a park and I received a written agreement. How long do I have to review it before signing and what should I check for?
generated_response:
You normally have 28 days to look over the written statement (agreement) before you sign it.  
Under the Mobile Homes Act 1983 the site owner must give you the document at least 28 days in advance, unless you write to them saying you are happy with a shorter period. You do not have to sign until those 28 days have passed, so use the time to make sure everything is clear and correct.

What to check:

1. Who and what  
   • Your full name, the site owner’s name and the exact pitch number or location.  
   • That it says it is an agreement under the Mobile Homes Act 1983 – this gives you security of tenure.  

2. Money matters  
   • Pitch fee – amount, when and how it must be paid.  
   • How and when the pitch fee can be reviewed or raised and your right to challenge increases.  
   • Any other charges (water, electricity, gas, service or admin fees) and how they are worked out.  
   • Whether a refundable deposit is mentioned and the conditions for getting it back.  

3. Your rights and duties  
   • The ‘implied terms’ set by law (they cannot be removed), such as:  
     – your right to keep the home on the pitch as long as you obey the rules and pay the fees;  
     – notice period and procedure the site owner must follow if they want you to leave;  
     – your right to sell or gift the home and to have certain family members inherit it.  
   • Any ‘express terms’ added by the site owner – make sure they do not contradict the implied terms.  

4. Park rules  
   • Rules about pets, vehicles, noise, sub-letting, age limits and any curfews.  
   • Check they are the same as the written rules lodged with the local council.  

5. Services and maintenance  
   • What the site owner must maintain (roads, lighting, communal areas).  
   • Your responsibilities for maintaining the home and pitch.  

6. Ending or transferring the agreement  
   • How you or the site owner can end the agreement and the notice required.  
   • Procedures and any commission due if you sell the home.  

Practical steps:

1. Read the agreement slowly and highlight anything you do not understand.  
2. Compare it with the model ‘implied terms’ (you can find them on GOV.UK or ask Citizens Advice).  
3. Ask the site owner in writing to clarify or amend anything unclear or incorrect.  
4. Keep a dated copy of every version of the agreement and all correspondence.  
5. If in doubt, get independent advice from Citizens Advice, LEASE Park Homes Advisory Service or a solicitor before you sign.

Further information:  
Park (mobile) homes – your rights and obligations (GOV.UK) https://www.gov.uk/park-mobile-homes

prompt: I need to find a service to dispose of old batteries and chemicals safely. How do I locate the correct local service for this in England?
generated_response:
You normally take household batteries and chemicals to facilities run by your local council. Here’s the quickest way to find the right place:

1. Go to the GOV.UK ‘Housing and local services’ section: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  
2. Choose ‘Bins, rubbish and recycling’.  
3. Select ‘Find a recycling centre’ (sometimes called a household waste recycling centre).  
4. Enter your postcode. The service will show:  
   • nearby battery drop-off points (many supermarkets, DIY and electrical shops)  
   • the council recycling centre(s) that accept hazardous items such as solvents, paints, weed-killers, cleaning fluids, motor oil and car batteries.  
5. Before you visit, click through to your council’s page or phone the number shown to check:  
   • which chemicals they accept  
   • any booking rules or ID needed  
   • the safest way to transport your items (for example, keeping chemicals upright in sealed, labelled containers).

If you have large quantities or business waste, the council page will list approved waste contractors who can collect it from you for a fee.

prompt: Can anyone other than the person who made the application appeal a minor commercial development decision?
generated_response:
No. In England, only the person (or organisation) that submitted the planning application can appeal a planning decision on a minor commercial development. Neighbours, local businesses or other interested parties cannot lodge an appeal themselves, although they can make comments on any appeal that is submitted by the applicant.  
  If you disagree with the council’s decision and you were not the applicant, your options are to:
  1. Make representations if the applicant does appeal – the Planning Inspectorate will invite comments.  
  2. Ask the local planning authority to take enforcement action if you believe the development is carried out contrary to the permission granted.  
  3. Seek legal advice about a judicial review if you think the council’s decision-making process was unlawful (this challenges the procedure, not the merits of the decision).

  For full guidance, see: https://www.gov.uk/appeal-planning-decision

prompt: How can I find out if I am eligible for legal aid during an eviction process?
generated_response:
You can check whether you qualify for legal aid in three quick steps.

1. Gather your financial details  
   • Monthly take-home pay, benefits and any other income.  
   • Savings, investments and property (other than the home you live in).  
   • Your partner’s finances, if you live together.

2. Use the online legal aid checker  
   Go to GOV.UK and complete the “Check if you can get legal aid” tool. It asks the questions above and tells you straight away if you are likely to qualify and whether you might have to pay a contribution.

3. Get specialist confirmation  
   a) Telephone Civil Legal Advice (CLA) on 0345 345 4 345 and say you are facing eviction. They can confirm eligibility and, if you qualify, put you straight through to a legal aid housing solicitor.  
   b) Alternatively, contact a local firm or Law Centre that holds a housing legal aid contract. Give them the same financial details and any papers about the eviction (e.g. notice from your landlord or court papers).  

If you already have a court hearing date, turn up even if you have not arranged help beforehand. You can see a duty solicitor under the Housing Possession Court Duty Scheme on the day; this service is free and not means-tested.

Extra support in Wales  
Shelter Cymru (08000 495 495) and your local council’s housing options team can also explain your legal aid position and help you find a solicitor.

Relevant GOV.UK pages  
• Check if you can get legal aid – https://www.gov.uk/check-legal-aid  
• Housing and local services – https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  

Keep all letters and emails about the eviction and any evidence of your income, as you’ll need to show these to prove both your case and your finances.

prompt: I'm a landlord in Scotland and I received a civil penalty notice for not checking my tenant's right to rent. How can I object to the penalty?
generated_response:
You can challenge (‘object to’) a civil penalty notice, but you must act quickly.

1. Check the basics  
   • The right to rent rules only apply to property that is let in England. If your property is wholly in Scotland, state this clearly – the Home Office should cancel the notice.  
   • Note the deadline printed on the notice. You normally have 28 days from the date it was given to object.  

2. Gather your evidence  
   • Copy of the tenancy agreement showing the property’s address.  
   • Any proof you carried out right-to-rent checks (if the property is in England).  
   • Any other documents that show the penalty is wrong or should be reduced (for example, proof you are a registered landlord in Scotland).  

3. Complete the Objection Form (sometimes called the ‘request for reconsideration’ form)  
   • The form and return details are usually enclosed with the penalty notice.  
   • Explain why you think the penalty should be cancelled or reduced (e.g. “Property is in Scotland – right-to-rent scheme does not apply”).  
   • Attach your evidence.  

4. Send the form within the 28-day deadline  
   • Post it to the address or email given on the notice.  
   • Keep copies of everything and get proof of posting if you send it by post.  

5. Wait for the Home Office decision  
   • They will write to you with the outcome.  
   • If they uphold the penalty and you still disagree, you can appeal to the county court (or the sheriff court in Scotland) within 28 days of that decision.  

Further guidance is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/landlord-immigration-penalties  

If you’re unsure, contact the Civil Penalty Compliance Team (details on your notice) or seek independent legal advice.

prompt: I'm 17 and have been homeless for a few weeks. How does Children's Services help me find a place to live and what options are available if returning home isn't safe?
generated_response:
If you’re 17 and can’t safely return home, your local Children’s Services (part of the council’s Social Services department in Wales) have a legal duty to help you.  

1. Get in touch straightaway  
   • Phone the council’s Social Services “duty team” and say you are 17, homeless and at risk.  
   • If you’re in danger right now, call 999 first.  

2. Ask for a “child in need” assessment  
   • Under the Social Services and Well-being (Wales) Act 2014 and the Children Act 1989, Children’s Services must assess your situation and needs.  
   • This should start the same day you contact them. You can ask to have a trusted adult or an independent advocate with you.  

3. Short-term (emergency) accommodation  
   • While they assess you, the council must give you somewhere safe to stay.  
   • They should avoid B&Bs; typical options are a foster placement, a supported lodgings host, or a staffed hostel with support workers.  

4. Longer-term options if home isn’t safe  
   • Become “looked after” under section 20 of the Children Act. You stay in foster care, a children’s home or semi-independent accommodation until you turn 18.  
   • Supported housing for 16–17-year-olds: your own room with on-site staff to help with budgeting, cooking, studying, etc.  
   • Staying-put or “when I’m ready” schemes: after 18 you may stay with your foster carer or in supported lodgings while you move into adulthood.  

5. Your rights while looked after  
   • A personal adviser and a pathway plan for education, training, work and housing up to age 21 (or 25 if in education).  
   • Financial help with living costs, setting-up grants, council tax discounts and priority for social housing later on.  

6. Practical next steps today  
   1. Find the council’s 24-hour Social Services number online or call 0800 169 0298 to be connected.  
   2. Keep any ID, letters or proof of homelessness with you.  
   3. Ring Shelter Cymru’s free helpline 08000 495 495 for independent advice.  
   4. If you feel pressured to return home, tell your social worker you believe it’s unsafe. They must take this into account.  

Further information  
GOV.UK – Housing and local services: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: If I get a Migration Notice, do I need to do anything else besides switching to Universal Credit?
generated_response:
Getting a Migration Notice means your current (‘legacy’) benefit will end and you must make a Universal Credit (UC) claim by the deadline on the letter.  You do not have to cancel your old benefit yourself, but you do need to complete the UC claim process.  

What to do:  
1. Note the deadline on your Migration Notice – you normally have 3 months to claim. Missing it could stop your payments.  
2. Gather the details you’ll need for the online claim (National Insurance number, bank details, rent amount, childcare costs, income).  
3. Make your UC claim online (or by phone if the letter tells you to).  
4. Verify your identity online or at the Jobcentre if asked.  
5. Attend any booked appointment and agree your Claimant Commitment – your UC can’t be paid until you sign this.  
6. Keep paying your rent and bills while your claim is assessed. Housing Benefit and other legacy benefits will stop automatically once UC starts.  
7. Report any change of circumstances straight away (for example if your student income or living situation changes).  
8. Check your first UC payment – if it looks low, ask about ‘transitional protection’ mentioned in your letter.  

That’s all. Once you have made a valid UC claim and completed the steps above, no further action is needed to close your old benefit – DWP and HMRC do that for you.  

More help: GOV.UK → Benefits → Universal Credit (https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits)

prompt: My child is doing a Foundation Apprenticeship in Scotland. Do I still get Child Benefit?
generated_response:
Yes. Child Benefit can continue while your child is on a Foundation Apprenticeship in Scotland, provided:

1. Your child is under 20.  
2. The Foundation Apprenticeship is classed as “approved education” (it is – it sits at SCQF Level 6, the same level as Highers).  

What you need to do  
1. Tell HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) that your child has stayed in approved education.  
   • If HMRC recently sent you a Child Benefit letter asking about your child’s plans after age 16, follow the instructions on that letter or use the online service.  
   • If you have not had a letter, phone the Child Benefit helpline (0300 200 3100) or download form CH297 from GOV.UK and post it.  
2. Keep HMRC up to date if your child:  
   • leaves the apprenticeship early, or  
   • turns 20 (Child Benefit stops then).  

Payments will continue automatically once HMRC have the details; you do not need to make a new claim.

Most relevant GOV.UK links  
Child Benefit when your child turns 16: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-16-19  
Manage or tell HMRC about a Child Benefit change: https://www.gov.uk/report-changes-child-benefit

prompt: I recently started a new relationship and moved in with my partner. Do I need to report this change to HMRC and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Whether you need to tell HMRC depends on what, if anything, you currently receive from them.  

1. If you get Working Tax Credit or Child Tax Credit  
   1. Report the change within 1 month – moving in with a partner counts as a change of circumstances.  
   2. Have both National Insurance numbers, dates of birth and income details for the current tax year ready.  
   3. Update your claim:  
      • online through ‘Manage your tax credits’ (you’ll need a Government Gateway user ID), or  
      • call the Tax Credits helpline on 0345 300 3900 (Mon–Fri).  
   4. HMRC will review your award and send a revised award notice. Check it and query anything that looks wrong.  

2. If you only receive Child Benefit  
   • You do not have to report moving in together, but you must tell HMRC if your or your partner’s individual adjusted net income goes over £50,000, as the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply.  

3. If you do not receive Tax Credits, Child Benefit or any other HMRC-administered benefit  
   • No report is required – HMRC does not need to know about your living arrangements for PAYE or Self Assessment purposes.  

Useful link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I'm a nurse and I'm temporarily unable to work because of a serious illness. How do I find out what benefits I could be entitled to and how much I might get?
generated_response:
Here’s a straightforward way to work out what help you could get while you’re off work.

1. Check Statutory Sick Pay (SSP)  
   • If you’re still on your employer’s payroll and earn at least £123 a week before tax, ask payroll/HR whether they can pay you SSP.  
   • SSP is £116.75 a week (2024/25 rates) for up to 28 weeks. Your employer pays it in the same way as wages.

2. Look at ‘New Style’ Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)  
   • You can claim ESA as soon as your sick pay has stopped or you’re not entitled to SSP.  
   • You need enough recent National Insurance contributions (most full-time nurses will have these).  
   • The basic rate is £84.80 a week if you’re aged 25 +; after 13 weeks this may rise to £96.40 or £128.85 depending on a Work Capability Assessment.

3. Check means-tested help  
   • If your household income or savings are limited, you may be able to claim Universal Credit (UC) alongside or after ESA.  
   • UC can include extra amounts for housing costs and for being too ill to work.  
   • If you reach State Pension age during your illness (currently 66), Pension Credit replaces UC.

4. Consider disability-related benefits  
   • Personal Independence Payment (PIP) is for help with daily living or mobility needs and is not means-tested.  
   • Attendance Allowance replaces PIP once you are over State Pension age, but you can keep PIP if you claimed it first.

5. Use an independent benefits calculator  
   • Because the exact amount depends on earnings, savings and household circumstances, the quickest way to see what you could get is to enter your details into an online calculator (for example, Entitledto, Turn2us or Policy in Practice).  
   • Have payslips, rent or mortgage details and savings figures handy when you do this.

6. Get personalised advice  
   • If online forms are difficult, contact Citizens Advice Cymru on 0800 702 2020 or your union’s welfare service.  
   • They can do the calculations for you and help with any applications.

Relevant GOV.UK page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I'm trying to apply for free school meals for my child, but I'm not sure which benefits I qualify for. How do I check if I'm eligible and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Free school meals are handled by your local council, but the basic rules are the same across England.

1. Check if your child already gets them automatically  
   • All children in Reception, Year 1 and Year 2 get free lunches without you applying.  
   • From Year 3 upwards you must be on certain benefits.

2. See which benefits qualify  
   You can usually claim if you receive any one of these:  
   • Universal Credit – with net earnings of £7,400 a year or less (after tax, and not counting benefits)  
   • Income Support  
   • Income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance  
   • Income-related Employment and Support Allowance  
   • Guarantee element of Pension Credit  
   • Child Tax Credit (but not Working Tax Credit) and your annual gross income is under £16,190  
   • Working Tax Credit ‘run-on’ (paid for 4 weeks after you stop qualifying for Working Tax Credit)  
   • Support under Part VI of the Immigration and Asylum Act 1999

3. Gather the details you’ll need  
   • Your National Insurance number (or NASS number if you have one)  
   • Your date of birth  
   • Your child’s name and school

4. Apply through your council  
   a. Go to GOV.UK, search “free school meals”, and enter your postcode to reach your council’s online form.  
   b. If you prefer, phone the council’s education or benefits team and ask for a paper form.  
   c. Fill in the form and send any proof they ask for (they can often check benefits automatically).

5. Wait for confirmation  
   The council will write to you and the school. If you qualify, meals start straight away and your child keeps the entitlement while they stay at the same school and you remain on a qualifying benefit.

If you are unsure which benefit you’re on or need help with the form, ring your council or the school office—they can guide you.

Further information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: If I'm over State Pension age and living with my partner who hasn't reached State Pension age, can I still claim Housing Benefit?
generated_response:
You can usually only start a new Housing Benefit claim if both members of a couple have reached State Pension age.  
Because your partner is still under State Pension age, you are classed as a “mixed-age couple”.  
For most mixed-age couples:

1. You cannot make a new claim for Housing Benefit (or Pension Credit).  
2. Instead, you normally have to claim Universal Credit to help with your rent.  
3. The exceptions are:
   • You (or your partner) already get Housing Benefit that has been paid continuously since before 15 May 2019 and you have not had a break in the claim or moved home.  
   • You live in certain types of temporary or supported accommodation where Universal Credit cannot cover the rent.  

What to do next  
1. If you already have a continuous Housing Benefit award from before 15 May 2019, keep notifying the council of any changes in circumstances so it can continue.  
2. If not, create a Universal Credit account and make a joint claim with your partner for help with housing costs.  
3. If you are in supported or temporary accommodation, contact your local council’s benefits team – you may still be able to claim Housing Benefit for the rent element.  
4. If you are unsure, call the Universal Credit helpline or your local council’s benefits service for personalised advice.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I'm the widow of someone who worked for the National Coal Board. How do I apply for the National Concessionary Fuel Scheme and get the cash allowance for fuel?
generated_response:
You can claim under the National Concessionary Fuel Scheme (NCFS) by contacting the National Concessionary Fuel Office (NCFO), which runs the scheme for widows, widowers and eligible dependants of former National Coal Board/British Coal employees.  

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You’re the surviving spouse or civil partner of someone who was entitled to concessionary fuel from the National Coal Board or British Coal.  
   • Your late partner did not opt out of the scheme before they died.  

2. Gather the documents you will need  
   • Your late partner’s full name, date of birth, National Insurance number and, if you have it, their pit number or NCFS reference.  
   • Your marriage or civil-partnership certificate and your partner’s death certificate (copies are accepted at first).  
   • Your bank or building-society account details (for the cash allowance).  

3. Ask for an application pack  
   Phone NCFO: 0345 759 0529  
   Email: coal.allowances@beis.gov.uk  
   Post:  
   National Concessionary Fuel Office (NCFO)  
   PO Box 29  
   Sheffield S3 7YX  

   Lines are open Monday–Thursday 08:30-17:00, Friday 08:30-16:30. If you prefer Welsh, say so when you call.  

4. Fill in the form  
   • Tick the option for the cash allowance rather than solid fuel.  
   • Attach copies of the documents listed in step 2.  

5. Send the form back to NCFO (address above). Keep copies of everything you post.  

6. What happens next  
   • NCFO will confirm your eligibility in writing.  
   • If approved, the cash allowance is paid every 4 weeks straight into your bank account.  
   • Payments are tax-free and do not affect State Pension or most other benefits.  

If you need help completing the form, ring the NCFO number above or visit a local Citizens Advice office.  

More information and updates are on GOV.UK (Benefits section): https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: What are the options for getting help and advice if I'm struggling with my housing benefit decision?
generated_response:
If you disagree with or don’t understand your Housing Benefit decision, you have several places to turn for help and advice.

1. Ask your council to explain the decision  
   • Phone or visit the Housing Benefit team shown on your decision letter.  
   • Ask for a written statement of reasons – it’s free and may clear things up.

2. Ask for a ‘mandatory reconsideration’  
   • If you still think the decision is wrong, you have 1 month from the date on the letter to ask the council to look at it again.  
   • Tell them what you think is wrong and send any extra evidence (for example payslips or rent statements).  
   • Keep a copy and note the date you sent it.

3. Appeal to an independent tribunal  
   • If the council doesn’t change its decision, you normally have another 1 month to appeal to the First-tier Tribunal.  
   • The appeal is free and the tribunal is independent of the council.

4. Get free, independent advice  
   • Citizens Advice – call 0800 144 8848, use webchat or visit a local office.  
   • Shelter – housing helpline 0808 800 4444.  
   • Law Centres or a local welfare rights service – search online or ask your council/library.  
   • If you’re under 18, you can ask a trusted adult (parent, carer, teacher or youth worker) to contact these services or the council with you. They can be named as your representative.

5. Other support  
   • If you’re struggling to pay rent while the decision is sorted out, ask the council about a Discretionary Housing Payment.  
   • You can also complain to the council if there were delays or mistakes in handling your claim.

Useful GOV.UK links  
   • Housing Benefit: https://www.gov.uk/housing-benefit  
   • Appeal a benefit decision: https://www.gov.uk/appeal-benefit-decision  
   • Benefits: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I live in Wales and am expecting my first child. Can I still get the Sure Start Maternity Grant, or do I have to apply for something else?
generated_response:
Yes. The Sure Start Maternity Grant is still available in Wales (it has only been replaced in Scotland). If you or your partner get one of the qualifying benefits, you can claim the £500 grant for your first child.

How to apply  
1. Check you get at least one of these benefits: Universal Credit, Income Support, income-based Jobseeker’s Allowance, income-related Employment and Support Allowance, Pension Credit, Child Tax Credit, or Working Tax Credit that includes a disability or severe disability element.  
2. Download claim form SF100 (Sure Start) or order one from Jobcentre Plus.  
3. Ask a health professional (midwife, doctor or health visitor) to complete and sign page 10 once you are 29 weeks pregnant or within 11 weeks after the birth.  
4. Post the completed form to the address on the form within:  
   • 11 weeks before the baby is due, or  
   • 6 months after the birth.  
5. You’ll get a decision letter. If approved, the £500 is paid into your bank account.

If you do not yet get a qualifying benefit, claim the benefit first and send the grant form once it is awarded.

Other help  
• Healthy Start NHS food vouchers are also available if you’re on certain benefits.  
• Contact your local Jobcentre Plus or Citizens Advice if you need help filling in the form.

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/sure-start-maternity-grant

prompt: I'm unsure whether I should appeal or complain about my tax credit decision. What's the difference?
generated_response:
Appeal and complaint do two different jobs.

1. Appeal (called a “mandatory reconsideration” first, then an appeal):
   • Use this when you think HMRC’s decision about the amount you’re owed, an overpayment, or your entitlement is wrong.  
   • You ask HMRC to look at the decision again (mandatory reconsideration).  
   • If you still disagree, you can take the case to an independent tribunal.  
   • You must do this within 30 days of the decision letter.

2. Complaint:
   • Use this when the decision itself is correct but HMRC’s service was poor ­– for example delays, rude staff, lost papers or not following their own process.  
   • Complaining won’t change the decision, but you might get an apology, explanation or compensation.  
   • There is no strict time-limit, but complain as soon as possible.

Deciding what to do:

Step 1. Read your decision letter. Highlight the parts you think are wrong.  
Step 2. Ask yourself, “Do I want the amount or entitlement changed?”  
   – Yes → start a mandatory reconsideration (appeal route).  
   – No, I just want to complain about how I was treated → use the HMRC complaints process.  
Step 3. If both apply, you can run them side-by-side: submit the mandatory reconsideration for the decision and a separate complaint about service.

How to start:

Appeal route  
1. Fill in form TC846 or write to the address on your decision letter asking for a mandatory reconsideration.  
2. Keep copies and post by recorded delivery.  
3. Wait for the “mandatory reconsideration notice”.  
4. If still unhappy, submit form SSCS5 to the tribunal within 30 days of the notice.

Complaint route  
1. Phone HMRC Tax Credits helpline or write to the complaints address on GOV.UK.  
2. Mark the letter “Complaint” and explain what went wrong and what you want.  
3. If unresolved, escalate to HMRC Tier 2, then the Adjudicator’s Office, then your MP for the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman.

Further guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits  

If you’re unsure, you can call the free Citizens Advice Scotland helpline on 0800 028 1456.

prompt: I'm expecting a baby and need to know what financial support I can claim. How do I check if I'm eligible for Maternity or Paternity Leave or Pay, or Maternity Allowance?
generated_response:
You can check what you’re entitled to in three main areas:

1. Statutory Maternity Leave and Pay (for the pregnant employee)  
   • Must be an employee, giving the employer at least 15 weeks’ notice before the baby is due.  
   • Must have worked for the same employer continuously for at least 26 weeks up to the 15th week before the due date.  
   • Average weekly earnings must be at least the National Insurance lower earnings limit (currently £123).  
   • If eligible, the mother can take up to 52 weeks’ leave and receive up to 39 weeks’ Statutory Maternity Pay (SMP).

2. Statutory Paternity Leave and Pay (for the father or partner)  
   • Must be an employee.  
   • Must have worked for the same employer for at least 26 weeks by the end of the 15th week before the due date.  
   • Average weekly earnings must meet the NI lower earnings limit.  
   • Provides up to 2 weeks’ paid leave (can be taken in one block or two separate weeks).

3. Maternity Allowance (for the mother if she does not qualify for SMP)  
   • Available to employed, self-employed or recently employed mothers who cannot get SMP.  
   • Must have worked (employed or self-employed) for at least 26 weeks in the 66-week period before the baby is due and earned at least £30 in any 13 of those weeks.  
   • Payable for up to 39 weeks.

How to check quickly:
1. Collect key details: due date, your and your partner’s employment start dates, average weekly earnings, and whether either of you is self-employed.  
2. Use the online Maternity and Paternity calculator on GOV.UK (found via the Benefits section link below). Enter the dates and earnings; it will confirm which payments each of you can claim and the exact amounts.  
3. Speak to your (and your partner’s) employer’s HR or payroll team. They will confirm eligibility for SMP or SPP and tell you what evidence they need (usually form MAT B1 for the mother’s due date).  
4. If the calculator or employer says the mother is not entitled to SMP, download form MA1 from GOV.UK, complete it and send it to Jobcentre Plus to claim Maternity Allowance.  
5. Keep copies of all forms and correspondence. If anything is unclear, call the HMRC Statutory Payments Helpline on 0300 200 3500.

Relevant link:  
GOV.UK Benefits section – start point for all the above guidance and the eligibility calculator: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: If I'm self-employed and report zero income, will I still get Universal Credit?
