generated_response:
Yes, you can still claim Universal Credit (UC) if you are self-employed and have a month where your business makes £0. How much you receive, or whether you receive anything at all, depends on whether the Minimum Income Floor (MIF) applies to you and on your wider household circumstances.

1. Check if the Minimum Income Floor applies  
   • If you have been self-employed for more than 12 months, UC usually assumes you earn a set amount each month (35 hours × National Living Wage for your age).  
   • If that MIF applies, UC is worked out as if you earned that amount—even when you report £0—so your UC payment could be reduced or reduced to £0.  
   • You can ask your work coach to suspend the MIF if you are still in a “start-up period”, have coronavirus-related problems, or exceptional circumstances.

2. Report your actual earnings and expenses each assessment period through your online UC account. Even if you earned nothing, you must still submit £0 so your claim stays live.

3. Make sure you meet the general UC rules  
   • Savings below £16,000 and household income (including any partner’s earnings) low enough once the MIF or actual earnings are taken into account.  
   • UK residency and other standard eligibility conditions.

4. If the MIF is switched off (for example, you are in your first 12 months of trading), UC will be calculated on your actual earnings—£0—so you are likely to receive a higher payment while your income is that low.

5. Next steps  
   1. Log in to your UC account each month and report £0 earnings if that is correct.  
   2. Ask your work coach whether the MIF is currently applied to your claim.  
   3. Use an independent benefits calculator or the GOV.UK “self-employed and Universal Credit” guidance to estimate your award.  
   4. If unsure, call the UC helpline or message your journal for a written decision.

Relevant GOV.UK pages:  
• Universal Credit and self-employment – www.gov.uk/universal-credit/how-your-earnings-affect-your-claim  
• Benefits calculators – www.gov.uk/benefits-calculators

prompt: Where can I apply for Housing Benefit in Wales?
generated_response:
You apply for Housing Benefit through the council area where you live in Wales.   

1. Go to the GOV.UK “Housing Benefit” page and use the postcode checker to find your local council’s Housing Benefit section.  
   • Link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits  
2. On your council’s website, choose “Housing Benefit” (sometimes under “Benefits” or “Housing”).  
3. Complete the online form (or download a paper form if offered).  
4. Send any evidence the council asks for (for example: recent payslips, tenancy agreement, proof of savings).  
5. The council will confirm receipt and tell you when you can expect a decision.  

If you need help completing the form, contact your council’s benefits team or call Advicelink Cymru on 0800 702 2020.

Note: If you’re of working age and not in supported or temporary accommodation, you’ll usually need to claim Universal Credit for help with rent instead of Housing Benefit.

prompt: What happens if I move house while receiving Universal Credit?
generated_response:
Moving home counts as a “change of circumstances” for Universal Credit. You must tell the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) straight away, even if the move is temporary.

1. Report the move  
   • Sign in to your Universal Credit online account.  
   • Select “Report a change” and give the new address, the date you’ll start paying rent there (or the date you move in if you’re an owner-occupier), and details of anyone new who will live with you.  
   • If you cannot get online, ring the Universal Credit helpline and ask them to note the change.

2. Give evidence of your new housing costs (if you rent)  
   • Upload or post a copy of your new tenancy agreement, rent statement or letter from your landlord.  
   • Do this within one month to make sure you do not miss out on help with rent.

3. Check how your payment may change  
   • The housing element is worked out again for the new address—your entitlement could go up or down.  
   • If you move to a different local council area, the Local Housing Allowance rate may differ.  
   • Your first payment after the change may be higher or lower than usual as DWP adjusts for the part-month at each address.

4. Keep paying your current rent until the tenancy ends  
   • Universal Credit will not cover rent at two addresses unless you meet a narrow set of exceptions (for example, fleeing domestic abuse).  

5. Update any Council Tax Reduction claim separately  
   • Tell your new local council you’ve moved—this is not done automatically through Universal Credit.

Universal Credit helpline (England): 0800 328 5644  
More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits/universal-credit

prompt: I live in Wales and I'm worried about my Council Tax bill. How can I check if I'm paying the correct amount and if I'm eligible for any reductions?
generated_response:
If you think your Council Tax bill in Wales may be wrong, or you might be entitled to pay less, try these steps.

1. Check your Council Tax band  
   • Find the band (A–I in Wales) shown on your bill.  
   • Use the “Check your Council Tax band” service on GOV.UK.  
     • Enter your postcode.  
     • Make sure the property address and band match what’s on your bill.  
   • If you believe the band is too high, you can challenge it. Follow the on-screen instructions or phone the Valuation Office Agency (VOA) on 03000 505 505 for guidance.

2. Review automatic discounts  
   • Single adult discount – 25% off if only one adult (18+) lives in the home.  
   • Empty/unfurnished property discount – contact your council; rules vary.  
   • Annexes, carers and disabled residents also have potential discounts.

3. Check Council Tax Reduction (CTR) – help with the bill if you’re on a low or moderate income  
   • Use the “Apply for Council Tax Reduction” page on GOV.UK.  
   • You’ll be directed to your local Welsh council’s online form or phone number.  
   • Have your National Insurance number, income and savings details ready.

4. Look at disability-related reductions  
   • If someone in the home needs a room, extra bathroom or space for a wheelchair because of a disability, you may qualify for a Disabled Band Reduction. Phone your council’s Council Tax team to apply.

5. Contact your local council’s Council Tax section  
   • Their phone number and email are on your bill.  
   • Ask them to:  
     a) Confirm how they worked out your charge.  
     b) Check you’re getting every discount and reduction you qualify for.  
     c) Send you a revised bill if anything changes.

6. Keep records  
   • Note dates, names and any reference numbers when you speak to the VOA or council.  
   • Keep copies of forms and letters.

Key GOV.UK links  
• Check your Council Tax band: https://www.gov.uk/council-tax-bands  
• Council Tax Reduction: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits/help-for-households  

If you cannot use the online services, ring your council or the VOA and ask them to post the forms or complete them over the phone.

prompt: I'm a teacher who was injured during a training exercise while in the reserves. How do I claim compensation for my injury?
generated_response:
You can claim under the Armed Forces Compensation Scheme (AFCS). The AFCS covers Regular and Reserve personnel who are injured, become ill or die as a result of service, including training exercises.

Follow these steps:  
1. Check eligibility  
   • The injury must have happened on or after 6 April 2005 while you were on reserve service or authorised training.  
   • You usually need to claim within 7 years of the incident (later claims can be accepted in certain circumstances).  

2. Gather information  
   • Your service number and unit details.  
   • The date, place and brief description of the training exercise and injury.  
   • Medical evidence (e.g. hospital or GP records, Defence Medical Services notes if you were treated on base).  

3. Complete the claim form  
   • Download form AFCS001 from GOV.UK or phone Veterans UK to ask for a paper copy.  
   • Fill in all sections, sign and date it. Keep a copy for your records.  

4. Send the claim  
   • Email: veterans-uk@mod.gov.uk, or  
   • Post: Veterans UK, Ministry of Defence, Norcross, Thornton-Cleveleys FY5 3WP.  
   • Include any supporting medical documents.  

5. What happens next  
   • Veterans UK will acknowledge receipt and may ask for further evidence or arrange a medical assessment.  
   • They decide whether you receive a tax-free lump sum and/or a Guaranteed Income Payment (regular monthly payments for more serious injuries).  
   • You can appeal to an independent tribunal if you disagree with the decision.  

6. If the injury happened before 6 April 2005  
   • Apply under the War Pension Scheme instead (use the same contact details – Veterans UK can guide you).  

7. Get help if needed  
   • Veterans UK helpline: 0808 1914 218 (free)  
   • Service charity support: Royal British Legion, SSAFA, BLESMA etc.  

Relevant link:  
https://www.gov.uk/armed-forces-compensation

prompt: I'm a nurse and I'm worried about my financial situation. Where can I get help?
generated_response:
Feeling worried about money is common right now and there is help available.  
Follow these steps to see what support you can get:

1. Check what benefits you could claim  
   • Use an independent benefits calculator on GOV.UK to see if you can get Universal Credit, Tax-Free Childcare, Council Tax Reduction (Wales) or other help.  
   • GOV.UK benefits page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits  

2. Look into cost-of-living support  
   • If you already get certain benefits you may receive automatic Cost of Living Payments.  
   • Help with energy bills: talk to your supplier about grants or a payment plan.

3. Get help that’s just for NHS staff  
   • NHS Wales offers an Employee Assistance Programme (EAP) with confidential money and debt advice – ask your manager or intranet for the phone number.  
   • The Royal College of Nursing (RCN) has a Benevolent Fund and hardship grants; Unison’s “There for You” charity offers similar help.

4. Speak to a free debt-advice charity straight away if you’re behind on bills  
   • StepChange: 0800 138 1111  
   • National Debtline: 0808 808 4000  
   • Citizens Advice Cymru: 0800 702 2020 or at your local bureau

5. Reduce outgoings where possible  
   • Apply for the Welsh Government’s Council Tax Reduction or Discretionary Assistance Fund if you face an emergency cost.  
   • Ask student loan or credit providers for temporary payment pauses if needed.

6. Build a simple budget and extra income  
   • MoneyHelper (0800 138 7777) has easy budgeting tools and guides.  
   • Check NHS and local authority job boards for bank or overtime shifts if you can take on extra hours safely.

7. Don’t struggle alone  
   • Talk to your union rep or occupational health.  
   • If money worries are affecting your mental health, call Samaritans free on 116 123 or text “FRONTLINE” to 85258 for the free, confidential text service.

prompt: I think my tax credit decision is wrong, but I'm not sure how to proceed. What should I do?
generated_response:
If you think HMRC has made the wrong decision about your tax credits, you can ask them to look at it again and, if needed, appeal.  

1. Check the date on your decision letter – you normally have 30 days from the date on the letter to act.  
2. Ask for a ‘mandatory reconsideration’.  
   • Phone the Tax Credits Helpline on 0345 300 3900 (or 0345 300 3909 for textphone) and say you want a mandatory reconsideration, or  
   • Write to the address on your decision letter using the heading “Mandatory Reconsideration” and explain why you think the decision is wrong. Include:  
     – your National Insurance number,  
     – the date of the decision letter, and  
     – any evidence that supports your case (for example, payslips, childcare receipts).  
3. Keep copies of everything you send and note the date you called or posted your letter.  
4. HMRC will send you a Mandatory Reconsideration Notice explaining whether they have changed the decision.  
5. If you still disagree, you can appeal to an independent tribunal within 30 days of the date on the Notice. The appeal form (SSCS5(TC)) and guidance come with the Notice.  
6. For free help, contact Citizens Advice Scotland or a local welfare-rights adviser.  

Useful link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: My business has debts and I want to close it. What are my options for dealing with the debts before closing?
generated_response:
Here are the main ways small businesses in the UK deal with debts before they close. Which route is open to you depends largely on the legal structure of your business (sole trader, partnership or limited company) and whether you can still make at least some repayments.

1. Talk to your creditors first  
   • Explain that you intend to close the business and ask about reduced repayments, extra time or a one-off settlement.  
   • Get any new agreement in writing.

2. If you trade as a sole trader or partnership  
   a) Informal repayment plan – agree lower monthly payments with each creditor.  
   b) Debt Management Plan – made through a licensed debt-management provider; covers most unsecured debts.  
   c) Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) – a formal, court-approved plan where you pay what you can (usually over 5 years); the rest is written off.  
   d) Bankruptcy – your assets are sold to pay creditors; remaining debts are written off. You can usually trade again after discharge (12 months) but your credit record is affected.

3. If you run a limited company  
   a) Company Voluntary Arrangement (CVA) – directors stay in control while an insolvency practitioner (IP) agrees a reduced repayment plan with creditors.  
   b) Creditors’ Voluntary Liquidation (CVL) – you choose to liquidate; an IP sells company assets and shares proceeds among creditors. Any remaining company debt is written off, but personal guarantees still stand.  
   c) Strike-off / dissolution – only possible if the company has no debts or the creditors all agree.  
   d) Administration – an IP takes control to rescue the business or get a better return for creditors; can lead to sale or liquidation.

4. Check for personal guarantees  
   • If you signed personal guarantees or traded while insolvent, you may be personally liable for some company debts. Get professional advice early.

5. Get professional help  
   • A licensed insolvency practitioner or a free debt-advice charity (e.g. Business Debtline 0800 197 6026) can explain the best route and handle paperwork.  
   • Their early involvement usually reduces costs and stress.

6. Tell HMRC and other authorities  
   • You must file final accounts and tax returns, cancel VAT/PAYE registrations and settle any remaining tax.  
   • If choosing bankruptcy, IVA, CVA or liquidation, the appointed practitioner will usually handle HMRC.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance  
Closing a limited company: https://www.gov.uk/closing-a-limited-company  
Company Voluntary Arrangement and administration: https://www.gov.uk/company-administration  
Bankruptcy: https://www.gov.uk/bankruptcy  
Individual Voluntary Arrangements: https://www.gov.uk/individual-voluntary-arrangements

prompt: I'm part of a community group in Wales and we want to start an unincorporated association. Do we need to register it, and what happens if we make a profit?
generated_response:
You do not have to register an unincorporated association in Wales (or anywhere in the UK). An unincorporated association is simply an agreement between a group of people to come together for a common, non-profit purpose.  

What to do:  
1. Agree a written constitution. This should cover:  
   • the group’s name and purpose  
   • how people become or leave being a member  
   • how decisions are made and meetings held  
   • how money is handled (bank account, two signatories, annual statement)  
   • what happens if the group closes  
2. Elect at least a chair, secretary and treasurer to act on the group’s behalf.  
3. Open a bank account in the association’s name (most banks will ask for the signed constitution and minutes showing who the officers are).  

If the group makes a surplus (profit):  
• Because an unincorporated association has no separate legal personality, the officers can be personally liable for its debts and any tax due.  
• Any surplus should be reinvested in the association’s activities.  
• If annual income goes over £1,000 (or at any level if you employ staff or own property) you may wish to register as a charity or another incorporated structure to limit personal liability.  
• If your yearly income exceeds £5,000 and you want charitable status you must register with the Charity Commission for England and Wales.  
• If you trade or provide paid-for services and your surplus after expenses is more than the annual Corporation Tax allowance (currently £50,000 for small non-corporate bodies) you must register with HMRC and file a tax return.  

Keeping the tax position safe:  
1. Keep clear records of all income and spending.  
2. Submit a Corporation Tax return if HMRC asks, or if you know tax is due.  
3. If you pay anyone, operate PAYE.  
4. Claim any available reliefs (for example, charitable exemption if you later register as a charity).  

Useful GOV.UK links  
• Setting up a business or community group: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business/setting-up  
• Corporation Tax for clubs and unincorporated associations: https://www.gov.uk/corporation-tax/non-resident-company-club-or-association  
• Charity registration requirements: https://www.gov.uk/running-charity/setting-up

prompt: I'm a tenant in Scotland and I suspect there's asbestos in the building I rent. Who is responsible for managing it?
generated_response:
In most rented homes in Scotland the responsibility for finding out whether asbestos is present and making sure it is safe rests with the landlord (or their letting agent).  

What you should do  
1. Tell your landlord or letting agent in writing why you think asbestos may be present (for example, crumbling insulation boards or old ceiling tiles).  
2. Ask them to confirm, in writing, what checks have been carried out and what they will do next.  
   • For common areas (stairwells, bin stores, lofts) they must, by law, have an asbestos survey and a management plan.  
   • Inside your flat they must meet the Repairing Standard, which means the property must be safe to live in. If asbestos-containing materials are damaged or likely to be disturbed, they must be repaired, sealed or removed by licensed contractors.  
3. Keep copies of all correspondence.  
4. If the landlord does not act within a reasonable time, contact your local council’s Environmental Health team for an inspection.  
5. As a last resort you can apply to the First-tier Tribunal for Scotland (Housing and Property Chamber) to enforce the Repairing Standard.  

Emergency situations  
• If asbestos-containing material is suddenly damaged (for example during DIY), leave the area, close the door, and call the landlord immediately.  
• Do not try to remove or dispose of asbestos yourself.  

Further information  
GOV.UK – Business and self-employed: Health and safety at work  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: How can I find out if a company has been wound up or is in the process of being liquidated?
generated_response:
To see whether a company has been wound up or is already in liquidation, follow these steps.  
1. Go to the Companies House “Find and update company information” service (free to use).  
   • Type the company’s exact name or registration number.  
   • The company’s page will show its current “Company status”. If you see “Liquidation”, “In Administration”, “Voluntary Arrangement” or “Dissolved”, the business is being wound up or has already closed.  
2. Check the Insolvency Service’s “Individual and company insolvency register”.  
   • Search the company name. This confirms if an official insolvency practitioner has been appointed.  
3. Look at notices in The Gazette (the UK’s official public record).  
   • Choose “All notices” and enter the company name.  
   • Winding-up petitions, liquidation appointments and dissolution notices must be published here.  
4. Still unsure?  
   • Phone Companies House on 0303 123 4500 (Monday–Friday). Ask an adviser to read out the current status.  
   • If you prefer Welsh, select the Welsh-language option when the call connects.  
5. Keep a copy of anything you find (print-out or note). You may need it for proof in claims or disputes.  

Relevant GOV.UK starting page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I recently sold some custom-made curtains online and a customer is returning them saying they are not as described. What should I do to handle this situation according to the law?
generated_response:
If you sell to a consumer in England you must follow the Consumer Rights Act 2015.  
When an item is “custom-made” the usual 14-day change-of-mind rule does not apply, but the buyer still has full rights if the goods are faulty or not as described.

What to do

1. Ask the buyer to explain exactly why the curtains are “not as described” and to send photos or other evidence.  
2. Compare their evidence with the wording and pictures in your listing, invoice and any messages.  
3. Decide:  
   a. If the curtains do differ from the description (size, colour, fabric, finish, etc.) they are “mis-described”.  
   b. If they match the description, you may reject the return but be prepared to justify this if the buyer escalates.  
4. Where they are mis-described:  
   • Offer the buyer a full refund within 14 days of receiving the returned curtains.  
   • Refund the original standard delivery charge.  
   • Arrange and pay for the return postage (or refund the buyer’s cost if they send them back).  
5. Keep written records of all messages, photographs, and the refund or refusal.  
6. If you and the buyer cannot agree, follow the dispute procedure of the selling platform or suggest free Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR).  
7. Review your listings to make sure future descriptions are clear and accurate.

Key GOV.UK guidance  
Accepting returns and giving refunds (for online sellers): https://www.gov.uk/accepting-returns-and-giving-refunds

prompt: I need to check if another business's VAT number is valid. How do I go about doing this and what information will I get once I've checked it?
generated_response:
You can check a UK-issued VAT number online and receive basic confirmation details.

1. Go to HMRC’s “Check a UK VAT number” service: https://www.gov.uk/check-uk-vat-number  
2. Enter the VAT number exactly as given (it will be 9 digits, or 12 digits for branches).  
3. Press ‘Check’.  
4. The service will tell you:  
   • whether the number is currently valid  
   • the date HMRC last confirmed its validity  
   • the registered business name and (if held) its principal place of business address  
5. Save or print the on-screen result for your records—HMRC accepts this as evidence that you took reasonable steps to verify the VAT number.  
6. If the service is unavailable, phone the VAT helpline (0300 200 3700, Monday–Friday) with the number and the business name you’ve been given.  
7. Checking a non-UK EU VAT number? Use the EU-wide VIES tool instead: https://ec.europa.eu/taxation_customs/vies/

What you will not see  
• No financial, turnover or contact information beyond the registered name and address.  
• Historic validity dates—only the current status and last checked date are shown.

prompt: I run a small business in Northern Ireland and I'm exporting goods to the EU. How do I know if I need to register for Intrastat and what are the thresholds for registration?
generated_response:
Intrastat is the system HMRC uses to collect statistics on the movement of goods between Northern Ireland (NI) and EU member states.  
You must register and submit Intrastat declarations if, in any calendar year (1 January – 31 December), either of the following applies to your NI-to-EU trade in goods:  
1. You dispatch (export) goods from NI to the EU worth more than £500,000.  
2. You bring in (import) goods from the EU to NI worth more than £24 million.  

Because you only export, the figure to watch is £500,000.

What to do if you reach or expect to reach the £500,000 dispatch threshold:  
1. Get an EORI number that starts with “XI” if you do not already have one (this is separate from a “GB” EORI).  
2. Email intrastatenquiries@hmrc.gov.uk or phone the HMRC Intrastat team on 03000 594 231 to tell them you have reached the threshold.  
3. HMRC will issue you an 8-digit Intrastat trader ID and details of how to file.  
4. Submit a monthly Intrastat return by the 21st of the month after the goods were dispatched (e.g. January movements by 21 February).  
5. Continue to submit returns for every month of the calendar year, even if your trade later falls below £500,000.  
6. Keep records of the value, commodity code, weight or units, and EU destination for each consignment for at least six years.

If your annual NI-to-EU dispatch value stays below £500,000, you do not need to register or submit Intrastat returns, but you must still complete export customs declarations as usual.

For detailed guidance and forms, start at:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I want to know if I can use trade mark decisions to decide whether to object to a trade mark. What steps should I take to evaluate the relevance of these decisions to my situation?
generated_response:
You can use earlier UK Intellectual Property Office (IPO) trade mark decisions as evidence when deciding whether to oppose a new application, but you need to check how closely those decisions match your own facts. Follow these steps:

1. Find the decisions  
   • Go to GOV.UK → Business → Intellectual property → “Trade marks” and open the “Trade mark decisions” collection.  
   • Download or read the full decision notices that look relevant (they are dated and numbered).

2. Filter for similar facts  
   • Look first at cases in the same Nice classes of goods or services as your mark.  
   • Short-list decisions where the earlier and later marks were visually, phonetically or conceptually similar to yours.

3. Read the grounds and evidence relied on  
   • Note which legal grounds succeeded or failed (e.g. likelihood of confusion under s5(2), passing off under s5(4), bad faith under s3(6)).  
   • Check what evidence the successful party provided (use in the market, surveys, sales figures, etc.).

4. Compare the IPO’s reasoning with your situation  
   • Mark similarity: are the words, logos or overall impressions alike in the same way?  
   • Goods/services: are they identical, similar or complementary to yours?  
   • Consumer perception: are the purchasing channels, prices and buyer attention levels similar?  
   • Distinctiveness: does your mark have a reputation or enhanced distinctiveness like the winning side in the decision?

5. Assess procedural points  
   • Time limits: an opposition must normally be filed within 2 months of publication (extendable to 3 months).  
   • Cost/complexity: check whether the earlier case involved hearings, cross-examination or written submissions only.

6. Decide your next step  
   • If the facts and reasoning line up closely with your case, the decision can strengthen an opposition or a letter before action.  
   • If in doubt, or if the match is weak, take professional advice from a trade mark attorney before filing form TM7.

Relevant GOV.UK starting point:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I want to start a business partnership with my friend. What steps do I need to follow to set up the partnership and register it with HMRC?
generated_response:
Below is the usual way to set up an ordinary business partnership and register it with HMRC.

1. Agree the basics  
   a. Decide what you will do, your start-date and the trading address.  
   b. Choose a business name (check it is not the same as an existing trade mark or company on GOV.UK “Companies House name availability”).  
   c. Write a partnership agreement – it is not a legal requirement but strongly advised. It should cover profit-sharing, decision-making, what happens if a partner leaves, etc. A solicitor can draw this up or you can adapt a template.

2. Choose the ‘nominated partner’  
   One partner must be named as the person who deals with HMRC. They submit the yearly Partnership Tax Return and keep the business records.

3. Register the partnership with HMRC  
   • The nominated partner registers the partnership for Self Assessment. Do this online or by post using form SA400.  
   • Deadline: by 5 October in the second tax year after you start trading (e.g. start April 2024 → register by 5 October 2025).  
   • HMRC will issue a Unique Taxpayer Reference (UTR) for the partnership.

4. Register each partner as self-employed  
   • Every partner registers separately for Self Assessment (online form or form SA401) using their own National Insurance number.  
   • HMRC will send each partner their own UTR.

5. Handle any extra registrations  
   • VAT – register if your UK turnover will exceed the VAT threshold (£85,000 in 2024/25) or if you choose to register voluntarily.  
   • PAYE – register as an employer with HMRC if you will take on employees (including paying yourselves wages through payroll).  
   • Licences/permits – check with your local council in Scotland if your trade (for example, food, taxi, child-care) needs licensing.

6. Open a business bank account  
   Keeps partnership and personal finances separate, making bookkeeping and tax returns simpler.

7. Keep records and meet yearly obligations  
   • Keep invoices, receipts, bank statements and mileage logs for at least 5 years after the 31 January submission deadline.  
   • File the annual Partnership Tax Return (SA800) by:  
     – 31 October (paper) or 31 January (online) after the end of each tax year.  
   • Each partner files their own Self Assessment return and pays:  
     – Income Tax on their share of the profit.  
     – Class 2 and Class 4 National Insurance (if applicable).  
   • Pay any VAT and PAYE on time if registered.

Optional – Limited Liability Partnership (LLP)  
If you prefer the partners to have limited liability, register an LLP with Companies House first, then follow HMRC steps above. An LLP must also file annual accounts and confirmation statements with Companies House.

Key GOV.UK pages  
Set up a business partnership: https://www.gov.uk/set-up-business-partnership  
Register for Self Assessment: https://www.gov.uk/register-for-self-assessment  
Partnership Tax Return (SA800): https://www.gov.uk/partnership-tax-return  
VAT registration: https://www.gov.uk/vat-registration

If anything is unclear, or your circumstances are unusual, call the HMRC Self-employed helpline on 0300 200 3310.

prompt: I'm a small business owner in Wales and I want to end my commercial lease early. What steps should I take to ensure I'm following the correct procedure?
generated_response:
To end a commercial lease early you usually need either a break clause in the lease or the landlord’s consent. Take these steps:

1. Check your lease carefully  
   • Look for a break clause (sometimes called a ‘tenant’s break option’).  
   • Note any conditions you must meet – for example, giving 3-6 months’ written notice, being up to date with rent and leaving the premises clear of goods.

2. Put your notice in writing  
   • Follow the wording and method set out in the lease (for example, recorded-delivery post to the landlord’s registered address).  
   • Keep copies of the notice and proof of delivery.

3. If there is no break clause – negotiate a ‘surrender’  
   • Contact the landlord to ask if they will accept the keys back early.  
   • Be ready to pay a premium or cover marketing costs and any rent until a new tenant is found.

4. Consider assigning or sub-letting  
   • Many leases let you pass the lease on (assign) or sub-let if the landlord agrees.  
   • You stay liable if the new tenant fails to pay unless the landlord releases you.

5. Settle end-of-lease obligations  
   • Pay any rent, service charges or business rates up to the surrender date.  
   • Arrange a joint inspection to agree the condition of the premises and any dilapidations (repairs you must pay for).

6. Get professional advice early  
   • A solicitor or chartered surveyor can confirm your rights and draft any surrender or assignment documents.  
   • Legal costs are usually lower if you act before you miss a notice deadline.

7. Update official bodies  
   • Tell HMRC and your local council when you stop occupying the premises so business rates and VAT records are corrected.

Further guidance: https://www.gov.uk/renting-business-property-tenant-responsibilities

prompt: What happens if I miss the deadline for filing my confirmation statement?
generated_response:
If you miss the deadline you must still file the confirmation statement as soon as possible.  

1. File the overdue statement straight away – use the Companies House online service or post form CS01.  
2. Pay the normal filing fee (£13 online or £40 by post). There is no extra late-filing fee, but delays have other consequences.  
3. Companies House will send warning letters. If the statement is not filed:  
   • The company and each director/secretary commit a criminal offence.  
   • Companies House can start prosecution – fines can reach up to £5,000 for each offence and continue at £500 a day until the statement is filed.  
   • The company can be struck off the register and dissolved, which may lead to bank accounts being frozen and assets becoming Crown property.  
4. If you have already been sent a final notice, contact Companies House immediately to explain the delay and confirm when you will file.  
5. Once filed, keep future deadlines in a diary or set up email reminders in your Companies House online account to avoid repeat problems.

Most companies solve the issue simply by filing late; however, the longer the delay, the higher the risk of fines and strike-off.

Find the filing service and guidance at: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I need to get a copy of a patent document to prove ownership for a court case. How do I request a certified copy and what is the process?
generated_response:
If you need an official, court-ready copy of a UK patent, you must order a “certified office copy” from the Intellectual Property Office (IPO).  
  
1. Find the patent number  
   • It is the seven-digit “GB” number on the patent certificate or on the IPO’s “Find a patent” search service.  
  
2. Fill in Patents Form 23 – “Request for certified office copies”  
   • Download it from GOV.UK or ask the IPO (0300 300 2000) to post or email it to you.  
   • Tick the document(s) you need – e.g. the full specification, certificate of grant, priority documents.  
  
3. Pay the fee  
   • £20 for the first document, £5 for each additional document ordered at the same time.  
   • Pay by debit/credit card (enter details on the form or ask the IPO to phone you), IPO deposit account or cheque payable to “Intellectual Property Office”.  
  
4. Send the form to the IPO  
   • Email: scan and email the signed form to information@ipo.gov.uk (the IPO will ring you for card payment).  
   • Post: Intellectual Property Office, Concept House, Cardiff Road, Newport, NP10 8QQ.  
  
5. Receive the certified copy  
   • The IPO usually issues it within 2–5 working days after payment.  
   • It arrives by recorded delivery bearing the IPO stamp and signature. Keep it sealed until you hand it to the court.  
  
6. Need help or an urgent turnaround?  
   • Call the IPO helpline on 0300 300 2000 (Mon–Fri, 9 am–5 pm); they can talk you through the form and, in pressing cases, flag the request as urgent.  
  
You can print free, uncertified versions from “Find a patent” for background reading, but only the stamped certified copy is accepted as legal proof of ownership.  
  
Relevant GOV.UK starting page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I want to complain about my insolvency practitioner, but I'm not sure where to start. What steps should I take to make sure my complaint is handled properly?
generated_response:
How to complain about an insolvency practitioner (IP) in England

1. Try to sort it out with the IP first  
   • Write to them, explain what you think they have done wrong and how you want it put right.  
   • Keep copies of every letter / email and note the dates you send them.  

2. Collect your evidence  
   • Case reference numbers, dates, and any letters, emails or reports from the IP.  
   • A clear timeline of what happened and why you are unhappy.  

3. If you are still unhappy, use the Insolvency Service Complaints Gateway  
   • This is the single route for complaints about IPs.  
   • You can submit the form online, by email or by post.  
     Online form: https://www.gov.uk/complain-about-insolvency-practitioner  
     Email: ip.complaints@insolvency.gov.uk  
     Post: IP Complaints, Insolvency Service, PO Box 10360, Nottingham NG2 9QR  
   • If you need help filling in the form, call 0300 678 0015 (Mon–Fri, 9 am–5 pm).  

4. What happens next  
   • The Insolvency Service checks the complaint and passes it to the IP’s authorising body (a Recognised Professional Body).  
   • That body investigates and will write to you with its decision.  
   • You may be contacted for more information.  

5. If you believe there is fraud or criminal activity  
   • Phone the Insolvency Service Enforcement Hotline on 0300 678 0017 or email Intelligence.Live@insolvency.gov.uk.  
   • You can also report the matter to the police.  

6. Keep records throughout  
   • Note dates, times and who you speak to.  
   • Keep copies of everything you send or receive.

prompt: How can I find out if a company has changed its name or address recently?
generated_response:
You can check recent name or address changes for any limited company or LLP by using the free online register kept by Companies House.

Follow these steps:  
1. Go to the GOV .UK business section and select “Companies House” (or search online for “Companies House service”).  
2. Enter the company’s name or registration number.  
3. On the company’s profile page:  
   • Look under “Registered office address” – the current address is shown here.  
   • Click “Filing history” – any changes to the company name or registered office are listed (for example ‘Change of registered office address’ or ‘Change of name certificate’).  
4. To see the full details, open the relevant document – it’s usually a scanned PDF you can view for free.  
5. If you want to keep track of future changes, use the “Follow this company” link on the profile page to get free email alerts whenever new filings are made.

For most companies, updates appear on the register within 24 hours of being accepted by Companies House.

Useful link  
GOV .UK business section: https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: I want to close my limited company in Scotland. What steps do I need to follow to strike it off the Companies Register?
generated_response:
Closing (striking off) a Scottish limited company that is no longer needed is straightforward if the business is solvent and has stopped trading.  

1. Check you are eligible  
   • The company must not have traded, sold assets, or changed its name in the last 3 months.  
   • It must not be threatened with liquidation or have an agreement with creditors (for example, a CVA).  

2. Put the company’s affairs in order  
   • Pay any outstanding wages, suppliers, tax, VAT and National Insurance.  
   • Submit final accounts and a Company Tax Return, telling HMRC they are the last.  
   • Cancel VAT registration, PAYE scheme and close the company bank account.  
   • Distribute any remaining assets or cash to shareholders (this may count as income or capital gains).  

3. Complete form DS01 – ‘Striking off application’  
   • Download the form or file online at Companies House.  
   • A majority of directors must sign.  
   • Pay the £10 fee (cheque or card if filing online).  

4. Tell anyone who might be affected  
   • Within 7 days of sending DS01 you must give a copy to employees, shareholders, creditors, landlords, banks, pension managers or trustees, and HMRC.  

5. Companies House and The Gazette notices  
   • Companies House will place a notice in the Edinburgh Gazette.  
   • If no objections are received within 2 months, a second notice is published and the company is dissolved.  

6. After dissolution  
   • Keep business documents for at least 7 years.  
   • Any assets still in the company when it is dissolved become property of the Crown, so make sure everything is distributed first.  

Relevant GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/strike-off-your-company-from-companies-register

prompt: I'm a nurse in Wales and I need to complain about an insolvency practitioner. How do I find out which professional body they belong to and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
If you are unhappy with an insolvency practitioner (IP), you must complain through the official “complaints gateway” run by the Insolvency Service.  
Follow these steps.

1. Gather basic information  
   • the IP’s full name and, if possible, their licence number  
   • the firm’s name and address  
   • details of the case (reference numbers, dates, what went wrong)  

2. Check which professional body licenses the IP  
   a) Look at any letters or emails the IP has sent you – by law they must show their licensing body and licence number.  
   b) If it is not clear, use the Insolvency Service’s free online search:  
      www.gov.uk/complain-about-insolvency-practitioner – choose “Search for an insolvency practitioner”.  
   c) If you cannot get online, phone the Insolvency Service on 0300 678 0015 (Mon–Fri, office hours). Give the IP’s name and they will tell you the licensing body.

3. Complain to the practitioner first  
   Write to them, explain the problem and give them a reasonable time (about 14 days) to put things right. Keep copies.

4. Escalate the complaint  
   If you are still unhappy, submit the complaint to the Insolvency Service complaints gateway. You can:  
   • complete the online form on the same GOV.UK page above, or  
   • email regulation@insolvency.gov.uk, or  
   • post the form to: Insolvency Service Complaints Gateway, IP Regulation Section, 4 Abbey Orchard Street, London SW1P 2HT.  

   The Insolvency Service will check the complaint and pass it to the correct professional body (for example ICAEW, ACCA, IPA, ICAS). The body must investigate and let you know the outcome.

5. Keep records  
   Save copies of all letters, emails and notes of any phone calls. These may be needed during the investigation.

Most complaints are resolved this way. If you need help completing the form, ask the Insolvency Service helpline or a trusted friend to assist.

Relevant GOV.UK link  
Complaints gateway and search tool: www.gov.uk/complain-about-insolvency-practitioner

prompt: I want to copy a photograph for a school project. How do I check if I need a licence and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Copying a photograph is usually covered by UK copyright law. Follow these steps to check whether you need a licence and, if you do, how to get one.

1. Find out who took the photo and when  
   • Copyright in a photo normally lasts for 70 years after the photographer’s death.  
   • If the photographer died more than 70 years ago, the image is in the public domain and you can copy it without a licence (but it’s still good practice to credit the source).

2. Look for any licence information where you found the image  
   • Many websites show a licence notice such as “Creative Commons” or “All rights reserved”.  
   • If it is already marked with a licence that allows educational use (for example, “CC-BY” or “CC-BY-NC”), follow the licence conditions (usually crediting the creator) and you will not need extra permission.

3. Rely on the “illustration for instruction” exception if the photo is still in copyright  
   • UK copyright law lets students copy limited amounts of a work for a non-commercial school project as long as:  
     – the use is genuinely for study, research or classwork, and  
     – you give a clear acknowledgement of the photographer and source.  
   • This exception covers handing the project in to your teacher or showing it in class, but not wider publication (for example, posting it online for the public).

4. If the photo is still in copyright and none of the above applies  
   • Contact the copyright owner (often the photographer, a picture agency or the website) and ask for permission or a licence.  
   • Explain exactly how you will use the image (for a school project, not for sale) and keep any written permission they give you.

5. Keep a note of what you decided  
   • Record where you found the photo, the licence (or permission) and the credit line you used. This will help if anyone later asks about your right to use the image.

Useful links  
Copyright overview and exceptions for education: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/exceptions-to-copyright

prompt: I'm a teacher in Wales and I need to know how to apply for a Start Up Loan for my business. What steps should I follow?
generated_response:
To apply for a government-backed Start Up Loan, follow these steps.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must be 18+ and starting a new business (or trading less than 36 months).  
   • You must live in the UK and have the right to work here.  
   • The business must be based in the UK.

2. Prepare the paperwork  
   • A simple business plan (the scheme provides free templates).  
   • A cash-flow forecast showing how you’ll use and repay the loan.  
   • Proof of identity and address.

3. Register your interest online  
   • Go to GOV.UK → Start Up Loans → “Apply”.  
   • Fill in the short eligibility check and create an account.

4. Work with a delivery partner  
   • You’ll be matched with a local partner (for Wales this is often Business in Focus, DSL Finance or another approved body).  
   • They’ll give free support to refine your plan and forecast.

5. Submit the full application  
   • Upload your plan, forecast and ID documents through the secure portal.  
   • State the amount you want to borrow (between £500 and £25,000).

6. Attend a short assessment call  
   • The partner will discuss your plan, affordability and repayment terms.

7. Receive the decision  
   • If approved, you’ll sign a loan agreement.  
   • Money is normally paid into your bank within a few days.

8. Use the after-care service  
   • You get up to 12 months of free mentoring and access to templates, webinars and discounts.

Key points  
• The loan is unsecured, fixed at 6% interest, repayable over 1–5 years.  
• You can apply alongside up to three other business partners (maximum £100k combined).  
• Applying is free and will not affect your credit score until you submit the full application.

Helpful link  
Start Up Loan scheme – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/start-up-loans

If you need help with forms or the online portal, phone the Start Up Loans Company on 0344 264 2600 or ask your local Business Wales centre for one-to-one support.

prompt: I need to request CCTV footage from a shop where I was present, but I'm not sure how to proceed. What steps should I follow to ensure my request is handled correctly?
generated_response:
To get a copy of CCTV footage that shows you, make a Subject Access Request (SAR) to the shop – this is your legal right under the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018.

1. Find the data-controller details  
   • Look on the shop’s website or ask staff for the “Data Protection Officer” or the person who handles data protection requests.  
2. Write your request  
   • State that you are making a Subject Access Request for CCTV images that identify you.  
   • Give the date, approximate time, location in the store and a brief description of what you were wearing or doing to help them find the footage.  
   • Ask for the footage in a viewable format (for example, a password-protected USB file or secure download).  
3. Prove your identity  
   • Attach a clear copy of photo ID (passport, driving licence or bus pass).  
   • Explain the easiest way for them to contact you (email, post or phone).  
4. Keep a record  
   • Send the request by email or recorded post and keep a copy. Note the date you send it.  
5. Wait for their reply  
   • They must respond within one month. They can extend by up to two further months if the request is complex, but they must tell you why.  
   • They cannot charge a fee unless the request is ‘manifestly unfounded or excessive’.  
6. What they can provide  
   • They should give you any footage where you can be recognised.  
   • They may blur or withhold images of other people to protect their privacy.  
7. If you do not get a satisfactory response  
   • Write a reminder, quoting the original date.  
   • If there is still no response or you think the shop is wrongly refusing your request, complain to the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO): ico.org.uk/make-a-complaint.  

Useful GOV.UK link  
Get a copy of information held about you (Subject Access Request): https://www.gov.uk/request-your-personal-data

prompt: I received a fine from a magistrates' court and am having trouble paying it. What steps should I take to get my fine reviewed?
generated_response:
If you can no longer afford the amount or instalments set by the magistrates’ court, you can ask for the fine to be reviewed.  
Follow these steps as soon as possible:

1. Find your fine details  
   • Check the notice or any letters from His Majesty’s Courts and Tribunals Service (HMCTS) for the reference number and the name of the magistrates’ court that imposed the fine.

2. Contact the Fines Office (HMCTS)  
   • Phone the HMCTS Fines Enquiry Centre on 0300 790 9901 (Monday to Friday, 9am–5pm).  
   • Tell them you want a “variation of fine” because your circumstances have changed.  
   • Have your fine reference, National Insurance number and recent income/outgoings to hand.

3. Ask for a Variation Order  
   • This lets the court lower the weekly/monthly instalment or give you more time to pay.  
   • The Fines Officer may be able to agree new terms over the phone, or they may list your case for a short hearing (called a “means hearing”).  
   • If a hearing is set, the court will send you a form to list your income, benefits, rent, bills and any dependants—fill it in accurately and attach evidence (payslips, bank statements, benefit letters).

4. Keep making affordable payments while your request is processed  
   • Paying even a small amount helps show you are not ignoring the fine and can prevent extra enforcement fees.

5. Attend any court hearing, if required  
   • Take proof of your finances and explain why you need the instalments reduced or more time.  
   • The magistrates can confirm, change or cancel the earlier payment terms. They will not increase the fine because you asked for a review.

6. If enforcement action has already started  
   • Contact the Fines Enquiry Centre immediately and explain the situation—acting early can pause or prevent enforcement measures such as deductions from earnings or bailiff action.

Further help  
Citizens Advice (0800 702 2020) can help you prepare the income/outgoings form and speak to the court.  
More information: https://www.gov.uk/pay-court-fine

prompt: I received a jury summons and need to respond. What are the steps to reply and what happens if I don't respond?
generated_response:
Here is what to do when you get a jury summons in Scotland.

1. Read the citation letter carefully  
   • It tells you which court, the start date of the trial-period, and the deadline (usually 7 days) for replying.

2. Complete the reply slip (or reply online if the letter gives a web link)  
   • Tick the box that says you can attend, or explain why you need to be excused or have the service deferred.  
   • Give any supporting evidence (for example medical certificate, exam timetable, pre-booked holiday).

3. Send your reply before the deadline  
   • Post the reply slip back in the prepaid envelope provided, or submit the online form.  
   • Keep a copy or photo of what you send.

4. Wait for confirmation  
   • The Scottish Courts and Tribunals Service (SCTS) will write to you if they excuse or defer you.  
   • If you are required to attend, phone the juror call-in number (printed on your citation) after 5 pm on the evening before the first day – it will tell you whether you must come in.

5. If anything changes after you have confirmed  
   • Contact the court office or the juror helpline straight away.

If you do not respond or fail to attend without a valid reason, the court can fine you up to £1,000.

Further help  
SCTS juror information line: 0800 030 0100  
Official guidance: https://www.scotcourts.gov.uk/coming-to-court/jurors

prompt: How do I find out who is entitled to inherit if someone dies without a will and what steps do I need to take to apply for probate?
generated_response:
If someone died in England without leaving a valid will (this is called dying “intestate”), the law sets out both 1) who inherits and 2) who is allowed to apply for the legal document that lets you deal with the estate (called a “grant of letters of administration” – on GOV.UK it is part of the probate service).  

1. Check who is entitled to inherit  
   • Look at the statutory order of relatives:  
     1. Spouse or civil partner (they inherit most or all of the estate, depending on whether there are children).  
     2. Children (or their descendants) share what is left after the spouse’s share, or the whole estate if there is no spouse/civil partner.  
     3. Parents.  
     4. Full-blood siblings (or their descendants).  
     5. Half-blood siblings (or their descendants).  
     6. Grandparents.  
     7. Uncles and aunts of the whole blood (or their descendants), then half-blood.  
     8. If none of the above exist, the estate passes to the Crown.  
   • The person highest on this list usually has the right to apply for probate (letters of administration).  
   • Detailed guidance: www.gov.uk/inherits-someone-dies-without-will  

2. Confirm there really is no will  
   • Search the person’s papers, ask their solicitor or bank, and check with the Principal Registry’s Search service if needed.  
   • If a will later turns up, the intestacy rules no longer apply.

3. Get the information you’ll need  
   • Original death certificate (order extra copies).  
   • Full list of assets and debts so you can value the estate.  
   • Details of any gifts made in the 7 years before death (needed for Inheritance Tax).

4. Work out and report any Inheritance Tax (IHT)  
   • Most estates are below the IHT threshold, but you must still send the relevant IHT form.  
   • Use form IHT205 (simple, low-value estates) or IHT400 (larger/complex estates).  
   • Pay any tax due (or arrange a payment plan) before you apply for probate. Guidance: www.gov.uk/inheritance-tax

5. Apply for the grant of letters of administration  
   Two ways:  
   a) Online (simpler estates) – complete the Probate Service application and upload the IHT receipt/reference.  
   b) By post – fill in form PA1A and send it with:  
      • The death certificate.  
      • The IHT form and HMRC reference.  
      • An official copy of any foreign death certificate translation (if relevant).  
      • The application fee (£273 at present) plus £1.50 per extra sealed copy you want.  
   • Guidance and forms: www.gov.uk/applying-for-probate

6. Swear/affirm the oath (if requested)  
   • For most online applications this is done by statement of truth; some postal applications still require a short appointment with a probate officer or commissioner for oaths.

7. Receive the grant and administer the estate  
   • Use the sealed copies to collect money from banks, sell property, pay debts and expenses.  
   • Distribute the remaining estate strictly according to the intestacy rules.  
   • Keep full records and estate accounts in case beneficiaries or HMRC ask for them.

Typical timescale: 4–8 weeks to get the grant once your application is accepted (longer if the estate is taxable or documents are missing).

Key GOV.UK links  
• Who inherits if someone dies without a will: www.gov.uk/inherits-someone-dies-without-will  
• Apply for probate (letters of administration): www.gov.uk/applying-for-probate  
• General wills, probate and inheritance guidance: www.gov.uk/wills-probate-inheritance  

If you’re unsure at any stage, consider speaking to a probate solicitor or calling the HM Courts & Tribunals Service Probate helpline (0300 303 0648).

prompt: I need to pay a court fee for a civil case in England. How do I know how much I need to pay and where to send the payment?
generated_response:
To work out the correct fee and how to pay it, follow these steps.

1. Identify the type of civil application  
   • Examples: ‘money claim’, ‘possession’, ‘injunction’, ‘divorce’, ‘probate’, an appeal, etc.  
   • The fee depends on the application type and (for money claims) the value of the claim.

2. Find the current fee  
   • Open the Court and tribunal fees guide (form EX50). It lists every civil fee and is updated whenever fees change.  
   • You can also use the online civil-court fee calculator or phone the Court and Tribunal Service Centre (0300 123 7050) and ask for the fee for your form.

3. Check whether you can pay online  
   • If you are filing the claim or application online (for example, through Money Claim Online or Possession Claim Online), the service will show the fee and take a card payment as part of the process.  
   • If you are filing a paper form or the court has asked you to pay a hearing or allocation fee, move to step 4.

4. Pay the fee  
   a. By phone – call the court or the Court and Tribunal Service Centre and pay by debit/credit card.  
   b. By post – send a cheque or postal order (payable to ‘HM Courts and Tribunals Service’) with the case number on the back. Post it to the court that is handling the claim.  
   c. In person – most Civil and Family court counters take card payments; some still accept cash or cheque. Phone first to check opening hours.

5. Keep proof of payment  
   • Ask for a receipt or keep the card-payment confirmation.  
   • If you post a cheque, keep a copy and use recorded delivery.

If you are on a low income or receive certain benefits, you may be able to apply for ‘Help with Fees’ (form EX160) before you pay.

Key links:  
• Court fees guide (EX50) – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice  
• Help with fees: https://www.gov.uk/get-help-with-court-fees

prompt: Can I bring someone with me to court to help me, and what are their limitations?
generated_response:
Yes.  If you are representing yourself (sometimes called a ‘litigant in person’) you may take a helper-often called a ‘McKenzie friend’-or any trusted adult for moral support.  

What they CAN do  
1. Sit with you in court.  
2. Quietly give you advice during the hearing.  
3. Help you organise papers and take notes.  
4. Remind you of points you want to raise.  

What they CANNOT do (unless the judge gives specific permission)  
• Speak to the judge, witnesses or the other side on your behalf.  
• Question witnesses or make legal submissions.  
• Sign documents for you or act as your legal representative.  
• Disrupt proceedings or give evidence themselves at the same hearing (if they are also a witness they must wait outside until called).  

Practical steps  
1. Choose someone reliable (friend, family member, charity worker, or a professional McKenzie friend).  
2. Tell them they must stay quiet and follow the judge’s directions.  
3. Take two copies of the short written note for the usher/judge stating:  
   • the helper’s name, relationship to you, and that you ask the court’s permission for them to assist you as a McKenzie friend.  
4. Arrive early and give the note to the usher; the judge normally agrees unless there is a clear reason not to.  
5. If the judge limits or refuses their help, accept the ruling; you can still ask for brief adjournments to speak to your helper outside the courtroom.  

Other support  
• If you are a victim or witness, the free Witness Service can provide a trained volunteer to stay with you.  
• If you need legal advice instead of (or as well as) a helper, consider applying for legal aid or paid representation.  

Key GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/represent-yourself-in-court

prompt: I want to suggest a new Community Payback project in my local area. What steps should I follow to submit a nomination and what information do I need to provide?
generated_response:
You can put your idea forward through the ‘Nominate a Community Payback project’ service.

1. Check the project is suitable  
   • It must benefit the local community (for example, improving a park, painting a community hall or clearing litter).  
   • The work must be safe for people serving Community Payback sentences and not replace paid jobs.  
   • The site owner (for example, the council, charity or church) must agree to the work being done.

2. Gather the information you will need  
   • A short description of the work you are proposing and why it would help the community.  
   • The exact location (address or nearest postcode).  
   • Confirmation that the land or building owner supports the idea, plus their contact details.  
   • An estimate of the tools, materials or protective equipment that would be needed (for example, paint, gloves, litter-pickers).  
   • Your own contact details so the Probation Service can reach you (name, phone number and email or postal address).  
   • Any deadlines or special considerations (for example, access restrictions or the need for qualified supervision).

3. Submit the nomination  
   a. Go to GOV.UK and open the online form: https://www.gov.uk/nominate-community-payback-project  
   b. Fill in the questions and attach any supporting documents or photos if you have them.  
   c. Press ‘Submit’. You will receive an acknowledgement on screen and, if you give an email address, a confirmation email.

4. What happens next  
   • A Community Payback coordinator for Wales will review the proposal.  
   • They may contact you or the site owner for more details or to arrange a visit.  
   • If the project is approved it will be scheduled and you will be told when the work is likely to start.

If you cannot use the online form, phone your local Probation Service office and ask for the Community Payback team. They can post a paper form or take the details over the phone.

prompt: I need to understand who can apply for probate and inherit if someone dies without a will. What are the steps to determine who is entitled to the inheritance and how do I apply for probate?
generated_response:
When someone dies without a will (this is called dying “intestate”) two questions need answering:  
1. Who is legally entitled to inherit the estate?  
2. Who is allowed to apply for the legal authority to deal with the estate (known as a ‘grant of letters of administration’, often called probate)?  

A. Work out who inherits  
1. Find out whether the person who died (the deceased) had a surviving spouse or civil partner.  
   • If yes, and there are no children or other direct descendants, the spouse/civil partner inherits the whole estate.  
   • If yes, and there are children, the spouse/civil partner receives:  
     – all personal possessions,  
     – a fixed sum (the “statutory legacy”) of £322,000, and  
     – half of the remaining estate.  
     The other half is shared equally between the children. (If a child has died, their share passes to their own children.)  
2. If there is no surviving spouse/civil partner, the estate passes in this order:  
   a. Children (in equal shares).  
   b. Grandchildren or great-grandchildren (if their parent who would have inherited has died).  
   c. Parents.  
   d. Brothers and sisters (or their children, if a sibling has died).  
   e. Half-brothers and half-sisters (or their children).  
   f. Grandparents.  
   g. Uncles and aunts (or their children).  
   h. Half-uncles and half-aunts (or their children).  
   i. If none of the above relatives exist, the estate passes to the Crown.  

B. Who can apply for probate (letters of administration)  
The right to apply follows the same order as the right to inherit. The person with the highest priority who is willing and able to act usually applies. For example:  
• If there is a surviving spouse/civil partner, they normally apply.  
• If there is no spouse but there are children, any child can apply (they should agree between themselves who applies).  
• Where more than one person has equal right, up to four can apply together.  

C. How to apply for letters of administration  
1. Value the estate: list all assets and debts; work out the net value.  
2. Check if Inheritance Tax is due. Complete the relevant HMRC form (IHT205 or IHT400 series) and send it to HMRC.  
3. Complete the probate application:  
   • Online: use the government’s “Apply for probate” service.  
   • By post: complete form PA1A (“Application for a grant of letters of administration”).  
4. Pay the probate fee (£273 for estates over £5,000; no fee below this).  
5. Send the original death certificate, the application form or online submission reference, the HMRC receipt or confirmation, and any requested documents (such as copies of ID) to the Probate Registry.  
6. Wait for the grant to arrive (currently around 8–16 weeks). Once issued, you can collect assets, settle debts, and distribute the estate according to the intestacy rules.  

Useful GOV.UK pages:  
• Intestacy: who inherits if someone dies without a will – https://www.gov.uk/inherits-someone-dies-without-will  
• Apply for probate (including letters of administration) – https://www.gov.uk/applying-for-probate  
• Inheritance Tax and probate forms – https://www.gov.uk/inheritance-tax

prompt: What should I do if I don't know about my single justice procedure case?
generated_response:
If you think a single justice procedure (SJP) case may exist against you but you have not had a notice or do not know the details, take these steps straight-away:

1. Check recent post and any old addresses  
   • The SJP Notice is sent by 1st-class post. If you have moved, ask the Royal Mail redirection service, your previous landlord, letting agent or current occupants whether any court papers have arrived.  
   • For motoring cases, make sure the address on your driving licence and vehicle log book (V5C) is up to date with DVLA.

2. Contact the prosecuting body  
   • Most SJP cases are brought by a police force, DVSA, TV Licensing, local council or the CPS. Phone or email the organisation you think may have prosecuted (for example the police force that stopped you or the council that issued a penalty) and ask if a charge has been laid under the Single Justice Procedure.  
   • Give your full name, date of birth and any reference number you have (e.g. fixed-penalty or notice number).

3. Speak to HM Courts & Tribunals Service (HMCTS)  
   • Call the Single Justice Service on 0300 123 9252 (Monday–Friday 9 am–5 pm).  
   • Ask whether any SJP case is registered in your name, quoting your personal details.  
   • If a case exists, ask for the case number, the prosecuting body and how to get copies of the papers.

4. Apply for copies of the papers  
   • HMCTS can email or post the SJP notice, evidence bundle and response form to you.  
   • Read them carefully: the notice tells you the charge, deadline for response and how to plead.

5. Respond as soon as you receive the papers  
   • Complete the plea form and return it online or by post before the deadline shown.  
   • If the deadline has already passed, send the form immediately and explain why you could not reply earlier (for example you never received the notice).  
   • If a conviction or fine has already been registered in your absence, ask the magistrates’ court for the case to be reopened under section 142 of the Magistrates’ Courts Act 1980 so you can put your case.

6. Get legal advice if unsure  
   • A solicitor or the local Citizens Advice can explain your options, especially if you are late or want to plead not guilty.  
   • Legal aid may be available depending on the seriousness of the offence and your finances.

Useful GOV.UK pages  
Single justice procedure notices and how to respond: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice  
Pay a court fine or check a case: https://www.gov.uk/justice  
(Select “Criminal courts” then “Fines, bail and record” for contact details.)

prompt: I was recently convicted in the Crown Court and want to appeal my sentence. What steps do I need to take to apply for permission to appeal and what happens if I get it?
generated_response:
How to ask for permission to appeal your Crown Court sentence

1. Act quickly  
   • You normally have 28 days from the date you were sentenced to start the process.  
   • If you miss the deadline you must explain why and ask the Court of Appeal for extra time – this is only granted in limited circumstances.

2. Get legal advice  
   • Speak to the solicitor or barrister who acted for you at trial, or another criminal appeal specialist.  
   • Legal aid is often available; your lawyer will tell you how to apply.

3. Fill in and lodge the notice and grounds of appeal (Form NG)  
   • Say which part of the sentence you think is wrong and the reasons (the “grounds”).  
   • Send the form to the Crown Court office where you were sentenced – they pass it to the Criminal Appeal Office.  
   • Include any documents that support your case (for example, sentencing remarks, pre-sentence report).

4. Wait for the “single judge” decision  
   • A Court of Appeal judge looks at the papers and either:  
     a) grants permission (“leave”) to appeal, or  
     b) refuses permission.  
   • You’ll be told the decision in writing.

5. If permission is refused  
   • You can ask for an oral hearing before 2 or 3 judges (“renew the application”).  
   • If they also refuse, that is usually the end of the matter and you may be ordered to pay some prosecution costs and may lose prison-time credit for the days spent on the appeal.

6. If permission is granted  
   • A full appeal hearing is listed.  
   • The Court of Appeal can:  
     – reduce the sentence, or  
     – leave it as it is.  
   • On a defendant’s own appeal the court cannot increase the sentence.  
   • If the appeal succeeds the sentence is changed immediately and any over-payment of fines or time in custody is adjusted.  
   • If the appeal fails you keep the original sentence and may have to pay costs and/or lose time credit.

Important points to remember

• Appealing does not automatically stop your sentence from starting – prison sentences continue to run while you wait, but community orders or fines are not put on hold unless the court orders it.  
• Make sure every letter you receive from the Criminal Appeal Office is answered on time.  
• Keep copies of everything you send.  
• If at any stage you feel unsure, phone the Criminal Appeal Office on 020 7947 6011 (Mon–Fri) or check the guidance on GOV.UK.

Further information  
GOV.UK – Appeal against a sentence or conviction  
https://www.gov.uk/appeal-against-sentence-conviction

prompt: If I see someone working illegally in the UK, what steps should I take to report it and what happens after I do?
generated_response:
If you think someone is working in the UK without the right to do so, you can let Immigration Enforcement know.  
You do not have to give your name and you should never put yourself in danger.

1. Gather the key facts  
   • Where the person is working (address and business name, if any).  
   • The person’s name or a good description.  
   • When you saw them working (dates and times).  
   • Anything else that might help (vehicle registrations, phone numbers, social-media pages).

2. Choose how to report  
   a) Phone Immigration Enforcement on 0300 123 7000 (Monday to Friday, 8 am–8 pm).  
   b) Outside those hours, or if you prefer not to speak to the Home Office, contact Crimestoppers on 0800 555 111 (24-hour, anonymous).  
   c) If you are comfortable online, you can fill in the secure form at gov.uk/report-immigration-crime

3. Give the information  
   • Explain what you have seen and why you believe the person is working illegally.  
   • Say whether you are willing to be contacted. You can stay anonymous, but giving contact details can help investigators if they need clarification.

4. After you report  
   • Immigration Enforcement will assess the information and may open an investigation.  
   • You will not usually be told what action is taken.  
   • If more details are needed and you gave contact details, an officer may get in touch.  
   • Do not investigate the matter yourself. If you see a crime in progress or anyone is in immediate danger, call 999.

What could happen next  
   • Officers may visit the workplace.  
   • If illegal working is confirmed, the employer can be fined or prosecuted and the worker could face immigration action.  
   • Your report remains confidential; the person you reported will not be told who passed the information on.

Relevant GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/report-immigration-crime

prompt: I need to know how to start using an enduring power of attorney for my mother who has recently lost her mental capacity. What steps should I follow?
generated_response:
Here is what you (or the attorney named in the document) need to do to start using an Enduring Power of Attorney (EPA) now that your mother no longer has mental capacity.

1. Check the EPA  
   • Make sure it was signed and witnessed before 1 October 2007 – only then is it a valid EPA.  
   • Confirm who is named as attorney (you or someone else) and whether more than one attorney must act together.

2. Get the EPA registered with the Office of the Public Guardian (OPG)  
   The attorney cannot use the EPA until it is registered.  
   a. Complete form EP1PG (application to register an EPA).  
   b. Notify your mother and every “named person” in the EPA that you intend to register it, using form EP2PG.  
   c. Keep copies of the completed EP2PG forms – you must wait 5 weeks from the date they’re sent before you can send the application to the OPG.

3. Gather what you need to send to the OPG  
   • The original EPA (or a solicitor-certified copy).  
   • The completed EP1PG form.  
   • Copies of all EP2PG notices and proof of posting.  
   • The registration fee of £82 (cheque payable to ‘Office of the Public Guardian’ or pay online – fee exemptions/remissions are available if your mother is on a low income or benefits).  
   • Optional but helpful: a short letter from a GP or other professional confirming your mother lacks capacity.

4. Send the application  
   Office of the Public Guardian  
   PO Box 16185  
   Birmingham B2 2WH  

5. Wait for the registration certificate  
   • The OPG will write to you if they need extra information.  
   • If no one objects, registration usually takes 8–10 weeks.  
   • When you receive the stamped EPA you can start using it to manage your mother’s property and financial affairs.

6. Use the EPA responsibly  
   • Act in your mother’s best interests and keep her money separate from your own.  
   • Keep clear accounts and receipts – the OPG can ask to see them.  
   • The EPA only covers property and finances. For health or welfare decisions you would need a separate Health and Welfare LPA or a decision from the Court of Protection.  
   • If circumstances change (e.g. you no longer want to act) contact the OPG for guidance.

7. If you need help  
   • OPG helpline: 0300 456 0300 (Mon–Fri, 9 am–5 pm)  
   • Citizen’s Advice or a solicitor can guide you through the forms if you prefer.

Most relevant GOV.UK pages:  
Enduring Power of Attorney – register an EPA: https://www.gov.uk/enduring-power-attorney-duties/register-an-enduring-power-of-attorney  
OPG fee remissions: https://www.gov.uk/power-of-attorney/refunds-and-fee-exemptions

prompt: I want to nominate a Community Payback project for my local area, but I'm not sure what information I need to provide. What should I include in my nomination form?
generated_response:
When you fill in the ‘Nominate a Community Payback project’ form you will be asked for the following:  

1. Your contact details  
   • full name  
   • phone number and email address  
   • the organisation you represent (if any)  

2. Location of the proposed project  
   • full address and postcode  
   • brief directions or description of the site (for example “community hall car park behind High Street shops”)  

3. A short description of the work you would like done  
   • type of tasks (for example litter-picking, repainting railings, clearing overgrown paths)  
   • why the work is needed and who will benefit   

4. Confirmation that the work is suitable for Community Payback  
   • outdoors or in a public-facing community space  
   • can be completed with basic tools and unskilled labour  
   • no specialised licences or professional qualifications required  

5. Permission and safety  
   • written permission from the landowner or person responsible for the site  
   • any known hazards (traffic, water, uneven ground, asbestos, etc.)  
   • on-site facilities such as toilets, first-aid equipment or shelter  

6. Practical details  
   • estimated size of the area or hours of work required  
   • dates or time-frames when the work can be carried out  
   • tools or materials available on site and anything the team would need to bring  

7. Community impact  
   • how local residents or groups will benefit  
   • any opportunity for local volunteers to work alongside the Community Payback team  

8. Accessibility  
   • parking or drop-off point for a minibus  
   • distance to the work site from parking  
   • whether there is level access for people using tools or wheelbarrows  

Having this information ready will let you complete the form quickly and helps the Probation Service decide if the project is suitable.  

You can read the full guidance and submit your nomination online here:  
https://www.gov.uk/nominate-community-payback-project

prompt: I need to check if my friend has a lasting power of attorney registered. How do I find out the details and what do I need to do to access it?
generated_response:
You can only find out if a lasting (or enduring) power of attorney (LPA/EPA) is registered by asking the Office of the Public Guardian (OPG) to search its register.

1. Collect the basic details about your friend (the ‘donor’):  
   • full name  
   • address (or last known address)  
   • date of birth  

2. Apply for a register search.  
   • Online: use the GOV.UK service ‘Search the power of attorney register’.  
   • By post: fill in form OPG100 (‘Apply to search the power of attorney register’) and send it to the OPG.  
   • Fee: £25 (cheque, postal order or card online). There is no fee if you qualify for help with fees.

3. Wait for the OPG’s reply.  
   • You’ll normally get a letter within 10 working days.
