   • The reply will confirm whether an LPA or EPA exists and, if it does, the names and contact details of the attorneys.

4. Getting a copy of the document or full details.  
   • If you are named as an attorney, your letter will include an ‘activation key’. You can create an online account and generate a secure access code to view or share the LPA.  
   • If you are not an attorney, you must ask one of the attorneys (or the donor while they have capacity) to show you the LPA or to give you an access code.  
   • If you have serious concerns about the donor’s welfare or the way an attorney is acting, contact the OPG’s safeguarding team.

Contact details  
Office of the Public Guardian  
PO Box 16185, Birmingham B2 2WH  
Telephone: 0300 456 0300 (Monday–Friday 9am-5pm)  

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/power-of-attorney/search-register

prompt: I was involved in a minor criminal case and want to know what kind of punishment I could face if found guilty. What are the possible sentences a magistrates' court can give?
generated_response:
Magistrates’ courts deal with most criminal cases, so they have a set list of sentences they can hand down if you are found guilty. From least to most severe, the main options are:

1. Absolute discharge  
   • No further action – the offence goes on your record but you get no punishment.  

2. Conditional discharge  
   • No immediate punishment, but if you commit another offence within a set period (up to 3 years) you can be re-sentenced for both offences.  

3. Fine  
   • You pay an amount set by the court, up to an unlimited sum for many offences.  
   • You may also have to pay a victim surcharge and prosecution costs.  

4. Compensation order  
   • The court can order you to pay money to anyone who suffered personal injury, loss or damage because of the offence.  

5. Community sentence (Community Order)  
   • You stay out of prison but must complete one or more requirements, for example:  
     – unpaid work (community service)  
     – a curfew or electronic tag  
     – drug or alcohol treatment  
     – rehabilitation activities  
   • The requirements can last up to 3 years.  

6. Suspended sentence  
   • A prison sentence of up to 6 months (or 12 months in total for more than one either-way offence) that is ‘suspended’ for up to 2 years.  
   • You do not go to prison unless you commit another offence or breach the suspension conditions during that time.  

7. Immediate custodial sentence  
   • For summary-only offences the maximum is set by law (often 6 months).  
   • For either-way offences the magistrates’ court can impose up to 12 months in prison in total (or send the case to the Crown Court if they think a longer term is necessary).  

8. Other orders  
   • Driving disqualification, forfeiture (e.g. of seized goods), banning from keeping animals, restraining orders, etc., depending on the offence.  

You can read more about court sentences on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

If you are unsure how any of these might apply to your case, consider talking to a solicitor or the duty solicitor at court.

prompt: I was sectioned under section 3 and have been detained for treatment for 5 months. When can I apply to the Mental Health Tribunal to change my community treatment order?
generated_response:
Answer:
You can only apply to the Mental Health Tribunal after a community treatment order (CTO) has actually been made.  
Once the CTO starts, you have:

1. One right of application in the first 6 months of the CTO.  
2. After that, one application in every 12-month period while the CTO continues.  
3. If you do not apply within those times, the hospital managers must refer your case to the Tribunal at the 6-month point and then every 3 years, so your case will still be reviewed.  

How to apply once the CTO is in place:

1. Ask your care co-ordinator or an independent mental health advocate (IMHA) for Form T132 (Application by a patient on a CTO).  
2. Fill in the form with the help of the IMHA or a solicitor (legal aid is usually available).  
3. Send the completed form to the Mental Health Tribunal office shown on the form.  
4. You will be given a hearing date and can ask for legal representation and for your nearest relative or others to attend.  

If you are in Scotland: the law is different (you would be under a Compulsory Treatment Order, not a CTO). Contact the Mental Health Tribunal for Scotland or an advocacy service for the correct timescales and forms.

More information:  
GOV.UK – Apply to the Mental Health Tribunal  
https://www.gov.uk/mental-health-tribunal

Justification:
• Assumed the user is in England/Wales because Section 3 and CTO are only in the Mental Health Act 1983.  
• The Act gives one application right in the first 6 months of a CTO, then one in each following year; included automatic referrals for completeness.  
• Gave clear, step-by-step instructions and sign-posted to further help and legal aid.  
• Flagged the different Scottish system because the user is in Scotland.  

Missing info: None

prompt: I've used a claims management company and am unhappy with the fees they charged me. How do I complain about their service and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
If you think a claims-management company (CMC) has over-charged you, you can follow these steps.  

1. Check your paperwork  
   • Find the contract, invoice and any emails or letters that show the fee you agreed to pay.  
   • Make sure the company is regulated – look for its Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) firm reference number (or search the FCA register).  

2. Complain to the company first  
   • Write or email the CMC saying why you believe the fee is unfair or wrong.  
   • State what you want (for example a refund or a lower fee) and give any evidence.  
   • Keep copies. The company has up to 8 weeks to reply.  

3. Escalate to the Claims Management Ombudsman (part of the Financial Ombudsman Service)  
   • If the company does not respond within 8 weeks, or you are not happy with its final reply, you can take the complaint to the Ombudsman.  
   • Do this within 6 months of the company’s final response letter.  
   • The service is free. You can complain online, by phone or post.  
   • Contact: 0800 023 4567 or https://www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk  

4. Report serious concerns to the FCA  
   • If you think the CMC is breaking FCA rules (for example misleading clients or charging above the 20 % statutory cap for some claims), report it to the FCA on 0800 111 6768. This will not resolve your individual complaint but helps the regulator act.  

5. Consider court action as a last resort  
   • If you are still out of pocket after the Ombudsman decision, you can use the small claims court (sometimes called ‘money claim’) to recover the fee, but you’ll need to pay a court fee and may need legal advice.  

Other help  
• Citizens Advice (03444 77 20 20) can give free guidance on preparing your complaint.  

Useful GOV.UK pages  
• Make a complaint about a claims management company: https://www.gov.uk/complain-about-claims-company  
• Financial Ombudsman Service: https://www.financial-ombudsman.org.uk

prompt: How long does it typically take to get a decision on a vehicle operator licence application?
generated_response:
Most applicants hear back in about 7 to 9 weeks after the Office of the Traffic Commissioner receives a complete and correct application.  
1. Week 0: You submit the online form, fee, and all supporting evidence.  
2. Within a few days you place the required advert in a local newspaper – this must run for 1 day but then stay open to objections for 21 days.  
3. After the 21-day objection period ends, the Traffic Commissioner’s staff assess your paperwork and finances.  
4. If there are no objections and nothing is missing, they normally issue a decision within the next 2-4 weeks.  
The process can take longer if any documents are missing, an objection is raised, or the Traffic Commissioner calls you to a public inquiry.

Link: https://www.gov.uk/being-a-goods-vehicle-operator/applications

prompt: I was disqualified for 56 days or more. Do I need to apply for a new licence before driving again?
generated_response:
Yes. If you were banned from driving for 56 days or more you must get a new licence before you drive again.  

What to do  
1. Check the end date of your disqualification. You cannot apply until the ban has finished (or as instructed by the court).  
2. Order form D1 (‘Application for a Driving Licence’) from the Post Office or download it from GOV.UK.  
3. Complete the form and send it to DVLA with:  
   • the fee (currently £34 if you apply online, £43 by post)  
   • original identity documents if DVLA does not already have them  
   • a passport-style colour photo if needed (check the form).  
4. If the court said you must take a theory and/or practical test again, book and pass those tests once your provisional licence arrives.  
5. Wait for DVLA to issue your new full licence before you drive.  

If you held vocational (LGV/PCV) or high-risk offender status, extra medical checks may apply. If you are unsure, contact DVLA or the court that sentenced you.

Most relevant link  
Driving disqualifications and reapplying for your licence: https://www.gov.uk/driving-disqualifications

prompt: I want to become an ADR driver but have a criminal record. Can I still qualify as an ADR driver?
generated_response:
Having a criminal record does not automatically stop you from getting an ADR driver training certificate.

1. Hold the right driving licence  
   • Category C or C+E for lorries over 3.5 tonnes (or the class of vehicle you plan to drive).  
   • Your licence must be valid – any driving bans must be over.  

2. Complete an approved ADR training course  
   • Book a course with an approved provider.  
   • Sit the multiple-choice tests set by the Scottish Qualifications Authority (SQA).  

3. Receive your ADR driver training certificate (‘ADR card’)  
   • The training provider sends your results to DVSA; the card is posted to you and is valid for 5 years.  
   • For most dangerous-goods classes there is no routine criminal-record check at this stage.  

4. Security check for certain loads only  
   • If you want to transport explosives (Class 1), radioactive material (Class 7) or other “high-consequence” dangerous goods, you must pass a Home Office security check.  
   • This uses a Disclosure Scotland (or DBS) criminal-record search. Serious, recent or terrorism-related convictions can lead to refusal.  
   • If the security check is refused you can still carry other classes of dangerous goods once you hold the standard ADR card.  

5. Check what appears on your record first  
   • Order a Basic Disclosure from Disclosure Scotland to see what employers and the Home Office will see.  
   • If you are unsure whether a conviction is “spent”, seek advice from Disclosure Scotland or a solicitor.  

6. Employment considerations  
   • Individual haulage firms may set their own vetting standards. It is best to be open with potential employers about any unspent convictions.  

Key links  
• Become qualified to transport dangerous goods: https://www.gov.uk/become-qualified-to-transport-dangerous-goods  
• Securing high-consequence dangerous goods: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/securing-high-consequence-dangerous-goods  
• Basic Disclosure Scotland: https://www.mygov.scot/basic-disclosure

prompt: How do I apply to tax my Heavy Goods Vehicle (HGV)?
generated_response:
You tax an HGV in almost the same way you tax a car, but you must also make sure the vehicle has a valid HGV annual test (MOT) certificate and (if it’s 12 tonnes or over) that the HGV Road User Levy has been paid.

1. Gather what you need  
   • Vehicle log book (V5C) in your name, or the green new-keeper slip (if you have just bought it).  
   • A valid HGV annual test (MOT) certificate.  
   • Current lorry insurance.  
   • Debit/credit card or bank details.  
   • Proof the HGV Road User Levy is paid (for vehicles 12 tonnes+). The online service checks this automatically.

2. Check the tax class and weight band on the V5C  
   The amount of Vehicle Excise Duty (VED) you pay depends on the vehicle’s revenue weight and axle setup. Make sure the details on the log-book match the plating certificate.

3. Pay the HGV Road User Levy (if 12 t or over) before you tax  
   Use the online service at GOV.UK – search “Pay HGV levy”. You’ll get an electronic receipt.

4. Tax the vehicle  
   a) Online (fastest)  
      • Go to gov.uk/vehicle-tax  
      • Enter the 16-digit reference number on your V11 reminder or the 11-digit reference on the V5C.  
      • Follow the prompts, choose to pay for 6 or 12 months, and complete payment.  
   b) At a Post Office branch that deals with HGV tax  
      • Take the documents listed in step 1.  
      • Pay by card, cash or cheque.  
   c) By phone  
      • Call DVLA Vehicle Service on 0300 123 4321 with your reference number and payment details.

5. Keep confirmation  
   You’ll get an email or receipt. You do not get a paper tax disc; enforcement is automatic through ANPR.

6. Display nothing, but keep documents up-to-date  
   Ensure the HGV stays insured, tested and the levy is renewed when due. Driving an untaxed HGV can result in a £1,000 fine and the vehicle being clamped.

Most people complete everything online in under 10 minutes if their test and levy are already recorded.

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/vehicle-tax

prompt: What do I need to do to legally ride an e-scooter in Wales?
generated_response:
In Wales you have only two legal options for riding an e-scooter at the moment:

1. Ride it on private land – for example, a garden or a privately owned car park – but only with the landowner’s permission.  
2. Travel to a rental-trial area in England and use one of the government-approved hire e-scooters there.  
   • You must hold at least a provisional driving licence (category Q is automatically included).  
   • The hire company’s insurance covers you.  
   • A cycle helmet is strongly recommended.

You cannot legally use a privately owned e-scooter on any public road, pavement, cycle path or other public space in Wales. If you do, the police can:

• Seize the scooter  
• Issue a £300 fixed-penalty notice  
• Add 6 points to your driving licence  

Keep an eye on future changes – the UK Government is reviewing the law but no date has been set for Wales yet.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How do I find out if my local council in Scotland offers dropped kerb applications and what the process involves?
generated_response:
To check whether your Scottish council lets you apply for a dropped kerb (sometimes called a “vehicle access” or “vehicular crossing”) and to see what’s involved, follow these steps:

1. Identify your council  
   • If you are not sure which council area you live in, type your postcode into any search engine along with “local council” to confirm the name.

2. Look for the “Roads” or “Planning” section on the council’s website  
   • Search the site for terms such as “dropped kerb”, “vehicle access”, “vehicular crossing” or “driveway access”.  
   • Most councils publish a guidance note explaining when a dropped kerb is allowed, the cost of the application, and who can carry out the work.

3. Read the council’s guidance carefully  
   • Check the eligibility rules (for example: minimum driveway length, sight-lines, nearby junctions, street trees or parking bays).  
   • Note any fees: there is usually a non-refundable application/inspection charge and you will have to pay a council-approved contractor for the work.

4. Make your application  
   • Complete the online form or download a paper form if offered.  
   • You’ll normally need: a simple sketch or plan showing your driveway, photos of the location, and permission from the property owner if that is not you.  
   • Pay the application fee; many councils take card payments online.

5. Site visit and decision  
   • A council roads officer will visit to check safety and drainage issues.  
   • You will receive a written decision (usually within 4–8 weeks). If approved, the letter will list approved contractors and any conditions.

6. Arrange the work  
   • Choose a contractor from the council’s approved list (or get the council to do the work if they offer that service).  
   • Agree a date—contractors must notify the council before starting so that inspections can be arranged.  
   • After completion, the council carries out a final check to sign the work off.

7. If your council does not mention dropped kerbs online  
   • Phone or email the council’s Roads & Transportation (or Planning) department and ask, “How do I apply for a dropped kerb at my property?”  
   • Staff will either send you the application pack or confirm that applications are not currently accepted.

For general driving-related services you can also start from: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I need to know the correct way to display my number plates on my car. What are the rules and what happens if I don't follow them?
generated_response:
To stay legal you must display number plates that meet the requirements set out in The Road Vehicles (Display of Registration Marks) Regulations 2001.

1. Where to fit them  
   • One plate at the front of the vehicle and one at the rear.  
   • They must be fixed so they are clearly visible at all times and lit at night.

2. Colour and reflectivity  
   • Front plate: white background with black characters.  
   • Rear plate: yellow background with black characters.  
   • Both must be reflective with no background patterns or tints.

3. Characters and spacing  
   • Use the standard “Charles Wright” typeface supplied by number-plate suppliers.  
   • Character height: 79 mm; width: 50 mm (except the figure “1”).  
   • Stroke (thickness): 14 mm.  
   • Space between characters: 11 mm; between the two groups: 33 mm.  
   • Margins (top, bottom, sides): at least 11 mm.  
   • No bolts or screws can distort any character.

4. National flags and identifiers (optional)  
   • You may include a Union flag, Cross of St George, Saltire or Red Dragon with the identifier “GB”, “UK”, “ENG”, “SCO”, “CYM” or “WALES”.  
   • Any other wording, images or tinted covers are not allowed.

5. Supplier’s marks  
   • The plate must show:  
     – the supplier’s name and postcode, and  
     – the British Standard number “BS AU 145e”.

6. Electric and zero-emission vehicles (optional)  
   • A green flash on the left side is allowed if the vehicle is fully zero-emission.

7. Penalties for non-compliance  
   • Police can issue a fixed-penalty notice of up to £100 on the spot.  
   • You risk a fine of up to £1,000 and your vehicle will fail its MOT.  
   • Persistent offenders can have the registration withdrawn, making the vehicle unusable on the road.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/displaying-number-plates

prompt: I'm 16 and want to learn to ride a moped. When can I take the theory test for this?
generated_response:
You can book and take the motorcycle theory test for a moped (category AM) as soon as you reach your 16th birthday, provided you already hold a provisional moped licence.  

What to do:  
1. Apply for a provisional driving licence (you can do this from 15 years 9 months).  
2. Once the licence arrives and you turn 16, book your motorcycle theory test online.  
3. Take and pass the theory test.  
4. You can also complete Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) at 16; after CBT you may ride a moped on L-plates while you prepare for the practical test.  

Book or find out more: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I received a penalty charge notice for a traffic rule violation. How do I challenge it and what are the steps involved?
generated_response:
Here is the usual route for challenging a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN) issued in Scotland for parking, bus-lane or other local-authority traffic offences.  
If your notice was issued by Police Scotland (a Fixed Penalty Notice) the procedure is different – see step 7.

1. Check the notice straight away  
   • Who issued it (name of council or Police Scotland)  
   • The contravention code, date, time and location  
   • The deadline for paying at the 50 % discount rate and the deadline for making a challenge  

2. Gather evidence  
   Take or keep anything that supports your case – photos of signs or road markings, receipts, witness details, a disabled-badge scan, vehicle-breakdown report, etc.

3. Make an informal challenge (only if the PCN was fixed to the windscreen or handed to you)  
   • Write to the council within 14 days of the notice date  
   • Quote the PCN number and your vehicle registration  
   • State clearly why the PCN should be cancelled and attach your evidence  
   • Keep a copy and proof of posting/sending (e-mail receipt or recorded delivery)

4. Wait for the council’s reply  
   • If they cancel the PCN, the case ends  
   • If they reject it, they will either allow the discount period to restart or send a Notice to Owner (NtO) to the registered keeper

5. Make a formal representation (after you receive the NtO or if the PCN was sent by post)  
   • You have 28 days from the service date on the NtO  
   • Use the council’s form or write your own letter with the same information and evidence  
   • The council must reply within 56 days

6. Appeal to the Parking and Bus Lane Tribunal for Scotland (independent)  
   • If the council issues a Notice of Rejection you may appeal within 28 days (no fee)  
   • Submit the appeal form online, by post or e-mail; attach all evidence  
   • You can choose a video/telephone hearing, attend in person or have it decided on papers  
   • The tribunal’s decision is binding on the council; if you win, the PCN is cancelled.  
   • If you lose, pay within the time stated to avoid the charge rising by 50 %.

7. If the notice was a Police Fixed Penalty Notice  
   • Follow the instructions on the ticket – pay the fine or tick the box requesting a court hearing within 28 days.  
   • The court will write to you with a date and you can present your defence there.

8. Throughout the process  
   • Never ignore deadlines – the charge can rise and bailiffs may be used.  
   • Keep copies of everything you send and receive.  
   • If in doubt, contact the issuing authority or get independent advice (e.g. Citizens Advice).

Relevant GOV.UK section (general driving penalties and fines):  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How do I apply for a provisional driving licence in Scotland?
generated_response:
You apply through DVLA (the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency) – the process is the same across the UK, including Scotland.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must be at least 15 years 9 months old (and 17 before you can drive a car).  
   • Meet the minimum eyesight standard.  
   • Have lived in the UK for at least the last 185 days.

2. Get together what you’ll need  
   • A valid UK passport (or another form of identity listed on GOV.UK).  
   • Your National Insurance number.  
   • The addresses where you’ve lived for the past 3 years.  
   • A debit or credit card to pay the fee (£34 online, £43 by post).

3. Apply online (fastest – licence usually arrives within a week)  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search “apply for a provisional driving licence” or use: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving  
   • Sign in with your Government Gateway or create one.  
   • Follow the on-screen questions, pay the £34 fee, and submit.  
   • DVLA will use the digital photo from your passport; if you don’t have one you’ll be told how to upload or send a photo.

4. If you prefer to apply by post  
   • Pick up form D1 ‘Application for a driving licence’ at a large Post Office.  
   • Complete the form, enclose an original identity document, a passport-style photo, and a cheque or postal order for £43 made payable to ‘DVLA’.  
   • Post to: DVLA, Swansea, SA99 1AD.  
   • Allow up to 3 weeks for the licence to arrive.

5. When your licence arrives  
   • Check the details are correct.  
   • You can start driving with a qualified instructor or supervising driver (age 21+, held a full licence for 3 years) with L-plates displayed and valid insurance.

prompt: What should I do if I need a paper copy of a DVLA form and can't print it myself?
generated_response:
If you need a DVLA form but can’t print it yourself, you have two easy options:  

1. Order a paper copy from DVLA  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search for the form (for example ‘D1 application for a driving licence’).  
   • On the form’s page, choose the option to ‘order a printed form’.  
   • Enter your name and address and DVLA will post it to you free of charge. Delivery usually takes about a week.  

2. Collect the form from a Post Office that deals with DVLA services  
   • Most larger Post Office branches in Wales keep the common DVLA forms (D1, D2, V62, V317, etc.).  
   • Take photo ID if the form needs it completed on the counter (for example, driving licence renewal).  
   • Ask the counter staff for the form you need – it’s free.  

Tip: If you’re unsure which Post Office stocks the form, phone ahead or use the branch-finder on the Post Office website.  

Relevant GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: How can I challenge a parking fine if I believe it was issued incorrectly?
generated_response:
If you think the ticket (Penalty Charge Notice, Fixed Penalty Notice or a private parking charge) is wrong, act quickly:  

1. Check who issued the ticket  
   • Council or Transport Scotland enforcement – heading usually says “Penalty Charge Notice (PCN)”.  
   • Police – “Fixed Penalty Notice”.  
   • Private land (supermarket, retail park, hospital car park) – “Parking Charge Notice” from a private company.  
   The challenge route and deadlines differ. The notice itself explains which applies.  

2. Gather evidence straight away  
   • Photos of your windscreen, signs, bay markings, pay-and-display ticket or payment app receipt.  
   • Witness statements, breakdown report or blue badge details if relevant.  
   • Keep the envelope if the notice arrived by post (shows the posting date).  

3. Council / Transport Scotland PCN  
   a. Within 14 days (or 21 days if sent by post) make an “informal challenge” online or by post to the address on the PCN. State why it’s wrong and attach evidence.  
   b. If rejected you’ll get a “Notice to Owner”. You then have 28 days to make a formal representation.  
   c. If that is rejected you can appeal free of charge to the independent adjudicator, the Parking and Bus Lane Tribunal for Scotland, within 28 days. Their decision is binding on the council.  

4. Police Fixed Penalty Notice  
   • You must request a court hearing within 28 days (instructions are on the notice) instead of paying. The court will decide. Get legal advice first if you’re unsure; penalties can rise if you lose.  

5. Private parking charge  
   a. Write to the company (follow their appeals procedure) within the time stated on the notice, enclosing evidence.  
   b. If they reject, ask for a “letter of rejection” with a POPLA (or IAS in Scotland) reference number.  
   c. Appeal free to the independent body named; decisions are binding on the company.  

6. Keep copies of everything you send and any replies. Do not ignore deadlines – the fine can increase or be registered as debt.  

Useful links  
• Appeal a parking ticket – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/appeal-penalty-charge-notice  
• Parking and Bus Lane Tribunal for Scotland: https://www.mygov.scot/parking-appeals

prompt: I am 17 years old and want to ride a motorcycle. Do I need to take CBT before I can ride on the road?
generated_response:
Yes.  
At 17 you must complete Compulsory Basic Training (CBT) before you can ride any moped or light motorcycle on public roads, unless you already hold a full motorcycle licence.

What to do  
1. Make sure you have a provisional motorcycle entitlement on your UK driving licence (most provisional car licences include this).  
2. Book and pass a CBT course with an approved training body. The course usually takes one day and gives you a DL196 certificate.  
3. After passing CBT you may ride:  
   • a motorcycle or scooter up to 125 cc (maximum 11 kW)  
   • with L-plates (D-plates in Scotland)  
   • no passengers and no motorways  
   The DL196 certificate is valid for 2 years.  
4. Before the 2 years are up you can:  
   • retake CBT, or  
   • pass the motorcycle theory test and practical tests to get an A1 (or higher) licence, which removes the L-plate and passenger/motorway restrictions.

Relevant guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: What happens if I have personalised number plates? Are there any special rules for them?
generated_response:
Personalised or “private” number plates are fine to use as long as you follow DVLA’s rules. The main points are:

1. Assigning the plate  
   • You must officially assign the private registration to your vehicle through DVLA before you fit the plates.  
   • DVLA will send you a new V5C log-book showing the private number. Keep it safe.

2. Making and fitting the plates  
   • Buy the physical plates from a DVLA-registered supplier – they’ll ask for photo ID and proof you own the registration.  
   • The plates must use the standard UK font, spacing and colour. Decorative fonts or extra bolts/spaces to change how it reads are illegal.

3. Age rules  
   • A private plate must not make a vehicle look newer than it is (e.g. you cannot put a “23” age identifier on a 2015 car). Older-looking marks are fine.

4. Everyday use  
   • MOT, tax and insurance work exactly the same; tell your insurer the plate has changed.  
   • If the plate is mis-spaced or hard to read you can be fined up to £1,000 and the vehicle can fail its MOT. ANPR cameras may also mark it as untaxed/uninsured.

5. Selling or scrapping the vehicle  
   • Decide first whether to keep the plate.  
      a. To keep it: apply to DVLA to put the plate “on retention” (you’ll get a certificate) or transfer it straight to another vehicle.  
      b. If you do nothing, the plate will stay with the car when it’s sold or scrapped.

6. Moving house or changing your name  
   • Update the V5C in the normal way. The private registration remains valid.

Relevant GOV.UK guidance: https://www.gov.uk/personalised-vehicle-registration-numbers

prompt: I have a mobility scooter that goes up to 8mph on the road and 4mph on footpaths. Do I need to pay vehicle tax for it?
generated_response:
You do not have to pay any vehicle tax for your mobility scooter, but you do need to register it with DVLA and keep its ‘nil-rate’ tax up to date.  

What to do  
1. Confirm it is a Class 3 invalid carriage (up to 8 mph on roads, limited to 4 mph on pavements).  
2. Register it with DVLA using form V55/4 (new) or V55/5 (used). The form and guidance notes are on GOV.UK or can be ordered by phone.  
3. DVLA will send you a log book (V5C). Your scooter will show as ‘tax class: nil’.  
4. Each year you must renew the tax online, by phone or at a Post Office that deals with vehicle tax – it is still free but keeps the record active.  
5. If you stop using the scooter on public roads, make a free SORN (Statutory Off Road Notification) instead.  

You do not need an MOT or to display a paper tax disc, and insurance is recommended but not compulsory.

Most relevant GOV.UK link  
Mobility scooters and powered wheelchairs: the rules – https://www.gov.uk/mobility-scooters-and-powered-wheelchairs-rules

prompt: How can I find out the school term and holiday dates for my child's school?
generated_response:
School term and holiday dates are set locally, so they vary from one council – and sometimes one school – to another.

1. Go to the GOV.UK “Education and learning” section and open the page called “School term and holiday dates”.  
   https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  
2. Enter your home or the school’s postcode when prompted.  
   • The service will take you to your local council’s website, where Scottish term-time calendars are published.  
3. Download or view the calendar for the current and next academic years.  
4. Double-check with your child’s school (website, newsletter or office) in case the school has “in-service” days or other changes that are different from the council calendar.  
5. Add the dates to your own calendar so you get reminders before each break.

prompt: I'm looking to become a teacher in England but I don't have a degree. What steps should I take to qualify for a teacher training course?
generated_response:
You’ll need a qualification that gives you both a degree-level award and Qualified Teacher Status (QTS).  
Here’s the usual route if you don’t yet hold a degree:

1. Check the basic entry requirements  
   • GCSEs (or equivalent) at grade 4/C or above in English and maths – and science if you want to teach primary.  
   • If you don’t have these, take a GCSE or an approved equivalency test before you apply.

2. Choose a degree course that includes QTS  
   • BA/BSc with QTS (3–4 years, full-time) – common for primary teaching but some secondary subjects are available.  
   • Bachelor of Education (BEd) – mainly for primary.  
   • These courses combine academic study, school placements and the professional skills you need, so you finish with both a degree and QTS.

3. Explore work-based options if you’d prefer to earn while you learn  
   • Teacher Degree Apprenticeship (pilot) or School-Centred Undergraduate Teacher Training (SCUTT) – limited subjects/areas but worth checking locally.  
   • You’ll be employed by a school, study part-time with a university and gain QTS and a degree on completion.

4. Build relevant experience and references  
   • Arrange some classroom experience to confirm teaching is right for you and to strengthen your application.  
   • Schools often welcome volunteers; contact local headteachers directly.

5. Apply through UCAS  
   • Use the UCAS undergraduate service to search for “QTS” courses.  
   • You’ll need a personal statement, academic reference and proof of GCSEs (or equivalency).

6. Finance your training  
   • Standard undergraduate student finance is available for degree courses with QTS.  
   • If you choose a salaried apprenticeship route, you’re paid by the school instead.

7. On completion, register for your QTS certificate and apply for your first teaching post.

Key links  
• Become a teacher – overview and routes: https://www.gov.uk/become-teacher  
• Education and training hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  

If you’re unsure which route fits your circumstances, call the Get Into Teaching advice line (0800 389 2500) or speak to the admissions team at universities offering QTS degrees.

prompt: If my child is not attending school regularly, what steps should I take to ensure they receive a proper education and avoid legal issues?
generated_response:
Your child must receive a suitable full-time education from the start of the school term after their 5th birthday until the last Friday in June of the school year in which they turn 16.

If they are missing school often, follow these steps:

1. Speak to the school straight away  
   • Phone or visit the attendance officer, form tutor or head of year.  
   • Explain the reasons for absence and ask how the school can help (for example, extra learning support, counselling or a part-time timetable for medical needs).

2. Agree an attendance plan  
   • Ask the school to put the plan in writing.  
   • Check it includes targets, support offered and review dates.  
   • Keep copies of all letters, emails and medical notes.

3. Work with the local authority if attendance does not improve  
   • The school will normally refer you to the Local Authority Education Welfare Service (sometimes called an Attendance Officer).  
   • You may be offered a parenting contract or an Education Supervision Order. These are voluntary but show you are co-operating, which can prevent legal action.

4. Consider alternative education only if it is right for your child  
   • You can educate your child at home, but you must write to the head teacher to remove them from the register and satisfy the council that the education is suitable.  
   • You can also ask the school and local authority about alternative provision or a managed move to another school.

5. Keep communication open and attend all meetings  
   • Turning up shows willingness and can stop matters escalating.  
   • If you need help with transport, language or special needs, tell the school in advance.

6. Know the possible legal consequences  
   • Fixed Penalty Notice: £60 if paid within 21 days (£120 within 28 days).  
   • Magistrates’ court: up to £2,500 fine, a Parenting Order or, in very serious cases, a short custodial sentence.  
   • Your child could also be issued a School Attendance Order.

Useful GOV.UK pages  
School attendance and absence (overview, your responsibilities, penalties): https://www.gov.uk/school-attendance-absence  
Educating your child at home: https://www.gov.uk/home-education  

If you are unsure about any step, contact the school’s attendance officer or your local council’s Education Welfare Service for advice.

prompt: What types of schools can I find information about on the government website?
generated_response:
You can use GOV.UK to read about almost every kind of school in the state and independent systems, including:  

1. State-funded mainstream schools  
   • Community schools  
   • Foundation and voluntary-controlled / voluntary-aided schools  
   • Academies and free schools (including studio schools and university technical colleges)  
   • Grammar schools  

2. Faith schools (state-funded or independent)  

3. Special-education settings  
   • Special schools  
   • Pupil referral units / alternative provision  

4. State boarding schools  

5. Independent (private) schools  
   • Day or boarding, including international schools operating in the UK  

6. Early years and nursery classes attached to schools (covered under the “Childcare and parenting” section)  

Where to look:  

• Go to GOV.UK > Education and learning > Schools and curriculum, then choose “Types of school” or use the direct link: https://www.gov.uk/types-of-school  
• Each school type page explains how it is run, who funds it, admission rules, curriculum, inspections and contact details.

prompt: My child is 12 and interested in dance. What are the age requirements for applying to a dance school or CAT?
generated_response:
For most dance Centres for Advanced Training (CATs) and full-time vocational dance schools, your 12-year-old is already the right age to apply.

1. CATs  
   • Typical entry age is 10 – 16.  
   • Applicants audition; no previous formal training is required.  
   • Your child can stay on the programme until they turn 18.  

2. Full-time vocational dance schools (e.g. Elmhurst Ballet School, Tring Park, Hammond, Royal Ballet School Lower School)  
   • Lower School entry is normally at 11+ (Year 7) or 13+.  
   • Most will consider applications up to around age 16 for the Lower/Intermediate sections.  

3. What to do next  
   1) Make a shortlist of CATs or dance schools you can reach. A full list of Dance CATs is on the National CAT website (search “Dance CAT scheme UK”).  
   2) Check each provider’s website for the exact age band and autumn audition dates.  
   3) Register online for an audition or open day.  
   4) Gather any recent school reports and a short dance video if they ask for one.  
   5) If you need help with fees, ask each organisation about Dance and Drama Awards (DaDA) or means-tested bursaries.  

Your daughter, at 12, meets the age requirement for every CAT and almost all vocational dance schools, so you can start applying this year.

prompt: My child is in Key Stage 1. How does the national curriculum work for this stage and what subjects are covered?
generated_response:
Key Stage 1 covers Year 1 and Year 2 (ages 5 to 7) in maintained schools in England.  
What your child is taught  
1. Core (compulsory) subjects  
   • English  
   • Mathematics  
   • Science  

2. Foundation subjects (also compulsory)  
   • Art and design  
   • Computing  
   • Design and technology  
   • Geography  
   • History  
   • Music  
   • Physical education (PE)  

3. Other legal requirements  
   • Religious education (the local authority sets the syllabus; you can ask for your child to be withdrawn).  
   • Relationships and health education (usually taught within PSHE).  

How teaching and assessment work  
• Schools follow the national curriculum programmes of study, but teachers can choose how lessons are organised and which topics or themes to use.  
• In June of Year 1 your child takes the phonics screening check.  
• At the end of Year 2 your child takes national curriculum tests (commonly called SATs) in:  
  – Reading  
  – Mathematics  
  – Grammar, punctuation and spelling (schools may decide whether to use this test).  
• Teachers use the test results, classwork and their observations to give a final teacher-assessment judgement for each core subject.  
• You will receive your child’s results and teacher assessments before the end of the summer term in Year 2.  
• The results are used to identify where your child is doing well and where extra support may help before moving to Key Stage 2.  

For more detail on what is taught and how children are assessed, see:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: I'm 17 and looking to start an apprenticeship. What do I need to do to apply and what are the requirements?
generated_response:
Apprenticeships are open to anyone in England who is:  
• aged 16 or over (you are)  
• living in England  
• not in full-time education when the apprenticeship starts.  

To get started:

1. Choose the type of apprenticeship  
   • Think about the job area you’re interested in (for example business, engineering, health care).  
   • Check typical entry requirements – many schemes ask for at least GCSEs (grade 4/C or above) in English and maths, but each employer sets its own.

2. Search for vacancies  
   • Go to the Find an apprenticeship service on GOV.UK.  
   • Create a free account, set up email alerts, and browse live vacancies near you.

3. Prepare your application  
   • Have your National Insurance number, predicted or achieved exam results, and a short CV ready.  
   • If you do not yet have grade 4/C in English or maths, you can still start – you’ll study these subjects alongside your apprenticeship.

4. Apply online  
   • Follow the instructions in the vacancy, answer any short questions, and attach your CV.  
   • You can apply for more than one apprenticeship at the same time.

5. Attend interviews/assessments  
   • Employers may invite you to an interview or assessment centre.  
   • Be ready to talk about why you want the role and what skills you can bring.

6. Accept an offer and enrol  
   • Once you accept, you’ll sign an apprenticeship agreement and a training plan.  
   • You’ll earn at least the apprentice minimum wage (£6.40 an hour from April 2024) and work towards a recognised qualification.

Extra help  
• Speak to your school or college careers adviser.  
• Call the National Careers Service free on 0800 100 900 for advice.  

Relevant GOV.UK pages  
• Apprenticeships and training: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  
• Find an apprenticeship: https://www.gov.uk/apply-apprenticeship

prompt: I'm trying to understand what subjects my child will be learning in primary school. How does the national curriculum work and what subjects are covered?
generated_response:
In the UK each nation runs its own curriculum, so what your child studies depends on where the school is.

1. If your child goes to school in Wales  
   • Schools follow the new Curriculum for Wales 2022.  
   • Learning is organised into six broad “Areas of Learning and Experience” instead of separate subjects:  
     1. Languages, Literacy and Communication (includes English, Welsh and other languages)  
     2. Mathematics and Numeracy  
     3. Science and Technology  
     4. Humanities (history, geography, RE, business, social studies)  
     5. Health and Well-being (PE, personal and social education)  
     6. Expressive Arts (art, music, drama, dance, film & digital media)  
   • Schools have flexibility to design topics that meet these areas while building the four core purposes: helping pupils become ambitious, enterprising, ethical and healthy individuals.  
   • Assessment is ongoing rather than through formal “SATs”.  

2. If the school follows the National Curriculum for England (some Welsh independent schools do)  
   • Primary education is split into:  
     – Key Stage 1 (ages 5-7)  
     – Key Stage 2 (ages 7-11)  
   • Statutory subjects are:  
     1. English  
     2. Mathematics  
     3. Science  
     4. Design & Technology  
     5. History  
     6. Geography  
     7. Art & Design  
     8. Music  
     9. Physical Education (including swimming)  
     10. Computing  
     11. Languages (from age 7; usually French, Spanish or German)  
   • Schools often also teach Personal, Social, Health and Economic education (PSHE) and Religious Education.  

Next steps for you  
1. Check which curriculum your child’s school follows (ask the school office or look at its website).  
2. Ask the school for its yearly or termly plan—schools must publish what they teach and how they assess progress.  
3. If you want more detail:  
   – Wales: see the Curriculum for Wales guidance on gov.wales.  
   – England: see the National Curriculum programmes of study on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/national-curriculum

prompt: I'm a parent in Scotland and my child is starting primary school. How do I know what subjects they will be learning and what standards they should meet by the end of each year?
generated_response:
In Scotland, every primary school follows the Curriculum for Excellence (CfE).  
This sets out:

1. Eight curriculum areas your child will study  
   • Literacy and English  
   • Numeracy and Mathematics  
   • Health and Well-being  
   • Sciences  
   • Social Studies  
   • Expressive Arts (art, music, drama, dance)  
   • Technologies (including computing)  
   • Religious and Moral Education  

2. National “Curriculum Levels” that describe the standards pupils should reach, rather than fixed targets for each separate year.  
   • Early Level – usually Nursery and Primary 1 (age 3 – 6)  
   • First Level – roughly Primary 2 to 4 (age 6 – 9)  
   • Second Level – roughly Primary 5 to 7 (age 9 – 12)  

Your child’s teacher checks progress against these levels in each subject, and reports whether your child is “Developing”, “Consolidating” or “Secure” at a level.

What you can do:

1. Ask the school for its handbook or look on the school website – every Scottish school must publish a summary of the curriculum areas taught and how progress is assessed.  
2. Attend the termly parents’ evenings (or request a meeting) and ask which Curriculum for Excellence level your child is working at in literacy, numeracy and health-and-well-being.  
3. Read the Scottish Government’s plain-English guide “Curriculum for Excellence: A Parent’s Guide”. You can download it or request a paper copy from the school office or your local council education department.  
4. If you want more detail, visit Parentzone Scotland (parentzone.educa tion.gov.scot) and select “Curriculum” → “What will my child learn?” for examples of the learning outcomes at each level.  
5. If anything is unclear, phone your council’s education service or the school and ask to speak to the Depute Head in charge of the curriculum.

Useful links  
• Parentzone Scotland – What children learn and CfE levels: https://education.gov.scot/parentzone  
• Scottish Government – Curriculum for Excellence guidance for parents (print-friendly): search “CfE parent guide” on gov.scot  
• General information on education: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

There are no school fees for these documents and the school must provide help if you need it in an alternative format or language.

prompt: How do I find out what courses and qualifications are available for students aged 14 to 19 in my area?
generated_response:
You can check what courses and qualifications are on offer locally in a few quick steps.  

1. Use the government’s course-search service  
   • Go to National Careers Service “Find a course” (search ‘find a course gov uk’ or use https://www.gov.uk/browse/education and follow the link).  
   • Enter your postcode and choose the age range “14 to 19”.  
   • Filter by subject, qualification type (GCSEs, A levels, T Levels, BTECs, apprenticeships, etc.) and distance to see providers near you.

2. Look at individual school and college websites  
   • Every maintained school, academy and further-education college must publish their 14–19 curriculum and prospectus online.  
   • Search for “sixth-form” or “key stage 4” on each site for detailed course lists and entry requirements.  

3. Check performance tables for more detail  
   • On the same education section of GOV.UK you can open “School and college performance tables”.  
   • Enter the provider’s name or your postcode to see which qualifications they teach and past results.  

4. Contact your local authority or the provider directly  
   • If a course you want is not listed online, phone or email the school/college admissions team.  
   • Your local council’s education department can also confirm what is available and any travel or financial support.

Helpful links  
• Education and learning on GOV.UK – starting point for all course-search tools: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  
• Find a course (National Careers Service): follow the link “Find courses and training”.

prompt: What are the possible sanctions a school can impose on a student for misbehaving, and how do they handle detentions?
generated_response:
Schools have a range of sanctions they can use when a pupil misbehaves. The main ones are:  

1. Verbal warning – the teacher tells the pupil what was wrong and what needs to change.  
2. Extra work or repeating unsatisfactory work.  
3. Loss of privileges – for example missing part of break, not being allowed on a school trip or to represent the school at an event.  
4. Confiscation – teachers can remove items that are causing disruption (for instance a mobile phone).  
5. Detention – kept back during break, lunchtime or after school (details below).  
6. Internal isolation – the pupil is removed from their normal class and works elsewhere in school for a period.  
7. Fixed-term exclusion – the pupil is sent home for 1–45 school days in an academic year.  
8. Permanent exclusion – the pupil is removed from the school roll.  

Detentions: how they are run  

• Timing – a detention can take place during break, lunchtime, or after school.  
• Notice – for after-school detentions the school should give at least 24 hours’ written notice so parents know when to collect the pupil. Consent is not required.  
• Travel home – it is the parent’s responsibility to arrange safe travel; however schools are expected to take a pupil’s age, any special needs and local transport into account when setting the finish time.  
• Supervision – a member of staff must supervise the pupil throughout the detention.  
• Lunchtime detentions – the school must allow time for the pupil to eat, use the toilet and have a short break.  
• Record keeping – schools keep a record of why the detention was set and that the pupil attended.  

All sanctions must be reasonable, applied consistently and take account of the pupil’s age, disability or special educational needs. If you feel a sanction has been applied unfairly you can raise the matter informally with the teacher or head of year, then follow the school’s complaints procedure if needed.

Further information:  
School discipline, behaviour and exclusions – GOV.UK  
https://www.gov.uk/school-discipline-exclusions

prompt: How do I apply for a school place for my child if I'm not satisfied with the current one?
generated_response:
If you want to move your child to a different state-funded school (often called an ‘in-year transfer’), you apply through the admissions team at your local council in Wales. The outline is the same for both primary and secondary places.

1. Find your local council’s school admissions page. You can start from GOV.UK → Education → School admissions, then select ‘Apply for a school place’ and enter your postcode to be taken to the right Welsh council website.  
2. Download or request the council’s in-year transfer (or ‘mid-term transfer’) form. Some councils let you apply online.  
3. Complete the form, giving:  
   • your child’s details and current school  
   • the schools you are applying for (list them in order of preference)  
   • your reasons for requesting a move  
4. Provide any supporting evidence the form asks for (for example, proof of address or a letter explaining why the move is in your child’s best interests).  
5. Submit the form to the council admissions team. They will:  
   • check if places are available at your preferred schools  
   • consult the schools’ admission authorities  
6. Wait for the written decision. Councils aim to respond within 15 school days (20 at most).  
7. If you get an offer, accept it by the deadline stated so the place is secured.  
8. If all your preferences are refused, you can:  
   • remain on the waiting list for each school  
   • appeal to an independent panel (instructions are sent with the refusal)  
   • ask the council for other local schools with vacancies.

Key points  
• Your child’s current place is not withdrawn until you accept another.  
• Places are allocated using each school’s published admission criteria; stating reasons such as bullying or a house move can strengthen your case but does not guarantee a place.  
• Faith schools, academies and voluntary-aided schools may ask for extra forms or evidence.  
• If you are moving house, apply as soon as you have proof of your new address.  

Useful link  
School admissions on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  

If you need help, phone or email your council’s admissions team—they can talk you through local deadlines and document requirements.

prompt: My child has special educational needs. How do I apply for school transport assistance, and what documentation is required?
generated_response:
Most school transport help for children with additional support needs (ASN) is arranged by your local council, not central government.  
Follow these steps:  

1. Find the right contact  
   • Go to your local council’s website and look for “School transport” or “Additional support needs transport”.  
   • If you are unsure which council you live in, use the postcode tool on GOV.UK to find it (https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council).  

2. Get the application form  
   • Download the “ASN/medical school transport” application form or ask the council’s education or pupil support service to post one to you.  
   • Some councils let you apply online via a parent portal.  

3. Gather the documents usually required  
   Councils differ, but you are normally asked for:  
   • Proof of the child’s home address (recent council-tax bill or utility bill).  
   • Proof that your child is enrolled at the named school (placement letter or recent school letterhead).  
   • Evidence of your child’s special or additional support needs – for example:  
     – Copy of the Coordinated Support Plan (CSP) or Individualised Education Programme (IEP); or  
     – Letter/report from the school’s Additional Support for Learning (ASL) team, educational psychologist, or paediatrician.  
   • Medical evidence if mobility, health or behavioural needs affect travel (e.g. GP or consultant letter).  
   • Any disability benefits award letter (e.g. Disability Living Allowance) if the council bases entitlement on this.  
   • If someone else is applying on your behalf, a signed letter of authorisation.  

4. Complete and submit  
   • Fill in all sections, sign, and attach the documents.  
   • Send it to the address or upload it as instructed. Keep copies.  

5. Wait for a decision  
   • Councils aim to reply within 4–6 weeks. They will tell you whether:  
     – Free or subsidised transport is approved;  
     – An escort will be provided; or  
     – More information is needed.  

6. If refused or your child’s needs change  
   • You can ask for the decision to be reviewed. Provide any new evidence (e.g. updated medical reports).  
   • If still unhappy, use the council’s complaints procedure or contact Enquire, the Scottish advice service for ASN (https://enquire.org.uk).  

Useful link:  
Find your local council – https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council

prompt: What if I'm doing work experience as a school student? Am I entitled to the National Minimum Wage?
generated_response:
If you’re still at school and you’re doing a short, school-arranged work-experience placement (for example the typical 1- or 2-week placement in Year 10 or Year 12) you’re not entitled to the National Minimum Wage.  
You can be given expenses (such as travel or lunch costs), but the employer does not have to pay you wages.  
If the placement is outside your school’s programme, lasts longer than a year, or you continue working for the employer once you’ve reached school-leaving age, the National Minimum Wage rules may start to apply.

prompt: I'm an intern at a company and I'm worried about my pay. Do I have the right to the National Minimum Wage?
generated_response:
Yes – most interns in England have the right to be paid at least the National Minimum Wage (NMW).

1. Check if you count as a ‘worker’.  
   You’re likely a worker if you:  
   • have set hours or duties  
   • do useful work (not just observing)  
   • must turn up when the company asks  
   • are promised a reward (money, vouchers, etc.)  
   If any of these apply, the NMW rules cover you.

2. When interns can be unpaid.  
   You are NOT entitled to the NMW only if:  
   • you are purely work-shadowing and do no productive work, OR  
   • you’re on a student placement that is part of a UK-based course and lasts 1 year or less, OR  
   • you’re a genuine voluntary worker for a charity or voluntary organisation (only receiving expenses).

3. Find the correct minimum rate.  
   – Aged 18–20: £7.49 per hour (from 1 Apr 2023).  
   – Aged 21–22: £10.18 per hour.  
   – Aged 23+: £10.42 per hour.  
   Rates usually rise each April – check the latest figure.

4. Work out what you’ve been paid.  
   a. Add up all pay received in a pay period (weekly or monthly).  
   b. Divide it by the total hours you actually worked.  
   c. Compare this hourly figure to the NMW rate for your age.

5. If you’re being underpaid:  
   1) Raise it informally with your manager or HR, showing your calculation.  
   2) If this doesn’t fix it, call Acas for free, confidential advice on 0300 123 1100.  
   3) You can make a formal complaint to HMRC; they can demand back-pay and penalties from the employer.  
   4) You have up to 6 years to claim unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or county court.

6. Keep evidence.  
   Save payslips, timesheets, emails setting your hours, and any contract or offer letter.

More guidance: https://www.gov.uk/national-minimum-wage-rates  
How to complain: https://www.gov.uk/pay-and-work-rights

prompt: I'm running a small business and want to know how much Statutory Sick Pay I need to provide to my employees if they're sick for a few weeks.
generated_response:
Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) is set by the UK Government, so every eligible employee gets the same basic rate—regardless of the size of your business.

1. Current weekly rate  
   • For sickness that starts on or after 6 April 2024 the rate is £116.75 a week.  
   • For sickness that started between 6 April 2023 and 5 April 2024 the rate was £109.40 a week.

2. How long you must pay it  
   • Up to 28 weeks for each period of sickness, as long as the employee is still off work and remains eligible.

3. When payment starts  
   • You start paying SSP from the 4th “qualifying day” the employee is off sick.  
   • The first 3 qualifying days are unpaid “waiting days” unless the employee has already had a linked period of sickness in the previous 8 weeks.

4. Calculating the amount for part-weeks  
   a. Work out the employee’s “qualifying days” (the days they normally work).  
   b. Daily SSP rate = weekly rate ÷ number of qualifying days in the week.  
   c. Pay that daily rate for each qualifying day after the 3 waiting days.

5. Paying and reporting  
   • Pay SSP through your normal payroll so tax and National Insurance are handled automatically.  
   • Keep records of sickness dates, payments made and any evidence you asked for (for example, Fit Notes).

6. Reclaiming SSP  
   • There is currently no scheme to reclaim SSP from HMRC, so your business covers the cost.

Full guidance is on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/statutory-sick-pay

prompt: I'm a teacher and I've been working for my school for several years. How does taking annual leave affect my continuous employment status?
generated_response:
Taking statutory annual leave does not break your continuous employment.  
As long as you remain under contract with the school, any holiday you take – whether during term breaks or in-term leave that your head-teacher authorises – simply counts as time worked for the purpose of calculating continuous service. Your start date therefore stays the same, and your length of service keeps running while you are on paid holiday. Continuous employment would only stop if:  
1. Your contract ends and you are not re-employed for at least one complete calendar week (Sunday to Saturday), or  
2. There is another type of break that the law treats as ending continuity (for example, a dismissal followed by more than one clear week out of work, or certain industrial action lasting longer than a week).  

This means your entitlement to rights that build up with service – such as redundancy pay, unfair dismissal protection and enhanced maternity leave – carries on uninterrupted while you are on annual leave.

Most teachers’ contracts also treat school closure periods (e.g. summer holidays) as paid leave, so those weeks continue to count towards your service in exactly the same way.

For more detail, see “Continuous employment” under Employment contracts and conditions on GOV.UK:  
https://www.gov.uk/employment-contracts-and-conditions/continuous-employment

prompt: I'm a parent in Wales and my baby is in neonatal care. How do I apply for Neonatal Care Leave and Statutory Neonatal Care Pay?
generated_response:
Neonatal Care Leave and Statutory Neonatal Care Pay are not available to claim yet.  
The Neonatal Care (Leave and Pay) Act became law in 2023, but the regulations that set out exactly how to apply, the form you’ll need and the start-date for payments have not been brought into force. The UK Government has said the scheme is expected to start in 2025.

What you can do now  
1. Speak to your employer as soon as possible about your situation. Until Neonatal Care Leave starts, you may be able to use:  
   • Paternity leave or maternity leave  
   • Shared Parental Leave  
   • Unpaid parental leave (up to 18 weeks per child)  
   • Time off for dependants (short, unpaid leave for emergencies)  
   • Annual leave or any compassionate leave your employer offers.  
2. Keep any hospital letters that confirm how long your baby is in neonatal care – you are likely to need this evidence once the new scheme opens.  
3. Check GOV.UK periodically, or sign up for employment law updates, so you know when the new leave and pay become available.  

If you need immediate financial help, contact your local Jobcentre Plus or Citizens Advice for guidance on benefits such as Universal Credit or Statutory Sick Pay.

Most up-to-date information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: Can I still get the National Minimum Wage if I'm doing a work placement as part of my studies?
generated_response:
In most cases, no.  
If a work placement is a compulsory part of a UK-based further- or higher-education course and lasts 12 months or less, the employer does not have to pay the National Minimum Wage (NMW). They may choose to pay you, but it isn’t a legal requirement.

You must be paid at least the NMW if any of the following apply:  
1. The placement is not required by your course (for example, you arranged it yourself after exams).  
2. It lasts longer than 12 months.  
3. You have a contract that makes you a “worker” (set hours, regular duties, expectation that you’ll do the work).  
4. You’re doing paid work alongside the placement.

What to do next  
1. Check your course documents or ask your tutor to confirm whether the placement is compulsory and how long it runs.  
2. Ask the employer to confirm in writing whether they see you as a student on placement or as a paid worker.  
3. If you think you should be paid but aren’t, use the National Minimum Wage calculator or call Acas (0300 123 1100) for free, confidential advice.  

More information: https://www.gov.uk/employing-people (see “Work experience and internships”).

prompt: I'm running a small business and need to understand the different employment statuses like worker, employee, and self-employed. How do these statuses affect my responsibilities as an employer?
generated_response:
Employment status is important because it decides which employment rights the individual has – and which duties you have as their employer.  
There are three main categories:

1. Worker  
   • Usually has a casual or zero-hours arrangement.  
   • Minimum rights: National Minimum Wage, 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday, rest breaks, protection from discrimination, and payslips.  
   • Your duties: check right to work, put them on PAYE and deduct tax/NI, pay holiday-pay, keep to working-time limits, provide a workplace pension if they meet the earnings threshold, and hold employer’s-liability insurance.

2. Employee  
   • Works under an employment contract with set hours or an expectation of ongoing work.  
   • Has all worker rights plus extra protection: Statutory Sick Pay, maternity/paternity/shared-parental leave, redundancy pay, minimum notice, protection from unfair dismissal, and the right to request flexible working.  
   • Your duties: everything listed for workers, plus: provide a written statement of employment particulars on or before day 1, keep disciplinary & grievance procedures, run Statutory Sick Pay and parental-leave schemes, handle redundancy correctly and follow a fair dismissal process.

3. Self-employed / independent contractor  
   • Runs their own business and decides how, when and where to do the work.  
   • Supplies their own tools, invoices for work, can send a substitute, and bears the financial risk.  
   • Rights are limited to health & safety and protection against discrimination.  
   • Your duties: agree the price and deliverables in a contract for services, pay the invoice, and ensure a safe working environment. You normally do NOT: deduct tax/NI, pay holiday or pension, or provide sick pay.  
   • Caution: if you treat a contractor like an employee (control, no substitution, obligation to provide work), HMRC or a tribunal may re-classify them, leaving you liable for tax arrears and employment rights. Use HMRC’s Check Employment Status for Tax (CEST) tool if unsure.

Practical steps for a small business

1. Before hiring, decide which status really reflects the relationship. Think about control, mutual obligation, and whether the individual can send a substitute.  
2. Confirm the status in writing:  
      • Contract of employment (employee) or statement of terms (worker).  
      • Contract for services (self-employed).  
3. Carry out the legal basics for workers/employees:  
      a. Right-to-work check.  
      b. Register as an employer with HMRC and put them on PAYE.  
      c. Pay at least the National Minimum Wage.  
      d. Enrol eligible staff into a workplace pension scheme.  
      e. Provide payslips and keep payroll records for 3 years.  
4. Keep track of holiday accrual and working hours; pay holiday on termination.  
5. Review the relationship regularly—if the way you work together changes, the status may change too.

Where to find more detail  
GOV.UK Employing people hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people  
(see ‘Employment status’, ‘Running payroll’, and ‘Worker, employee and self-employed’ sections).

prompt: How can I ensure that my employees understand and follow the anti-bribery policy?
generated_response:
Making an anti-bribery policy work in practice comes down to good communication, training and follow-up.  

1. Put the policy in plain English  
   • Keep it short, avoid legal jargon and give everyday examples of what is and is not acceptable (e.g. small gifts, hospitality, cash offers).  

2. Launch it formally  
   • Explain why it matters (Bribery Act 2010 penalties, safeguarding patient trust, personal consequences).  
   • Ask every member of staff to sign that they have read and understood it. Keep the records.  

3. Train everyone, then refresh regularly  
   • Run short, scenario-based sessions during induction and at least once a year.  
   • Use practical nursing-related cases—e.g. suppliers offering perks or patients’ families giving gifts.  
   • Give managers extra training so they can spot risks and answer questions.  

4. Make it easy to ask questions or report concerns  
   • Set up a confidential channel (email or phone line) and publicise it.  
   • Reassure staff that issues will be taken seriously and there is no retaliation for raising concerns.  

5. Build checks into everyday procedures  
   • Require two people to approve orders and invoices.  
   • Record all gifts and hospitality in a central register and review it monthly.  

6. Enforce consistently  
   • Investigate any breach promptly.  
   • Apply clear, proportionate disciplinary action and feed lessons back into training.  

7. Review the policy at least annually  
   • Look at incident reports, audit findings and changes in law or NHS guidance.  
   • Update the policy and training material accordingly.  

Useful starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I've been experiencing some unfair treatment at work and I'm not sure how to handle it. What steps should I take to address this issue?
generated_response:
If you feel you’re being treated unfairly at work, you can take the following steps:

1. Keep a written record  
   • Note dates, times, what happened, who was involved and any witnesses.  
   • Keep copies of emails, messages or other documents that show the behaviour.

2. Check your workplace policies  
   • Look at your contract, staff handbook or intranet for policies on bullying, discrimination or grievance procedures.  
   • This tells you the exact process your employer expects you to follow.

3. Speak informally first  
   • If you feel safe, raise the issue with the person involved or your line manager.  
   • Explain what has happened, why it feels unfair and the outcome you’d like.  
   • Sometimes issues can be resolved quickly at this stage.

4. Get independent advice and support  
   • Talk to your trade union rep if you have one.  
   • Call Acas (0300 123 1100) for free, confidential advice – Acas covers Scotland as well as England and Wales.  
   • Citizens Advice Scotland can also guide you on workplace rights.

5. Raise a formal grievance  
   • If the problem continues, put your complaint in writing to your employer, following their grievance procedure.  
   • State the facts clearly, attach your evidence and say what you want done.

6. Attend the grievance meeting  
   • You have the right to be accompanied by a colleague or union rep.  
   • Take notes of what is discussed and agreed.

7. Consider further action if unresolved  
   • If your grievance is rejected or not handled properly, you can appeal internally.  
   • After going through grievance and Early Conciliation with Acas, you may decide to take a claim to an employment tribunal. The time limit is usually three months less one day from the date of the last incident.

8. Look after yourself  
   • Unfair treatment can be stressful. Talk to friends, family or a counsellor and see your GP if it affects your health.

Useful links  
• Overview of employment rights and problems at work: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people  
• How to raise a grievance (Acas guidance): https://www.acas.org.uk/raising-a-grievance  
• Citizens Advice Scotland: https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/scotland/

prompt: As a retired individual in Wales, I want to hire someone to help me with gardening tasks. What are the legal requirements I need to be aware of when employing them?
generated_response:
Below are the main things you must check before someone starts gardening for you in Wales.

1. Confirm their employment status  
   • If the gardener runs their own business (sets their hours, uses their tools, invoices you) they are likely to be self-employed and will deal with their own tax and National Insurance (NI).  
   • If you control the hours, provide equipment or could dismiss them, they are probably your employee.  
   Use HMRC’s “Check employment status for tax” tool on GOV.UK.

2. Check their right to work in the UK  
   Ask to see original documents (for example a UK passport) and keep a dated copy.

3. Give basic written terms if they are an employee  
   Within two months give them a written statement setting out pay, hours, holidays and notice periods.

4. Pay at least the National Minimum Wage  
   Current rates apply to all workers, including part-time or casual staff.

5. Register with HMRC and run PAYE (employees only)  
   • Register as an employer before the first payday if you pay £123 a week (£533 a month) or more, or if they already have another job or pension.  
   • Deduct tax and employee NI, pay employer NI where due, and submit Real Time Information (RTI) returns.  
   • Keep payroll records for 3 years.

6. Workplace pension duties  
   If the gardener is aged 22–State Pension age and you pay £10,000+ a year, you must auto-enrol them in a pension and contribute.

7. Holiday, sick pay and other rights  
   • At least 5.6 weeks’ paid holiday a year pro-rata.  
   • Statutory Sick Pay if they meet the earnings threshold.  
   • Rest breaks and limits on weekly working hours (unless they opt out).

8. Insurance and safety  
   • Employers’ Liability Insurance is not compulsory for purely domestic staff, but many household policies exclude it—check with your insurer and consider arranging cover.  
   • Provide a safe working environment (e.g. safe access to electricity, proper storage of chemicals).

9. Keep records and review regularly  
   Keep copies of right-to-work checks, contracts and pay records. Review pay each April to stay in line with minimum wage changes.

If the gardener is genuinely self-employed  
• Agree the scope and price in writing.  
• You pay the invoice they issue; they handle tax, NI and insurance.  
• Still check their right to work and satisfy yourself they have public liability insurance.

Further guidance can be found at:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: If I give an employee a company car, do I need to inform HMRC and what forms should I use?
generated_response:
Yes. Company cars (and any fuel you provide for private use) count as a taxable benefit, so you must tell HMRC.

1. As soon as the car is first made available, complete form P46(Car) – either online or on paper – and send it to HMRC within 28 days after the end of the tax quarter in which the change happened (quarters end 5 July, 5 October, 5 January, 5 April).  
2. If the car is returned, replaced or another car is provided later in the year, submit another P46(Car) for that change.  
3. At the end of the tax year:  
   a. Fill in form P11D for each employee who had the car (unless you’ve registered to ‘payroll’ the benefit).  
   b. Submit form P11D(b) to report and pay Class 1A National Insurance on the benefit.  
   Deadline for both forms and payment is 6 July following the tax year.  
4. If you prefer to add the car benefit to the employee’s monthly pay instead of filing P11Ds, register for ‘payrolling benefits’ with HMRC before the start of the tax year. In that case you still submit a P11D(b) but not a P11D.  
5. Keep records showing: date car was provided/returned, list price, CO₂ emissions, fuel provided, and any employee contributions.

GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I want to check if I'm being paid the correct National Minimum Wage. How do I use the calculator provided on the website?
generated_response:
To check whether you’re being paid at least the National Minimum Wage (or National Living Wage), you can use the GOV.UK “Check your pay” calculator. Have your most recent payslip to hand, then follow these steps:  

1. Go to GOV.UK and search for “Check your pay” (or “Am I getting minimum wage?”).  
2. Select the calculator link and choose “Check your pay and rights”: this opens the online form.  
3. Enter your date of birth – the calculator uses your age to decide the correct minimum-wage rate.  
4. Select the pay period shown on your payslip (weekly, fortnightly, monthly, etc.).  
5. Enter:  
   • Your gross pay for that period (before tax and other deductions).  
   • The total hours you actually worked in the same period.  
6. If you receive accommodation from your employer, overtime premia, tips or deductions for uniforms, the tool will ask further questions. Answer these as they appear.  
7. The calculator will show:  
   • Your effective hourly pay.  
   • The statutory minimum you should receive for your age.  
   • Whether you’re being underpaid and, if so, by how much.  
8. Save or print the results if you need evidence. The page also links to guidance on raising the issue with your employer or reporting underpayment to HMRC.

If you cannot gather every detail right away, you can save the form and return later.

Relevant GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: Can I use a Share Incentive Plan to set up employee ownership?
generated_response:
Yes. A Share Incentive Plan (SIP) is one of the government-approved share schemes you can use to give employees shares in your company and build an employee-owned culture.  It works best for gradually moving ownership, rather than transferring the whole business in one go.

What a SIP does  
• Lets you award up to £3,600 of free shares per employee each tax year.  
• Allows employees to buy “partnership” shares from their pre-tax pay (up to £1,800 or 10 % of salary a year).  
• Lets you give up to 2 “matching” shares for every partnership share bought.  
• Lets employees reinvest dividends into more shares.  
• Shares are held in a SIP trust. If kept for 5 years, employees pay no Income Tax or NICs on them and no Capital Gains Tax on sale.

Typical steps to set one up  
1. Decide your aims (e.g. % of company you want employees to hold and how quickly).  
2. Draft SIP rules showing: who’s eligible (must include all UK employees on similar terms), how many free, partnership and matching shares you will offer, and any performance conditions.  
3. Appoint an independent SIP trustee (many specialist providers offer this service).
