   4. Specialist charities – e.g. homelessness or refugee charities for warm clothing, or organisations that refurbish laptops for schools. Contact first to check what they need.

4. Recycle items that can’t be reused  
   1. Take electronics (including cables and batteries) to your council Household Waste Recycling Centre. Look for the WEEE bay.  
   2. Large retailers that sell electrical goods must take back a similar item when you buy a new one, or accept small WEEE for free even if you’re not buying.  
   3. Clothes that are too worn can go in textile recycling banks at recycling centres or some supermarkets.

5. Avoid common legal pitfalls  
   • Do not leave bags beside charity banks or on the street – it counts as fly-tipping.  
   • Do not sell or give away electronics that still contain other people’s personal data.  
   • Follow any local council rules on waste collections; check collection times if using kerbside textile sacks.

Further information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/environment-countryside

prompt: How can I find out if there are any new stretches of the King Charles III England Coast Path that are open near me?
generated_response:
New sections of the King Charles III England Coast Path are announced by Natural England and listed on GOV.UK.  
Here’s the quickest way to check what is open near you:

1. Go to the interactive England Coast Path map run by Natural England: https://www.openaccess.naturalengland.org.uk/coastalaccess  
   • Type your postcode or zoom in on the map.  
   • Green lines show stretches that are fully open; blue lines show stretches that have been approved but are not yet open.  

2. Click on any stretch for a summary of its status, length and a link to the full GOV.UK report.  

3. For written updates instead of a map, visit the England Coast Path collection on GOV.UK and choose your county or stretch name: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/england-coast-path-improving-public-access-to-the-coast  

4. If you would like automatic alerts, use the “Get emails about this page” button on the collection page; you’ll get an email whenever Natural England publishes a new decision or opening notice.  

5. Local councils and National Trail information boards often put up notices a few weeks before a stretch opens. If you prefer to phone, ring the access team at your county council and ask for “England Coast Path updates”.  

Relevant GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/environment-countryside

prompt: How do I report someone who is exporting waste illegally in Scotland?
generated_response:
If you think someone is exporting waste illegally from Scotland, report it straight away to the Scottish Environment Protection Agency (SEPA).

1. Gather any details you have  
   • What you saw – type of waste, vehicle/container numbers, dates, times, routes, destination (if known).  
   • Who is involved – names of people or companies, registration plates, site addresses.  
   • Any photos or documents (only if it’s safe and legal for you to obtain them).

2. Contact SEPA’s 24-hour Pollution Report Line  
   • Telephone (free): 0800 80 70 60  
   • Tell the operator you want to report a suspected illegal waste export.  
   • You can choose to remain anonymous.

3. Alternatively, use SEPA’s online form  
   • Go to sepa.org.uk/report (or search “SEPA report environmental event”).  
   • Select “waste” and give as much detail as you can.

4. If you believe a crime is happening now or you feel unsafe, call Police Scotland on 101 (or 999 in an emergency).

5. Keep a note of the reference number SEPA or the police give you. This makes it easier to add information later.

If the waste is being moved through a UK port, Border Force or HMRC may also investigate, but SEPA will pass the information on for you.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/environment-countryside

prompt: Can I attend any events organized by the Farming Advice Service?
generated_response:
Yes, as a rule you can book onto Farming Advice Service (FAS) events if you are:  
• a farmer, land manager or agricultural adviser with land in England, or  
• someone who supports those businesses (for example an agronomist or consultant).  

Most events are free but you normally have to book a place in advance.

What to do  
1. Check you’re eligible – FAS is funded by Defra and its events focus on the rules that apply in England (cross-compliance, greening, water protection, etc.). If your holding is in Scotland you may find the Scottish Farm Advisory Service more useful.  
2. Find an event:  
   • Go to the Farming Advice Service events page and browse the calendar.  
3. Book your place:  
   • Click the event link and fill in the online booking form, or  
   • Phone the FAS helpline on 03000 200 301 (Monday–Friday, 9 am–5 pm).  
4. Get confirmation by email – bring this on the day.  
5. If the event is full, ask to be put on the reserve list or enquire about future dates.

If you farm in Scotland  
Visit the Scottish Farm Advisory Service (www.fas.scot) or phone 0300 323 0161 for events that cover Scottish rules and schemes.

More information  
Farming Advice Service on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/government/groups/farming-advice-service-fas

prompt: I'm a trader selling biomass fuel to consumers. What specific information do I need from my suppliers to register on the biomass supplier list?
generated_response:
To register on the Biomass Suppliers List (BSL) as a trader you will need to enter details about every fuel you intend to sell.  
Before you start the online application, ask each producer-supplier you buy from for the information below and keep a copy of their evidence (invoices, certificates, e-mails) – BSL may ask to see it at audit.

1. BSL fuel authorisation number  
   • The unique BSL number for each fuel you purchase (for example “BSL-1234-5678”).  
   • The trading name of the supplier exactly as it appears on their BSL approval.

2. Fuel specification exactly as registered  
   • Fuel type (pellet, chip, log, briquette).  
   • Size/grade and moisture content band.  
   • Species or feedstock where stated.  
   This must match what you will resell.

3. Sustainability assurance used by the supplier  
   • Whether the supplier relies on an approved forest certification scheme (e.g. FSC, PEFC) or on BSL “default” evidence.  
   • The relevant certificate number or other reference if certification is used.

4. Raw-material origin and transport distance (if not certified)  
   • Postcode or map reference of the harvesting site.  
   • Road distance from the harvesting site to the processing site (in km).  
   You only need this if your supplier is not using a recognised certification scheme and therefore had to provide these details for their own approval.

5. Greenhouse-gas (GHG) value entered for the fuel  
   • The g CO₂-eq/MJ figure calculated in the BSL calculator, or confirmation that the default value was accepted.

6. Proof of purchase  
   • A recent invoice or delivery note showing the fuel name, quantity and date.  
   • Keep invoices for at least 5 years – the BSL audit team can ask for them at any time.

Once you have the items above you can create your trader account, list each fuel by entering its BSL number and expected annual sales volume, upload one recent invoice for each fuel, pay the registration fee and submit.

For full guidance and to start the application visit:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/environment-countryside  (search “Biomass Suppliers List”).

prompt: I just had a stillbirth and need to register it. How do I know who can register the stillbirth and what documents I need to provide?
generated_response:
I’m very sorry for your loss.  
In Wales, a stillbirth (a baby born after 24 weeks of pregnancy who shows no signs of life) must be registered within 42 days at a register office.

1. Who can register the stillbirth  
   • Either parent if you are married or in a civil partnership with each other.  
   • The mother, if you are not married or in a civil partnership and the father/second parent cannot attend.  
   • The father/second parent, if you are not married or in a civil partnership and the mother cannot attend but has given written permission.  
   • If no parent can attend, one of the following may register:  
     – someone present at the birth  
     – the occupier of the hospital or house where the baby was born  
     – the person responsible for the baby after the birth (for example, a funeral director)  

2. Documents to take  
   • Medical certificate of stillbirth (Form 6) – this is given to you by the doctor or midwife and is essential.  
   • (If available) Parents’ passports, birth certificates or driving licences – these help the registrar spell names correctly, but the registration can still go ahead without them.  
   • (If married or in a civil partnership) Your marriage or civil partnership certificate – bring it if you have it.  

3. How to register  
   1. Phone the register office in the district where the stillbirth occurred and book an appointment.  
   2. Attend the appointment with the documents listed above.  
   3. Check the details the registrar enters and sign the register.  
   4. You will receive:  
      • a Certificate of Registration of Stillbirth (free)  
      • a Stillbirth Certificate (also free) – you may order extra copies for a small fee if needed.  

If you have any doubts, contact your local register office—they can confirm who should attend and what to bring.

Most relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/register-a-stillbirth

prompt: After registering my newborn's birth, what benefits can I apply for and how do I claim them?
generated_response:
Here are the main payments and help you can usually claim once your baby’s birth has been registered.

1. Child Benefit (most families qualify)  
   1. Get the Child Benefit claim form CH2 (it comes in the “Birth registration” pack or download it).  
   2. Fill it in, add your baby’s original birth certificate and post it to: Child Benefit Office, HMRC, BX9 1AS.  
   3. Payment is £24.00 a week for the first child, £15.90 for each extra child, paid every 4 weeks into your bank.  
   4. You can phone 0300 200 3100 if you need the form or help.  
   Tip: Back-dating is only allowed for 3 months, so claim as soon as you can.

2. Universal Credit (UC) or Child Tax Credit – if you already get them or your income is low  
   • Already on UC: sign in to your online account, choose “Report a change”, add your new child.  
   • Already on Tax Credits: ring 0345 300 3900 within 1 month and give the baby’s details.  
   • Not on either: use the quick eligibility checker at GOV.UK, then apply for UC online or by phone.  
   Adding a child increases your standard UC or Tax Credit award.

3. Sure Start Maternity Grant – one-off £500 payment (only if you get UC, certain legacy benefits or Child Tax Credit)  
   • Complete form SF100 (Sure Start) and post it to Freepost DWP SSMG.  
   • You must claim within 6 months of the birth.

4. Healthy Start prepaid card – help with milk, fruit, veg and vitamins (for low-income families on UC/Tax Credits)  
   • Apply online or call 0300 330 7010.  
   • Worth £8.50 a week for babies up to 1 year.

5. Tax-Free Childcare (if you pay for childcare and each parent earns under £100k)  
   • Open an online childcare account on GOV.UK.  
   • For every £8 you pay in, the government adds £2 (up to £2,000 a year).  
   • Works alongside 10 hours a week of free early education for 3- and 4-year-olds in Wales.

6. National Insurance credits for parents who stay at home  
   • Claiming Child Benefit automatically protects your State Pension record until the child is 12.

How to decide what to claim  
• Check GOV.UK “Benefits” pages or speak to Citizens Advice Wales (0800 702 2020) if you are unsure which low-income benefits you qualify for.  
• Keep your National Insurance number, bank details and the baby’s birth certificate handy when you apply.

Most relevant GOV.UK pages:  
https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit  
https://www.gov.uk/universal-credit  
https://www.gov.uk/sure-start-maternity-grant  
https://www.healthystart.nhs.uk  
https://www.gov.uk/tax-free-childcare

prompt: I'm considering using a surrogate to have a child, but I'm not sure about the legal steps involved. What should I know about becoming the legal parent after the child is born?
generated_response:
Using a surrogate does not automatically make you the legal mother in UK law – even if you are genetically related to the child.  The woman who gives birth is always treated as the child’s legal mother at birth and (where she is married or in a civil partnership) her spouse or civil partner is the second legal parent.  As an intended parent you must transfer legal parenthood to yourself after the birth.  In Scotland the usual route is a Parental Order.  

Steps to become the legal parent  

1. Check that you (and, if you have one, your partner) meet the Parental Order criteria  
   • At least one of you must be genetically related to the child.  
   • You must be over 18 and either married, in a civil partnership, living together in an enduring relationship, or applying on your own.  
   • The child must be living with you in the UK.  
   • The surrogate (and any spouse/civil partner) must freely and fully consent once the child is 6 weeks old.  

2. Register the birth  
   • The surrogate (and her spouse/civil partner, if applicable) registers the birth in Scotland and is named on the original birth certificate.  
   • Ask for a short birth certificate – you will need its details for the court application.  

3. Apply to the court for a Parental Order  
   • You can apply any time after the child is 6 weeks old and before their 6-month birthday.  
   • Use Form C51 (‘Application for a Parental Order’) and lodge it with the Sheriff Court that covers where you live.  
   • Pay the court fee (around £215) unless you qualify for help with fees.  

4. Court process  
   • A court-appointed social worker (usually from your local authority) prepares a report on the surrogacy arrangement and your suitability.  
   • The court confirms the surrogate’s consent and checks that no payments beyond “reasonable expenses” were made.  
   • If satisfied, the Sheriff grants the Parental Order, making you the legal parent(s) and extinguishing the surrogate’s parental rights.  

5. Re-register the birth  
   • Send the Parental Order to National Records of Scotland to obtain a new birth certificate showing you as the parent(s).  
   • Keep both certificates – the original remains on file but is sealed.  

6. If you do not meet the Parental Order rules  
   • Adoption is the alternative route to transfer legal parenthood.  
   • You will need to apply to the court for an Adoption Order; seek specialist legal advice.  

7. Surrogacy abroad  
   • You will still need a UK Parental Order (or Adoption Order) when you return, even if you are named on a foreign birth certificate.  
   • Check immigration and nationality rules early if the child will need a UK passport.  

8. Get advice early  
   • Speak to a solicitor experienced in Scottish surrogacy law before signing any agreement.  
   • Consider counselling for everyone involved.  
   • Keep receipts for all payments to the surrogate – the court will ask for them.  

Key GOV.UK guidance  
Surrogacy and becoming a child’s legal parent: https://www.gov.uk/becoming-a-childs-legal-parent  

If anything is unclear, or your situation is complex, contact a family law solicitor or Citizens Advice Scotland.

prompt: I want to correct a mistake in my marriage certificate, like my job title being wrong. How do I go about getting this corrected?
generated_response:
You can usually put right a simple mistake (such as an incorrect job title) on a marriage certificate by asking for a correction to the marriage register entry. The corrected entry is then used to issue new certificates.  

What to do  
1. Find the register office that holds the marriage register  
   • If you were married in a register office or approved venue, contact that local register office.  
   • If you were married in a church or other religious building, contact the register office for the district where the building is located.  

2. Ask the register office for a “marriage correction form” (sometimes called a ‘schedule 6’ form).  
   • Explain exactly what is wrong and how it should read.  
   • The form must normally be signed by one of the people who signed the original marriage entry (for example, you, your spouse, the registrar or authorised person).

3. Provide evidence that the original wording is wrong – for a job title this could be:  
   • a payslip, contract, or letter from your employer dated around the time you married  
   • any other document that clearly shows the correct occupation at the date of the marriage.

4. Pay the statutory fee  
   • £75 if the correction can be authorised locally.  
   • £90 if it has to be referred to the General Register Office (GRO).  
   The register office will confirm which fee applies and how to pay.

5. Wait for the correction to be approved  
   • Straightforward corrections are usually dealt with in a few weeks, but the GRO may take longer if they need more evidence.

6. Order new marriage certificates (optional)  
   • Once the register has been corrected you can buy updated certificates from the register office or the GRO (currently £11 each).

If you need help at any stage, the safest next step is to phone the register office that holds the record or contact the General Register Office on 0300 123 1837.

Relevant GOV.UK link:  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/births-deaths-marriages

prompt: I need to report a workplace accident that happened at my job. How do I contact the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) and what information should I provide?
generated_response:
You can report the accident directly to the Health and Safety Executive (HSE) if your employer has not already done so or if you want to make sure it is recorded.

Contact HSE  
1. In an emergency (life-threatening injury, fire, gas leak, etc.) call 999 first.  
2. For fatal or “specified” serious injuries:  
   • Phone the HSE Incident Contact Centre on 0345 300 9923 (Mon–Fri 08:30–17:00).  
3. For all other reportable accidents (e.g. over-7-day absence from work):  
   • Complete the RIDDOR online form at www.hse.gov.uk/riddor/report.htm.  
   • A copy of the report is emailed to you once you press “Submit”.  
4. If you cannot use phone or internet, post your completed form to:  
   Incident Contact Centre, HSE, Caerphilly Business Park, Caerphilly CF83 3GG.

Information to have ready  
• Your full name, job title and contact details.  
• Injured person’s details (if different): name, age, job title.  
• Employer’s name, address and main business activity.  
• Date, exact time and location of the accident.  
• Clear description of what happened and the immediate cause (machinery involved, substance spilled, etc.).  
• Nature of the injury or illness (fracture, burn, loss of consciousness, etc.) and whether the person went to hospital.  
• Estimated time the person will be off work (if known).  
• Names and contact details of any witnesses.  
• Any immediate actions taken to make the area safe or prevent a repeat.  
• The RIDDOR category you think applies (e.g. “specified injury”, “over-7-day absence”)—don’t worry if you’re unsure; HSE will advise.

Keep copies of everything you submit and note the reference number HSE gives you. If you later find new information (such as the length of absence), you can update the report by contacting the Incident Contact Centre.

prompt: What information do I need to provide when contacting the Motor Insurers' Bureau to claim compensation for damage caused by an uninsured driver?
generated_response:
To start a claim with the Motor Insurers’ Bureau (MIB) you will have to give enough information to show what happened, who was involved and what it has cost you.  Gather the following before you phone MIB or use its online claim form.

1. Your details  
   • full name, current address and contact number  
   • driving-licence number (if you were driving)  
   • vehicle registration, make, model and a copy of the V5C log-book  

2. Accident details  
   • exact date, time and place of the collision (road name or junction, town, postcode if possible)  
   • a short description of what happened, including the direction each vehicle was travelling  
   • photographs, dash-cam footage or sketches of the scene (if you have them)  

3. Other driver’s details (even if they turned out to be uninsured)  
   • registration number, make and model of their vehicle  
   • name, address and phone number (if they gave them)  
   • insurance company and policy number (if they gave one)  

4. Police information  
   • the reference number you were given when you reported the crash (often called the CAD or crime reference)  
   • name of the police force and, if you know it, the officer or station dealing with the report  
   • the date you reported the collision (must be within 24 hours for injury or 5 days for property damage)  

5. Witnesses  
   • names, addresses and phone numbers of anyone who saw the collision  

6. Evidence of your losses  
   • repair estimates, invoices or a written confirmation if your vehicle is a write-off  
   • receipts for towing, storage or other costs  
   • if you were injured: copies of medical notes, GP or hospital letters, proof of loss of earnings  

Once you have these, go to Claim compensation if you’re hit by an uninsured or untraced driver on GOV.UK (https://www.gov.uk/insurance-claims-uninsured-drivers) and follow the link to the MIB online claim portal, or call MIB on 0345 165 2800.

Keep copies of everything you send and note all dates and reference numbers.  If you are unsure about any document, ask MIB – they may accept alternative evidence.

prompt: How do I make a claim for unclaimed court money if I'm close to the last claim date?
generated_response:
If the money is held by a court in England or Wales (Court Funds Office)

1. Check the list of unclaimed balances.  
   • Go to ‘Get money from a Court Funds Office account’ on GOV.UK and download the spreadsheet of unclaimed funds.  
   • Note the court account (pay-in) number and balance that relates to you.

2. Complete form CFO 320 – ‘Request to pay money out of court’.  
   • You can fill it in on screen, then print and sign, or print a blank copy and fill it in by hand.  
   • List every person who might have an interest in the money.

3. Gather proof of identity and entitlement.  
   • One piece of photo ID (passport / driving licence).  
   • One proof of current address dated within the last 3 months (utility bill, bank statement, council-tax bill).  
   • Any paperwork linking you to the original court case (for example the court order, probate documents if claiming for someone who has died).

4. Send everything straight away.  
   • Scan the form and evidence and email them to courtenquiries@justice.gov.uk with “URGENT – unclaimed balance, deadline [date]” in the subject line.  
   • Post the signed originals or certified copies by Royal Mail Special Delivery to:  
     Court Funds Office, Sunderland, SR43 3AB.  
   • Keep your proof of posting.

5. Telephone the Court Funds Office enquiry line on 0300 0200 199 as soon as you have emailed, quote the pay-in number and ask for confirmation that your claim has been logged before the deadline.

6. Respond immediately to any follow-up requests.  Once your claim is accepted, payment is usually made by bank transfer within 4–6 weeks.

If the money is held by a Scottish court

The Court Funds Office does not deal with Scottish cases.  Contact the Accountant of Court (0131 240 6789, accountantofcourt@scotcourts.gov.uk) without delay and ask for the procedure for claiming consigned funds or unclaimed court money.  Provide the court reference and explain that you are close to the final claim date.

Most relevant GOV.UK page:  
Get money from a Court Funds Office account – https://www.gov.uk/get-court-funds-account-money

prompt: What happens if I'm caught driving under the influence of drugs and I'm not prescribed them?
generated_response:
If the police in Scotland stop you and find you’re driving after taking drugs that were not prescribed to you, you can be charged with a “drug-driving” offence. The main consequences are:  

1. Immediate police action  
   • Roadside test – the officer can use a saliva swab or field-impairment tests.  
   • Arrest – if you fail the test or they think you’re impaired, you’ll be taken to a police station for a blood or urine sample.  

2. Automatic penalties if convicted (first offence)  
   • Minimum 12-month driving ban.  
   • Unlimited fine.  
   • Up to 6 months’ imprisonment.  
   • Criminal record that stays on your driving licence for 11 years.  

3. Longer-term effects  
   • Sharply higher insurance costs (many insurers refuse cover).  
   • Possible job loss or difficulty getting work that involves driving.  
   • Travel restrictions – some countries may refuse entry with a drug-driving conviction.  

4. Aggravating circumstances  
   • If you cause death while drug-driving, the sentence can be up to life imprisonment, an unlimited fine and a minimum 5-year driving ban.  

5. No medical defence  
   • Because the drugs were not prescribed to you, the medical defence does not apply. Being over the specified limit or impaired is enough for conviction.  

6. What to do next  
   • If you’ve been charged, get legal advice immediately from a solicitor experienced in road-traffic law.  
   • Do not drive until you’re legally allowed again—doing so would be a separate “driving while disqualified” offence.  
   • If you have a drug problem, consider contacting a support service such as NHS Scotland’s helpline (0800 12 13 14).  

For full details see GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/drug-driving-law

prompt: I have a death certificate that needs to be legalised for use abroad. What are the options for getting it legalised and how long does it take?
generated_response:
If you need to present a UK death certificate overseas you must have it ‘legalised’ (an Apostille stamp from the Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office ‒ FCDO). You have two main ways to do this:

1. Standard postal service  
   1. Apply online for legalisation and pay the fee (£30 per document).  
   2. Print the cover sheet that is generated, put it, the original death certificate and a prepaid return envelope in one package, and post or courier it to the Legalisation Office, Milton Keynes.  
   3. The FCDO aims to process documents within about 2 working days of receipt, but you should allow up to 10 working days in total to cover any peak-time delays plus return delivery.  

2. Premium (same-day) service – London  
   1. Book an appointment online (slots are limited).  
   2. Take the original death certificate and your appointment confirmation to the Premium Service Desk in central London before the stated cut-off (usually 9.30 am).  
   3. Pay the premium fee (£75 per document).  
   4. Collect the legalised document later the same day (normally after 3.30 pm).  
   Note: you (or a representative/courier) have to attend in person – it cannot be done by post.  

Alternative: If you cannot travel, a commercial legalisation agent can handle either service on your behalf for an extra charge.

Key timings  
• Standard post: allow around 1–2 weeks door-to-door.  
• Premium in-person: same working day once you attend.  

Full service details, current fees and booking links are on GOV.UK under ‘Get a document legalised’. Start from: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: I need to report a concern about an attorney managing someone else's finances. What steps should I follow to ensure my report is handled properly?
generated_response:
If you think an attorney (or deputy) is mis-using someone’s money, the Office of the Public Guardian (OPG) is the body that investigates.  
Follow these steps.

1. Check it is an OPG matter  
   • OPG can act if there is a registered Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA), Enduring Power of Attorney (EPA) or a deputy order from the Court of Protection.  
   • If there is no power of attorney and you believe a crime is being committed, report it to the police instead.

2. Gather the key information  
   • The donor’s full name, address and date of birth.  
   • The attorney’s (or deputy’s) full name and address.  
   • Details of what you think is going wrong – dates, amounts, bank details, receipts, witness names, anything you have seen or heard.  
   • Your own contact details (you can ask OPG not to share these with the attorney).

3. Make the report to OPG  
   • Online: fill in the “Report a concern about an attorney or deputy” form on GOV.UK.  
   • Phone: 0300 456 0300 (choose option 3).  
   • Email or post: safeguardingunit@publicguardian.gov.uk or Office of the Public Guardian, PO Box 16185, Birmingham B2 2WH.

4. If the person is in immediate danger  
   • Call 999, or  
   • Contact the local adult safeguarding team via the council.

5. After you report  
   • OPG will acknowledge your report and may ask for more evidence.  
   • They can ask the attorney for explanations, request bank statements, visit the donor, suspend or remove the attorney, or refer the case to the Court of Protection or the police.

Keep copies of anything you send and note the date you made the report.

Relevant GOV.UK link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

prompt: How can I ensure my employer handles my flexible working request reasonably?
generated_response:
You can’t force your employer to say “yes”, but you can take practical steps to make sure they follow the law and treat your request fairly.

1. Check you qualify  
   • You must be an employee and have worked for the school for at least 26 weeks.  
   • You can make one statutory request every 12 months.

2. Read the school’s flexible-working policy  
   • Most schools have one in the staff handbook or on the intranet.  
   • Note who to write to, what to include and any deadlines tied to the academic year.

3. Make a clear written request (a “statutory request”)  
   Include:  
   a. The date and a statement that it is a statutory request for flexible working.  
   b. The change you want (e.g. three days a week, late start on Fridays).  
   c. When you’d like it to start.  
   d. How you think it will affect pupils, colleagues and cover, plus your ideas to deal with that.  
   e. A line confirming whether you’ve made a previous request and the date of it.  
   Keep a copy.

4. Engage constructively in the meeting  
   • Your employer should invite you to discuss the request; you can take a colleague or union rep.  
   • Use the meeting to answer concerns and suggest compromises or a trial period.

5. Expect a decision within three months (unless you both agree a longer period)  
   • The decision must be in writing and give one of the eight legal business reasons if it’s refused (e.g. “detrimental impact on quality”).  
   • You should be offered an appeal if refused.

6. Keep records  
   • Save emails, meeting notes and the written outcome. This is vital if you later challenge the process.

7. If you think the process wasn’t reasonable  
   a. Use the school’s appeal or grievance procedure.  
   b. Contact ACAS Early Conciliation within three months minus one day of the decision if it’s still unresolved.  
   c. You can then take a claim to an employment tribunal for failing to handle the request reasonably or for discrimination.

Helpful guidance: https://www.gov.uk/flexible-working

prompt: I work as a nurse in Scotland and recently had a grievance about my workload. How do I formally raise this and what should I expect during the process?
generated_response:
If you believe your workload is unreasonable and an informal chat hasn’t resolved it, you can make a formal grievance. The usual steps are:

1. Find the policy  
   • Look up the NHS Scotland Grievance Policy on your Board’s intranet or ask HR for a copy.  
   • Note any time-limits for bringing a grievance (often 3 months from the issue).

2. Put your grievance in writing  
   • State that it is a “formal grievance”.  
   • Describe the workload problem, when it began, how it affects patient care and your wellbeing, and what outcome you want (for example, a workload review or extra staff).  
   • Send it to the named person in the policy—usually your line manager or HR.

3. Attend the grievance meeting  
   • You should be invited to a meeting within a reasonable time (usually 5–10 working days).  
   • Take a trade-union rep or work colleague for support.  
   • Explain the issue, provide evidence (rota copies, incident reports, emails), and suggest solutions.

4. Receive the decision  
   • You’ll get the decision in writing, normally within 5 working days of the meeting.  
   • The letter should set out any action the employer will take.

5. Appeal if needed  
   • If you think the decision is wrong or the process was unfair, write an appeal within the timeframe in the policy (often 10 working days).  
   • A more senior manager—who has not been involved before—will hear the appeal.

6. After the internal process  
   • If the appeal doesn’t resolve matters, speak to your union or ACAS (0300 123 1100) about early conciliation and, if appropriate, an Employment Tribunal claim (must normally be started within 3 months minus 1 day of the last incident).

What to expect  
• Confidentiality: only staff involved in resolving the issue will see your paperwork.  
• Fair treatment: you must not be victimised for raising a grievance.  
• Right to be accompanied: by a union rep or colleague at every formal meeting.  
• Records: you’ll receive notes of meetings and decisions. Keep your own copies too.

Useful link  
GOV.UK – Problems at work overview: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I've been told my job might be affected by a change in our company's operations. What steps should I take to understand if I'm at risk of redundancy and what my rights are during this process?
generated_response:
It helps to break the situation into four areas: finding out what is happening, checking whether you are at risk, understanding your legal rights, and preparing next steps.

1. Ask for clear information  
   1.1 Write to your head-teacher or HR department asking whether any formal redundancy process has started and whether your role is “in scope”.  
   1.2 If your workplace has a trade-union rep, arrange a meeting. Unions must be consulted where 20 or more redundancies are proposed.  
   1.3 Request a copy of any business-case or “at risk” letters relating to your post.

2. Look for the formal signs of redundancy  
   • A written “at risk” or “consultation” letter.  
   • An invitation to a consultation meeting.  
   • A selection-criteria “matrix” showing how posts will be chosen.  
   • Notice of when any final decision will be made.

3. Know your key rights  
   • Fair selection – the criteria must be objective and not discriminate (for example by age or gender).  
   • Individual consultation – at least one meeting to discuss alternatives, even if fewer than 20 jobs are affected.  
   • Statutory notice – 1 week per full year of service (max 12 weeks) or any longer period in your contract.  
   • Statutory redundancy pay – if you have 2 years’ service:  
     – 1.5 weeks’ pay for each full year aged 41 or over,  
     – 1 week per year aged 22-40,  
     – 0.5 week per year aged under 22,  
     capped at 20 years’ service and a weekly pay limit (£643 from Apr 2023).  
   • Time off to look for work or training (paid up to 40% of a normal week) if you have 2 years’ service.  
   • The right to appeal the decision or raise a grievance if the process seems unfair.

4. Gather evidence and alternatives  
   4.1 Collect recent appraisals, job description and any emails that show the value of your role.  
   4.2 Ask about redeployment to other teaching posts within the authority or flexible options such as part-time or job-share.  
   4.3 Consider pension implications; teachers in Scotland can take pension from age 55 but reductions may apply—contact the Scottish Public Pensions Agency for a forecast.

5. Get independent advice early  
   • Contact ACAS on 0300 123 1100 for free, confidential advice.  
   • If you belong to the EIS or another teaching union, use their legal team.  
   • Citizens Advice Scotland can review the redundancy letter and pay calculation.  
   • For complex cases, a specialist employment solicitor can review within the 3-month time limit for tribunal claims.

Useful GOV.UK pages  
• Redundancy: your rights – https://www.gov.uk/redundancy-your-rights  
• Statutory redundancy pay calculator – https://www.gov.uk/calculate-your-redundancy-pay

prompt: I'm starting a small business and need to hire staff. How do I determine the correct employment status for each role and what are the legal implications?
generated_response:
Before you advertise or agree any terms, you need to decide whether each person will be:  
• an employee  
• a worker (casual or zero-hours)  
• self-employed (independent contractor)  

Each category has different rights and triggers different obligations for you.

1. List the tasks and work pattern for each role.  
   • How much control will you keep over hours, place of work and how the work is done?  
   • Will you supply the tools or equipment?  
   • Can the person send someone else to do the work (substitute)?  
   • Is there a continuing obligation for you to offer work and for them to accept it?

2. Use HMRC’s ‘Check employment status for tax’ (CEST) tool to confirm whether HMRC will treat the role as employed or self-employed for tax. Keep a copy of the result.  

3. Match the facts to the legal categories:  
   a) Employee – you control the work, it’s ongoing, they must do the work personally.  
      • You must run PAYE, deduct tax/NICs, pay at least National Minimum Wage, give paid holiday, sick pay, automatic pension enrolment, parental leave, protection from unfair dismissal (after 2 years) and redundancy pay.  
   b) Worker – fewer mutual obligations, often irregular hours.  
      • You must pay NMW, holiday pay, protect health and safety, and avoid unlawful discrimination. PAYE usually still applies if they are not invoicing you as a business.  
   c) Self-employed contractor – they decide how/when to work, can make a profit or loss, usually provide their own kit and can send a substitute.  
      • They invoice you and pay their own tax/NICs. You must still meet health and safety duties and avoid discrimination. Off-payroll rules (IR35) may apply if the contractor works through their own company.

4. Issue the correct paperwork:  
   • Employees and workers must receive a ‘written statement of employment particulars’ on or before their first day.  
   • Contractors should receive a clear contract for services setting out deliverables, fees and invoicing.  

5. Register as an employer with HMRC before the first payday if you have any employees or workers paid through PAYE.

6. Keep records (contracts, CEST output, hours worked, payments) for at least 3 years in case HMRC or an employment tribunal asks for evidence.

If you are unsure, get professional advice or check the detailed guidance under “Employing people” on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: Can I bring my family with me if I get an International Sportsperson visa and what are the rules around working a second job?
generated_response:
Yes.  If you are granted an International Sportsperson visa you can:

1. Bring dependants  
   a. Who qualifies  
      • Your husband, wife, civil partner or an unmarried partner you have lived with for at least 2 years.  
      • Your children under 18 (including those born in the UK) – children 18 or over can only stay as dependants if they’re already in the UK on your current visa.  

   b. Money you must show (unless your sponsor ticks the “maintenance” box on your certificate of sponsorship)  
      • £1,270 for yourself  
      • £285 for your partner  
      • £315 for the first child  
      • £200 for each additional child  
      The money must have been in your or your partner’s bank account for 28 consecutive days before the application.  

   c. How they apply  
      • They apply online as a “dependant of an International Sportsperson”.  
      • They pay the visa fee and the immigration health surcharge.  
      • Their visa will end on the same date as yours.  

   d. What they can do  
      • Partners can work (except as a professional sportsperson or coach) and study.  
      • Children can attend school or college.  

2. Take a second job  
   a. Automatic allowance (no extra application needed)  
      • Up to 20 hours a week, outside the hours of your sponsored job.  
      • The second job must be either:  
        – in the same occupation code as your sponsored job, or  
        – on the Home Office shortage occupation list.  

   b. Anything beyond this  
      • If you want to work more than 20 hours a week, or in a different type of job, you must apply to update your visa with a new certificate of sponsorship for the additional employer before you start the extra work.  

For full guidance and the online application forms see GOV-UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: My Start-up visa is ending, and I want to continue working in the UK. Can I switch to an Innovator Founder visa, and what does that involve?
generated_response:
Yes – you can apply inside the UK to switch from a Start-up visa to an Innovator Founder visa, provided you meet the Innovator Founder requirements.  
Below is what the process usually involves.

1. Check you qualify  
   • Your business must be innovative, viable and scalable, and either:  
     a) be the same business you developed on your Start-up visa, and it still meets the Innovator Founder criteria, or  
     b) be a new idea that meets those criteria.  
   • You need an endorsement letter dated within the last 3 months from an approved endorsing body.  
   • You must show you can speak English to at least CEFR level B2 (normally already met on the Start-up route).  
   • You must have enough money to support yourself (£1,270 held for 28 days unless already in the UK for 12 + months).

2. Secure an endorsement  
   • Contact an approved endorsing body, submit your business plan and evidence of progress.  
   • If satisfied, they will issue the endorsement letter needed for the application (fee set by the body).

3. Apply online before your Start-up visa expires  
   • Complete the Innovator Founder visa (switch in-country) form on GOV.UK.  
   • Pay the application fee (£1,036) and the immigration health surcharge (about £624 per year).  
   • Upload supporting documents (passport, endorsement letter, bank statements, etc.).  
   • Book an appointment to give fingerprints and a photo at a UK Visa and Citizenship Application Services (UKVCAS) centre.

4. Wait for a decision  
   • Standard processing is up to 8 weeks once biometrics are given.  
   • You can stay in the UK and keep working on your Start-up business while you wait.

5. After you receive the visa  
   • The Innovator Founder visa is normally granted for 3 years.  
   • You must meet your endorsing body for progress checks at 12 and 24 months.  
   • After 3 years you may be able to apply for settlement (‘indefinite leave to remain’) if your business meets the success criteria.

If you are unsure about any requirement, consider getting professional immigration advice or speak to an approved endorsing body.

Relevant GOV.UK link:  
Visas and immigration – Innovator Founder visa (switch in the UK): https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: I'm planning to visit my family in the UK, but I'm not sure if I need a visa or an ETA. How do I decide which one to apply for?
generated_response:
To work out whether you need (a) a Visitor visa, (b) the new Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA), or (c) no application at all, the Home Office first looks at the passport you will travel on. Some nationalities must get an ETA, some must get a Visitor visa, and a few do not need either for short family visits.  

Could you let me know which country issued your passport (and whether you already have any UK immigration status, such as settled status)? Once I have that, I can point you to the right option and the steps to apply.

prompt: I'm a child and my relative in the UK has protection status. Can I apply to join them, and what do I need to do?
generated_response:
You may be able to come to the UK under the “refugee family reunion” rules, but it depends on your exact relationship to the person who has refugee status or humanitarian protection.  

1. Check if you qualify  
   • You must be under 18 and be the child of the person who was granted refugee status or humanitarian protection in the UK.  
   • If you are not their child (for example you are a grand-child, niece/nephew, brother or sister), you usually cannot use the family-reunion route unless there are “exceptional circumstances”. In that case you would need specialist immigration advice.  

2. Make the family-reunion application (if you qualify)  
   1. Apply from outside the UK – the application is free.  
   2. Complete online form VAF4A and Appendix 4.  
   3. Upload evidence:  
      • Your full birth certificate showing both parents’ names.  
      • Evidence of the parent’s refugee or humanitarian-protection status (Home Office grant letter or BRP).  
      • Passports or travel documents for you and, if possible, your parent.  
      • Any custody or adoption papers if relevant.  
      • Certified translations of any documents not in English or Welsh.  
   4. Book a biometrics appointment at a visa application centre to give fingerprints, photo and submit your passport.  

3. After you apply  
   • You will normally get a decision within 12 weeks, though it can take longer.  
   • If approved, you will receive a 30-day entry vignette in your passport. Travel to the UK within that period.  
   • On arrival, collect your Biometric Residence Permit (BRP) within 10 days. Your leave will normally last for the same period as your parent’s protection status.  

4. If you do not qualify as a child of the refugee/protected person  
   • Seek advice from a regulated immigration adviser as other routes (e.g. “exceptional circumstances”, child dependent visa, or a student visa) may be possible but have different requirements and fees.  

Most people start by reading the family-reunion guidance on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/visas-immigration

prompt: I'm applying for early years pupil premium because my child is 3 and I get 15 hours free childcare. How do I know if I qualify and what steps should I take to apply?
generated_response:
Early Years Pupil Premium (EYPP) is only available in England. If you live in Scotland you can’t apply for EYPP, but you may be able to get other support through your local council’s early learning and childcare schemes.  

If you do live in England, you can check and apply as follows.  

Eligibility  
You may qualify if your 3- or 4-year-old gets the universal 15 free hours and you receive at least one of these:  
1. Income-related benefits (e.g. Universal Credit with household income ≤ £15,400 after tax, Child Tax Credit and income ≤ £16,190, Income Support, income-based JSA or ESA).  
2. Working Tax Credit run-on.  
3. Support under the Immigration and Asylum Act 1999.  
4. The child is looked after by a local authority, has left care under a special guardianship order, child arrangements order or adoption order.

How to apply  
1. Ask your childcare provider (nursery, childminder or school) for their EYPP form.  
2. Fill in the parent/carer details, including your National Insurance number (or NASS number if you have one).  
3. Return the form to the provider; they pass it to the local authority, who will run an eligibility check.  
4. The local authority pays the extra funding directly to your provider (up to £302 per year); it doesn’t affect any benefits you receive.  
5. The provider should tell you how they plan to use the money to support your child.

If you live in Scotland  
Contact your local council’s early learning and childcare team to ask what additional funding or support is available alongside the funded 1,140 hours for 3- and 4-year-olds. Each council has its own schemes for children who need extra help.

Further information  
GOV.UK childcare and parenting pages: https://www.gov.uk/browse/childcare-parenting

prompt: I'm a retired individual in Wales and I'm considering applying for the disability access fund for my child's early years education. What are the requirements and how do I apply?
generated_response:
Answer:
The Disability Access Fund (DAF) is an England-only scheme that gives early years providers a one-off payment of £910 per year for each eligible child so they can make reasonable adjustments (for example, buy specialist equipment).  

Because you live in Wales, your child will not qualify for DAF. Instead, support for disabled children in early years settings is organised by your local authority through the Early Years Additional Support Grant (sometimes called the Additional Needs or Inclusion Grant).  

What to do next  
1. Contact the Family Information Service (FIS) for your Welsh local authority. Explain that your child has a disability and you need help with early years or childcare costs.  
2. Ask about:  
   • Early Years Additional Support or Inclusion Funding (to help the setting meet your child’s needs).  
   • Whether your child can receive extra hours or financial help under the Childcare Offer for Wales.  
3. If your child gets Disability Living Allowance (DLA), keep the award letter handy—you will usually need to give a copy to the childcare provider or the council as evidence.  
4. Choose a registered childcare or nursery setting. The setting will complete the funding application to the council and may ask you to sign a short declaration.  
5. The council pays the grant directly to the setting; there is no impact on your benefits or tax position.

If your child will be attending a setting in England (for example, you live near the border), DAF may apply. In that case:  
• The child must be aged 3 or 4, receiving DLA, and taking up the 15-hour universal early education entitlement.  
• Give the provider a copy of the DLA award letter.  
• The provider claims the £910 from the English local authority; you do not apply yourself.

You can find general guidance on childcare support here: https://www.gov.uk/browse/childcare-parenting

Justification:
• DAF is only available in England, so the user’s Welsh address makes Welsh schemes more relevant.  
• Eligibility always hinges on the child (age 3–4 and receiving DLA), not the parent’s employment or income.  
• In both nations the claim is made by the childcare provider, so steps focus on contacting the provider and council, not filling in a personal application form.  
• Pointing the user to their local Family Information Service is the standard first step recommended by Welsh Government guidance.  
• Including the cross-border note covers the possibility the child attends an English setting.

Missing info: None

prompt: I'm considering adopting a child and I'm unsure about the age requirements. Can you explain the age limits for adopting a child in England and Wales?
generated_response:
In England and Wales you must be:

1. At least 21 years old on the date you apply to adopt.  
2. There is no legal upper age limit, but adoption agencies will look at whether you have the health, energy and life-expectancy to parent a child through to adulthood. Many agencies use a guideline of being no more than about 45–50 years older than the child, but they can and do approve older applicants where this is in the child’s interests.  

If you meet the minimum age and the agency is satisfied you can meet a child’s long-term needs, age alone will not prevent you adopting.  

For full eligibility details and next steps, see:  
https://www.gov.uk/child-adoption/eligibility

prompt: I need to sign up for Tax-Free Childcare as a childcare provider. What do I need to provide and how do I get started?
generated_response:
To receive Tax-Free Childcare payments from parents you first need to register with HMRC as an approved childcare provider.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must be regulated by Care Inspectorate Wales (CIW). Have your CIW registration number to hand.  
   • You need a UK bank account in the name you trade under.

2. Gather the details HMRC will ask for  
   • CIW registration- or certificate-number.  
   • Business or trading name and address.  
   • Bank account sort code and account number.  
   • Your National Insurance number (or Unique Taxpayer Reference if you’re a company/partnership).  
   • An email address and phone number they can contact you on.

3. Set up (or sign in with) a Government Gateway user ID  
   • If you do not already have one, you can create it during the sign-up. Keep the user ID and password safe – you’ll need them to manage your account later.

4. Sign up online  
   • Go to GOV.UK and search “sign up for Tax-Free Childcare as a provider”, or use: https://www.gov.uk/sign-up-childcare-provider  
   • Log in with your Government Gateway details.  
   • Follow the on-screen steps to add your childcare setting, enter your bank details and accept the provider terms.

5. Wait for confirmation  
   • HMRC will usually email you within 10 working days to confirm you’re activated.  
   • Once approved your setting appears in the Tax-Free Childcare search and parents can start sending payments.

6. Tell parents your setting name or postcode  
   • Parents use this to link their online childcare account to you.  
   • Payments arrive in your bank account three working days after the parent makes them.

If you run more than one setting, repeat the add-a-setting step for each.  

Need help? Call the Childcare Service Helpline on 0300 123 4097 (Mon–Sat) or read the guidance under “Childcare providers” on GOV.UK.

prompt: I'm 67 and want to pay voluntary Class 3 National Insurance to fill gaps in my record. How long will it take for my payment to update my National Insurance record and when can I expect to see changes in my State Pension forecast?
generated_response:
Most payments of voluntary Class 3 National Insurance reach your National Insurance record in about 6 to 8 weeks after HMRC receive the money. At busy times it can take up to 12 weeks.  

Once the year has been added to your record, the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) picks up the change automatically. Your State Pension forecast (or the amount you’re already getting if you’ve claimed) usually updates within a further 1–2 weeks.  

What to do  
1. Pay the Class 3 amount using the 18-digit reference HMRC gave you and keep the receipt.  
2. Wait 8 weeks, then sign in to ‘Check your National Insurance record’ on GOV.UK to see if the year now shows as ‘full’.  
3. When the record shows the new year, go to ‘Check your State Pension’ and get a fresh forecast (or look for a revised award letter if you’re already receiving your pension).  
4. If the payment has not appeared after 12 weeks, phone HMRC’s National Insurance helpline and give them your payment details.  
5. If the record is updated but your pension figure has not changed after another couple of weeks, contact the Future Pension Centre (if you have not yet claimed) or the Pension Service (if you have) and ask for a review.  

Relevant GOV.UK pages  
• Voluntary National Insurance contributions: https://www.gov.uk/voluntary-national-insurance-contributions  
• Check your National Insurance record: https://www.gov.uk/check-national-insurance-record  
• Check your State Pension: https://www.gov.uk/check-state-pension

prompt: If my VAT liability decreases by 20% or more, can I request a reduction in my payments on account, and how do I go about doing that?
generated_response:
Yes.  If you expect your net VAT liability for the current year to be 20 % or more below the amount HMRC used to set your Payments on Account (POA), you can ask HMRC to reduce the instalments.

What to do

1. Work out your likely net VAT liability for the whole accounting year and make sure the reduction is at least 20 % below the reference year HMRC used.  
   • Keep supporting evidence (e.g. management accounts, sales forecasts, signed contracts).

2. Complete form VAT 9 ‘Application to adjust payments on account’.  
   • You can download the form from GOV.UK or phone the VAT Helpline (0300 200 3700) to ask for a copy.

3. Send the form, together with your evidence, to:  
   VAT Written Enquiries Team  
   HM Revenue & Customs  
   Alexander House  
   21 Victoria Avenue  
   Southend-on-Sea  SS99 1BD

4. Carry on paying your existing POA amounts until HMRC writes back confirming the new (lower) figures and the date they take effect.

5. When you submit your normal VAT Return, pay or reclaim any balance in the usual way.

Key points

• You can only ask for one reduction in each accounting year.  
• If HMRC agrees, the change applies to the remaining instalments for that year; they will not refund amounts you have already paid.  
• Tell HMRC at once if circumstances change and the reduction is no longer justified—penalties can apply if you knowingly under-pay.

Relevant GOV.UK page: https://www.gov.uk/guidance/vat-payments-on-account

prompt: I'm part of a community amateur sports club in Wales. What tax reliefs are available to us and how do we ensure we're compliant with tax regulations?
generated_response:
Community Amateur Sports Clubs (CASCs) registered with HMRC can benefit from several UK-wide tax reliefs that apply equally in Wales. To make sure you receive them and stay compliant:

1. Check you meet – and keep meeting – CASC conditions  
   • Open to the whole community (no discrimination, membership fee limits, etc.)  
   • Amateur and non-profit making (surpluses reinvested)  
   • Main purpose: providing facilities and promoting participation in eligible sports  
   • 50% or more of members must participate in the sport  
   • Income limits: trading income under £100,000 a year and property income under £50,000 a year  

2. Register (or stay registered) with HMRC  
   • Complete form CASC(A1) and send it with your governing document and accounts.  
   • Keep HMRC informed of any changes to your constitution, activities or income levels.

3. Claim the available tax reliefs  
   a) Corporation Tax  
      • Exemption on trading income up to £100,000 and property income up to £50,000.  
      • Interest and dividend income are fully exempt.  
      • File a Corporation Tax return only if you exceed the exempt limits or HMRC asks for one.  
   b) Gift Aid on donations  
      • Individual cash gifts qualify; you can reclaim 25p for every £1 donated.  
      • Keep donor Gift Aid declarations and records for 6 years.  
   c) Relief on business rates  
      • Mandatory 80% relief from local authority; they may grant up to 100% discretionary relief.  
      • Apply to your county or county-borough council in Wales and give proof of CASC status.  
   d) Stamp Duty Land Tax and Capital Gains Tax  
      • Exemption when selling assets or property if gains are reinvested in the club’s sporting purposes.  
   e) VAT  
      • CASCs follow normal VAT rules. If taxable turnover exceeds the registration threshold (£85,000) you must register and can claim input VAT on eligible costs.

4. Keep good records  
   • Minute books, membership lists, accounts and Gift Aid paperwork.  
   • Retain for at least 6 years in case HMRC requests them.

5. Review annually  
   • Check income thresholds and membership fees.  
   • Submit annual accounts to members and update HMRC if circumstances change.

Useful GOV.UK pages:  
Community Amateur Sports Clubs: https://www.gov.uk/register-a-community-amateur-sports-club  
Running a CASC and tax reliefs: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/community-amateur-sports-clubs-detailed-guidance-notes  
Gift Aid for charities and CASCs: https://www.gov.uk/claim-gift-aid  

If in doubt, speak to HMRC’s Charities helpline or a qualified adviser specialising in CASCs.

prompt: I'm starting a new limited company and need to understand how to handle Corporation Tax. What are the key steps I should follow to ensure I'm compliant?
generated_response:
To stay on top of Corporation Tax for your new limited company, follow these steps.

1. Register for Corporation Tax  
   • Do this with HMRC within 3 months of starting any business activity (selling, advertising, employing staff, etc.).  
   • You can register online at the same time as you register your company with Companies House or afterwards using your company’s Government Gateway account.

2. Keep accurate accounting records  
   • Maintain bookkeeping software or spreadsheets that show all income, costs, assets, and liabilities.  
   • Keep invoices, receipts and bank statements for at least 6 years.

3. Choose your accounting period  
   • Your first accounting period starts on your company’s incorporation date and usually ends on the last day of that month the following year.  
   • Tell HMRC if you need to shorten or lengthen it.

4. Work out your taxable profits  
   • Start with your profit and loss figure, then add back non-deductible expenses (e.g. client entertainment) and deduct any allowable reliefs (e.g. capital allowances).

5. File a Company Tax Return (CT600)  
   • Deadline: 12 months after the end of each accounting period.  
   • Submit it online using HMRC software or commercial software. Attach full statutory accounts in iXBRL format.

6. Pay your Corporation Tax  
   • Deadline: 9 months + 1 day after the end of your accounting period.  
   • Pay electronically by Faster Payments, CHAPS, Bacs, debit card, or Direct Debit.  
   • Interest is charged on late payments.

7. Claim any reliefs and allowances  
   • Small companies can use the £2,000 Employment Allowance to cut National Insurance, which reduces taxable profits.  
   • Consider R&D Tax Relief, Annual Investment Allowance, or the Patent Box if relevant to your activities.

8. Plan for quarterly instalments (large companies only)  
   • If your taxable profits are above £1.5 million (shared between associated companies), you may have to pay in quarterly instalments. Most new small businesses fall below this.

9. Keep HMRC up to date  
   • Report changes such as a new accounting period, a different company address, or if you become dormant.  
   • Close your Corporation Tax account when you wind up the company.

Key link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm looking for free internet access and help using it in my area. How do I find the nearest Online Centres Network centre?
generated_response:
You can locate your nearest Online Centres Network (now run by Good Things Foundation) in a couple of minutes.

1. Go to the “Find a Centre” page: https://www.learnmyway.com/find-a-centre  
2. Type your home postcode or town (e.g. “CF10 1EP” or “Cardiff”) into the search box and press Enter.  
3. A map and list of centres will appear, showing:  
   • the centre name and address  
   • distance from your postcode  
   • phone/email details so you can book a slot or ask questions.  
4. Pick the centre that suits you and contact them to confirm opening hours, availability of free internet access and any digital-skills sessions.  
5. If you cannot use the website:  
   • Call Good Things Foundation on 0114 349 1666 and ask them to search for you.  
   • Or ask your local library or council customer-service point to look up centres in your area.

Most centres offer: free or low-cost internet access, one-to-one help, and beginner-friendly courses such as Learn My Way.  

If none of the listed centres are convenient, your local library often provides free Wi-Fi and basic digital help—check your council’s “libraries” page on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services/libraries

prompt: I need to find my local council's website to access housing services. How do I do this step by step?
generated_response:
Follow these steps to locate your local council’s website and its housing services:

1. Open a web browser and go to GOV-UK’s “Find your local council” service: www.gov.uk/find-local-council  
2. Enter your home postcode, then press ‘Find’.  
3. The next page will show the name of your local council. Click the council’s name – this takes you to its official website.  
4. Once on the council site, look for a section called “Housing”, “Housing services”, or similar (often listed under “Resident services” or “Council services”).  
5. From there you can:  
   a. Apply for social housing or housing benefit.  
   b. Report repairs or problems with your council property.  
   c. Get advice on homelessness or private renting.  
   d. Find contact details if you need to speak to a housing officer.

Tip: If you can’t see a clear “Housing” link, use the site’s search box and type “housing” or “homelessness”.

Most Welsh councils also publish a direct phone number for their housing team on the same page if you prefer to talk to someone.

prompt: Who can appeal a listed building consent decision?
generated_response:
Only the person who applied for the listed building consent (or someone authorised to act for them, such as an agent or solicitor) can appeal the local planning authority’s decision. Neighbours, local groups and other third parties do not have a right of appeal, though they can make representations during the application process.

You must submit the appeal to the Planning Inspectorate within:
1. 6 months of the date on the decision letter if consent was refused or granted with conditions you disagree with, or  
2. 6 months from the date the authority should have decided, if no decision was made.  

Guidance and online appeal form: https://www.gov.uk/appeal-planning-decision

prompt: What should I do if I think there's an illegal sale of alcohol happening in my area?
generated_response:
If you suspect alcohol is being sold illegally, take these steps:

1. Make a note of what you saw  
   • location, dates and times  
   • description of the people involved  
   • what was being sold and how (e.g. from a house, after licensed hours)  

2. Check the risk  
   • If violence or danger is happening now, call 999.  
   • If it is not an emergency, call the police on 101 or contact your local council’s licensing team.

3. Report it to your local council’s licensing or trading standards team  
   • Use the council’s website or phone number.  
   • You can find the correct council here: https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council

4. You can also report anonymously to Crimestoppers on 0800 555 111 or online.

5. Keep any evidence safe (photos, receipts) but do not put yourself at risk.

The council or police will investigate and can take action such as fines, licence suspension or prosecution.

prompt: How can I find out about local consultations in my area in Wales?
generated_response:
You can keep track of upcoming or open consultations in your local area by following a few quick steps.

1. Identify your local authority  
   • Use “Find your local council” on GOV.UK (https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council) and note the council’s web address.  
   • For most of Wales this will be your county or county-borough council (for example, Cardiff Council, Gwynedd Council).

2. Check the council’s “Consultations” or “Have your say” page  
   • Almost every Welsh local authority keeps a dedicated page listing current consultations, surveys and public meetings.  
   • Pages usually let you sign up for email alerts or RSS feeds.

3. Visit the Welsh Government consultation hub  
   • Go to https://www.gov.wales/consultations for national-level consultations that may still have local events or online responses.  
   • You can filter by subject or region and subscribe for updates.

4. Set up automatic alerts  
   • On GOV.UK, create an email alert for “local consultations” plus your council name, or use Google Alerts with the same keywords.  
   • Follow your council on social media (Twitter/X, Facebook or Nextdoor) where new consultations are often promoted first.

5. Ask at local libraries or community hubs  
   • Councils send printed consultation notices to libraries, community centres and town councils—helpful if you prefer offline information.  

6. Contact the council directly  
   • The consultation or democratic services team can add you to stakeholder mailing lists or tell you when topics you care about are coming up.

Relevant GOV.UK link  
Housing and local services hub: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: Can I find out how to dispose of garden waste through my council's website?
generated_response:
Yes. Here’s the quickest way to get to the right page on your council’s website:

1. Go to GOV.UK and search for “Check your bin collection day” or use this direct link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services  
2. Select “Bins and recycling”.  
3. Enter your postcode when prompted and choose your address.  
4. You’ll be taken to your local council’s website. Look for a section called “Garden waste”, “Green waste” or “Garden bin collections”.  
5. The page will normally tell you:  
   • Whether the council collects garden waste, and on which days  
   • If you need to sign up (some councils charge an annual fee or require a permit)  
   • How to order or replace a garden-waste bin or sacks  
   • What can and can’t go in the garden-waste bin  
   • Where your nearest household recycling centre is if you’d rather take it yourself  
6. If you can’t find the information once on your council’s site, use the site search for “garden waste” or call the council’s customer-services number (usually shown in the footer of the page).

prompt: I found some illegal posters on my street and want to report them. How do I find the correct authority to report this issue and what information do I need to provide?
generated_response:
If posters have been put up without permission, your local council is usually the body that can remove them or take enforcement action.

1. Find the right council  
   • Go to GOV.UK and use the “Find your local council” tool: https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council  
   • Enter your postcode and note the council’s website and phone number.

2. Report the posters  
   • Look on the council’s website for sections called “street cleansing”, “environmental crime”, “fly-posting” or “highways”.  
   • If you cannot find an online form, phone the council’s general enquiries line and ask how to report fly-posting.

3. Give clear details  
   • Exact location (street name, nearest house number or landmark, postcode).  
   • Description of each poster (size, content, what it’s fixed to).  
   • How many posters you can see.  
   • When you first noticed them.  
   • Photos if you can take them safely (attach to the form or offer to email).

4. Keep a note  
   • Record the date you reported the issue and any reference number the council gives you, in case you need to follow it up.

prompt: I was issued a fine for not following the rules around drivers' hours. How do I pay it and what happens if I don't?
generated_response:
How to pay the fine  
1. Find the Fixed Penalty Notice or deposit notice you were given – it shows the reference number, amount and the deadline (usually 28 days).  
2. Choose one of these payment methods:  
   a. Online – go to https://www.gov.uk/pay-dvsa-fixed-penalty, enter the notice reference, then pay with a debit or credit card. The service is open 24 hours a day.  
   b. By phone – call the HMCTS Fixed Penalty Office on 0300 123 1487 (Monday to Friday, 9 am to 5 pm). Have your reference number and card ready.  
   c. By post – send a cheque or postal order (payable to ‘HMCTS’) with the payment slip to the address printed on the notice. Allow time for delivery.  
3. Keep the confirmation or receipt – it is your proof that the penalty has been settled.  

What happens if you do not pay on time  
• The fine is automatically registered as a court debt.  
• The amount increases by 50% and an additional £9 victim surcharge is added.  
• HM Courts and Tribunals Service can pass the debt to enforcement agents (bailiffs) who may seize goods, clamp a vehicle or take money directly from earnings.  
• DVSA can also immobilise or prohibit the use of the vehicle until any outstanding amount (and storage fees, if applicable) is paid.  
• Unpaid court debts can affect your credit rating and future borrowing.  

Paying by the deadline avoids extra costs and enforcement action. If you think the notice is wrong, contact the Fixed Penalty Office straight away – do not ignore it.

prompt: I'm 65 and want to start learning to drive again. What steps do I need to follow to get a provisional licence and prepare for my theory test?
generated_response:
Here’s what to do to get back on the road.

1. Check you’re eligible  
   • You must be able to read a car number plate from 20 metres (with glasses / contacts if you use them).  
   • There is no upper age limit, but you need to meet the medical standards for driving.  

2. Apply for a car (category B) provisional licence  
   a) Online (fastest)  
      • Go to GOV.UK → Apply for your first provisional driving licence.  
      • Have ready: a valid UK passport (or another identity document), your National Insurance number, your addresses for the last 3 years, and a debit or credit card (£34 fee).  
      • Most licences arrive within a week.  

   b) By post  
      • Pick up form D1 from most Post Offices or order it online.  
      • Enclose original identity documents, a passport-style photo and the £43 fee.  
      • Postal applications take longer.  

3. Receive your licence and start practical training  
   • You can drive a car in Wales or anywhere in GB as a learner once the provisional licence arrives, provided you:  
     – are supervised by a qualified driver aged 21+ who has held a full licence for 3+ years;  
     – display L-plates (D-plates in Welsh-speaking areas if you wish);  
     – are insured on the vehicle.  
   • Consider professional lessons with an Approved Driving Instructor (ADI).  

4. Prepare for the theory test  
   • Study the three official DVSA publications:  
     – The Highway Code.  
     – Know Your Traffic Signs.  
     – The Official DVSA Theory Test for Car Drivers.  
   • Use the free practice questions and hazard-perception clips on GOV.UK.  
   • Apps and DVDs that carry the DVSA logo use the real question bank.  
   • If Welsh is easier, request Welsh-language test materials and choose Welsh when booking.  

5. Book and take the theory test  
   • Cost: £23.  
   • Book only through GOV.UK → Book your theory test (avoids extra fees).  
   • Take your photocard licence to the test centre.  
   • You’ll sit:  
     – 50-question multiple-choice test (43 to pass, 57 minutes).  
     – Hazard-perception video test (44 out of 75 to pass).  
   • You must pass both parts to receive the theory test certificate (valid for 2 years).  

6. Next steps after passing the theory test  
   • Book your practical driving test when your instructor says you’re ready.  
   • Continue practising until the test date, always under supervision and insured.  

Key tips for older learners  
• Consider slightly longer lessons at quieter traffic times until you rebuild confidence.  
• If you have a medical condition, check the “Driving with medical conditions” section on GOV.UK or speak to your GP.  
• Keep practising the hazard-perception clips—reaction time matters.  

Useful links  
• Driving and transport on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving  
• Apply for a provisional licence: https://www.gov.uk/apply-first-provisional-driving-licence  
• Book your theory test: https://www.gov.uk/book-theory-test  
• Practice theory questions: https://www.gov.uk/take-practice-theory-test  

Good luck with your learning—taking it step-by-step will get you safely to a full licence.

prompt: I received a fine from DVLA for not informing them that I no longer own a vehicle. How do I appeal this fine?
generated_response:
If you think the penalty is unfair or you have proof you told DVLA about the sale, you can challenge (appeal) the fine rather than pay it.  
 
1. Check the deadline  
   • Your penalty notice will say how many days you have to reply (usually 14 days from the date on the letter).  
 
2. Collect evidence  
   • Any copy of the V5C/3 (‘new keeper’ slip) you posted.  
   • A Certificate of Posting or recorded-delivery receipt.  
   • A bill of sale, receipt or email showing when and to whom you sold or transferred the vehicle.  
   • Any emails or letters you previously sent to DVLA.  
 
3. Write your appeal letter or email  
   • Quote the penalty reference number, your full name, current address and the vehicle registration.  
   • State clearly that you are contesting the ‘failure to notify disposal’ penalty.  
   • List and enclose copies (never originals) of the evidence you gathered.  
   • Explain briefly why you believe the penalty is wrong, for example: “I sent the V5C/3 on 10 January 2024 (Certificate of Posting attached)”.  
 
4. Send it to DVLA Enforcement  
   Post:  
   DVLA – Enforcement Centre  
   Longview Road  
   Swansea  
   SA99 1ZZ  
   or email the scanned letter and evidence to: vencasework@dvla.gov.uk.  
   Use recorded delivery if you post, and keep the receipt.  
 
5. Wait for a reply  
   • DVLA will write back to confirm whether the penalty is cancelled or remains payable.  
   • If they reject your appeal you can either pay the out-of-court settlement or wait for a court summons (where you can contest the case again, but costs may rise if you lose).  
 
6. If you no longer have proof  
   • Contact the person or garage that bought the vehicle and ask for a dated receipt or a short signed note confirming the sale date.  
   • Include that with your appeal.  
 
7. If you sold the vehicle recently but never sent the V5C/3  
   • Fill in form ‘V62 – application for a registration certificate’ and send it with a cover letter explaining the situation. This will not cancel the original penalty but shows you are now putting things right.  
 
8. Keep copies of everything you send  
   • They are your only record if the case goes to court.  
 
Relevant guidance  
How to tell DVLA you’ve sold or transferred a vehicle and the penalties for not doing so: https://www.gov.uk/selling-vehicle

prompt: Can I use the online service to check my own driving licence information?
generated_response:
Yes. You can view your driving licence details online.

1. Go to the GOV.UK service: https://www.gov.uk/view-driving-licence  
2. Select ‘Start now’.  
3. Enter:  
   • your driving licence number (on your photocard)  
   • your National Insurance number  
   • the postcode on your licence  
4. Read the security declaration and continue.  
5. Your licence will open on-screen showing: licence validity, penalty points, provisional entitlements and the last 50 driving licence checks you have made.  
6. When you have finished, sign out or close the browser window.

The service is free and available 24/7. You can also generate a one-time ‘check code’ to share your licence details with an employer or car hire company if you need to.
