     – PAYE coding notice: write a letter quoting the PAYE reference.  
     – Other taxes (e.g. VAT, Corporation Tax): use the specific appeal form or write to the office that issued the decision.  
   • State clearly:  
     – “I am appealing against the decision dated …”,  
     – the reasons you think it is wrong, and  
     – the outcome you want.  
   • Enclose copies (not originals) of your evidence and keep proof of posting.

5. Ask for an HMRC internal review (if offered)  
   • A different officer will look at the case.  
   • This is optional but can avoid a tribunal.

6. Consider Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) for complex facts or communication problems.  
   • ADR uses a trained facilitator to help you and HMRC reach agreement.

7. Appeal to the independent tax tribunal (if still unresolved)  
   • First-tier Tribunal (Tax) is separate from HMRC.  
   • You usually must appeal within 30 days of HMRC’s review decision.  
   • No fee for most income-tax appeals; you can represent yourself.

8. Make a service complaint if HMRC’s handling (not the decision itself) was poor  
   • Follow HMRC’s two-tier complaints process.  
   • Escalate to the Adjudicator or Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman if needed.

9. Keep records throughout  
   • Save copies of all letters, forms, emails and notes of phone calls with dates and names.

Useful link  
GOV.UK: Tax appeals and complaints – https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm separating from my partner and we have children. What steps should I take to ensure we both provide financial support for our children?
generated_response:
It’s usually best to sort out child maintenance (financial support) as soon as you separate.  

1. Work out what your children need  
   • List regular costs such as food, clothing, school meals, transport and housing.  
   • Include extras like clubs or school trips if you can agree on them.  

2. Try a “family-based arrangement” first  
   • Talk to your ex-partner and agree who will pay what and when.  
   • Put the agreement in writing, sign it and keep copies.  
   • You can use the Child Maintenance Calculator on GOV.UK to get a guideline figure.  

3. Record and review payments  
   • Use a separate bank transfer or standing order so payments are traceable.  
   • Review the amount each year (or sooner if incomes or children’s needs change).  

4. If you cannot agree, use the Child Maintenance Service (CMS)  
   • Call the Child Maintenance Service to open a statutory case.  
   • CMS will work out the amount and, if needed, collect payments and pass them on (there are collection fees).  
   • The paying parent’s income is taken directly from earnings or benefits if necessary.  

5. Consider mediation before going to court  
   • A mediator can help you reach an agreement on money and wider parenting issues.  
   • If you still need a court order for child maintenance (rare), you must normally show you’ve considered mediation first.  

6. Keep other support under review  
   • Check whether you or your ex-partner can claim or must update Child Benefit, Universal Credit or Tax-Free Childcare.  
   • Update wills, life-insurance beneficiaries and emergency contacts.  

Relevant link  
GOV.UK – births, deaths, marriages and care: https://www.gov.uk/browse/births-deaths-marriages  

If you need tailored advice, contact Child Maintenance Options (0800 953 0191) or a family law solicitor.

prompt: How can I complain about a service I received that didn't meet my expectations?
generated_response:
Follow these steps to make an effective complaint.

1. Gather the facts  
   • Note what went wrong, when, where and who was involved.  
   • Keep evidence such as receipts, emails, photos or witness names.

2. Complain to the service provider first  
   • Check their website or any paperwork for “complaints” or “customer care” details.  
   • Write (email or letter) explaining clearly:  
     – what happened  
     – how it fell short of what was promised  
     – what you want done (refund, repair, apology, etc.).  
   • Keep a copy and give a reasonable deadline for a reply (usually 14 days).

3. Escalate inside the organisation  
   • If you get no reply or an unsatisfactory one, ask for the matter to be reviewed by a senior manager or the dedicated complaints team.  
   • State that you are following their complaints procedure.

4. Use an independent complaints body  
   • If the service is from a public body in Wales, you can take it to the Public Services Ombudsman for Wales once you’ve completed the organisation’s own process.  
   • For most private services there is an Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) or ombudsman scheme (e.g. Financial Ombudsman Service, Energy Ombudsman, etc.).  Check the provider’s website or search “sector ADR scheme UK”.  
   • Citizens Advice (England & Wales) can tell you which scheme applies: 0800 144 8848 (Monday–Friday).

5. Consider further action  
   • If the ADR/ombudsman cannot help, you can explore a claim in the county court (the ‘small claims’ process) for up to £10,000 in England and Wales.  
   • Get legal advice first; see the “Courts and tribunals” section on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice

Key tips  
• Always communicate in writing so you have a record.  
• Be polite, concise and stick to the facts.  
• Keep copies of all correspondence and notes of phone calls.  
• Act promptly—some schemes have strict time limits.

prompt: I want to apply for a secondary school place for my child in Wales. What are the key steps I should follow and what information do I need to prepare?
generated_response:
Applying for a Year 7 (secondary) place in Wales is done through your local council.  Follow these steps and gather the documents listed.

1. Check key dates  
   • Visit your council’s website (or phone their school admissions team) to confirm when applications open (usually early September) and the closing date (typically late October of Year 6).  
   • Note National Offer Day – 1 March – when you’ll receive the decision.

2. Research schools and set your preferences  
   • Read each school’s admissions criteria (catchment area, faith, banding tests, sibling priority, etc.).  
   • Visit open evenings or virtual tours if offered.  
   • List at least three schools in order of preference – putting only one does not increase your chance of getting it.

3. Complete the online application form (or request a paper form if needed)  
   • Go to GOV.UK → ‘Apply for a secondary school place’ and enter your postcode; it links straight to your council’s portal.  
   • Fill in:  
     – Your child’s full name, date of birth and current primary school  
     – Your name, address, email and phone number  
     – Up to five preferred schools, in order  
   • If applying to any faith, foundation or selective schools, download their supplementary information form (SIF) and send it directly to the school by their deadline.

4. Upload or send supporting evidence  
   • Proof of address (one recent council-tax, utility or tenancy document)  
   • Your child’s full birth certificate or passport  
   • Any additional evidence that matches the school’s criteria, for example:  
     – Baptism certificate or priest’s letter for a faith school  
     – Social services letter if the child is looked-after or previously looked-after  
     – Medical or social need letters (GP, consultant, social worker)  
   • Keep copies.  Some councils ask for scanned PDFs when you submit the form; others will request originals later.

5. Submit and keep confirmation  
   • Submit the form before the deadline – late applications are only considered after on-time ones.  
   • Save or print the confirmation email/reference number.

6. After you receive the offer (1 March)  
   • Accept the place online by the stated date (usually mid-March).  
   • If unhappy, you can:  
     – Stay on the waiting list (automatically held until at least 31 August)  
     – Appeal to an independent panel – follow the instructions in the refusal letter and lodge the appeal by the given deadline.

Most councils also let you amend preferences up to the closing date, and you can still apply after moving house if you provide new proof of address.

Useful link  
Apply for a secondary school place: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: I am a carer in Scotland and receive Carer's Allowance. What happens if I move to England or Wales?
generated_response:
Carer’s Allowance is a UK-wide benefit paid by the Department for Work and Pensions (DWP).  
If you move from Scotland to England or Wales:

1. Tell DWP about your new address as soon as you move.  
   • Phone the Carer’s Allowance Unit: 0800 731 0297 (textphone 0800 731 0317) or use the change-of-circumstances form on GOV.UK.  
   • Your payments will carry on as long as all the usual conditions are still met (for example you care for at least 35 hours a week and the person you care for gets a qualifying disability benefit).

2. Bank details – no change is needed if you keep the same UK bank account; payments will continue every 4 weeks.

3. National Insurance – moving within the UK does not affect your National Insurance record linked to Carer’s Allowance.

4. Carer’s Allowance Supplement (the twice-yearly top-up paid by Social Security Scotland) will stop once you are no longer resident in Scotland. There is no equivalent payment in England or Wales. You do not have to repay anything you have already received.

5. Other help – check whether you can claim:  
   • Council Tax Reduction from your new local council.  
   • Carer Premium/Element if you get Housing Benefit, Pension Credit or Universal Credit.  
   Use your new postcode on GOV.UK “Benefits calculators” to see what you may be entitled to.

6. Keep evidence – keep a note of the date you reported the move and any letters or emails you receive.

If you need help, contact Citizens Advice or your new local council’s welfare rights team.

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/carers-allowance

prompt: I am in Northern Ireland and need to change the details on my Blue Badge. How is this process different from the rest of the UK?
generated_response:
Changing the details on a Blue Badge is handled differently in Northern Ireland (NI) from the rest of the UK.

In Northern Ireland  
1. Phone the Blue Badge Unit on 0300 200 7818 or email bluebadgeunit@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk and ask for a “Change of details” form.  
2. Fill in the form. You will be asked for:  
   • Your badge number (on the front of the badge)  
   • The details that have changed (for example, your name or address)  
   • Proof of the change – for example, a utility bill for a new address or a marriage certificate for a name change.  
3. Post the completed form, the original Blue Badge and copies of your evidence to:  
   Blue Badge Unit, PO Box 186, Antrim, BT41 9AX.  
4. The Unit will cancel your old badge and post a new one to you. There is no fee for a replacement in NI at the moment.  
5. Destroy the cancelled badge as soon as your new one arrives.

In England, Scotland and Wales  
• You go through your local council using the GOV.UK online service (Replace or change details on a Blue Badge).  
• Most councils charge up to £10 for a replacement and you can upload your evidence online instead of posting the badge.

Key differences  
• In NI you deal directly with the central Blue Badge Unit, not your council.  
• The process is paper-based (phone/email for a form, then post it back).  
• There is currently no replacement fee in NI, whereas other parts of the UK may charge.  
• The GOV.UK online service does not work for NI addresses.

If you need help filling in the form, ask a friend, family member or your local Citizens Advice office. If you are unsure about any step, call the Blue Badge Unit first.

Most relevant GOV.UK link (for comparison only – not for NI applications): https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I need to find contact details for a lost pension. How do I use the online service to find this information?
generated_response:
You can use the Government’s free Pension Tracing Service to look up the latest contact details for any workplace or personal pension you’ve lost track of.

Follow these steps:

1. Go to GOV-UK and type “Find pension contact details” in the search box, or start from the Working section: https://www.gov.uk/browse/working  
2. Select “Find pension contact details (Pension Tracing Service)”.  
3. Choose the type of pension you’re looking for:  
   • workplace pension,  
   • personal pension, or  
   • civil service, NHS, armed forces, teachers or other public-sector pension.  
4. Enter as much of the following as you know:  
   • the name of your employer or the pension company,  
   • any previous names the employer/pension used,  
   • the employer’s address or postcode (even an old one).  
5. Review the search results. The service will list the scheme’s current name, address, telephone number and (where available) email or website.  
6. Contact the pension scheme directly using those details to ask about your own pension record and how to access it. Have your National Insurance number ready when you get in touch.

The online search is free. If you prefer to speak to someone, you can phone the Pension Tracing Service on 0800 731 0193 (Monday–Friday, 9 am–5 pm) or write to them; the address is shown on the same GOV-UK page.

prompt: As a teacher with a child under 16, can I get help with childcare costs and how do I apply for it?
generated_response:
You may be able to cut the cost of childcare in three main ways.

1. Tax-Free Childcare  
   • Who can use it: working parents of children up to 11 (16 if disabled) who each earn under £100,000 a year and at least about £167 a week.  
   • What you get: for every £8 you pay into an online childcare account, the government adds £2 (up to £2,000 a year per child). You can use the account to pay nurseries, child-minders, after-school and holiday clubs.  
   • How to apply:  
     1. Go to GOV.UK and search ‘Tax-Free Childcare’ or start from the Childcare and Parenting page (link below).  
     2. Set up a Government Gateway user ID if you do not already have one.  
     3. Open your childcare account and add money.  
     4. Give the carer your account’s reference so they can receive payments.  
     If you get stuck, call the Childcare Service Helpline on 0300 123 4097.

2. 30 hours free childcare for 3- and 4-year-olds (if your child is that age)  
   • Eligibility is the same earnings test as Tax-Free Childcare.  
   • Apply through the same online form; you will receive a code to give to your chosen nursery or school.

3. Free early-education places  
   • All 3- and 4-year-olds receive 15 hours a week during term time, whatever your income.  
   • Ask your local nursery or council to arrange a place.

Application tips for teachers  
• Apply as soon as possible; you must reconfirm your details every three months.  
• If you already use the old Childcare Voucher scheme through salary sacrifice you can keep it, but you cannot receive both vouchers and Tax-Free Childcare for the same child.  
• Keep receipts for any extra costs (e.g. holiday clubs) for your own records.

Useful link  
Childcare and parenting services on GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/childcare-parenting

prompt: Where can I find the nearest postcode finder tool to locate my area?
generated_response:
To look up a postcode for any address in Scotland (or the rest of the UK), you can use the official Royal Mail postcode finder that GOV.UK links to.

Steps  
1. Go to GOV.UK’s postcode lookup page: https://www.gov.uk/find-postcode  
2. Select the link on that page to the Royal Mail “Postcode finder”.  
3. Enter as much of the address as you know (house number, street name, town or city).  
4. Click “Find postcode”.  
5. The tool will list the full address(es) and the correct postcode.  

Tip: If you only know a place name or landmark, type that in first—then narrow it down using the results list.

prompt: If I have a British passport, do I still need to apply for an ETA when visiting the UK?
generated_response: No. If you hold a valid British passport you are a British citizen and are not subject to UK immigration control, so you do not need to apply for an Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) to enter the UK. Just travel with your passport—no extra paperwork is required.

prompt: I'm 68 and live in Scotland. I've been receiving Disability Living Allowance (DLA) for many years. What changes will happen to my benefit and how do I apply for the new Scottish Adult Disability Living Allowance?
generated_response:
What will happen  
• Disability Living Allowance (DLA) is gradually being replaced in Scotland.  
• For adults, Social Security Scotland is introducing Adult Disability Payment (ADP).  
• People who already get DLA (or Personal Independence Payment) do not need to make a new claim. Your award will be moved over automatically.  
• You’ll get two letters before the change:  
  – one telling you DWP will pass your case to Social Security Scotland  
  – one from Social Security Scotland confirming the date your first ADP payment will arrive.  
• Your payments will continue at the same rate, paid on the same dates, and there will be no break in entitlement.  
• If you are over State Pension age when the transfer happens, you will still receive ADP – the age limit only applies to new claims.

How the transfer works – what you need to do  
1. Keep an eye out for the letters mentioned above. They usually arrive a few weeks apart.  
2. Check the personal details in the letters and phone the number on the letter if anything is wrong.  
3. Keep being paid as normal. You do not need to fill in a form, have a medical or send any evidence unless Social Security Scotland contacts you for clarification.  
4. Once you are on ADP, payments will come from Social Security Scotland instead of DWP. They will tell you the new payment reference so you can recognise it on your bank statement.  

If your circumstances change (for example your care or mobility needs increase), you can still report the change now by phoning the DLA helpline or, after transfer, by phoning Social Security Scotland on 0800 182 2222.  

Need more help  
• DLA helpline (DWP): 0800 121 4600  
• Social Security Scotland: 0800 182 2222  
• General benefits guidance: https://www.gov.uk/browse/benefits

prompt: I'm a nurse in Wales and I work on a farm. Do I have the right to paid holiday and sick pay as an agricultural worker?
generated_response:
Yes – if you are classed as an agricultural worker in Wales you are entitled to both paid holiday and sick pay.  

1. Check whether your job counts as “agricultural work”.  
   • You are an agricultural worker if your day-to-day duties involve farming activities such as caring for livestock, growing or harvesting crops, maintaining the land or handling produce.  
   • Jobs that only support the farm (for example, office administration or purely clinical nursing for farm staff) are not covered. In that case you would still have general statutory rights, but the specific agricultural rules would not apply.

2. If you are an agricultural worker:  
   a. Holiday entitlement  
      • You must receive at least 5.6 weeks paid holiday a year (the UK legal minimum) PLUS two additional “agricultural holidays” set out in the Agricultural Wages (Wales) Order.  
      • Holiday pay should be based on your normal weekly pay (including overtime and regular bonuses).  
   b. Sick pay  
      • After 52 weeks’ continuous employment you qualify for Agricultural Sick Pay (ASP).  
      • The current Order allows up to 10 weeks’ ASP in any 12-month period, paid at the same rate as your basic pay.  
      • Until you reach 52 weeks’ service, you may still qualify for Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) if you earn at least £123 a week.

3. If you are NOT classed as an agricultural worker:  
   • You are still entitled to the standard minimums:  
     – Paid holiday: 5.6 weeks a year.  
     – Statutory Sick Pay: up to 28 weeks at £109.40 a week (2023/24 rate) if you meet the earnings test.  
   • Any better terms would come from your contract or employer’s policy.

4. What to do next  
   1. Look at your contract and job description to see whether it says you are employed as an “agricultural worker” or doing “agricultural work”.  
   2. If it is unclear, ask your employer (in writing if possible) to confirm which set of rules they are applying.  
   3. If you believe you are an agricultural worker and are not receiving the correct holiday or sick pay, raise it formally with your employer.  
   4. For advice or to pursue a claim you can speak to Acas (0300 123 1100) or use the dispute resolution service on gov.uk.

Relevant link:  
Employing people (includes pay and working conditions) – https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I'm expecting my first child and I live in Scotland. When should I apply for the Pregnancy and Baby Payment?
generated_response:
You can send in your Pregnancy and Baby Payment claim any time from 24 weeks of pregnancy (the start of your 24th week) up to the day before your baby turns 6 months old.

If you are adopting, have a parental order or kinship care, you can apply within 6 months of the child being placed with you, as long as the child is under 1 year old.

How to apply  
1. Decide when you want to apply within the window above.  
2. Apply online at mygov.scot/best-start-grant, phone Social Security Scotland free on 0800 182 2222, or request a paper form.  
3. Have your National Insurance number and bank details ready before you start.  
4. Submit the form. Social Security Scotland will usually tell you the result within a few weeks and pay the money into your bank if you qualify.  

If you are not sure about your dates or need help with the form, call Social Security Scotland on the number above or speak to your midwife.

prompt: I need to know if I have to get an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) for my small shop that's under 50 square metres and not used for residential purposes. What are the rules around this?
generated_response:
In England, the general rule is that you must have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) when you sell or rent out a commercial property.  
There is, however, an exemption for very small, stand-alone buildings:  

1. If your shop is a detached building (ie it is not physically connected to another building) and the total usable floor area is less than 50 m², you do not need an EPC.  
2. If the shop is attached to another building (for example, it forms part of a terrace or is connected to neighbouring premises), the exemption does not apply. In that case you must get an EPC before you market, sell or rent the property.  
3. If you later make major changes to the building (for example, add an extension that takes it over 50 m² or connect it to another building), re-check the rules—you may then need an EPC.  
4. If you are unsure whether your premises count as “detached”, contact a qualified non-domestic energy assessor or your local authority’s building control team for confirmation.  

You can read the full guidance and find accredited assessors here:  
https://www.gov.uk/energy-performance-certificate-commercial-property

prompt: How can I apply for a disabled person's bus pass in England?
generated_response:
You need to apply through the local council for the area in England where you live or work.

1. Check you qualify  
   You can usually get a disabled person’s bus pass if you:  
   • are blind or partially sighted, profoundly or severely deaf, unable to speak, unable to walk far without severe discomfort, have lost the use of both arms, or have a learning disability.  
   • get certain disability-related benefits (for example, PIP at the mobility rate).  

2. Find the right council page  
   • Go to GOV.UK and type “disabled bus pass” plus the name of your English council, or use the postcode-finder on GOV.UK to reach the correct council page.  

3. Gather the documents the council asks for  
   • Proof of disability (e.g. benefit award letter, consultant’s letter).  
   • Proof of identity and address (e.g. passport or driving licence, recent utility bill).  
   • A recent passport-style photo.  

4. Apply  
   • Most councils let you apply online; some offer a printable form or let you apply at a library or council hub.  
   • Upload or attach scanned copies of your evidence (or take the originals if applying in person/post).  

5. After you apply  
   • The council will normally confirm within 2–4 weeks.  
   • Your free bus pass will be posted to you and is usually valid anywhere in England from 09:30 to 23:00 on weekdays and all day at weekends and bank holidays.  
   • Keep a note of the expiry date (typically every 5 years) and re-apply before it runs out.  

If you are currently living in Scotland, apply instead for the Scottish National Entitlement Card through your Scottish local authority—passes issued there are not processed by English councils.

prompt: Is there a way to get a parking permit without going through my local council?
generated_response:
For any parking space that is on a public road, verge or council-run car park, the permit is issued only by the local authority that manages that area. In practice, that means:

1. Find out which council controls the street or car park (enter your postcode at https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services and follow the parking/permits link).  
2. Apply online or by post using that council’s resident, visitor, business or Blue Badge permit process.  
3. If you need parking at work, a hospital, a retail park or a private housing estate, speak to the site owner or managing agent—those permits are private and sit outside the council system, but they are valid only on that land.  
4. For temporary parking on public roads (e.g. tradespeople, removals), ask the council for a short-stay or suspension permit; again, only they can issue it.

In short, for any parking right that allows you to leave a vehicle on public highway land, you must go through the relevant local council; there is no alternative authority you can use.

prompt: I have a mental health condition and need to modify my home for better accessibility. What types of adaptations can I get funding for through a Disabled Facilities Grant?
generated_response:
In most of the UK, a Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) can help pay for changes that make it easier and safer for you to live at home. Grants are given for works that are “necessary and appropriate” to meet your needs and are “reasonable and practicable” for the property.  Typical adaptations that may be funded include:  

1. Making it easier to get in, out and around the home  
   • widening doorways, installing ramps or a step-lift  
   • fitting handrails or grab-rails in halls and corridors  

2. Making essential rooms safer or easier to use  
   • converting a bathroom into a level-access (walk-in) shower or wet-room  
   • adapting a kitchen so you can prepare food safely (e.g. lowered worktops, easy-use taps, improved lighting)  

3. Providing or adapting a room  
   • creating a downstairs bedroom or bathroom  
   • enlarging an existing room for wheelchair turning space  

4. Improving control over heating, lighting or security  
   • easy-reach switches and sockets  
   • environmental controls or smart-home systems that reduce stress or anxiety  

5. Ensuring you can care for yourself or be cared for safely  
   • fitting door or window sensors, safety locks or padding if you are at risk of wandering or self-harm  
   • sound-proofing or calming spaces if sensory triggers worsen your condition  

6. Making access to the garden possible  
   • level or ramped paths, secure fencing or handrails so you can use outdoor space for therapy or relaxation  

Because you live in Scotland, the funding route is slightly different: Scottish councils offer a mandatory Scheme of Assistance grant for “essential adaptations” under the Housing (Scotland) Act 2006. The types of work are much the same as the DFG list above, and mental-health-related needs (for example reducing anxiety triggers or making the home safer if you have periods of confusion) can be taken into account.  

Next steps  
1. Contact your council’s housing adaptations team (or social work department) and ask for an occupational therapy (OT) assessment.  
2. Explain how your mental health condition affects your day-to-day living and what changes would help. The OT will recommend the specific adaptations.  
3. The council will tell you if the works are eligible for a grant, whether any means-tested contribution applies, and what contractors can carry out the work.  
4. Keep copies of all correspondence and do not start work until the grant is approved in writing.  

More information: https://www.gov.uk/disabled-facilities-grants

prompt: I have a terminal illness and need to make my home more accessible. What can a Disabled Facilities Grant cover?
generated_response:
A Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) is meant to fund the changes that let you live safely and independently at home. It can normally cover:  

1. Accessibility changes  
   • Widening doorways and putting in ramps or step-free thresholds.  
   • Installing stairlifts, through-floor lifts or banister rails.  

2. Essential room adaptations  
   • Converting a bathroom to a level-access (walk-in) shower or wet room.  
   • Installing a downstairs toilet or bathroom if you can’t use the stairs.  
   • Lowering worktops, sinks or cupboards, or fitting rise-and-fall units so you can prepare meals.  

3. Safety and heating adjustments  
   • Improving or relocating heating controls so you can reach and use them.  
   • Adding specialist lighting, alarms or intercom systems to help you see, hear or answer the door.  

4. Moving points of access  
   • Changing where doors or windows are positioned, or creating a new entrance, if that is the safest option for getting in and out.  

Key points  
• The works must be “necessary and appropriate” for your needs and “reasonable and practicable” for the property – your council’s occupational therapist usually decides this.  
• Grants can cover the full cost up to the local maximum (up to £30,000 in England, £36,000 in Wales, £25,000 in Northern Ireland).  
• In Scotland the process is different: you apply to your local council for a housing adaptations grant under the Scheme of Assistance, but the type of work they fund is broadly the same as listed above.  

Next steps (Scotland)  
1. Contact your council’s housing adaptations team and ask for an occupational therapy (OT) assessment.  
2. The OT will visit, decide what adaptations you need and confirm what costs the grant can meet.  
3. Get at least one written quote for the agreed work (your council may have an approved contractor list).  
4. Submit the application form and quote; the council will tell you how much of the cost they can cover.  
5. Do not start work until you have written grant approval.

prompt: I'm a student in Scotland and I need to apply for student finance. How do I apply online and what should I do if I can't use a computer?
generated_response:
If you normally live in Scotland and are studying on an eligible course in the UK, you apply for funding through the Student Awards Agency Scotland (SAAS), not Student Finance England.

Applying online  
1. Go to the SAAS website (www.saas.gov.uk) and choose “Register / log in”.  
2. Create a SAAS account. You’ll need:  
   • your National Insurance number  
   • the course and university/college details  
   • your bank account details  
   • household-income figures (if you want income-assessed support).  
3. Once registered, select “Apply for funding” and work through each section.  
4. Upload or post any evidence SAAS asks for (for example proof of identity or income).  
5. Submit the application. You should get an email confirming it’s been received.  

Apply as early as you can—ideally by 30 June before the academic year starts—to make sure your funding is in place for the first term.

If you can’t use a computer  
• Phone SAAS on 0300 555 0505 and ask for a paper application pack.  
• You can also get help completing the form at:  
  – your college or university student-finance office  
  – a local Citizens Advice Bureau  
  – most public libraries (staff can help you get online or print the forms).  
Return the completed paper form and any evidence to the address on the form.

Keep copies of anything you send and note the date you post it.  

Further information  
SAAS guidance and deadlines: www.saas.gov.uk

prompt: I'm a teacher and I've noticed some students with disabilities aren't getting the support they need in class. What steps should I take to ensure they're treated fairly and have access to the resources they require?
generated_response:
Under Welsh law and the Equality Act 2010 every pupil with a disability or additional learning need (ALN) is entitled to “reasonable adjustments” and, where necessary, an Individual Development Plan (IDP). As a class teacher you play a key part in triggering and delivering that support.

1. Gather evidence  
   • Keep short notes of the barriers you have observed (e.g. difficulty accessing printed text, sensory overload, mobility issues).  
   • Collect examples of work and any strategies you have already tried.

2. Check the school’s ALN policy  
   • Read the policy and the ALN Code for Wales (2021) so you know the agreed process and time-scales.  
   • Identify who the Additional Learning Needs Co-ordinator (ALNCo) is (sometimes still called SENCo).

3. Raise the concern formally  
   • Email or speak to the ALNCo, sharing your evidence.  
   • Ask for the pupil to be placed on the school’s ALN register (if not already) and for an “initial concern” meeting to be arranged with parents/carers.

4. Meet with the learner and their parents/carers  
   • Discuss strengths, needs and what helps at home.  
   • Agree short-term classroom adjustments (e.g. enlarged print, assistive tech, movement breaks, visual timetables).

5. Put reasonable adjustments in place immediately  
   • Differentiate tasks, adapt resources, review seating and lighting, use accessible formats, arrange peer or adult support.  
   • Record what you have done and how it affected progress.

6. If needs are significant or persistent, request an IDP  
   • Through the ALNCo, ask the school to start the statutory IDP process.  
   • Provide your evidence; outside specialists (e.g. educational psychologist, speech and language therapist) may be brought in.  
   • The IDP sets out long-term outcomes, provision, review dates and who is responsible.

7. Review and monitor  
   • Track progress against the agreed outcomes at least termly.  
   • Adjust support where necessary and keep parents informed.

8. Escalate if support is still insufficient  
   • The headteacher must ensure the IDP is delivered; if not, parents (or the young person) can appeal to the Education Tribunal for Wales.  
   • You can also raise safeguarding concerns if a lack of provision risks harm.

9. Keep your own practice up to date  
   • Attend ALN/SEND CPD offered by the school or local authority.  
   • Share successful strategies with colleagues to promote whole-school inclusion.

Further information  
GOV.UK general education pages: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education  
(For Welsh-specific guidance, see gov.wales “Additional learning needs” pages.)

prompt: I'm worried about an older person in a care home because they seem unhappy and aren't getting proper care. What should I do to report this and ensure they're protected?
generated_response:
If you think an older person in a care home is being neglected or abused, act as soon as you can.  

1. Check for immediate danger  
   • If you believe the person is at serious risk now, call 999 and ask for the police.  

2. Raise the issue with the care home (if it feels safe)  
   • Speak to the care home manager or senior nurse.  
   • Keep notes of what you saw, dates, times and who you spoke to.  

3. Contact the local council’s adult safeguarding team  
   • Every council has a ‘safeguarding adults’ duty team that must look into concerns.  
   • Phone the council where the care home is based. Tell them you are making a safeguarding referral for an adult at risk in a care home.  
   • Give as much detail as you can: the resident’s name (if known), the home’s name and address, what you have witnessed and when. You can ask to stay anonymous.  

4. Report to the Care Quality Commission (CQC)  
   • The CQC regulates care homes in England and can inspect or take enforcement action.  
   • Call 03000 616161 or fill in the online form: go to GOV.UK, search “CQC raise a concern about a care service”.  
   • The CQC cannot resolve individual complaints but the information helps them act to protect residents.  

5. Make a formal complaint (optional but helpful)  
   • Ask the care home for its complaints procedure and put your concerns in writing or email.  
   • If you are not happy with their reply, you can escalate to the Local Government & Social Care Ombudsman.  

6. Keep records and follow up  
   • Note who you contacted and the reference numbers they give you.  
   • If the situation does not improve or you see new risks, call the safeguarding team or police again.

Relevant GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice (Reporting a crime and people at risk sections).

prompt: I need to know when the clocks change this year because I'm planning my schedule around it. When exactly does the clock change in the UK?
generated_response:
In 2026 the UK clocks change on the following dates.  

1. Clocks go forward 1 hour (start of British Summer Time)  
   • At 1am on Sunday 29 March 2026 the time jumps to 2am.  

2. Clocks go back 1 hour (return to Greenwich Mean Time)  
   • At 2am on Sunday 25 October 2026 the time returns to 1am.  

Tip: Add both dates to your rota planner and any electronic calendars so automatic updates don’t catch you out, especially for early or night shifts.

prompt: What are the requirements to start learning to drive a car in Wales and what is the process for getting a provisional licence?
generated_response:
To start learning to drive a car in Wales you need to meet the legal requirements and hold a provisional driving licence.

1. Basic requirements  
   • Be at least 17 years old (16 if you receive the enhanced rate of the mobility component of Personal Independence Payment).  
   • Be a resident of Great Britain.  
   • Meet the eyesight standard – you must be able to read a car number plate from 20 metres away (with glasses or contact lenses if you normally wear them).  
   • Have no driving disqualifications that would prevent you from holding a licence.

2. Getting a provisional driving licence  
   a. Apply online (fastest)  
      1. Go to GOV.UK – Apply for your first provisional driving licence.  
      2. Have ready: a valid UK passport (or another acceptable identity document), your National Insurance number, and your addresses for the last 3 years.  
      3. Pay the £34 fee by debit or credit card.  
      4. DVLA normally posts the licence within 1 week.  

   b. Apply by post  
      1. Collect form D1 (‘Application for a driving licence’) from a Post Office.  
      2. Send the completed form, original identity documents or your passport number, a passport-style photo if needed, and the £43 fee to DVLA, Swansea, SA99 1AD.  
      3. Allow up to 3 weeks for the licence to arrive.  

3. After your provisional arrives  
   • Display L-plates (or D-plates in Welsh-speaking areas) on any car you drive.  
   • The car must be taxed, insured for learner drivers and have a valid MOT if over 3 years old.  
   • You must be supervised by someone who is at least 21 and has held a full car licence for 3 years.  
   • You can then take professional lessons and practise for the theory and practical tests.

Most relevant GOV.UK page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I'm a nurse in Scotland and I've been called in for a disciplinary meeting. What steps should I take to prepare and ensure my rights are protected during the process?
generated_response:
Below is a simple checklist you can use before, during and after the meeting.

1. Read your employer’s disciplinary policy  
   • Ask HR for a copy if you do not already have one.  
   • Check the policy covers the steps they must follow and the possible outcomes.

2. Ask for the allegations and evidence in writing  
   • You have the right to know exactly what the meeting is about and to see any documents, witness statements or records that will be used.

3. Arrange a companion  
   • You are entitled to take a trade-union representative or a work colleague to the meeting.  
   • Tell your employer as soon as possible who will be with you.  
   • Your companion can speak on your behalf, take notes and give you private breaks.

4. Collect information to support your side  
   • Make notes of events, dates and any witnesses who can help.  
   • Gather relevant emails, rotas or other records.  
   • Put everything in date order so it is easy to follow.

5. Prepare what you want to say  
   • Write down your points and any questions you need answered.  
   • Practise with your companion so you feel comfortable.

6. At the meeting  
   • Take notes or ask your companion to do so.  
   • If something is unclear, ask for it to be explained.  
   • You can ask for breaks to talk privately with your companion.

7. After the meeting  
   • Ask for a written record of what was decided.  
   • If you disagree with the decision, you normally have at least 5 working days to appeal—check your policy for the exact time limit.  
   • Put any appeal in writing, stating the reasons and any new evidence.

8. Get further help if needed  
   • Your trade union, a professional body (such as the Nursing and Midwifery Council) or an independent adviser like Acas can guide you.  
   • If you think the process is unfair or discriminatory, consider speaking to an employment-law solicitor.

Useful GOV.UK starting point  
https://www.gov.uk/browse/employing-people

prompt: I run a small business in Scotland and I'm unsure if my waste is classified as hazardous. How can I determine this?
generated_response:
To work out whether the waste your shop produces is classed as hazardous:

1. List what is in the waste  
   • Note every product, chemical, battery, oil, paint, electrical item or other material you throw away.  
   • Keep the product labels and any Safety Data Sheets (SDS) – suppliers must give you these on request.  

2. Check the waste code and hazard codes  
   • Look up the product or material in the European Waste Catalogue (the “List of Wastes”).  
   • Each entry has a six-digit waste code; the ones marked with an asterisk (*) are hazardous.  
   • Compare the hazard statements on the SDS (e.g. H314, H400) with the guidance in the catalogue – if any match, the waste is hazardous.

3. Decide if any mixture is still hazardous  
   • If your waste is a mix (for example, rags soaked in solvent), work out the percentage of each dangerous substance.  
   • Use the thresholds in the guidance – if the concentration is above the limit, the whole mix is hazardous.

4. Keep written evidence  
   • Record how you reached your decision and keep copies of SDSs and calculations; you must show this if asked by the Scottish Environment Protection Agency (SEPA).

5. Get advice if you are unsure  
   • Your waste contractor, trade association or an environmental consultant can confirm the classification.  
   • You can also contact SEPA directly.

Once you know the waste is hazardous you must follow the separate rules on storage, transport and disposal.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/dispose-hazardous-waste

prompt: What happens if I don't comply with the battery takeback scheme and fail to collect used batteries from my shop in Scotland?
generated_response:
If you sell portable batteries in Scotland and fail to offer a free in-store take-back service or refuse to collect the used batteries you’ve taken in, you are breaking the Waste Batteries and Accumulators Regulations 2009.

What can happen  
1. Inspection – Officers from the Scottish Environment Protection Agency (SEPA) can visit your premises, request records and check whether you are offering the service.  
2. Compliance notice – SEPA can issue a notice telling you to start the service or put specific measures in place by a set date.  
3. Enforcement action – If you ignore the notice, SEPA can:  
   • issue a fixed monetary penalty (usually £300) or a variable monetary penalty (up to £40,000)  
   • prosecute you in the criminal courts. On summary conviction the fine can be up to the statutory maximum (currently £5,000) and, on indictment, an unlimited fine.  
4. Additional costs – You may have to pay SEPA’s investigation costs and any court costs.  
5. Reputational impact – Non-compliance can be publicised by SEPA, which may affect customer trust.

How to avoid penalties  
1. Check whether you sell more than 32 kg of portable batteries a year (about one 4-pack of AA batteries a day). If you do, you must offer free take-back.  
2. Provide a clearly-marked collection container in-store and accept any brand of portable batteries free of charge.  
3. Arrange free collection of the full containers through a registered battery compliance scheme.  
4. Keep the paperwork (collection notes, transfer notes) for at least four years.  
5. Train staff so they know they must accept returned batteries and store them safely.

If you are unsure  
Contact SEPA’s Producer Responsibility Unit or visit GOV.UK for detailed guidance:

• Waste batteries: distributor and retailer responsibilities – https://www.gov.uk/browse/environment-countryside

prompt: I'm a customer with a disability and I want to ensure I'm treated fairly when using public services. What steps should I take if I believe I've been discriminated against?
generated_response:
If you think a public service has treated you unfairly because of your disability, you can take these steps:

1. Keep evidence  
   • Note the date, time, place and what was said or done.  
   • Keep letters, emails or photos that back up your complaint.  

2. Raise the issue with the service straight away  
   • Ask to speak to a manager or put your complaint in writing.  
   • Say that you believe the treatment breaches the Equality Act 2010.  
   • Explain the reasonable adjustment you needed (for example, extra help, accessible format) and how it was refused or not provided.

3. Follow the service’s formal complaints process  
   • Every public body must have one and tell you how to use it.  
   • Keep copies of everything you send and receive.  

4. Go to the next-level complaints body if you are not satisfied  
   • For most Scottish public services (councils, NHS, colleges, the Scottish Government) you can take the matter to the Scottish Public Services Ombudsman (SPSO) after you finish the service’s own process: www.spso.org.uk  
   • Complaints about the DWP or other UK-wide departments can be taken to the relevant Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman via your MP.  

5. Get specialist advice early  
   • Contact the Equality Advisory and Support Service (EASS) on 0808 800 0082 for free advice on disability discrimination.  
   • A local Citizens Advice Bureau or law centre can explain your options and any legal aid you might get.  
   • You normally have 6 months minus one day from the act of discrimination to start legal action in the sheriff court or county court.  

6. Consider legal action if the issue is not resolved  
   • Court action can lead to compensation and an order for the service to change its practices, but costs and time limits apply.  
   • A solicitor experienced in equality law can tell you the merits of your case.  

7. Look after yourself  
   • Dealing with discrimination is stressful—talk to friends, family or a support group.  

Useful links  
• Equality Act rights – GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/justice  
• Scottish Public Services Ombudsman: https://www.spso.org.uk  
• Equality Advisory and Support Service: https://www.equalityadvisoryservice.com

prompt: I want to install an electric vehicle chargepoint at home, but I'm not sure where to start. What should I do first?
generated_response:
Here is the usual starting-point for getting a home electric vehicle (EV) chargepoint installed in England.

1. Check you have a suitable place to put the charger  
   • You normally need your own off-street parking space (for example a driveway or garage).  
   • If you live in a flat or rent your home you can still apply, but the rules are slightly different.

2. See if you can get money off through the EV chargepoint grant  
   • The Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) will pay up to 75 % of the cost, capped at £350.  
   • The grant is only available if you use an installer approved by OZEV.  
   • Full details: https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/government-grants-for-low-emission-vehicles#ev-chargepoint-grant

3. Choose an OZEV-approved installer  
   • Search the list on GOV.UK or ask charger companies to confirm they are approved.  
   • Get at least two quotes so you can compare prices and warranty cover.

4. Agree the installation date  
   • The installer will check your home’s electrics and tell your local network operator if any upgrades are needed.  
   • They will also apply for the grant on your behalf—you only pay the balance.

5. Keep the paperwork  
   • After fitting, you should receive a certificate of installation, a warranty and confirmation that the grant was claimed.  
   • Keep these in case you move house or sell the car.

6. Check if any permission is needed  
   • Planning permission is rarely required for a wall-mounted charger under 0.2 m³ in size, but ask your local council if the property is listed or in a conservation area.  
   • If you rent, get your landlord’s written consent before ordering.

If anything seems unclear, ring a couple of approved installers—they deal with these steps every day and can guide you through the grant form.

prompt: I own a small business in Scotland and I'm trying to understand how much I'll have to pay for water and sewerage rates. How does the charging work and am I eligible for any discounts?
generated_response:
Water and waste-water services for non-domestic properties in Scotland are provided by Scottish Water but billed through a choice of licensed suppliers (for example Business Stream, Castle Water and others). What you pay depends on the tariffs approved by the Water Industry Commission for Scotland (WICS) and applied by the supplier you pick.

How the charges are made  
1. Water supply charge  
   • Standing (fixed) charge – based on the size of your meter.  
   • Volumetric charge – price per cubic metre of water recorded by the meter.  

2. Sewerage (foul water) charge  
   • Standing charge – again linked to meter size.  
   • Volumetric charge – normally 95 % of the water you use is assumed to return to the sewer unless you can prove otherwise (for example by installing a sub-meter for water that is not discharged).  

3. Drainage charge  
   • Property drainage – linked to your property’s rateable value.  
   • Roads drainage – a small fixed amount set by Scottish Water.  

Discounts and reliefs you may be able to get  
• Water Charges Reduction Scheme – most small businesses with a rateable value up to £20,000 automatically receive around 25 % off the wholesale element of their bill. Check with your supplier that it is being applied.  
• Charitable exemption – registered charities and Community Amateur Sports Clubs with annual turnover under £300,000 can apply for up to 100 % exemption from water, sewerage and drainage charges.  
• Vacant property relief – if the premises are empty and the water is turned off you pay drainage only.  
• Switching supplier – all suppliers must offer the default tariff but can also give discounts or tailored contracts; shopping around can cut bills by 10–20 %.  
• Water efficiency – fitting self-closing taps, urinal controls or checking for leaks lowers the volumetric element.  

What to do next  
1. Find an old bill (or ask the current supplier) so you have:  
   • Supply Point ID (SPID).  
   • Meter size and last meter reading.  
   • Your property’s rateable value.  
2. Visit the Water Industry Commission for Scotland comparison site or contact at least three licensed suppliers for quotes.  
3. Ask each supplier to confirm that the Water Charges Reduction Scheme has been applied and whether you qualify for any other relief.  
4. Choose a supplier and sign a contract, or remain on the default tariff if that suits you.  
5. If you think you may be eligible for charitable exemption, complete the application form supplied by Scottish Water or your chosen provider.  
6. Consider a simple water audit to spot efficiency savings.  

Further information  
Choose a water supplier for your business (GOV.UK): https://www.gov.uk/choose-water-supplier-business

prompt: How can I apply for a needs assessment by social services if I'm caring for a disabled person?
generated_response:
You can ask your local council’s social work department for a carer’s assessment (sometimes called a ‘carer’s needs assessment’) at any time.

1. Find the right council  
   • Go to mygov.scot/find-your-local-council and enter your postcode.  
   • Note the phone number and webpage for the Social Care or Social Work team.

2. Make the request  
   • Call the Social Care/Social Work team and say you want a carer’s assessment.  
   • Many councils also let you fill in an online self-referral form—look for “Carer’s assessment” or “Support for carers” on the council website.

3. Give the information they ask for  
   • Your name, address and contact details.  
   • The name of the person you care for and their GP (if known).  
   • How caring affects your day-to-day life (work, study, health, finances, time off, etc.).  
   • Any support you already get.

4. What happens next  
   • A social worker or carer support worker will contact you to arrange a meeting—by phone, video, or in person.  
   • They will talk through what help would make caring easier (respite breaks, training, equipment, help with household tasks, etc.).  
   • You will get a written support plan and details of any services or grants you can receive.  

5. If you need urgent help  
   • Tell the council it’s urgent—every council has a duty to consider emergency respite or short-term help.  

6. If you disagree with the outcome  
   • Ask the council for a review.  
   • You can also contact the Scottish Public Services Ombudsman if you’re still unhappy.

Extra help  
• Carers Scotland helpline: 0808 808 7777  
• Your local Carers Centre (search at careinfoscotland.scot)

There is no charge for the assessment itself, and having one will not affect any benefits you or the person you care for already receive.

prompt: What are the key considerations when buying a leasehold property in Scotland?
generated_response:
When you buy a flat or house in Scotland you normally get outright ownership (called “heritable title”).  A small number of homes are still held on a long lease, and the rules are different from England.  Before you commit, take these steps:

1. Check the length of the lease  
   • Ask your solicitor to confirm how many years remain.  
   • Anything under 80 years can be hard to re-sell or mortgage; below 60 years is high-risk.

2. Find out the annual costs  
   • Ground rent: most very long Scottish leases charge a nominal (“peppercorn”) rent, but older leases can rise over time.  
   • Service or factoring charges: get the latest accounts and planned works for the building.

3. Read the lease conditions carefully  
   • Repair and maintenance: who fixes the roof, stairs or garden—and how costs are shared.  
   • Alterations: many leases require the landlord’s written consent even for minor changes.  
   • Sub-letting and pets: check any restrictions.

4. Ask about management of shared areas  
   • Is there a factor (managing agent)?  How are they appointed and removed?  
   • Are there any outstanding disputes with other owners or the factor?

5. Check for planned major works  
   • Your solicitor should request Section 6 (Tenement) or factor notices that warn of upcoming costs such as roof or stonework repairs.

6. Confirm insurance arrangements  
   • Flats are usually covered by a block policy arranged by the factor; make sure the premium is paid up to date and covers the full reinstatement value.  
   • For houses on leasehold, you may need your own buildings insurance—ask the landlord what cover is required.

7. Understand your options for extending or buying out the lease  
   • Very long leases (over 175 years with low rent) were automatically converted to ownership in 2015 under the Long Leases (Scotland) Act 2012.  
   • For shorter leases, you normally negotiate directly with the landlord; there is no set statutory process as in England, so start early and budget for legal fees.

8. Use a Scottish solicitor experienced in conveyancing  
   • They will carry out the title search, explain your obligations in plain language and record the deed in the Land Register of Scotland.

9. Budget for ongoing costs after purchase  
   • Mortgage, factor’s bills, ground rent, insurance and a float for unexpected repairs.

10. If anything is unclear, pause the purchase  
    • Ask your solicitor to raise formal “observations” with the seller or landlord until you are satisfied.

GOV.UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services (see “Buying or selling your home in Scotland”).

prompt: Can I get paid for my magistrate work if I'm retired?
generated_response:
Magistrates (called ‘Justices of the Peace’ in Scotland) are unpaid volunteers, whether you are working, unemployed or retired.  
You will not receive a salary, but you can still claim:

1. Travel allowance – to cover the cost of getting to and from court.  
2. Subsistence allowance – to cover meals or overnight stays if needed.  
3. Caring-dependant allowance – to pay someone to look after children or other dependants while you are in court.

Because you are retired, you will not normally qualify for the “financial loss” allowance, which is only for people who lose earnings or profits while sitting.

How to claim expenses:
1. Keep receipts for any travel, meals or care costs.  
2. Ask the court staff for an expense claim form each time you sit.  
3. Fill in the form, attach receipts and hand it back to the court office (or follow any local online process).  
4. The court will pay the approved amount straight to your bank account.

If you need more detail, contact the clerk at your local Justice of the Peace court or check the “Become a magistrate” section on GOV.UK.

prompt: How do I book a theory test for a car in the UK?
generated_response:
Here’s how to book your car theory test in the UK.

1. Get your details ready  
   • Your Great Britain (or Northern Ireland) provisional driving licence number  
   • A debit or credit card for the £23 test fee  
   • An email address or mobile number so you can get the confirmation

2. Go to the official booking service  
   • Visit GOV-UK, choose Driving and Transport, then ‘Book your theory test’ (under Driving tests).  
   • Follow the on-screen instructions to pick:  
     a. the type of test – ‘car theory test’  
     b. the test centre that’s easiest for you to reach  
     c. a date and time that suits you

3. Pay and confirm  
   • Enter your details, pay the £23 fee, and press ‘Confirm’.  
   • You’ll get an email or text straight away with:  
     – your booking reference number  
     – the time, date and address of the test  
     – what ID to bring (your photocard licence)

4. On the day  
   • Arrive at least 15 minutes early.  
   • Bring your photocard provisional licence (both parts if it’s the older paper licence + passport).  
   • You may be turned away without the correct ID and you’ll need to pay again.

5. If you need extra help  
   • You can book by phone on 0300 200 1122 (Welsh language line 0300 200 1133).  
   • You can ask for Welsh voice-over, extra time, or other support when you book.

Relevant link: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I want to sell my business in Wales. What steps should I take to ensure I follow the correct procedures and get the necessary support?
generated_response:
Below is a simple, step-by-step outline of what to do when you plan to sell your business in Wales and where to get help.

1. Clarify what you are selling  
   • Decide whether you are selling the whole limited company/partnership or just its assets (stock, goodwill, equipment).  
   • Gather up-to-date accounts, contracts, employee records, leases and licences so a buyer can carry out “due diligence”.

2. Get the business valued  
   • Use a professional valuer, accountant or business transfer agent so the asking price is realistic.  
   • Ask Business Wales for a list of accredited advisers and any subsidised support.

3. Take professional advice early  
   • Accountant – to check the tax position (Capital Gains Tax, Entrepreneurs’ Relief/Business Asset Disposal Relief, VAT).  
   • Solicitor – to draft or review the sale agreement, handle property transfers and ensure you meet employment law.  
   • If you have employees, make sure you follow TUPE rules on informing and consulting staff.

4. Prepare for the legal sale process  
   • Sign a confidentiality (non-disclosure) agreement with potential buyers.  
   • Provide an information pack (financials, contracts, licences).  
   • Negotiate Heads of Terms setting out price and key conditions.  
   • Your solicitor then prepares the Sale and Purchase Agreement (SPA).

5. Notify the authorities and complete tax formalities  
   Limited company  
   • File form SH03 at Companies House if shares are transferred.  
   • Update company officers and Persons with Significant Control if you keep the company shell.  
   Sole trader or partnership  
   • Tell HMRC you have stopped trading using form Cessation of Business.  
   • Submit a final Self Assessment return showing any gains or losses.  
   All structures  
   • Pay any outstanding PAYE, VAT or Corporation Tax.  
   • Cancel licences, permits and business rates when ownership passes.

6. Handle employees correctly  
   • Give staff written notice of the sale and the proposed transfer date.  
   • Transfer employees’ contracts and accrued rights to the buyer under TUPE unless agreed otherwise.

7. Collect the sale proceeds and keep records  
   • Make sure you receive payment in the form agreed (lump sum, staged payments, earn-out, etc.).  
   • Keep all sale documents and accounts for at least 6 years in case HMRC asks for evidence.

8. Seek post-sale support  
   • Talk to an independent financial adviser about investing the proceeds and managing any tax liability.  
   • If you plan to retire, check your State Pension forecast and consider National Insurance contributions.

Key sources of help  
• Business Wales helpline: 03000 6 03000  
• “Closing or selling your business” guidance on GOV.UK – https://www.gov.uk/browse/business

prompt: My employer says they can't contribute to my workplace pension because I earn less than £520 a month. Is that allowed?
generated_response:
Yes and no – it depends on how much you earn over the whole tax year.

1. Auto-enrolment  
   • Your employer only has to put you into a pension automatically if you earn more than £10,000 a year (about £833 a month).  

2. Opting in with employer contributions  
   • If you earn at least £6,240 a year (that’s £520 a month or more), you can write to your employer and ask to join the workplace pension.  
   • Once you do that, by law they must:  
     1. Enrol you in the scheme within six weeks.  
     2. Pay the minimum employer contribution (currently 3% of qualifying earnings).  

3. Earning under £6,240 a year  
   • If your yearly pay is below £6,240, you can still ask to join, but the employer does not have to contribute (they may choose to).

4. What to do next  
   1. Check your total expected earnings for the tax year.  
   2. If you will reach £6,240 or more, write to your employer asking to join the workplace pension and quote the Pensions Act 2008 “opt-in” right.  
   3. Keep a copy of your request and any reply.  
   4. If they refuse or do not act within six weeks, contact The Pensions Regulator on 0345 600 1011 or use the form on GOV.UK.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/workplace-pensions/joining

prompt: I'm a landlord in Wales and I want to rent out a room. Do I need to have a written tenancy agreement, and what should it include?
generated_response:
You do not always have to give a written agreement, but in most cases it is strongly advised – and in many situations the law in Wales now says you must.

1. Work out the resident’s status  
   • You will ALSO live in the property and share kitchen/bathroom – they are probably a lodger (‘excluded occupier’).  
   • You will NOT live there – or you live elsewhere in the building but they have their own bedroom and share only communal areas – they will normally have a ‘standard occupation contract’ under the Renting Homes (Wales) Act 2016.

2. If it is a standard occupation contract (you do not live there):  
   You MUST give the contract-holder a written statement of the terms within 14 days of the date they move in. Include:  
   a. Names and addresses of you (the landlord) and the contract-holder.  
   b. Address of the property and the room let.  
   c. Start date and whether it is fixed-term or periodic.  
   d. Rent amount, when it is due and how it can be increased.  
   e. Deposit amount, which government-approved protection scheme you are using, and the scheme’s prescribed information.  
   f. Services included (e.g. utilities, Wi-Fi, cleaning) and any extra charges.  
   g. Repair responsibilities, right to peaceful occupation and other fundamental terms set by law.  
   h. Any supplementary terms (for example rules about pets, smoking, parking, guests).  
   i. Any additional terms you and the contract-holder have agreed.  
   j. Your Rent Smart Wales registration and licence numbers, emergency contact details, plus copies of:  
      • annual gas safety certificate (if applicable)  
      • electrical installation condition report  
      • Energy Performance Certificate (EPC)  
   Keep a copy signed by both parties.

3. If it is a lodger arrangement (you live in the home):  
   The law does not force you to have a written agreement, but it is good practice. A simple lodger agreement should cover:  
   • names and address  
   • room being rented and any shared areas  
   • rent, payment dates, what is included in the rent  
   • deposit and when it will be returned  
   • notice period for ending the arrangement (often 28 days)  
   • house rules (use of kitchen, guests, smoking, pets, parking, quiet hours)  
   • when you may enter the room for inspection or repairs.

4. Check other legal duties  
   • You must be registered and (if you manage the tenancy yourself) licensed with Rent Smart Wales.  
   • If five or more people will live in the property and share facilities, you may need an HMO licence from the local council.  
   • Fit smoke alarms, carbon-monoxide alarms (in rooms with solid-fuel or gas appliances) and test them on the first day of occupation.

5. Where to get more help  
   • Rent Smart Wales helpline: 03000 133 344  
   • Citizens Advice Cymru: 0800 702 2020  
   • Welsh Government guidance on the Renting Homes (Wales) Act 2016.  
   A template written statement is available on gov.wales.

prompt: What happens if I lose my driving licence abroad?
generated_response:
If your full UK driving licence is lost or stolen while you are abroad, you must:

1. Report it locally  
   • Go to the nearest police station as soon as possible.  
   • Ask for a written report or crime reference – you may need this for insurance or car-hire purposes.

2. Tell your hire-car company or insurer (if relevant)  
   • They will advise whether you can keep driving and what extra documents they need.

3. Decide if you need a replacement right away  
   • You can usually continue driving the hire car if you still have your passport and the police report, but some countries insist on a physical licence.  
   • If you must keep driving and the authorities won’t accept a police report, you will need someone in the UK to send you a replacement licence.

4. Replace the licence  
   Option A – Apply online (only if you still have your UK address, a valid UK passport and can receive the licence at a UK address):  
      a) Go to GOV.UK and search “replace a lost driving licence”.  
      b) Pay the £20 fee by debit or credit card.  
   Option B – Apply by phone from overseas:  
      a) Call DVLA on +44 300 790 6801 (Monday to Friday 8 am–7 pm, Saturday 8 am–2 pm UK time).  
      b) Have your passport number and National Insurance number ready, plus a payment card.  
   • DVLA only posts replacement licences to UK addresses. Arrange for someone you trust to receive it and courier it to you if you still need it abroad.

5. On your return to the UK  
   • If you did not replace the licence while away, apply for a replacement straight away before driving here.  
   • Keep the police report in case DVLA asks for evidence.

Relevant GOV.UK page: https://www.gov.uk/browse/abroad

prompt: My child is 15 and still in education. They live in Wales and go to their nearest school, which is 3 miles away. Can they get help with school transport?
generated_response:
Your child is likely entitled to free home-to-school transport.  
In Wales, local councils must provide free transport for learners aged 11-16 who:  
• attend their nearest suitable school, and  
• live 3 miles or more from that school.  

Because your 15-year-old attends the nearest suitable school and lives 3 miles away, they meet the distance rule.

What to do next  
1. Find your local council’s website (you can search “home to school transport” plus your council name).  
2. Download or request the school transport application form. If you prefer, phone the council’s education or transport team and ask them to send a paper form.  
3. Complete the form with:  
   • your child’s name, address and date of birth  
   • the school they attend  
   • proof of address (for example, a council tax or utility bill).  
4. Send the form and any documents by the deadline the council sets (some accept applications all year; others have term-time cut-offs).  
5. The council will write to you to confirm if transport is approved and explain pick-up times or provide a bus pass.

If you need help filling in the form or confirming eligibility, ring your council’s education department – they must advise you.

More information: https://www.gov.uk/browse/education

prompt: Can I hail a taxi anywhere or only in certain areas?
generated_response:
You can only hail (flag down) a taxi that is licensed to pick up passengers without a booking.

1. Black cabs in London and “hackney carriage” taxis in other towns and cities can be hailed on the street or taken from a taxi rank, as long as their “TAXI” or “For Hire” light is on.  
2. Private-hire vehicles and minicabs (including most app-booked cars) are not allowed to pick you up if you wave them down. By law they must be pre-booked through a licensed operator.  
3. A taxi may drop you outside the area where it is licensed, but it can only pick up (when hailed or at a rank) inside its own licensing area.  
4. If you are not sure, look for:  
   • a meter inside the vehicle  
   • a council licence plate on the back or side  
   • the illuminated “TAXI” sign on the roof (London) or “For Hire” sign (elsewhere).  
5. If no licensed taxi is available to hail, phone or use an app to book a private-hire car, wait for the driver’s confirmation, and check the vehicle and driver details before getting in.

For more on taxi and private-hire rules see GOV.UK: https://www.gov.uk/browse/driving

prompt: I need to contact HMRC because I have a mental health condition and find it hard to communicate over the phone. What options are available to me for getting support?
generated_response:
You do not have to rely on the phone if that makes things difficult.  The main alternatives are:  

1. Use HMRC’s webchat  
   • Go to GOV-UK, choose the tax topic you need help with and select “Ask HMRC online” or “webchat”.  
   • Write your questions in real time and save a transcript when you finish.  

2. Ask HMRC’s Extra Support Team for help  
   • Either mention “I need extra support because of my mental-health condition” in webchat or include it in a letter (see step 4).  
   • They can: arrange longer appointments, give you a single named adviser, or meet you face-to-face if that is easier.  

3. Appoint someone to deal with HMRC for you  
   • Fill in form 64-8 (or use the “trusted helper” service in your Personal Tax Account) to authorise a family member, friend or adviser.  
   • Once authorised, that person can speak or write to HMRC on your behalf.  

4. Write instead of calling  
   • The postal address is on the relevant GOV-UK page or on any recent HMRC letter.  
   • Mark the top of your letter “Extra Support Team – mental-health condition” and include your National Insurance number.  

5. Use Relay UK or BSL video relay (if you prefer text relay or sign language)  
   • Dial 18001 followed by the usual HMRC number for a text relay call, or use the free video relay link on GOV-UK.  

6. In Scotland you can also get practical help from Citizens Advice Scotland (0800 028 1456) if you need someone to sit with you while you contact HMRC.  

Relevant GOV-UK starting point: https://www.gov.uk/browse/tax

prompt: I'm considering taking in a child for more than 28 days. Do I need to register with the local council and what happens during the process?
generated_response:
If the child is not your own or a close relative (grand-parent, brother, sister, aunt, uncle or step-parent) and they will be living with you for 28 days or more, this counts as a private fostering arrangement and you must tell your local council.

What to do  
1. Contact the children’s services (social work) team at your local council.  
   • If the move is planned, you should give them at least 6 weeks’ notice.  
   • If the child arrives in an emergency, tell the council within 48 hours.  

2. Give basic details about:  
   • you and anyone else who lives in your home  
   • the child and their parents  
   • how long the arrangement is expected to last  
   • the care you’ll be providing.  

3. The council will:  
   • arrange home visits and interview you, anyone aged 16 + in the household, the child and their parents  
   • carry out safeguarding checks (for example PVG/Disclosure Scotland) and may ask for references  
   • assess that your home is safe and suitable, and advise on any changes needed.  

4. If the council is satisfied, the arrangement can go ahead. They will continue to:  
   • visit at least every 6 weeks during the first year, then every 3 months  
   • offer advice and support to you, the child and the child’s parents  
   • review the arrangement until it ends or becomes formal fostering/adoption.  

Failing to notify the council is an offence and could lead to enforcement action.

Find out more: https://www.gov.uk/private-fostering

prompt: I need to apply for school transport for my child who has special educational needs and disabilities. How do I find out if my child is eligible and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Most free home-to-school transport for children with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND) is arranged by your local council.

1. Check the council’s rules  
   • Visit GOV.UK and enter your postcode at “Apply for home to school transport” (https://www.gov.uk/apply-for-home-to-school-transport).  
   • You will be taken to your council’s page, which explains its SEND transport policy, the distance limits and any medical or mobility criteria.

2. Gather the information you will need  
   • Your child’s name, date of birth and home address.  
   • The name and address of the school they attend or have been offered.  
   • Details of your child’s SEND (for example a copy of their Education, Health and Care (EHC) plan or medical letters).  
   • Any benefits you receive that give automatic entitlement (for example, the highest rate of Disability Living Allowance mobility component).

3. Apply to the council  
   • Most councils let you download a paper form or request one by phone if you prefer.  
   • Fill in the form and send any evidence they ask for (EHC plan, medical proof, benefit letters).  
   • Keep copies of everything you send.

4. Wait for the decision  
   • Councils usually reply within 20 working days.  
   • If agreed, they will tell you the type of transport (for example a minibus, taxi or travel pass) and the start date.

5. If the application is refused  
   • You can appeal. The refusal letter will explain how to do this and the time limits.  
   • Provide any extra evidence that supports your child’s needs.

If you are unsure or cannot get online, phone your council’s education or SEND transport team and ask them to post the application pack.

prompt: I'm a retired teacher in Wales and I'm worried about paying Council Tax. How can I find out if I qualify for a discount and what happens if I don't pay?
generated_response:
Worrying about Council Tax is common and there is help available.  
Follow these steps to check for discounts, get help and avoid problems if you fall behind.

1. Check the main discounts and reductions  
   a. Single person discount – 25 % off if you live alone.  
   b. Council Tax Reduction (CTR) – means-tested help for pensioners and people on a modest or low income in Wales. Even a small pension or savings do not automatically disqualify you, so it is worth applying.  
   c. Disability reduction – if your home has been adapted for a disabled adult or child.  
   d. Carer discount – if you care for someone in your home at least 35 hours a week.  
   e. Severe mental impairment exemption – for someone medically certified as such.  

2. Apply or ask about eligibility  
   • Find your local council’s Council Tax team: enter your postcode at gov.uk/find-local-council.  
   • Phone and ask for a “Council Tax Reduction application” or a “discount review”. Ask them to post a paper form if online forms are difficult.  
   • Have your National Insurance number, pension details and any other income information to hand.  

3. If you are already struggling to pay  
   a. Contact the Council Tax team immediately – before the payment is missed if possible. Ask for:  
      • a revised instalment plan (they can spread payments over 12 months instead of 10)  
      • short-term deferral while a discount or CTR application is processed  
   b. Get independent advice if needed – Age Cymru (0800 022 3444) or Citizens Advice (0800 702 2020).  

4. What happens if you do not pay and do nothing  
   • Reminder notice after the first missed payment – giving 7 days to catch up.  
   • Court summons and £ costs added if still unpaid.  
   • Liability order – the council can then:  
     – take the money from your pension or benefits, or  
     – pass the debt to enforcement agents (bailiffs) who add their own fees and can take belongings.  
   Contacting the council early almost always prevents it reaching this stage.  

Relevant link  
Council Tax guidance and local-council finder: https://www.gov.uk/browse/housing-local-services

prompt: I'm looking to get a parking permit for my home in Scotland. How do I find out if my local council offers this and what steps should I take?
generated_response:
Resident parking permits are handled by your local council. Each council sets its own rules, prices and ways to apply, so the first step is to confirm which council area you live in and whether it runs a resident-permit scheme.

1. Find your council  
   • Go to GOV.UK and type “find your local council” or use this direct link: https://www.gov.uk/find-local-council  
   • Enter your home postcode and press ‘Search’. The page will show the name of your council and a link to its website.

2. Check if permits are offered  
   • Follow the link to your council’s website.  
   • Look for sections called “Parking”, “Resident permits” or “Transport and streets”.  
   • If you don’t see anything, use the site’s search box and type “resident parking permit”.

3. Read the permit rules  
   • Note who can apply (e.g. residents only, blue badge holders) and which documents you need, such as proof of address (utility bill or Council Tax bill) and vehicle log-book (V5C).  
   • Check the cost, how many permits you can have, and any restrictions on visitor permits.

4. Apply  
   • Most councils let you apply online. There is usually a form where you upload scans or photos of your documents and pay by debit or credit card.  
   • If you prefer to apply by post or in person, the council website should give the address and phone number.  
   • Keep a copy of your application reference or receipt until the permit arrives.

5. Display or activate the permit  
   • Some councils issue a paper permit to place in your windscreen; others use a virtual permit linked to your vehicle registration. Follow the instructions in the confirmation email or letter.

6. Renew on time  
   • Permits normally last 6 or 12 months. Make a note of the expiry date and allow a week or two for renewal, as parking fines can be issued if a permit lapses.

If you cannot find any permit information on the council website, phone the council’s general enquiries number (shown on the same GOV.UK results page) and ask for the parking team.
